Extreme Mortman: When DC Laughed at Itself: Dan Glickman and the Golden Age of Political Comedy

C-SPAN(1.8M) ·40:22 ·229 views · 2026-05-07
Dan Glickman (Former U.S. Secretary of Agriculture) Howard Mortman (Host, C-SPAN)
Former Secretary of Agriculture Dan Glickman argues that self-deprecating humor is an essential, yet vanishing, tool for effective political leadership and bipartisan cooperation.
TL;DR Former Secretary of Agriculture Dan Glickman argues that self-deprecating humor is an essential, yet vanishing, tool for effective political leadership and bipartisan cooperation. Leadership Utility: Glickman cites President Eisenhower’s belief that a sense of humor is...

TL;DR

Former Secretary of Agriculture Dan Glickman argues that self-deprecating humor is an essential, yet vanishing, tool for effective political leadership and bipartisan cooperation.

The Strategic Value of Political Humor

  • Leadership Utility: Glickman cites President Eisenhower’s belief that a sense of humor is essential for leadership, as it makes a leader more likable and persuasive.
  • Self-Deprecation: Defusing Criticism: Glickman posits that when politicians highlight their own "blemishes" through humor, it takes the "edge off" the jokes made by others and humanizes them to a skeptical public.
  • The "Carson" Model vs. Modern Comedy:
      • Glickman contrasts the "low intensity," unifying humor of Johnny Carson with the "high intensity," overtly partisan humor of modern late-night hosts like Stephen Colbert and Jimmy Kimmel.
      • He argues that modern political comedy often prevents viewers from "relaxing," whereas classic humor helped bridge divides.

Bipartisanship and the "Golden Age" of Cooperation

  • The Dole-Clinton Dynamic: Despite being political rivals, President Bill Clinton and Senate Minority Leader Bob Dole maintained a genuine fondness for one another.
  • Unlikely Advocacy: Secretary of Agriculture Appointment: Glickman reveals that Bob Dole (a Republican) was the primary advocate who convinced Bill Clinton (a Democrat) to appoint Glickman to the Cabinet after Glickman lost his 1994 re-election bid.
  • Confirmation Record: Glickman was confirmed by the Senate with a 93-0 vote, a feat he believes would be nearly impossible in the current polarized political environment.

Career Transitions: From the Heartland to Hollywood

  • USDA Leadership: As Secretary, Glickman navigated the transition of American agriculture into the 21st century, focusing on innovation and maintaining the "envy of the world" status of the U.S. food system.
  • MPAA Tenure: After the USDA, Glickman served as Chairman and CEO of the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA).
      • Industry Focus: His work centered on intellectual property, film piracy, and overseeing the movie rating system.
      • Personal Connection: His favorite film is *Animal House*, which he claims mirrored his own chaotic fraternity experience at the University of Michigan (1962–1966).

Advocacy for Transparency and C-SPAN

  • Early Support: Glickman was a seminal supporter of C-SPAN, working alongside founder Brian Lamb (whom he met while interning in the 1960s).
  • Institutional Access: Glickman played a key role in the effort to allow C-SPAN cameras into the House of Representatives, arguing that it was vital for the public—and Congress members themselves—to see the legislative process in action.

Notable Quotes

"The key to late-night political humor is that it targets those qualities that we, the politicians, don't want you to see. But if we were to bring out our blemishes into the open, it takes the edge off the jokes that other people make about us." — Dan Glickman
"Dole said to me, 'I just have one question for you... Will this get you out of Kansas forever?' I said, 'Well, if you're asking if I would ever run against you, no.' And so Dole was a big helper." — Dan Glickman
"I decided that I had an interesting career... but I also felt like the humor that exists in this country is kind of missing in action when it comes to natural self-deprecating humor in the political sphere." — Dan Glickman
"I picked him for this job because the Department of Agriculture and rural America, more than ever, need a leader who is experienced, aggressive, and innovative. I know he will be an advocate and a spokesman for making sure that American agriculture enters the 21st century on a prosperous and solid foundation. Our agricultural system is the envy of the world, and it must remain so. I also chose Dan Glickman for his common sense and his good humor. He says he always wears a sunflower on his lapel to remind him of where he's from, the values of the heartland that make him what he is. I hope and expect he will keep wearing that sunflower and keep us in a sunny disposition. Mr. Glickman." "Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you. I better get a supply of them." "That was President Bill Clinton, alongside Dan Glickman, both appearing at the White House in the fabulous Rose Garden. It was December 28th, 1994, just three days after Christmas and just 23 days after Hanukkah. So President Clinton gave Dan Glickman a lovely seasonal present: a post in his cabinet. You heard Dan Glickman open with a joke. That's something he's done many, many times, and it's something you'll experience firsthand in this episode of C-SPAN's podcast 'Extreme Mortman', because we have a very special guest in our studio. It's Dan Glickman himself. We're talking politics and comedy with the former Congressman, the former Agriculture Secretary, the former top lobbyist for the movie industry, a current fellow at the Bipartisan Policy Center, Chairman of the Board of Hunger Free America, and the author of 'Laughing at Myself: My Education in Congress, on the Farm, and at the Movies'. We're thrilled to have him here in person in the glass-enclosed nerve center of Extreme Mortman in beautiful downtown Capitol Hill. So get ready to throw your head back and roar in laughter with an American original and a true political junkie, Dan Glickman!" "Thank you, Howard. Such an introduction, I've never had one like this before." "Oh, you've never had any introduction before? That's right, the first one, nothing like that. All right, all right, it was so much, I want to talk about the book and about your career and all that. Walk us through that clip with President Clinton introducing you in 1994." "Well, the interesting thing is the story of how I got the job. So I had lost my election about a month and a half beforehand. I was part of the sweep out in the Gingrich revolution; historically we remember 1994 when Gingrich had the 'Contract with America', and so I lost, and by a pretty good margin. And so I was for about three days, I was kind of down, thinking, 'What am I going to do the rest of my life?'. And then somebody said to me—it was actually Pat Roberts, who was a senator from Kansas at the time—he said, (he was actually the congressman from Kansas, he became a senator) and a good friend, he said, 'Well, I think you could be Secretary of Agriculture if you really pushed it.' And it turned out that the then-then Secretary of Agriculture, his name was Mike Espy, and he kind of got in a little bit of trouble and the President had to let him go, and he had to get somebody to replace him. And the Democrats had just lost the Congress, both houses of Congress. But I had a secret person that was helping me besides Roberts, and it was Bob Dole. And Bob Dole was the senator from Kansas at the time and so I went to Dole and I said, 'You know, I think that they may be interested in me being Secretary of Agriculture.' And Dole said to me, he says, 'Well, I just have one question for you.' I said, 'What's that?' He says, 'Will this get you out of Kansas forever?' And so I said, 'Well, if you're asking if I would ever run against you, no.' And so Dole was a big helper; Dole was majority leader. And so I had Bob Dole, and Dole went to Clinton and Clinton, I think was nice, thought that I probably could get confirmed, and I'd been on the Agriculture Committee. But the fact that Dole was helping me was the icing on the cake. And so I was nominated and confirmed 93 to zero. I don't know where the other seven went, trying to find them since then. But can you imagine being confirmed with no negative votes whatsoever? Today, I don't think anybody's been confirmed unanimously, at least not for a cabinet position." "You know, the thing, one thing about that story that also is amazing is that the Republican leader is the one who went to a Democratic president and said, 'Here is your cabinet pick,' which also is unheard of now." "It was unheard of, and you know, they liked each other, even though Dole was—could be quite partisan, but he was also a very effective leader. Like, we haven't had anybody like him since then, I don't think. And Clinton always admired Dole; they were different personalities. You know, they were like, Clinton was the boisterous, could talk to anybody anywhere at any time, and Dole was more taciturn, although he had a great biting sense of humor. And—but there was a general—genuine fondness there, and I think I was the beneficiary of that." "Okay, so I want to get to the jokes and the comedy in the book, but I have to point out, you have been a long-time booster and fan and supporter and friend of C-SPAN. And why—tell me about your history with C-SPAN that goes all the way back." "Well, I knew Brian Lamb, who was basically the founder of this, because he worked for Senator Peter Dominick for years. He was a senator from Colorado, Republican senator, and I was an intern in Dominick's office when I was in law school at GW. So I knew Brian well and he liked me and I liked him, and he had a great personality. And then of course, he started C-SPAN, and then and then I was elected to Congress, and I did everything I could to help C-SPAN. Back in the early days, C-SPAN could not get its cameras into the House, and then later into the Senate. And then even though they got their cameras in, you couldn't show them in House offices. And there was a member of Congress who didn't particularly want—didn't like C-SPAN or didn't want them there, his name was Charlie Rose from North Carolina, who was also a good friend of mine. And so I went to Brian, I said 'I want to help you get C-SPAN into the House,' and it was—we accomplished it. I think it was a seminal moment in C-SPAN's life because you know, the whole essence of C-SPAN was showing what was happening in the Congress, and now that the Congress can see themselves in action, it made a big difference." "Okay, great. I want to talk about the book 'Laughing at Myself'. Why did you write the book 'Laughing at Myself'?" "I wanted to see myself on the New York Times bestseller list. And it didn't happen—it happened on my bestseller list, but anyway. No, I decided that I had an interesting career that—this you know, son and grandson of Jewish immigrants who became a congressman and and and became active in agriculture. And then I went into—became a lobbyist for Hollywood for a while and I had this interesting career, coming from the heartland. I also felt like, and I still feel like, there's—there's the humor that exists in this country is kind of missing in action when it comes to natural self-deprecating humor in the political sphere. And so our great presidents, the really great ones, were always ones that had very good senses of humor. You go backwards and Reagan, Roosevelt, Abraham Lincoln, and and there were others, and and so I—just—it's missing, and and it was missing in terms of in Congress and the political humor. And the humor that was there was almost always negative, mean, biting humor, and I said, 'Well, you know what, it made a big difference in my life.' And Eisenhower, who was a Kansan, and we you know, claim him at least as a Kansan, used to say that a good sense of humor was essential for leadership. Because if you—if you're funny and people liked you, they would do what you wanted them to do; it was easier to persuade them. And he talked about that all his time, including up until D-Day, he used to talk about the necessity of a good sense of humor. So that's why I kind of wrote the book." "You know, it's interesting, so many people in Washington write books about their career and the book's focus is how great that person is and how lousy their opponents are and how important their advice was and how they influenced the course of American history. You wrote a book about jokes, which seems that doesn't put you in the club, like that's like nobody writes books like that." "Well, part of that I had parents who were—they just they told it was humor. The whole life was filled with humor, and it wasn't always love and happiness in my family, I don't want to tell you that, this was a Jewish family, we had our problems. But but it was almost it was always a good sense of humor and and so my dad was from the the kind of Rodney Dangerfield, Henny Youngman, the Borscht Belt schtick humor, and my mother was more natural, she could make funny jokes out of what was happening in the world. So that was very much a part of our lives and and so I took that with me and I think it helped me in my years in public life a lot." "You mentioned your family. And actually, I want to read an excerpt from your book. By the way, I'm going to ask you one more question about the book and you probably got this question a lot as you've been talking about it and so forgive me if if you're tired of hearing this question. What is your book's ISBN number?" "I can't tell you because I was in the intelligence world and I have several things that I can't tell you and that's one that I can't tell you." "That's one you can't tell you can't disclose, I totally respect that, totally respect that. Okay, what the hell is he talking about? Totally respect that. Okay, here's an excerpt from the book: 'My mother used to tell us this joke about their marriage. I never once thought of divorcing your father, she said. Murdering him, yes. My dad told us a similar joke: When your mother turns 40, I'm going to trade her in for two 20s.' Yes, did that kill in the Glickman household?" "That was—that was kind of the joke, you know, he once said, 'You know, I proposed to your mother in a garage and then I couldn't back out.' You know, you know so so this was kind of the—we had a lot of that. And my dad, it wasn't all natural, he used to subscribe to joke services, this would be before the internet. And when he died, we found ten legal pads filled—he had handwritten jokes—and then people would call him from all over the country. Former Speaker Jim Wright, who was born on the same day as my dad, they became friends as a result of my service here. Wright used to call my dad up eight, ten times a year and said, 'Milton, I'm giving this speech. Do you have a joke for me?' It was like, you know, who would have thought? Maybe my dad made Jim Wright's career, who knows." "Okay, all right, we're going to play some clips now and get Secretary Dan Glickman to respond and react. Okay, we'll start with two from Dan Glickman's time as a member of Congress. The first clip is from early on, a House floor speech, December 5th, 1980." "I would like to follow up on some of the comments of my colleague from New Jersey. I think there are three issues in this case. One is, is there responsibility on the part of the Army, even if there is no legal liability? And I think that the answer is yes. We have a man who, over 20 years ago, was force-fed LSD during the course of an investigation without his consent. And I would like to read a portion of a letter that the General Counsel of the Army wrote to our Chairman, George Danielson, on December 1st. And I quote: 'The circumstances surrounding the prolonged detention and interrogation of Mr. Thornwell, the surreptitious administration of LSD to him in June 1961, and the subsequent failure by the Army to advise him that he had been given LSD or to provide him appropriate care for almost 15 years after the fact, are set out in my predecessor's report. Suffice it to say that, due to the unconscionable nature of the wrongs done to him,' here is the United States Army saying, 'due to the unconscionable nature of the wrongs done to him, we feel that the Army has a moral obligation to compensate Mr. Thornwell adequately for his injuries.' Both the Department of Justice and the Department of the Army have acknowledged that this man was wronged by being force-fed LSD, and therefore we've made the judgment, yes, there is some responsibility to compensate him. Incidentally, while we have kind of downplayed his condition, the facts indicate that the man has during this period of time and afterwards become somewhat of using this term in its kindest senses I can, a mental vegetable, and therefore the LSD did have enormous effect." "Okay, I want to get, I want you to explain a little bit more about that. But the first thing that comes to mind, to my mind, when you say 'mental vegetable', that's a foreshadowing of you being Secretary of Agriculture. Is that right?" "That's right, and we did a lot of research on that, we could never find a mental vegetable. This reminds me of the time that Bill Clinton gave a State of the Union message and he talked a lot about bad federal spending, you know fraud, and he talked about, believe it or not, the Agriculture Department is giving money for plant stress. And I was Secretary at the time and I said, 'Mr. President, the plant's not going to a psychiatrist. The stress is due to heat, stress and disease.' So maybe I had all this on my mind, the relationship between agriculture and mental health. But that was an issue, I think there was a relative of the Thornwell family that came to me and came to the Judiciary Committee, on which I served, and we had the authority to provide special legislation to compensate people for wrongs the government did. And so I'm remembering that case more and more as I'm hearing my own voice talk about it. But it's been a long time that I haven't dealt with this—that issue." "By the way, what does it feel like to hear your own voice from 1980?" "It's rehabilitating in my own soul." "Nice, nice, nice, very good. All right, and here is a second clip we want to play from Secretary Glickman's time in Congress, from May 21st, 1992, toward the end of his time in Congress. Here's Dan Glickman." "Mr. Speaker, this country has a lot of problems. Jobs moving overseas, lack of access to healthcare, violent crime, urban problems. But one thing that keeps us in good spirits is our sense of humor. A recognition that none of us is so important that we can't laugh at ourselves. Tomorrow night is Johnny Carson's last night on The Tonight Show. Johnny Carson is a uniquely American comedian from the heartland of America, who has never so wrapped up in his own self-importance that he couldn't relate to the problems and needs of average Americans. This country will suffer a profound loss when Johnny Carson leaves The Tonight Show. While I won't miss his frequent jabs at Congress, which have been very frequent lately, America will miss his good-natured and genuinely funny and uniquely American humor. That humor has helped to keep Americans laughing in bad times and in good times, and it has kept even the most important people in America very humble indeed. We will miss you, Johnny. This country will miss you very much. We hope that your humor stays alive and that we will continue to see you in this great country of ours." "So I did that to try to get on the Johnny Carson Show." "You took away my one question I had about that clip, and it didn't work. But I will have to tell you, I could do that same speech right now. And the fact of the matter is, there was nobody like Johnny Carson, and you know, generationally most people don't even know know who he is. But he could be so incredibly funny without being overtly political. Today the people who are on late-night television, they're funny, you know Colbert's funny and and Jimmy Fallon, Jimmy Kimmel are funny, but it's almost all political. It's like it's not what I call warm human what—I remember the lady who had the funny-looking potato chips and Johnny ate one while she was sitting on the side, you know? Or the 102-year-old farmer that was doing something crazy or or remember when he threw the—the hatchet at Ed Ames, you know?" "Yeah, the tomahawk, the tomahawk, and they said, you know, the jokes—I didn't know you were Jewish. Yeah, didn't know you're Jewish." "But anyway, but Carson was just incredibly kind of like relaxing, and you know, my parents used to stay up every night and watched him, and you know it's like—and it was low intensity rather than high intensity comedy. But I loved it." "Do you now, for late-night comedians, you mentioned a couple current ones, do you watch the shows, you watch them on YouTube?" "I watch all—I mean episodically I watch all the shows, and they're interesting, and they politically sometimes they make me feel good because they're almost—well, Jimmy Fallon's more of a vaudevillian, he's not as political. But the other two, Kimmel and and Colbert are very political. And especially Colbert. They're—but they're funny. But they're political, and you know, you don't relax when you—when you hear their humor. You know, you relax with Johnny Carson." "All right, you took away the one question I had: Did you get on the Carson Show? And you didn't. But did they ever acknowledge or did—did anybody notice that you said nice things about him on the floor of the US Congress?" "No, but indirectly there was a other thing that during Saturday Night Live in the early days, it was a 1980 presidential election and they must have—they had a cameraman whose name was Glickman, no relation to me. But anyway for a while they were doing the presidential race and they had Carter, Reagan, and Glickman. And of course I claimed that the Glickman was me, and in the—it was a third, a third, a third of the total vote. And maybe that's what ultimately got me interested in Johnny Carson. Obviously Reagan won that race." "He won that race and that's why Bob Dole wants you you not run for Senate. All right, now we move into Dan Glickman's time as Secretary of Agriculture. First off, from March 21st, 1995, his confirmation hearing in the Senate, here's someone we've talked about, Kansas Republican Bob Dole." "I think we need an experienced and committed advocate at the Department of Agriculture. This is a very difficult time. And I might agree with Pat Leahy: I know Dan Glickman is very smart, in fact when he was chairman of the Intelligence Committee I used to tell people he's smarter than any of us, but he can't tell us anything because they're all secret. Now he can tell us all these things he knows. But when he made this decision to take this job, some may question his his judgment, but I—I didn't. I think it's—I think it's an honor for Dan Glickman, it's certainly an honor for the state of Kansas, because we have someone who'll do the job and do it right. That's—that's the important thing, somebody that we know, somebody we can trust, somebody we can talk to." "All right, thoughts on Bob Dole?" "Well, it brings back fond memories, because you know, Dole was instrumental in me getting the—getting the job. I remember I was in his office as they were voting, and they voted 94 to zero, which I think had a lot to do with Dole, and Dole's relationships over there and being from Kansas and and whatever, you know? And Dole wasn't necessarily all that funny when he announced me. But we had a—good times together and and it was the best job I ever had, to be honest with you, you know." "By the way, Bob Dole mentioned the Intelligence Committee, you mentioned earlier, is there any secret that you've never told that you can tell the listeners of this podcast?" "Well, there's two-three people in this room and I would probably have to kill you." "Oh, what a way to go! Are we all in? Are we all—yes? No, different all right, okay. Another clip, now January 15th, 1997, Secretary Glickman appears at the legendary National Press Club. It's lunchtime, and he's introduced by club president Sonya Hilgren." "Had things gone a bit differently, our speaker today, Agriculture Secretary Dan Glickman, might instead be a US Senator from Kansas. That's a job he always seemed to have his eyes on while representing the Wichita area during nine terms in the US House. Even as a student, while serving as an intern for Colorado Senator Peter Dominick, Glickman climbed into the Senator's chair because he wanted to see what it would feel like to be a sitting Senator. And I heard that from Brian Lamb of C-SPAN, who was here earlier this month." "And then Secretary Glickman opened with this line." "Sonya, it is a pleasure to be here with you, that you have had a very distinguished one-year period of time. I was there at the beginning, kind of. And and I've enjoyed watching you as you have been treated well by C-SPAN viewers. You have hosted people like Benjamin Netanyahu and Carol Channing. I guess I'm somewhere in the middle of those two people." "I thought that was really funny, so." "Yeah, well, you know, I used to tell people I actually wasn't the first Jewish Secretary of Agriculture. I was the first Jewish Secretary of Agriculture in America. But Joseph in the Bible was the first Secretary of Agriculture because remember he got the Pharaoh to do this—six years on, seven years-one year off in terms of fertilizer, making sure that the soil was fertile and having soil banks or having a grain reserves. And these were—lasted till today, so from Joseph to Glickman, you know." "Okay, I want to—I do want to ask about the convention. What's it like being in front of the convention, a national convention, to speak?" "You know, the—truth of the matter is, the larger the crowd it actually the easier it is to—to speak. But all I was thinking about was that 'Why didn't they nominate me?'" "That's so funny. Finally, we want to play a clip from Dan Glickman's time as Chairman and CEO of the Motion Picture Association of America. Here he is, December 1st, 2004, speaking to students at George Washington University." "I just wanted to say, nobody asked me my favorite movie, and I'm kind of nervous to tell you this, because it may speak to my intellectual acuity, but my favorite movie is 'Animal House'." "'Animal House', Secretary Glickman." "Yeah, you know, it's true, I've seen—I've seen that movie about 50 times maybe, and it reminded me of my fraternity at the University of Michigan where we there were no values, no—no barriers, and and in history. Fortunately none of those things came up during any of my confirmation hearings. But but you know, John Belushi and crew, it was such a great movie." "Wait, what year? You were at Michigan, what years?" "62 to 66." "The same year as Faber College in 'Animal House' would have been roughly the same year." "Roughly the same type Faber, I didn't know the Dean—Dean Wormer, that's right. But there were several characters in that book, and in my fraternity was—was truly an 'Animal House'. I wasn't like that. There were two of us in the fraternity that were kind of half-serious, but we obviously coped with the craziness. And we in fact I remember, they didn't have separate bedrooms in the fraternity, we all slept in one room, there were 70 beds. And there was—the windows were kept open because they didn't have heat up there, and we all had electric blankets. And the house ultimately burned down, and I remember my father came to visit me and he said, 'This isn't going to last, this this house is going to get demolished by fire,' and it did happen." "It strikes me, one way to problem have a problem with electric blanket burning is have a waterbed right next to it, for an emergency, is that..." "It could be, something, but we didn't—we didn't have that. Anyway, anyway, so no, that was certainly an interesting time of my life." "I want to—we're going to conclude shortly, and I want to talk about the book one more time and and all that. But your time at the Motion Picture Association of America, tell us a little bit about that and your son is a legend in Hollywood as well." "Well, I was you know the Motion Picture Association came up when Jack Valenti, who had been the head of it for years, decided to leave. And he knew me because I'd been on the House Judiciary Committee and was very involved with intellectual property issues and piracy issues. So then I had be—then I became obviously Secretary of Agriculture and I think he wanted when he decided to retire, he wanted somebody with some status, which I provided, and also somebody who knew something about film piracy and intellectual property issues. And my son had already become a producer and later is still a producer, my son's now the CEO of Miramax Studios in in Los Angeles, so he's been involved in the making of lots and lots of movies. And that was a very interesting job, particularly the part of the job I loved was I ran the rating system. So if a movie was going to be rated what, everything from G to—we didn't have X anymore, but NC-17—and everything in between. And I was very much involved in that system. It gave me a lot of power to be honest with you. And I liked that." "Okay, I tell us again about the book, how to get your book and where we can find, if people want to buy your wonderful book about jokes." "Well, they can always get it on Amazon or the University of Kansas Press, because I did come home to get it published. But but it's available on Amazon." "Oh, you went through an academic publisher for a joke book? Pardon?" "The University of Kansas Press, an academic publisher." "That hadn't even occurred to me, that's great. Okay." "You know, you you've elevated it." "You know, I was told if I had if to get to the New York Times, probably an academic publisher wasn't the way to go. But I said, 'I'm going to try anyway.' But in any event, so it's the book is out and it's done okay, and you know, it's like—it reads the way I would want it to read and I'm—it's all me in the book, and it's a lot about my family, and and it's a lot about comedy and how it's impacted my life and how it can impact other people's lives as well. So." "How many editions of the book have been printed?" "This is the second—second-two editions have been printed." "Okay, here's a joke that you as a guest and as a thank you to appearing, here's a joke all yours to use, you don't even have to cite me. Say the book is in its second printing, the first one got smeared. Do you want me to say that? Yeah, no, you could use that whenever you want." "The book is in its second printing, the first one got smeared." "Yeah, perfect, you nailed it. You nailed the ending on that. All right, all right, and now a bonus clip. Dan Glickman doesn't just tell funny jokes, he also sings funny songs, like the time he competed in the Funniest Celebrity in Washington contest, January 29th, 2003, and once again appearing at DC's premier comedy venue, the National Press Club." "Okay, since I left USDA I've given a lot of thought about what I want to do with my political future, and I decided instead of telling you jokes tonight, I'm going to sing you my life. And I hope there's nobody here from American Idol judging me tonight, but I would like the band to start in this a little rendition of what I want to be when I grow up. Two years ago this winter I left USDA. I didn't want to go but Bush told me I could not stay. He hired Veneman and then I really got annoyed. Golly goodness, I was unemployed. Dear Daniel R. Glickman, what are you to do? The Bushies are in power and there's nothing for you. Your next chance for comeback will be in '04, looks like you're going to have to go for Gore. Bet on Gore, bet on Gore, bet on Gore, Gore, Gore. You're going to have to bet on Gore." "You know, I was the—the first Jewish Agriculture Secretary and people ask me, 'What are your qualifications for this job?' and I said, 'Well, my mother would say: eat, eat, eat!'" "But Al Gore isn't running and Edwards is too new. Dick Gephardt lost the House and Joseph Lieberman's a Jew. John Kerry lacks charisma and I've never heard of Dean, how the heck are we supposed to win? Dear Daniel R. Glickman, don't be so forlorn. You have to get a job that doesn't legislate corn. You get or get busy, you better get fast. Check out the school in Cambridge, Mass." "Okay, with apologies to Stephen Sondheim. What was going through your mind, Secretary Glickman?" "I don't know, because I—that's I'm—thank God I've never heard that since that time. And I didn't win that year. I won at a later time when I was on again, but not singing, many years—future years. You must have—they must have not got the clip for that particular year." "I don't have a clip for what—what year do you remember what year?" "No, but it would have been in the last eight-ten years. But I did win that year and there was a little clip in the paper that I had won, you know so that made me feel good. But nobody picked up my singing voice. Actually, I sing better than I just heard, I want you to know that. And 'Officer Krupke', West Side Story, they have didn't come after me for copyright infringement, even though I'd been at the MPAA all those years. But a parody, a parody is often exempt from copyright, anyway, so." "Oh, I didn't know that." "Not always, it depends it depends on the nature of the song and where you do it." "I love how you maneuver between the—the legislature, the policymaker, and the comedy maker, just it's—you navigate that quite nicely." "Yeah, I'm very slick that way." "I I have to tell you, since we pulled the clip, I have not been able to get 'Officer Krupke' out of my mind, and it's just every morning I wake up and I sing to myself 'Officer Krupke' and I have to thank Secretary Dan Glickman for that. That's quite a milestone. By the way, you we've played three clips and we're almost over here from the National Press Club, you telling jokes. Are you going to create resonance at the National Press Club, like would that be your permanent venue to see the comedy stylings of Secretary Dan Glickman?" "You know, it's a lot easier than the—Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel at the Academy Awards, you know. So—so yeah, to the extent they want me, I'm available." "All right, well Secretary Glickman, thank you for joining us on the Extreme Mortman podcast. Hope you had a good time. Huge thank you for joining us. The book again, 'Laughing at Myself: My Education in Congress, on the Farm, and at the Movies'. And when you rush to order it right now on Amazon, make sure to tell them Extreme Mortman sent you. Any final words from you before we go to our closing music?" "I'm just thinking about my next few years and what I'm going to do to top what I've done in the past." "Fabulous, and now for our closing music, we return to 2003 and the Funniest Celebrity in Washington contest. Once again, sit back, relax, and enjoy the musical stylings of Daniel R. Glickman." "And then those crazy midterms provided me a plan. If Liddy Dole can do it then you bet Dan Glickman can. From Cabinet to Senate, it's the step that works for me. I'll be like Lamar from Tennessee. Yes, Daniel R. Glickman, I think this is best. Tell Summers that you're quitting and be like Cornel West. The Senate's your place, you belong on the floor. I don't think we're in Kansas anymore. Anymore, anymore, anymore, more, more. Yes, we're not in Kansas anymore."

Why Trump Can't Stand Still: The Iran War He Doesn't Know How to End

The Long Game Podcast(14.3K) ·68:12 ·4.7K views · 2026-05-07
Donald Trump (President, United States) Jake Sullivan (National Security Advisor) Jon Finer (Deputy National Security Advisor)
> "Iran has found itself in a standoff with a U.S.
TL;DR: The Trump administration is reportedly nearing a framework agreement to end the war with Iran and begin a 30-day negotiation period, even as military readiness declines and a diplomatic rift with Germany triggers a significant drawdown of U.S. troops in Europe. ...

TL;DR: The Trump administration is reportedly nearing a framework agreement to end the war with Iran and begin a 30-day negotiation period, even as military readiness declines and a diplomatic rift with Germany triggers a significant drawdown of U.S. troops in Europe.

The State of Play: Iran War and Potential Diplomacy

  • Negotiation Framework: A report from *Axios* suggests the U.S. and Iran are close to a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). This would initiate a 30-day period to negotiate a deal involving the opening of the Strait of Hormuz, limits on Iran’s nuclear program (potentially a moratorium on enrichment), and U.S. sanctions relief.
  • The Leverage Paradox: Sullivan and Finer argue that the current conflict has not improved the U.S. bargaining position.
      • Entrenched Hardliners: Iran’s leadership, specifically the IRGC, is more dug-in post-conflict, meaning the U.S. may have to offer better terms (e.g., primary sanctions relief) than were available pre-war.
      • Nuclear Baseline: A major hurdle is the lack of a current "nuclear baseline." The U.S. currently lacks verified data on Iran's uranium stockpiles and centrifuge counts, making a verifiable deal difficult to finalize quickly.
  • Trump’s Tactical Impatience: Jake Sullivan notes that Trump is frustrated by the regime’s resilience.
      • Inconsistency: The administration has vacillated between blockading the Strait of Hormuz and announcing humanitarian escort missions, only to cancel them shortly after.
      • Strategic Stubbornness: Iran appears to be waiting for the U.S. to blink under economic pressure, while Trump seeks any "button he can press" to end the war without appearing to validate the Obama-era JCPOA.

Economic Impacts and Military Readiness

  • The Market "Doom Loop": Jon Finer describes a cycle where Trump dangles the prospect of a deal to calm oil markets.
      • Price Creep: Despite messaging, national gas prices have hit roughly $4.50 per gallon.
      • Market Complacency: When markets remain calm, it ironically removes the political pressure on the White House to finalize a settlement, allowing the conflict to drag on.
  • Munitions Depletion: The conflict has severely taxed U.S. military inventories.
      • Inventory Crisis: Reports indicate that four out of seven primary munitions (including Tomahawks, Patriots, and SM-3/SM-6 interceptors) have fallen by more than half of their pre-war levels.
      • Recovery Timeline: Experts suggest it will take one to four years to rebuild these stockpiles, potentially leaving the U.S. vulnerable in other theaters like the Taiwan Strait or Eastern Europe.

Strategic Shifts in the Gulf: UAE Exits OPEC

  • Economic Diversification: The UAE’s decision to leave OPEC is driven by a desire to maximize oil production now to finance a transition toward clean technology and Artificial Intelligence.
  • The Saudi-UAE Rift: The move signals a structural rift between Abu Dhabi and Riyadh.
      • Proxy Competition: The two nations are increasingly on opposite sides of regional conflicts (Sudan, Yemen) and are competing for status as the region’s dominant economic and tech hub.
      • U.S. Impact: While a fractured OPEC may lower oil prices in the short term (benefiting the U.S. consumer), the lack of a unified Gulf partner complicates long-term Middle East security strategy.

Transatlantic Tensions and Troop Drawdowns

  • Germany Withdrawal: Trump has ordered the removal of 5,000 U.S. troops from Germany, seemingly in retaliation for critical comments made by German Chancellor Friedrich Merz.
  • The Drawdown Debate:
      • The Case for Reduction (Finer): Proponents argue that Russia is currently depleted by the Ukraine war (90% combat power committed) and that the U.S. must prioritize the "priority theater" of Asia. Furthermore, modern warfare's reliance on autonomous systems reduces the need for large-scale human footprints.
      • The Case for Presence (Sullivan): Critics argue that pulling troops undermines the "Long Peace" and the credibility of the U.S. nuclear umbrella. A precipitous withdrawal could encourage European nations (Poland, Germany) to pursue their own nuclear deterrents and embolden Putin to divide NATO.
"Iran has found itself in a standoff with a U.S. President who frankly doesn't know how to stand still... he gets impatient and then says, 'we got to try something else.'" — Jake Sullivan
"It’s the markets and geopolitics version of 'The Gift of the Magi.' The market thinks this is going to end fast, so prices don't go up. Because they don't go up, the conflict drags on." — Jon Finer
"Four of the seven main munitions that we need to keep fighting the war have fallen by more than half from their pre-war inventory, and rebuilding them will take one to four years." — Jake Sullivan (citing CSIS data)
What's also not helping is that he keeps getting surprised by things that shouldn't surprise him. So it's like from his perspective, "Damn, the regime didn't crack. Damn, they closed the strait. Damn, we couldn't bomb them into submission. Damn, we can't seem to blockade them into submission." And frankly, with each realization, he gets impatient and then says, "We got to try something else." To kind of sum this up, I think Iran has found itself in a standoff with a US President who frankly doesn't know how to stand still. Trump sort of signaled it a lot during his first term. In my conversations with European friends and former counterparts, I have been telling them, "Be ready for this," because I do not think we get through four years without the United States reducing its European footprint. There are too many people in the Trump administration who want this, and the President himself is just too inclined in this direction to not have it happen, and now it seems like it's playing out. Welcome back to *The Long Game*. I'm Jake Sullivan, and I'm Jon Finer. Jake, it's just us again today. It's quiet in here, but we're going to fill the time, I think, quite constructively with a look at a topic we've covered extensively on this podcast but haven't really gone deep on in a couple weeks, which is the state of play in the Iran war. There's been a dizzying set of developments recently, including right up until the present moment, that we will recap and analyze and try to give some sense of where all this is headed. This includes some of the implications of this conflict that have started to spin out, like the UAE's withdrawal from OPEC and kind of the broader implications of that for energy markets, energy security, and for Gulf relations, which we have hinted at previously but haven't gone deep on. Then we'll talk about another topic that is related to this conflict but only tangentially, which is the relationship between the United States and Europe and the increasing tension that the conflict is generating. This is up to and including the President announcing in recent days the potential withdrawal—the likely withdrawal, I guess—of thousands of American troops from Germany amid a spat between Trump and the German Chancellor. So we'll conclude with a rap at the end of all that, but there are a lot of topics to dig into, and I'm looking forward to getting started. I'm also looking forward to it, Jon. I have to report to you and to our listeners that it is AM Pacific time. I'm out on the West Coast, and I want to say that both because it reflects the full measure of my devotion to this podcast—that I am up, I'm showered, I'm in front of this screen ready to pod with you at AM—but the more functional reason I am time-stamping this episode (this is Wednesday morning, AM Eastern Time, AM Pacific Time) is because we're going to spend quite a bit of time talking about Iran, and things are changing by the hour. So this episode, by the time it comes out, by the time people listen to it, something will surely have changed. And that's because, I think you used the word dizzying, it really has been a quite dizzying and dynamic situation where things have evolved even since last night when we last touched base on the state of play. So we'll get into all that. I did want to come back to the topic of showering. I mentioned I had showered because I had real difficulty operating the shower in my hotel room this morning. And it reminded me that one of the most stressful and vexing aspects of traveling the world on behalf of the United States as a US government official, both at the State Department and the White House, was that every shower in every hotel in every country in the world was just different enough. In the morning, in the midst of jet lag, you had to take time to figure out how it operated. You know, some of them have multiple shower heads, some of them have multiple knobs, some of them have kind of the stealth shower head where you don't even know where the water's going to come out from—from the ceiling, from the side, whatever. And I have to say, there is a certain prosaic humiliation that goes with standing there, usually in the dark, trying to figure out how the heck you're going to get clean so that you can go about the business of the US government and American diplomacy that day. Is this just me, Jon, or is this something where I speak to a silent majority of American diplomats and officials? It's not just you. It might be just us for all I know, but I have myself multiple times had to call the front desk of the hotel to ask how to get the shower working or how to shift from a bath faucet to a shower. One time I remember somebody had to be sent up to the room to explain this to me in person. So yeah, it's a bit of a basic intelligence test that I have failed a couple times, at least. I do wonder if that's what it is. It's like the universe, you know, making us go through trials just to prove that we're staying mentally sharp or reminding you to be humble. Yeah, yeah, right, exactly. One thing I learned, actually, in the midst of all this, when you're in Europe especially, they have this new design of the showers where they only have half a pane of glass, so you're kind of taking the shower open in the room with just half a pane of glass. The reason they do that is, A, glass is a lot easier to clean than the old-school showers, but then B, based on what I have been informed in debriefings on the subject, having half a pane of glass as opposed to the full glass enclosure means you don't have hinges, which means the thing doesn't break. So it's just a much easier thing to maintain, but apparently American guests really don't like it. I personally am agnostic. Maybe that's enough shower talk, but these are the kinds of things you have to worry about. I will now admit that as with many topics, you've gone deeper on this than I have, but you know, I'm here to learn as well, so I appreciate the shower briefing. Okay, let's actually turn from the ridiculous to, well, maybe the sublime and what's happening in Iran. So do you want to—or to more ridiculous, let's bring the listeners up to speed on—or more ridiculous, yeah, exactly. So let me turn it over to you to get us going in terms of a review of the bidding of where we are and where we've come from. All right, so I do think, just because again we've not done a deep dive into this in a couple weeks and there's been a ton that's happened, it's worth just recapping some of the main events since we last went at length on Iran. The United States and Iran declared a ceasefire that involved no additional concessions really from either side, including Iran, despite the fact that the US had made a bunch of demands of Iran leading up to the ceasefire. The theory of that was that it would give space for talks between the two countries. J.D. Vance, Vice President, led a delegation to Pakistan for those talks. They lasted about a day. He announced at the end that nothing had been achieved. Sometime after that, the United States then added its own blockade of the Strait of Hormuz to the blockade that Iran already had underway. The intention of the US blockade was to stop Iran from exporting its hydrocarbons products, which it had been able to do during the course of the war and generate revenues from that. The United States decided that can't happen anymore, so added its own blockade. Then the United States announced after imposing the blockade for a week or more that it would be launching some sort of what it described as a humanitarian escort mission to allow stranded ships in the strait that were not tied to the conflict, flagged by countries unrelated to the conflict, to actually make it out and transit and get on their way. In response to which, Iran then attacked both the US Navy and the UAE, which has—and we'll discuss this more—suffered the brunt of the attacks from Iran during the course of this conflict more than any other country, including the United States. But the President and the Defense Department described these attacks as not enough to count as a violation of the ceasefire, even though they looked to the rest of the world like they were a violation of the ceasefire. And then just in the last 24 hours, the President has called off the escort mission, citing a request from Pakistan and maybe also from Saudi Arabia, citing the supposed enormous military success of the campaign against Iran, which he's pointed to on other occasions, and citing again what he calls progress towards a deal with Iran based on, I guess, some indirect talks that are still going on even though the two sides have not met again face-to-face. So my head is spinning a bit at all of this, but I did think it was worth just kind of recapping, reviewing the bidding, as we head into another week that is likely to be something of an inflection point in this war one way or the other, or potentially just more of the same as we continue to muddle through. But is that basically the rundown of where things stand? I think that lays it out very nicely. And keep in mind, all of that is compressed into a really short timeline in the broader context of geopolitics—you know, basically just a couple of weeks everything you said unfolded. And now here we are again Wednesday morning, May 6th, and the latest development building off of that statement that President Trump made is a report in Axios from Barak Ravid, who you and I both know well. He's basically saying—and he's said this multiple times over the course of the conflict, so how much validation to give it is up in the air—but what he basically says is that they are close to a MOU that would lead to an end of the war in the region. This would be the start of a 30-day period of negotiations on a detailed agreement that would open the strait, that would limit Iran's nuclear program, and that would lift US sanctions. So he goes into some detail on this. The Iranians have sort of already come out, at least through some of their spokespeople, and said, "No, this is more a list of American demands." It's unclear exactly what the state of this proposal is. But what does seem to be clear is that the Trump administration believes, or at least wants to project its belief to the public, that they are making progress towards some sort of framework, some kind of document that would allow them to declare an end of the war and that would launch some period of negotiations to try to resolve the big open issues in this. What's your take on this element of it? How do you assess what's come out in the reporting now in terms of the progress, or at least purported progress, maybe the lack of progress on the negotiations? So I guess I'd start by saying I love Barak; he's a friend of mine and I think one of the best reporters in Washington, but there was a funny tweet this morning that said he has predicted seven out of the last zero agreements, I guess is the way it was phrased. Seven out of the last zero, right. He keeps foreshadowing that there's going to be a deal and ultimately there isn't. So, you know, I take this with a big grain of salt. This is, I think, American messaging, and by the way, I think not a coincidence that it happens early in the week and is intended to kind of influence market reaction, which it is already. You've seen oil markets react to this. We'll talk a little bit more about how the United States has kind of continued to talk down market reaction to the war fairly successfully, although not perfectly. That said, where I think he is onto something and where I think ultimately this is worth digging into is that I think you and I would both agree that ultimately if there is going to be any sort of deal, it is going to need to involve the sorts of elements that Barak lays out in his piece. This is because ultimately that is the only way to get a deal that can address Iran's nuclear ambitions. It is going to involve, as he says, a significant measure of financial benefit for Iran. Exactly what form that takes and the magnitude of it is going to be the subject of negotiations. It's going to involve some limits on Iran's nuclear program. You know, he flags the possibility of a moratorium—a period in which Iran does not enrich uranium. By the way, Iran is not currently enriching uranium and doesn't really have the prospect of enriching uranium in the near term given the damage that's been done to its program. But how long that moratorium would be in place and then what happens after that is going to be another obvious topic for negotiations and for a possible deal. And then the subject of how this deal would be monitored and implemented and enforced is going to be a major topic of discussion. And so when you see these categories laid out that are part of this report, it's not surprising because I think these are the core elements of the only deal that's ever been reached with Iran on the nuclear program, which is the JCPOA, which we talked about at length and both of us have worked on. So I think we could dig into some of the details of this because I think the details do matter, but not at all surprising that this is where the United States is trying to steer the conversation. And I think the real question is: do we, does the United States, have enough leverage over Iran to get any sort of deal at all, or is Iran more inclined to just sort of hold out given how angry they are at having been attacked and given that they still believe, I think, that the longer this goes on the more pressure is on us, economically and otherwise, to try to end it on terms that are even better for Iran? So that's kind of how I see things. I want to put a pin in this question: will Iran do a deal that's close to or in the zone of what's been laid out in this Axios report? That is a really important question, and underlying it is this question you just posed of whether we have enough leverage to make that happen. But let me take a step back for a minute and connect your first observation, which is that we've had this dizzying array of developments over the last two weeks, with an observation you just made a second ago that there is a certain inevitability to all of this. At the end of the day, the only way in which there is an end to this war, an end to this crisis, is through some kind of negotiated settlement. And, you know, I find those two facts, those two kind of bookends to this overall crisis, really important to keep in view as we follow the day-to-day developments. For me, going back to the beginning of this crisis, the Iranians in a way have been fairly straightforward to predict. We could have predicted that when you take out the Supreme Leader and threaten the whole regime, that they would close the Strait of Hormuz because that was, for lack of a better term, their trump card all along. We would have predicted that they would strike Gulf bases and infrastructure to try to raise the costs—economic costs—in the United States and the rest of the world. We would have predicted that they would hold firm on their basic position that they were not going to surrender their entire nuclear program. We would have predicted that they weren't going to rapidly fracture or collapse and the regime was not going to give way to some new government that was significantly more friendly to the United States or more democratic. And frankly, we would have predicted that they would settle into a position of strategic stubbornness. They're basically playing their hand out about as expected. Now, President Trump, unsurprisingly, has been a little bit less predictable. And what's not helping from my perspective is that he didn't know why he really got into this in the first place, so his strategy has never had a strong mooring. What's also not helping is that he keeps getting surprised by things that shouldn't surprise him. So it's like from his perspective, "Damn, the regime didn't crack. Damn, they closed the strait. Damn, we couldn't bomb them into submission. Damn, we can't seem to blockade them into submission." And frankly, with each realization, he gets impatient and then says, "We got to try something else." So, you know, to kind of sum this up, I think Iran has found itself in a standoff with a US President who frankly doesn't know how to stand still. So it's just hard to say what he'll do next. He plainly doesn't want to go back to war. He also doesn't seem to want to just settle into a long-term staring contest. But he's damn nervous about a diplomatic compromise that looks a lot like the Iran nuclear deal. So Donald Trump in a way would kind of like to choose none of the above, but he lost that option when he launched the war. He has to do something, and I think that's why even when he tries new things like this Operation Freedom, or he threatens civilizational destruction—remember that happened not too long ago—or he blockades the blockade, these are all in service of trying to find some button he can press to get out of this war. But all of them lead back to the fundamental reality that there is only one durable way out, and that is a deal. And, you know, that is where he keeps coming back to; that's where Axios has put us again today. And I guess the question, Jon, of whether or not there is a zone that the US and Iran can land in, that President Trump can say, "Okay, I'll take that," and Iran can say, "Okay, I'll take that"—do you think that is plausible, and how do you answer the question you posed on whether or not the US has enough leverage to get there? So I guess I'd say a few things. One is that I think a deal along these lines was probably plausible before this war. And in fact, I think something along these lines is what was under discussion between the United States and Iran, both last summer before Israel and the United States launched the 12-day war and disrupted what were ongoing negotiations, and then again this year, when the United States chose to interrupt negotiations by returning to conflict. I think a deal along the lines that we've been discussing was very much on the table and within the realm of possibility. And I guess I feel like now the fact that we have waged this damaging conflict—damaging to us, damaging to the global economy, damaging to our allies in the region, damaging obviously also to Iran at least in terms of its military capability, but ultimately strategically, as we've argued many times, not beneficial to the United States given where things stand today—and are still talking about basically the same terms of the deal, just continues to bring me back to the question of why we bothered to go down this path in the first place and cause all this harm. I also think it is now harder to get this deal than it was previously because you've got a more entrenched, more dug-in, more hardline set of decision-makers in Iran, and a more discredited set of people who might actually argue that doing a deal is in their favor. I think they're going to have—the Foreign Minister who we know and have talked about on this pod, the Speaker of the House, National Security Advisor—these people are maybe the ones who are inclined to do a deal if it's good enough for Iran. And I think they're going to have an even harder time now, after this war, getting the people who are ultimately making the decisions—maybe it's the Supreme Leader, much more now the IRGC leadership—to sign off and say yes. And so that means the terms that the United States is going to offer will have to be better now than they would have been before having gone to this war. The idea that you go to war to generate terms that are better for the adversary is generally not how and why you enter into conflicts. Quite the opposite. So, you know, we look at sanctions relief. There's going to be billions of dollars on the table in assets—Iranian assets that are currently frozen that'll be unfrozen. What other sanctions relief are we going to provide to get this deal? Certainly we'll enable Iran to sell oil; it already was doing so even under previous sanctions. We will probably authorize that. Are we going to do more? Are we going to offer sanctions relief under what we call our primary embargo, which is, you know, direct US sanctions on even doing business with the United States or touching the United States financial system, which by the way the JCPOA that the Obama administration did did not include any sanctions relief under that? Is that now on the table to try to induce Iran into a deal? And then what are we going to get on the nuclear side? There are some indications that we might get actually some significant things from the Iranians, maybe even beyond what was in the JCPOA, like this moratorium. The JCPOA, we should acknowledge, did not include a period in which Iran was not permitted to enrich. It seems like they're at least discussing some period—five years, ten years, whatever it is—with Iran. That would be of value. But I suspect strongly that we will have to pay quite a lot if we're going to get Iran to agree to a period like that. So the details here really do matter, and as we've indicated previously, we do not have exactly our most detail-oriented US officials at the forefront of these talks. Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, who by the way have other jobs, are not full-time US government employees and are not bringing a lot of US government expertise into these conversations from what we can tell. So I'm a bit pessimistic, to be honest, that this gets done. So there's a bunch in what you just said that I would love to unpack, but let me zero in on two things. One is the point you and I have both made over the course of several episodes: that we could have gotten a deal without going through this war and all of the costs that have been imposed on the United States: human costs, economic costs, and frankly, maybe even most long-lasting and significant strategic costs to the United States. We could have done all this without that. And I think part of the reason that President Trump hasn't been prepared to do a deal to date is because he's just so nervous about conceding that President Obama and Secretary Kerry were frankly right all along—that there has to be compromise if you're going to get a durable nuclear deal. I think ultimately he will end up acting on that realization, but we've gone down this road because he just has not wanted to stare that reality squarely in the face. I think something else has been going on though too, which is the people in his ear are telling him a series of things that continue to reinforce his mistaken assumptions. So, you know, we had H.R. McMaster and Matt Pottinger on the podcast two weeks ago. They're not in his ear; I don't think they're talking to him day-to-day, but they reflect the kind of people who are. And H.R.'s proposition that the regime is going to collapse at some point soon—he said that with relatively high confidence—that's something that a lot of other people around Trump have said to him. There's a guy named Marc Thiessen who writes a column for the *Washington Post*, who reportedly has been talking to President Trump on a regular basis. A few days ago he wrote a piece saying Iran is 14 days from collapse. These are the kinds of things that I think President Trump is hearing, and basically he's being told, "You have the cards, you have the leverage, Iran doesn't, they can't endure the economic pain, they can't continue to endure the blockade because they're ultimately going to have to shut down their oil production." These are the kinds of things I think that have led us down a problematic path to begin with and also mean that President Trump is not, in my view, properly assessing the risks and benefits of various courses of action in this war. And I have a question as to whether ultimately there's going to be something that makes him say, "Okay, just do it, just go do the deal." Could that be gas prices hitting $5 a gallon? Could that be his increasing concern of the risk of some wildcard, something, you know, a US ship getting hit? Could it be the summit with Xi Jinping next week? Does he want to have shown really substantial progress between now and then? I think these questions about how Trump's calculus around what he's prepared to accept in the way of compromise are very real. And then on the Iranian side, there is the question of the extent to which they want to say, "Look, we could just hold out, we could keep trying to increase the pressure on the United States, but heck, there's a deal on the table that helps us deal with some of our problems too." How they run that calculus over the course of the coming days and weeks—we'll have to look at both sides of that. On the terms of the deal itself, the thing that I find most challenging, beyond the fact that we don't have detail-oriented people actually negotiating it, is we do not right now have a good sense at all of what the baseline of Iran's nuclear program is—actually how much enriched uranium they have, actually how many centrifuges they have, actually what the status of their facilities beyond the main facilities are, whether or not they've used the last year to divert capabilities to places we don't know about. We have very little sense of that. That's a huge change from before the JCPOA when we had a good picture of every element of Iran's nuclear program and were able to use that picture to shape a deal that we could confidently look the American people in the eye and say, "This deal will stick." So it doesn't seem to me like that's even really in discussion—how to get that baseline where we have the confidence that whatever we agree to is actually rooted in the reality of Iran's nuclear program today. And I'll be curious how they grapple with that challenge in the period ahead. But the question, I guess, sitting here today Wednesday is: do the two sides actually go sit back down at the table and try to hammer this out? Will this continue to be done in this more indirect way or this standoff way? And you speak of your pessimism on it. I guess I've tended to be, compared to you, a little more optimistic that ultimately they'll land in a zone because there's no other outcome here and eventually President Trump will come to that realization and Iran will want to do a deal, at least at some level. So I'm a bit more optimistic than you are, but I still do think it's going to take a lot of time to unfold. Yeah, I guess maybe to qualify my pessimism. I'm pessimistic that there will be a deal that has, kind of, sufficient detail and verification and this accounting that you just described of even the status quo of Iran's nuclear program so, you know, as one of our colleagues has called it, "We know what we're buying if we give them a measure of sanctions relief." That would seem to be a prerequisite for doing this the right way. I'm not, I guess, pessimistic that we could ultimately reach a kind of one-page framework along the lines that's been reported to kind of "pause the pause" and give space for talks yet again that may ultimately not lead anywhere. I do think it is possible that that kind of agreement could be announced and the two sides could return to negotiations. But, you know, a one-page framework that has some general language on all these topics is pretty different from the hundred-plus pages of detailed technical agreement and annexes and everything else that went into—and that would go into—a real arms control agreement and that went into the Iran nuclear deal from back in 2015-2016. And obviously that's not going to get done in 30 days, the amount of time, the window of time that supposedly would be given for these talks if they even get off the ground. So I guess where I come down is: yes, possibly some sort of framework could be reached, very unlikely that that would actually lead to meaningful changes or ultimately a serious deal of some kind. But maybe that's a way out of this conflict for a Trump administration that seems to badly want one and seems unable to get one on the sort of dominating terms that they have been seeking up until now. How much do you think what President Trump is guided by is his look at markets or his look at—which really haven't moved much—or his look at gas prices, which are now creeping up, having gotten to around $4.50 a gallon for the national average? Do you think that that's playing a big role in his thinking or he's kind of setting that aside and trying to, you know, figure this out without as much reference to some of these things that we used to think were really core to his calculus? I mean, on some level, how could it not? I mean, you know, we've discussed how we lived through people walking into our office during periods of crisis in the Middle East and telling us, "You know, this is actually now having broader implications on the economy, on the political standing of the administration," and, you know, the national security considerations are paramount but they're not the only considerations when it comes to these sorts of crises. So I'm sure that's the case in this White House. We've also though talked about this paradox of when Trump projects that we are on the cusp of a deal, the market's reaction tends to be then more muted to the crisis because nobody wants to miss the sort of surge that will come when a real deal is announced. And so market reaction is much more limited, but then that blaseness, if that's actually a word, actually puts less pressure on the administration to end the war because the market reaction is so diminished. Over the last week or two, when there started to be indications of—I wouldn't call it market panic, but at least market reaction commensurate to the level of the crisis—the price spikes that we saw paradoxically actually put more pressure because people started to believe that the war might go longer, putting more pressure on the Trump administration, I think, to actually put more effort into ending it. So there is this loop that we end up in where, you know, the markets actually have not pressured Trump as much as I would have expected them to to put a stop to this. And he's been quite successful at always dangling the possibility of a deal that people don't want to miss and limiting the reaction in that way, and maybe he'll be able to keep doing that for a while. Although as you say, the higher the prices climb—and there is this scoreboard that people can look at with their own eyes out in the country—it's going to be harder. You know, it's interesting, it's the markets and geopolitics version of that short story "The Gift of the Magi." The market thinks that this is going to end fast, so prices don't go up that much. And because they don't go up that much, the conflict drags. And then people think, "Oh, maybe it's going to drag," so then prices start going up, but that would mean it would more likely end. It is a crazy doom loop, the likes of which just we really haven't seen that frequently in these kinds of conflicts, and it is worth keeping an eye on in the days ahead. Because if an announcement of progress and talks leads to downward pressure on oil prices, which almost certainly it will, does that then take the pressure off Trump and make him think, "Okay I can keep trying the blockade for a while longer"? We'll just have to see how that particular dynamic plays out. One other thing I just wanted to make sure that we touched on. I mentioned this risk of a wildcard. Dan Kaine, the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said something interesting. He said that since the ceasefire went into place, there have been ten efforts by the Iranians to hit US naval vessels. I think it was US naval vessels in the Gulf. You and I lived through the Houthis working to try to shut down the Red Sea and the Bab el-Mandeb and trying to directly hit US naval vessels. And we saw very clearly the skill and capability of the US Navy in being able to intercept the inbound projectiles, shoot them down, and protect the ships. But we also saw a lot of close calls and the fact that you have to be right every single time. And so, I think we should—we have gotten kind of used to the fact that our Navy can just defend itself against inbound missiles and drones, inbound fast boats, whatever the risk may be. But the enemy only has to be right once to cause a strike that could take further lives of sailors, that could disable a ship, and certainly that would create a whole different strategic context. And I think we just should make sure we don't take our eye entirely off that ball. And my guess is that the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs is telling President Trump, "Look, I have every confidence in my operators to be able to defend our fleet and defend our ships, but also I'm sure he's being straightforward that this risk remains very elevated and something for us to watch." We could wake up any day and have had a US ship hit in a way that just creates an entirely different strategic equation. Yeah, I think it's safe to say we've been both good and lucky up until now. And actually, maybe not as lucky as the public narrative would suggest, as we're also starting to learn. I mean, there have been now increasing reports that damage to US military facilities in the region is more extensive than the Trump administration has acknowledged. Billions of dollars in damage; we probably don't know the full extent of that. They've tried to keep that under wraps, I think, in large part to avoid letting the Iranians know where they've been successful and in large part to project the military dominance that's such a part of the Trump administration's messaging. You know, we also had attacks that have killed US service members and could have killed more US service members. We had an aircraft that went down in Iran with a crew on board that we were able to rescue. That also was not a foregone conclusion. So there have been these moments where things could have gone significantly worse and then would have put significant pressure on the Trump administration to do more to escalate in retaliation and in response. And as you say, if a naval vessel gets hit or, you know, God forbid people are killed or the vessel is sunk, there will be massive, I think, escalatory pressure on Trump to respond. So that is one of these wildcards. The other thing I think we should just note, and we've talked about it before, is there are also increasing reports of how depleted American munitions are as a result of this conflict. This refers both to being depleted in terms of our ability to perpetuate this war indefinitely—you know, keep ships at sea, keep shooting things out of the sky, keep attacking Iran—all of these supplies now are reaching critical levels, and being depleted in the context of other potential things that could happen in the world: the Russians messing around in Eastern Europe, the Chinese taking aggressive action in the Taiwan Strait or the South China Sea. There are real questions, I think, based on how much material we've used in this war, about how ready we are for these other things. And that has to be part of the calculus at least for Dan Kaine and the Pentagon in advising the President about how long this can go. No doubt about it. The reports coming out about exactly how much we've blown through in terms of Tomahawks, this new missile the Prism, and some of our key interceptors like Patriots and SM-3s and SM-6s, it's really quite striking. CSIS said four of the seven main munitions that we need to keep fighting the war have fallen by more than half from their pre-war inventory, and that rebuilding them will take one to four years. I mean, really, really quite striking. And that is a factor that the United States military will absolutely be briefing up to the President and his key advisors, and will be very much on their mind as they think about the scenario in which this war gets restarted and is sustained over a period of time. So, you had mentioned at the outset something important, which is that in the midst of all of this going on in the Persian Gulf between the US and Iran, we've had the United Arab Emirates, the UAE, make a big decision, which is to pull out of OPEC. And that has both the oil market implications that are real and direct and also a broader set of strategic implications. How are you reading that situation? So it's interesting. On the one hand, and I think we both know this from personal experience, this is something that the Emirates has been looking to do and dangling the possibility of doing for quite some time. I think they have been frustrated for a long time about the quotas that are imposed by OPEC on their production, on their exports, because they, like the Saudis, but—are two of the countries and they believe they are maybe the one with the most spare capacity to put on the market. So they believe that the OPEC quotas have hurt them in some ways more than they have hurt other countries. And they have been making that case privately, quietly to us for quite some time and increasingly publicly over the years. But second, and I think this was interestingly pretty well laid out in an op-ed in the *Wall Street Journal* by Yousef Al Otaiba, the Emirati ambassador who we know well. He argued, "Look, unlike some of the other OPEC countries, we are not a hydrocarbons-dominated, oil-dominated economy." It's less than a quarter now of their overall GDP. And so they are more focused on energy security and on the broader performance of the global economy because they are more intertwined in the global economy than they used to be and more bound up in the global economy than maybe some of the other OPEC members. And so they believe that their goal should be safe, stable energy markets, much more so than generating higher prices, which is at least part of how they see OPEC's motivation that is maybe misaligned now with the Emirates' strategic goals and with their own economic interest. So they basically see a divergence between the goals of the OPEC cartel and their own economic strategy driving their desire to be out. I will say though, and want to get your reaction to kind of which of these factors is the most prominent or if it's something else, there has also been hanging out in the background of all of this and certainly looming quite large before this war, this political, personal, strategic dispute between the Emirates and Saudi Arabia. It was just starting to boil over when the war erupted and forced everybody to at least on the surface align against a common enemy, a common adversary in Iran. That dispute has not gone away.
