And I think that is also part of why, in the current moment where maybe you might otherwise want to profess a degree of unity, the Emirates was comfortable saying, "We're stepping away from this institution," which Saudi Arabia has informally dominated for quite some time as the largest producing OPEC country. But do you see these as being the main factors? Which among them? Or is there something else going on here? Look, I do think the proximate reason that the UAE pulled out is because it wants to maximize its oil production now, to maximize its revenues now, to hasten the transition of its economy away from hydrocarbons, and to finance clean tech and especially artificial intelligence. So I do think they had a clear calculus about why the restrictions they were living under in terms of how much oil they could produce and sell on the market at OPEC didn't make sense for them. They want to be able to pursue their own national growth strategy. I think that's the proximate reason for this. I don't think it was purely triggered by the war; I think they've been thinking about this for a couple years and now they've done it. But I think the larger implications are going to be the real story as we go forward. And the larger implications are, I believe, a structural rift between the UAE and Saudi Arabia that will be a major feature of the regional landscape for years to come. Now there's an interesting and immediate dimension to this, which is who's Iran hitting when it fires against Gulf countries to try to put pressure on the United States and to try to flex its muscles and show its leverage? It's not hitting Saudi these days; it's hitting the UAE. And who's involved in the diplomacy with Pakistan to try to bring an end to this war and position itself in the post-war era as a key broker? It's Saudi. So already in the larger dynamics here, you see the UAE and Saudi occupying very different positions. And I think the Saudi effort to establish itself as the dominant player in economics and technology, the dominant player in terms of regional geopolitics and security affairs, and for MBS himself, the Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia, to establish himself personally as the key leading figure in the region—these are all driving towards I think a more fundamental change in the UAE-Saudi relationship. This will have implications not just in places like Yemen and Sudan where they found themselves on opposite sides of proxy conflicts, but in virtually every country in the region and in terms of how each of them relate to Iran. And so I think this is a very big deal, and the UAE move here is one move in a larger context that from my perspective we'll be talking about for years to come—this kind of UAE-Saudi dynamic and how everyone else in the region and even countries outside the region are going to have to relate to things. And so, as you mentioned, for many weeks in the fall on our podcast we'd say, "Hey watch out for this, we're going to come back to it." Well, here we are, coming back to it because events have forced us back to it. And I predict that in the months ahead we'll be doing deeper dives on this issue as well because it's going to be very much with us as something that, you know, two, three, four, five years ago was not a significant factor and now is. So I think that's very well put. I would add that to a large extent this is not good for the United States. Not good that two of our closest friends, partners, allies in the region are at odds with each other, are on different sides of different conflicts taking place, still taking place—Sudan, Somalia, Yemen, etc. in the region—and now are increasingly at odds over the sort of state of play on Iran and the current conflict that the United States is engaged in. I will say there's one maybe small way in which this is not so bad for the United States. In a time of high energy prices, which we lived through, the idea that the United States has one address to go to to try to increase production, bring prices down, give the economy domestically for the United States a bit of a boost—and that destination is OPEC—gives OPEC a significant amount of bargaining power and leverage in these discussions with the United States. And the fact that there now may well be multiple addresses to go to to try to improve and enhance energy security in ways that benefit the US economy—not all bad, I think, for the United States. So overall, I think strategically not a great situation here, with maybe a small silver lining at a time that we're currently in of high energy prices coming out of this conflict. OPEC may prefer a slightly higher overall price of oil than the United States government would for our own economic interests in many cases, and the Emirates may now be a partner in that going forward. I think that's well summed up. Strategically, I think it's challenging to have these two countries at odds. From the economic point of view, we don't like OPEC, so fewer countries in OPEC is probably better from the US perspective. All right, let's zoom out even further because the war in Iran is having ripple effects that go way beyond just Gulf dynamics. They're affecting the transatlantic relationship as well. And in particular, President Trump and his team have made an announcement that they are going to reduce the overall level of US forces in Germany, take out 5,000 forces, and it seems like the trigger for this was the German Chancellor, Friedrich Merz. He said that the US was getting humiliated by the Iranians in the Iran war. He said this in front of a kindergarten class I think, like in front of a bunch of kids. It was an odd format for it. He says we've been humiliated. Trump says, "I'm going to draw down our forces in Germany," and the Pentagon comes out and announces 5,000 fewer forces. They say it was a result of an extensive process, but of course the relevant forces learned about it in real time. This suggests that in fact it was directly connected to President Trump reacting to what he saw as a direct slight from the German Chancellor. President Trump has also said there are likely to be more reductions in US forces from Germany and said other countries may face reductions in US forces as well, including name-checking both Spain and Italy, and reserving some choice words especially for Spain. So Jon, we're going to do a Red Team/Blue Team on whether it makes sense for the United States to do a significant material drawdown in our overall force posture and troop presence in Europe. But before we get to that, any reactions to these developments over the course of the past few days, culminating in this announcement of a reduction in US troops in Germany? No, other than to say something we've said previously, which is none of this is good; all of this is beneficial to Russia—the higher price environment, the tension in the transatlantic relationship, the reduction of US support for our allies and for Ukraine. But we are where we are, and so we thought it was worth discussing on the merits the prospect the President has put on the table of reducing further the US footprint and the US commitment to Europe. As always in our Red Team/Blue Team conversations, we start with a caveat that the arguments that we may be making are not necessarily our personal views. Particularly I think that'll be the case for me this time around because I'm going to argue for reducing the footprint. I think you're going to argue against and, you know, we'll see how it goes. Yeah, and I think just to set the terms because pre-Ukraine war or pre-the buildup to the Ukraine war, the US had somewhere in the range of, call it 65,000 to 70,000 troops in Europe. That got up to as high as 100,000 as we fortified the eastern flank and prepared for contingencies that might take the Ukraine war beyond the boundaries of Ukraine. That has now come down a bit over the last couple of years. At different points there are different levels of troop presence, but it's between 80,000 and 100,000. So this debate is not about minor adjustments at the margins; it's really a debate about whether we should have a substantial structural reduction in US forces on the continent. And since you're the one arguing for it, at least in this Red Team/Blue Team exercise, not necessarily reflecting your actual views, I think it probably makes sense for you to go first and lay out the case for why we should do that, and then I'll tell you why you're so very wrong. As usual. All right, excellent. So we don't like to acknowledge it—no country likes to acknowledge it—but even the richest and most powerful nation on earth has limited resources and we have to prioritize how they are used and be strategic about it. At the height of the Cold War, when Russia was by far and away the most important threat that the United States faced, we had 400,000 troops in Europe and 250,000 in West Germany alone. That actually made sense at the time. It also made sense to drastically reduce that number afterwards and then to increase it again, as the Biden administration did from about 65,000 as you said in early 2022 back up to 100,000 when Russia invaded Ukraine. But that increase was never intended to be permanent. And since then, something—significant things—have changed that I think are worth noting. One, Russia has taken more than a million casualties on the battlefield in Ukraine and is using something like 90% of its combat power there. So it is pretty focused on that one theater and a lot less focused on menacing and threatening by traditional means the NATO countries that the United States is in Europe to defend. Second, the US has launched not one but two new wars, or at least mini-wars, in the Caribbean and in Iran, as we've discussed in this episode. That has had major implications on our readiness and on our force posture in ways that we have to take into account strategically. Third, warfare has become or at least is becoming increasingly dependent on autonomous systems and less dependent on manpower. And that's only going to increase over time as aerial, sea, and sub-sea drones are supplemented by even land-based platforms that don't require the same number of human operators on board. Fourth, the main threat to Europe, and particularly to Germany now from Russia, in my view at least, is not a traditional military assault in the way that Russia has conducted against Ukraine, but is something more akin to what we call gray zone tactics—still violent, but much more below the radar. Sabotage, even drones used somewhat covertly to menace airports and infrastructure and other things. This is less true I think for frontline states, the Baltics and others where we should maintain a presence, but it's certainly I think true for the threat to Germany where our largest numbers of US troops are located. And then meanwhile, in our priority theater, which is Asia, focused on China, we've seen a net outflow of capabilities over recent months, which is just strategic malpractice if that's the place that we are most focused on. So look, I am not for doing this the way the President is doing it. It it of course should not look punitive to our allies, to Germany because of what Merz said, to Italy or to Spain because of what their leaders have said and done. This should not be us lashing out on them or doing it in a way that undermines our Article 5 commitment. In fact, it should be coupled with an enhanced deterrence message, maybe even increased kind of high-end capabilities to Europe that require fewer personnel. So not infantry, but things like long-range strike capabilities, which we were discussing sending to Germany when we were still in office. It also should not come alongside the shameful backing away we've seen in support for Ukraine, which is fighting Russia in a very successful, I believe, way at enormous cost and without a single American or European troop engaged in direct active combat. So not a troop-intensive way to kind of continue to impose costs on Russia. Does this increase the risk, drawing down, that that Russia will do something reckless? Maybe, maybe marginally. But the core of our deterrent there I do not believe is the number of forces we have, but our nuclear umbrella and the perception of our commitment to defend NATO. So as long as that remains high and viable and credible, I believe the deterrence will hold, and that combination is what's kept Russia, by the way, from directly attacking NATO even as our support for Ukraine has caused so much harm to Russia during the course of the recent conflict. And to give the President some credit, he's successfully gotten the European countries to make greater commitments to their own defense. Germany is raising a much bigger army now, doubling or more the size of its standing army. As a result, in some sense, of of the Russian invasion, of our pressure, of Trump's pressure, and if Germany's army is twice the size that it once was, that should mean that there are fewer troops required from the United States. So back to where I started: responsible foreign policy in a world of scarce resources means accepting some risk where you can so you can decrease that risk where you must. To me that is Asia. So can we afford to be a bit lighter in Europe? Maybe even substantially lighter? I think we can. I'd much rather we didn't launch wars in Iran and the Caribbean, but we have. There are forces committed to those places, we should wrap those places up, but the place we should shift those resources to is Asia where our deterrent I think is most needed and most in question. All right, very well said, and I am going to make the case that we should not make a structural material drawdown in US forces at this time or in the near-term future. And I want to start with three numbers that Graham Allison has laid out: 80, 80, and 9. 80 years since we've had Great Power war, 80 years since a nuclear weapon has been used, and 9, only 9 countries with nuclear weapons in the world. And if you had said to people in 1945 that these three things would be true in 2025 or 2026, they would have said you're crazy. There'll be dozens of nuclear powers, there certainly will be another nuclear weapons use, and how can we possibly go 80 years without the Great Powers fighting since we've just had two World Wars in the span of just a few decades? And yet it's happened. And Graham Allison calls it the long peace. And NATO has been absolutely central to that. And because of the role Russia is playing today as one of the chief threats to the long-term viability of that long peace, NATO remains absolutely central to that. And yes, it costs money. It takes resources. But prevention always costs money. A war in Europe, precipitated by Russia believing that it could take more risk on its side to attack countries in NATO on the eastern flank, would cost a whole lot more money and a whole lot more than money. So this is kind of like that support beam in your house that you get tired of, you'd like to—it's not aesthetically pleasing, you'd like to take it out. You realize you take out that support beam and the whole house comes down. Now your argument is, okay fine, NATO remains central, but really the things we have to worry about are two: one, the Article 5 guarantee, and two, the extended deterrence commitment that comes with the American nuclear umbrella. And I agree those two things are very important. But they are directly connected to the confidence our allies have in extended deterrence and in the credibility of our commitment, and that is directly connected to whether or not we're sending them a signal that we are reducing, withdrawing, weakening the overall American presence. So I do not believe it is possible to substantially draw down American forces in Europe and not make our allies question: are they still as committed to Article 5 as they were before, because frankly they don't have the forces there to enforce it? Are they as committed to the extended deterrence nuclear umbrella because they've just said Asia's more important than Europe? And so therefore all of these pieces are integrally interconnected, and if you pull one piece out, I think you could end up with the whole of it collapsing. And I would go further and say even if one day it's right to draw down forces in part for the basic arithmetic you laid out, that as the Europeans build up their capability, it will mean that you need fewer American boots on the ground, that day is not today or tomorrow. First of all, the European buildup is going to take years, many years. Secondly, there's a whole range of capabilities including strategic enablers like intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance, airlift, the long-range strike capabilities you described, that they're not going to have for quite some time, and so there needs to be a decent interval where the United States is still present at scale while the Europeans are building up. Second, President Putin right now today is doing his level best to break NATO. In particular he's looking to destabilize the East, and he's looking to send a message to all NATO allies: you can't count on the United States anymore. This would play into his hands in a massive way. He could turn around and say, I told you so, these guys are pulling out, they're drawing down, you're going to have to make some accommodation with me, or you're going to have to knuckle under to my threats because you cannot count on the United States. And in this moment right now today, this would be I think a deeply destabilizing act for the United States. Then there's another dimension here that I think at least should be named, because you talked about Germany doubling the size of its army. There is some question about the extent to which having Germany be the overridingly dominant military power on the European continent is a great thing. Now I say that a little facetiously because I think the Germany of today is not the Germany of the 1930s by—in any way, shape or form. But there was a really interesting piece by a Lithuanian think tank leader in the New York Times a few days ago, where he basically says, Europe needs German power, but, and I'll quote this, "German power remains less worrying when it is embedded in a unified Europe and anchored by the United States. And in a Europe that competes within itself is not only less capable but also far easier for Russia to divide and intimidate." And so I do think how the United States draws down, over what timetable, and with what conditions around it to ensure that Europe remains cohesive and that a growing European defense capability is not divisive and fracturing to the continent but rather unifying and reinforcing, is a really important point. And I think if we do something precipitous like a significant drawdown right now, we're going to create real problems for ourselves. This brings me finally to the nuclear umbrella. Because right now France is saying, hey we have nukes, and we'll you know sort of offer it to others in terms of defending them, but that's going to be a French decision, not a European decision. I think if the United States begins to raise questions in Europe as to the long-term viability of our security commitment to the continent, other countries in Europe are going to want to get nukes—Germany, Poland, you name it, and then the whole thing kicks off. So I think we should not take the risk that would be created by a significant muscle movement and the drawdown of forces for this reason as well. And then the final point that I would make is with respect to China. It's true the US-China competition is a central strategic factor of the 21st century. But what happens in Europe very much matters to that competition, because it's not just about the military posture in Asia. It is about whether or not we have strong, capable, determined allies working with us as opposed to working against us or being divided from us when it comes to confronting the massive-scale challenge that China poses. And if we do a significant drawdown in Europe, Beijing would cheer that on and it would lead the Europeans, I think, to continue to de-risk to a greater extent from the United States and try to cut their own deals with China to our strategic disadvantage. So for all of those reasons, I think it is worth continuing to put the resources into the kind of stabilizing factor that US force presence in Europe has represented over the course of the past 80 years and can represent going forward, and we should not do a material significant reduction of US forces for the foreseeable future. So I'm looking forward to the angry messages I get from European friends for having laid out this argument, although you might get a few from the Germans for having called into question the, you know, negative potential implications of German power. But I guess what I would say is I I basically buy your argument, your version of this. I do think the part of my argument that I had the most conviction around—there are some things I argued somewhat I think disingenuously—is that it does matter how you do this. And the way that we are going about this is probably the worst possible way in terms of kind of maximizing the risks that you're describing and mitigating them. And so I worry a lot in the current context, and I've been worried for quite some time, even before the Iran war, that that this is where we were headed. Trump sort of signaled it a lot during his first term. And in my conversations with European friends and former counterparts, I have been telling them, be ready for this because I do not think we get through four years without the United States reducing its European footprint. There are too many people in the Trump administration who want this, and the President himself is just too inclined in this direction to not have it happen, and now it seems like it's playing out. And I will just say that I'm probably more personally relaxed about the German military buildup than I presented in my Blue Team side of that argument. But I think it's really important to listen to the voices of countries on the eastern flank, to European voices, who say, yes it's important, Germany needs to build up, but this has to happen in a context that is not destabilized by the dramatic drawdown of US capability and forces on the continent because there are risks associated with this. And I think whether or not you assign a high level of risk to that or a low level, it is a factor that has to be taken into account. All right, should we do rap? We should. Do you have a recommendation? So I'm reading a couple of books right now—actually I'm reading one and listening to the other, both by Erik Larson. So I'm reading "The Demon of Unrest," which is about the onset of the US Civil War, and I am listening to "The Splendid and the Vile" as I drive back and forth between home and work, which is about the London Blitz. And it's really kind of interesting to be doing double-duty on Erik Larson, but I just find his ability to spin a yarn, to tell the story and paint the characters even as he gives a very rigorous account of the actual history, to be so compelling. So I recommend, even though I'm not done with either of them, I recommend both of them to our listeners. How about you? All right, I'm going to recommend two podcasts actually that I have recently started to dig into. One—and these will be familiar names to you and to probably many of our listeners—one is called "Power and Purpose." It's hosted by our former colleague at the National Security Council, Amanda Sloat, and Nathalie Tocci, who I think we both worked with in her capacity as a European diplomat and policy official. And it's interesting to me because it focuses yes on foreign policy, but to a large extent on the sort of human side of policy jobs, policy-making, and they've had a series of interesting guests, many of whom are familiar to us, and recommend that people take a listen. And the other is called "The View From Here" with Jasmin El-Gamal, who worked at the Pentagon in the Obama administration. This one tries to connect foreign policy with the people who are either making it or people who are living through the implications of it in countries that are affected by it. She's recently launched that, she lives in London now but is a former US government official. So two foreign policy podcasts in our broad zone, in our broad lane, that kind of have a different approach and a different take on on the issues that we talk about as well. Let me double down on both those recommendations, they're excellent listens and yeah, highly recommend them to our listeners. So Jon, what's ahead? What should people be looking for? So obviously coming up just next week is one of the biggest geopolitical events that's going to take place in the early part of this year and that's—at least we expect it to take place—the summit between President Trump and President Xi Jinping in Beijing. This is obviously much anticipated, it's been delayed once because of the Iran war, by all indications it's going to go ahead even though the war is not yet resolved. And I think we and others are going to be closely watching both the body language and the messaging and the pageantry around it, and also whatever deliverables or deals are announced as part of it, which I think we know have been under debate and discussion and negotiation for months now between the teams from Washington and the teams from Beijing. So we'll be looking at the economic dimensions of this, the security dimensions of this, the technology dimensions of this, and I think we'll go deeper on this next week in our in our podcast. It'll be timed, I think, well to this meeting actually taking place. Yes, we will do a preview podcast the day before the summit kicks off towards the end of next week. We'll be doing a podcast going deep on every aspect of the Trump-Xi summit, what to expect, what may be unexpected. And one of the elements that you didn't mention here which will very much be looking at is the connection back to the war in Iran. The Iranian Foreign Minister is actually in Beijing now, or on his way there to talk to the Chinese, and it will be interesting to see how that topic plays out in the context of the meeting between the two leaders. But we will get into all of that in detail next week—a high-stakes summit, a Super Bowl of summits in a way, between the two most significant leaders in the world, and there will be a lot to discuss and a lot that still remains up in the air. So we're looking forward to that, and with that, that's all for today. We look forward to having you back with us next week. Thanks everybody. Well, that's all for today. We'll be back next week with a new episode of The Long Game. Don't forget to subscribe to our YouTube channel so you never miss an episode, and send us your questions and comments at longgame@voxmedia.com. The links are in the show notes.

AI dealmaking surges, from roll-ups to Anthropic's compute push — 5/7/2026

CNBC Television(3.4M) ·56:56 ·4.1K views · 2026-05-07
Alex Taubman (CEO, Long Lake) Madhu Namburi (Managing Director, General Catalyst)
The AI sector has shifted from experimental technology to a "transformation economy" characterized by the rise of "AI roll-ups" taking legacy public companies private and a fundamental move toward autonomous "agentic engineering" where AI now writes 100% of its own code.
TL;DR The AI sector has shifted from experimental technology to a "transformation economy" characterized by the rise of "AI roll-ups" taking legacy public companies private and a fundamental move toward autonomous "agentic engineering" where AI now writes 100% of its own code....

TL;DR

The AI sector has shifted from experimental technology to a "transformation economy" characterized by the rise of "AI roll-ups" taking legacy public companies private and a fundamental move toward autonomous "agentic engineering" where AI now writes 100% of its own code.

The Rise of the "AI Roll-Up" Strategy

Venture capital firms are moving beyond selling software to enterprises; they are now acquiring legacy service companies outright to implement AI-driven productivity gains internally.

  • The Long Lake-Amex GBT Deal: Long Lake, a three-year-old venture-backed company, agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for $6.3 billion at a 65% premium.
      • Strategy Focus: The goal is to apply AI to "non-tech" service industries (healthcare, insurance, property management) that have historically struggled to scale without massive headcount increases.
  • "Service as Software": Madhu Namburi (General Catalyst) describes a shift where high-retention service businesses are transformed by AI to drive operating leverage, allowing companies to grow revenue by 20%+ without adding staff.
  • Nexus Platform: Long Lake uses a proprietary AI platform to customize models for specialized workflows (e.g., HOA management), reportedly performing 5x better than off-the-shelf models.
  • Long-Term Outlook: Long Lake CEO Alex Taubman plans to "own and operate these businesses forever," suggesting that these AI-integrated service companies may eventually return to public markets as a new category of "Applied AI" stocks.

The Founder Landscape and the "AI Gap"

Morgan Stanley’s inaugural Founders Summit revealed a significant disconnect between the perceived importance of AI and the actual support available for its implementation.

  • The 23% Support Gap: While 95% of founders identify AI as critical to their business, only 23% feel supported in its deployment—the widest gap found in the bank’s research.
  • Operational Challenges: The struggle is no longer about the technology itself but "operationalizing" it—managing change, integrating it into workflows, and keeping up with the rapid pace of new model releases.
  • Liquidity Trends: Despite the abundance of private capital, two-thirds of founders still plan to IPO eventually. However, "secondary" paths (tenders and private share lending) have become mainstream; Morgan Stanley handled $20 billion in tender transactions last year alone.
  • Founder Performance: Research confirms that founder-led companies continue to outperform the broader market once public.

Industrial-Scale Compute and PE Partnerships

The demand for compute is currently exceeding even the most "extreme internal forecasts" at frontier labs, leading to massive new infrastructure deals and private equity vehicles.

  • SpaceX & Anthropic Deal: Anthropic announced a massive compute partnership with SpaceX to utilize the Colossus 1 data center in Memphis, bringing 220,000 Nvidia GPUs and 300 megawatts online this month.
  • Private Equity Shift: Traditional PE firms are now striking direct deals with AI labs to fund deployment:
      • OpenAI: Launched a $10 billion deployment vehicle with TPG, Brookfield, Advent, and Bain.
      • Anthropic: Formed a $1.5 billion joint venture with Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman, and Goldman Sachs.
  • Expanding Infrastructure: This is in addition to Anthropic's existing 5GW partnerships with Amazon and Google, and Microsoft’s $30 billion partnership with Nvidia.

From "Vibe Code" to Agentic Engineering

Anthropic's development team reports a "phase change" in how software is created, moving toward a world where humans manage autonomous agents.

  • 100% AI-Written Code: One year ago, 90-95% of Anthropic’s "Claude Code" was written by the model. Today, that figure is 100%. Engineers no longer write source code; they manage agents that manage other agents.
  • A "Printing Press Moment": Boris Cherny (Anthropic) compares the current moment to the invention of the printing press, predicting that the democratization of software creation will trigger a new industrial revolution.
  • Proactive Agents: New features like "Loops" (scheduling tasks) and "Routines" (event-based responses) allow AI to work autonomously behind the scenes without constant human prompting.
  • The Evolving Role of Engineers: The focus for human developers is shifting from syntax and execution to "judgment, taste, and deciding what is worth building."

Notable Quotes

"Software, SaaS, the definition was software as a service. I believe it's going to be service as software... the era has come and I think it's going to happen a lot more." — Madhu Namburi, General Catalyst
"We took growth from zero to 5 percent up to over 20 percent revenue growth. When you grow 20 percent... you need more people. Even with the productivity gain, we're creating a lot of jobs." — Alex Taubman, Long Lake
"To me this is like a printing press moment. Everyone is starting to become able to write software in a way that was not the case before... Without reading and writing the Renaissance could not have happened." — Boris Cherny, Anthropic
"The 95 percent point speaks to the consensus that the Fourth Industrial Revolution is here... But the 23 percent who are struggling with it? That's the teething process of moving from being awed by the technology to operationalizing it." — Mandell Crawley, Morgan Stanley
Hi everyone. No pullback for the AI trade and this week alone tells you why. There were major deals everywhere that you looked. A venture-backed roll-up taking a public company private, the two biggest AI labs both striking just enormous private equity partnerships on the same day, and then you had Anthropic announcing a massive new compute deal with SpaceX. It is Thursday, May 7th. I am live from Napa Valley at Morgan Stanley's inaugural Founders Summit. So let's get into it. Start with the deals. On Monday, a three-year-old venture-backed company called Long Lake agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for 6.3 billion dollars, a 60 percent premium. That is the second time in five months that a venture-backed roll-up has crossed into public markets. It's called an AI roll-up. If you haven't heard of it, you certainly will. The CEO of Long Lake, Alex Taubman, and General Catalyst's Madhu Namburi, they are on the stream today breaking down the strategy and talking next targets. Okay, so that is the new buyout opportunity. But traditional private equity is looking at AI transformation in a different way. They're doing deals directly with the AI labs. This week, OpenAI and Anthropic each announced major partnerships with private equity firms. OpenAI launched a 10 billion dollar deployment vehicle with TPG, Brookfield, Advent and Bain. Anthropic announced a one and a half billion dollar JV with Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman and Goldman. And then there's compute. We are always talking about compute. On Wednesday, Anthropic announced a deal with SpaceX to take over all the—to take over capacity at their Colossus 1 data center in Memphis. 220,000 Nvidia GPUs, 300 megawatts coming online this month. And that is on top of 5 gigawatts with Amazon, 5 gigawatts with Google and Broadcom, and a 30 billion dollar partnership with Microsoft and Nvidia. So the co-heads of Claude Code, this is now sort of the massive engine within Anthropic, Boris Cherny and Cat Wu, they also are going to be joining the live stream to talk about compute and how growth is running above their most extreme internal forecast. Which brings me to where I am right now, Morgan Stanley's inaugural Founders Summit in Napa Valley. This is the bank that sits in the middle of all of this. They led the hottest IPOs of the last few years, including Reddit, CoreWeave, and Figma. They're the front-runner on SpaceX, and this week they published research on the founders that are building this trade. Lots of really good stats in there. And joining me first to break all of that down—it's just out, by the way, so we get this exclusive look at this report—we've got Mandell Crawley, Chief Client Officer at Morgan Stanley, who is hosting this summit, which I have to say has been incredible. I mean, I feel like everywhere I walk, I'm running into founders that, you know, are behind some of the biggest companies, biggest deals. The research is really great too. So you're the exact right person to start with. Sure. Sure, Deirdre. First of all, you know, thank you so much for having me. And it is great of you to join us here at this conference. As you mentioned, it's the inaugural Founders Summit for Morgan Stanley. We've- we've long had this ambition at the firm to deliver our integrated platform to clients, and we want to do that as consistently and systematically as we can. And when you think about the clients that we serve, what better community than founders? I mean, they are tailor-made for the integrated firm in action. And what I mean by that is that it's a community—and you touched on this at the opening in terms of just they're builders. They're so critical to the economy. It's where job creation happens. It's where innovation ultimately lives. But it's also a community that has a unique set of needs. And we've got founders represented across each of the stages, from Series A to Series C plus. But they're all focused on growth. They're all thinking about capital, liquidity and the varying paths to liquidity. And of course, talent matters. And so when you put all of that together and I think about the platform that Morgan Stanley has to literally be their partner throughout their life cycle, whether that's in the corporate context, the founder context or the employee context. And so this conference is all about creating those connections. Right. I know we're focused on sort of the private founder here before you reach the public markets, but it's so interesting when you look at the stats, right, in public markets. It's founder-led companies that tend to outperform. So really interesting to, like, get them at this moment and see what's on their mind. I wonder why now? Why do your first one now? Is there something special or unique about this cohort of founders? And I know your research says that a lot of them are actually thinking about that path to being a public company. Well, and the why now question is—it's a- it's a fair one, but in many respects we've been doing this for a long time. As you know, we've been doing our TMT conference for over 30 years. We do a great Next Gen conference in Utah. So we've been in this ecosystem for a very long time. And you know our track record in the- in the capital markets space in terms of being on this journey with founders from the private space to the- to the- to the public market. But, you know, we wanted to be—just given the- the trends that we're seeing, whether it's the blurring of lines between private markets and public markets, companies are staying private for longer. When I think about things that are happening in the secondaries context which obviously has connectivity to this community and Morgan Stanley, we've been quite intentional about the secondary market. That- that was part of the industrial logic of our acquisition of Equidate. We built this massive workplace platform. So when you put all of that together, we felt like this community was worth having a summit devoted to it all on- unto itself. And I have to say, thank you for having me here because I know this is closed to press. All of this stuff is off-record. We're lucky enough to have you here to tell us what's going on. But you coincided it with this Founders report, which is really interesting. And by the way, this is Mandell's first live stream. So get your questions in and comments on real-time. We'll ask it to him, like, right here. We already have a few rolling in. But let me ask you first about the report. Is there anything that surprised you the most about it? There were a number of takeaways from the report and—and I don't know how much time we have, but—but there are a number of things I could touch on. I would say there are three- two or three, maybe three or four themes that jump out to me. Number one, which was somewhat obvious, they're all focused on- on growth. There's a great deal of urgency around, you know, growing their companies and making sure that—you know, they're growing revenue responsibly and frankly at all costs. Second was this notion of liquidity. And as we know, the path to liquidity has diversified. Historically, liquidity was essentially when you went to IPO. Now there are many other tools, right, for founders to access liquidity. The third thing that jumped out to me, which kind of gets back to this idea of partnerships and why networks are so important, was founders who have outperformed or reached, you know, breakout status. They were the ones to really talked about, you know, the competitive edge that comes by having high-quality networks and partnerships and what have you. And then the last was something that won't be a surprise to you, this idea that personal wealth and business success are like two parallel paths? They're one. It's one path, right? So those are the big themes that really jumped out to me. Okay, let me give you the thing that surprised me the most. Okay. Everyone's talking about AI here, right, obviously. That is the least surprising thing in the world. What is AI? Yeah, yeah, yeah. No, no. The thing that was surprising actually came from your report. Let me read this out. 95 percent of founders told you that AI is critical. Okay, like, obviously. Only 23 percent feel supported on it and that was the widest gap in the whole survey. Does that surprise you? It- it did. Especially because you have such a sort of cohort from here that lives in tech and I would have thought that maybe like especially the private founder sorted it out. Like where does that leave the public market ones? Well, here- here's how I think about that, right? The 95 percent point, like I think it just speaks to the consensus that the Fourth Industrial Revolution is here. The massive platform shift that is AI, I think everyone's a believer in that. So that I don't think is all that controversial. But the 23 percent who are struggling with it, that's not entirely surprising to me either. And the reason I say that is because now we've moved away from being awed by the technology and now it's operationalizing it. Like the change management that comes with it and now you're dealing with people, you're figuring out how to get it into your workflows, etc. And the word that we have been using is that there's this teething process that I think everybody is going through. But what I get excited about is a couple of things. One, at Morgan Stanley, again, we're doing a tremendous amount of work to operationalize this in the context of our client coverage teams. We're going to be able to do more, spending a lot more time on high-quality, high-value work with clients, move out some of the mundane tasks. So again, really excited about that. This idea that we can do more with our existing staff. And then you think about the abundance of the capital spend. We're in this massive CapEx cycle and as a financing matter, Morgan Stanley is in the middle of that. You know, the debt financing in particular where we're a market leader in that context. And then bringing it back to this conference and founders and what are we doing to help them address a bit of the disconnect that's implied in the question—that is a couple of things. One is convening forums like this where you get this community together to share ideas, best practices and frankly, oftentimes you get folks who are willing to actually do some business together. So that's a positive. But then at Morgan Stanley, we have this track record of really working with founders and getting them ready for enterprise. I guess it surprised me because when you look at like the public markets, right, the AI trade especially in the last few weeks has just been like up and to the right and there's been less talk about that ROI. So it's really interesting to hear from founders themselves and say like, we're not quite there yet. There's still this gap. We're all using it, but do we feel totally supported? Like not yet. How does that compare though? Because this is one slice of what you look after as Chief Client Officer. How does that compare to non-tech or non-founder clients? I think it's consistent. I think this is a ubiquitous feeling that most folks are having. Again, because it's like we know that this is going to be great and now how do we incorporate this technology responsibly into not just again, I talked about our client-facing businesses, but into our infrastructure stack, our control functions, and how do we do all of that stuff in a very responsible way. And so there's an- anxiety is probably not the right word but there's just a- and by the way, the other thing you have to remember is just the pace of change. That's the other thing that I think is driving a little bit of some of that sentiment that came through in the survey is just that just when you think you've got your arms around it, there's a new model, there's a new capability. And so I just think the pace and the expectations and the human aspect of the change management dynamic is the piece that creates a little bit of the complexity. Right. Let me get to a question from the live stream. This is from Carson, loyal viewer to us. Thank you, Carson. What are the ages of the founders of AI-oriented companies? I would think younger people would come up with the breakthrough new AI company business models. The demographic definitely skews a bit—a bit younger. But if you just were to do a quick demographic check of the 70, 65 or 70 founders that we have here, it's a little bit of all over; it's all of the above. So you have first-time founders, you've got folks who are on their third or fourth company. And frankly, that's the power of this new technology. Yeah, I actually was talking to a founder yesterday, was on his fourth company, was an AI company, did things that were totally different before that. Let's talk a little bit about IPOs. You said this at the beginning, Morgan Stanley has really been like a leading IPO banker in the valley for a long time on some of the hottest ones. How are founders feeling about that milestone? Because—this is kind of anecdotal, but I've been, you know, reporting in San Francisco on tech for 10 years and it feels like the IPO fell out of favor a little bit. You would hear often founders say, you know, why would I go public? There's liquidity and there's huge amounts of capital in the private markets. I'm going to be on a hamster wheel with quarterly reporting. Do you think that's changing? Look, I think that there continues to be a bit of this debate around private or public. I'll go back to the survey. About two-thirds of our founders still plan to have an IPO liquidity moment in their future. So I do- I do think a disproportionate percentage of founders still plan to ultimately go public over time and the discipline that ultimately comes with that. There- there are many who view that as a feature. But to your point about just the abundance of capital, there are still some folks who prefer to stay in the private context. I think the thing that we've been excited about is just the diversity of options that founders now have as relates to liquidity. Whether it's tenders, secondaries, which I talked about before, and then a relatively new feature in the lending context, you know, private share lending. As you know with tender offers, this gives your employees an opportunity to get access to liquidity without altering your IPO plans in a material way. Just give you a little bit of nuance here: last year we transacted 20 billion dollars of tender transactions at Morgan Stanley. This year we've already done as many, right, which just gives you a sense of the level of engagement that we're seeing from clients for that particular option. Secondaries, as I said earlier, I mean, it's a space that we're really bullish on. Again, a big part of why we bought Equidate. A third of our founders in the survey talked about having a secondary path, you know, in their not-too-distant future, but they still plan to go public over time. And then as I mentioned, you know, we've been doing some really cool co-creation with clients around—in the corporate and in the asset management space—around private share lending. One thing that I appreciated about this report is you asked founders about the macroeconomic environment and I don't think we talk enough about that here. It's almost like it doesn't exist when you're in this sort of AI bubble. And it was interesting, how much does that go into their decisions to raise- to raise capital, to fundraise? Look, I- I think that, you know, founders almost by definition, you know, they've got these big visions and they have this deep-seated belief that they can do the impossible. And they gotta convince people that they can ultimately achieve that. You know, these are the folks who ultimately are going to give them capital over time. But, yeah, the macroeconomic environment obviously matters. We've got a geopolitical landscape that's created a little bit of instability. But, you know, through the founders that we talk to, our clients, there is an underlying sentiment of being very constructive and they're seeing what's happening in the capital market, whether it's in the IPO context and the M&A context. I mean the vibrancy there I think is giving them the confidence that there's- there's- brighter days ahead. It's interesting. There's some founders that want to raise more because they're nervous about the macro. Or their investors are. Ali Ghodsi is one of them. He told us like a month or two ago that he raised more because he wasn't sure what was going to happen this year. And then some founders don't want to raise more than what they need. There's a whole—a whole spectrum. Let me ask you about this idea. We often talk about wartime and peacetime CEOs. And it's interesting applied to founders. Like, is a founder always wartime because it's do or die for them? Where- where are we right now in this moment, do you think? Well, look, I think depending on one's perspective, you can- you can take an overly optimistic view of the world or you can get pretty pessimistic. Again, depending on one's perspective. I think when you're a founder, and I think most founders will tell you and by the way, you go back generations, I think there's certain things that are just evergreen. The tension around growth, and growth continues to be the main thing. But then capital, and it's not just about, you know, raising capital at any price. It's who am I taking capital from? And what are the benefits, right, in the spirit of partnership—you know, can they deliver my company? I think the real point of tension is liquidity. Right? Because you have the tension around your early investors as well as your- as well as your talent, right? It becomes a—it's a really important part of the overall business strategy. And then—again, back to this idea of being—of partnerships and making sure that you're partnering with the right companies that are giving you the right advice as relates to who you should be raising capital from. A lot of talk about growth, capital. What about profitability? Where are we? Are we growth at all costs? Are founders concerned with profitability right now when you have an OpenAI and Anthropic burning billions? I- I think it varies by segment, but I think the bias continues to be top-line growth. Top-line growth. Yeah. Well, thank you for giving us some of your time and inviting us here. Will you do it again next year? Absolutely. I mean, just hearing from, you know, the founders who are here and—and by the way, they brought their partners with them. And the sentiment has been off the charts. And so like I said at the very beginning, this is a group that is tailor-made for Morgan Stanley, the integrated firm and—and seeing that in action. But I really appreciate you being here. And you'll do the report next year too so we can see how that sentiment is changing? Looking forward to that. Absolutely. Absolutely we will. And maybe we'll have some of these in the public markets if this IPO, if it continues. Mandell, thank you so much. Pleasure to chat with you. Thank you. Thanks a lot. Thank you. So Mandell just laid out the gap. 95 percent of founders say that AI is critical. Only 23 percent feel supported on it. So this is a deployment issue everyone's been trying to solve for years. So venture capital is what I want to get to next. Venture capital is doing something different. It's called the AI roll-up. Instead of selling AI to big companies, telling them how to use it or dropping in for a few months, they're buying the companies outright. So put your own engineers inside, run them with AI immediately. On Monday, this trend crossed into public markets for the second time in just a few months. Long Lake, a three-year-old venture-backed company probably haven't heard of, it agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for 6.3 billion dollars, a 60 percent premium. General Catalyst is backing them. Josh Kushner's Thrive running a similar playbook. You've got Lightspeed, Andreessen, Khosla, they're in this trend too. The targets are industries that tech has never really touched, like healthcare, insurance, property management, construction. Wall Street should definitely be paying attention to this because this is an emerging M&A theme. A new buyer, a new playbook and a new exit for public companies that cannot make the AI transition on their own or for a public market that may be a little less patient. I sat down this week with Alex Taubman, the CEO of Long Lake, and Madhu Namburi, the General Catalyst managing director leading this strategy. Here's what they told me. Alex, Madhu, thank you so much for joining me here on the set today. Madhu, let me start with you because the simple version of this story is that General Catalyst has built an AI roll-up strategy. Five months ago you took Jance Henderson public—private, I mean. Yesterday you announced GBT through Long Lake. So walk me through how GC ended up here. When did this become the strategy? General Catalyst has been doing this, actually, now three, four years. And it has only accelerated a lot more specifically with AI. Put in place a lot of tools. So we are at a point where we have- we have helped amazing companies like Long Lake who are becoming category definers in the- in the verticals they play and giving them all the tools needed to really become the big transformation play in the industry. When you think of venture capital though, you think about technology companies, earlier stage startups. Was it always the strategy to go after non-tech or did the AI age enable that? If I step back, services is a 20 trillion dollar market. One of the biggest issues in services was its inability to scale. Growth is slower, margins are lower because every time these services companies had to grow, they had to add a lot of bodies. And at the heart of it, AI is enabling a fundamental transformation. Where these services companies—by the way, they have amazing customer retention, amazing stickiness of the business—but now AI is enabling to grow these companies without adding more head count. Right. So does that mean that the head count stays the same? And I understand no one wants to say we're going to cut, but more CEOs publicly have been saying that like Marc Benioff, Andy Jassy. We saw Coinbase this week. Is- is that something that you, you know, someone like you doesn't want to say or is that just the reality of the work you're doing? No. I mean, look, we're- we're generally creating jobs. And so look, the simple anecdote is, if you have an HOA manager, for example, who's now three times as productive, you don't say—you want to have more of those people. They're basically generating more productivity for customers, for the company. And so we've actually been creating jobs in all these industries. And one of the things is as we acquire more businesses, we're actually scaling our distribution. That allows us to make our products and services even better. We've become the best place to work in all these industries and we're actually able to pay our people more. Madhu, zoom out then to this broader AI roll-up strategy. Do you see this ultimately as actually adding jobs even to these companies like AMX GBT, without going into specifics there? That these workforces actually become larger? So at the core of General Catalyst's strategy, exactly as Alex mentioned, we believe AI transformation has to drive an enhanced customer experience, which in our view will drive growth. And that's how we drive the operating leverage, as opposed to, hey, where do we take the heads out. I think that was the old-school way of thinking, but that's not how we are approaching. I mean, this is where we think these people will transform how business is delivered. And I think, you know, I use this word and it's been said before—software, SaaS, the definition was software as a service. I believe it's going to be service as software. And I think that's the era has come and I think it's going to happen lot more. It was so interesting that increasingly, and this is on the GC side, you're looking at public targets, the billions of dollars of value. What are you looking at, Alex, in terms of what you think is viable to sort of change, bring private—why do they need to be private to do so? Well, we think, first of all, we think every company in America needs an AI transformation strategy. And that's what we formed Long Lake three years ago with the ambition of being the premier partner. And whether that's a private company or a public company, we think every company is going to need a partner that looks somewhat like us or a strategy that looks somewhat like us to be—to sort of manage this next technology transformation. And in the 30 companies that we've bought so far, we've seen significant outsized growth, we've created a lot of jobs, our- satisfaction of customers, team members going way up. And so I think every company wants that, whether you're small, medium or large. If we can drive that kind of an outcome—I think—so I- I do think we're going to- you're going to see more of these larger deals happening with people that look like us and we certainly, you know, expect to be part of that. Are you looking at more public companies? You know, we're always staying flexible and if there's great companies that, you know, fit our strategy, you know, we'd- we'd like to partner with them. What's the profile of a company that fits your strategy? So we typically look for businesses like AMX GBT that have many years, in this case over 100 years of customer trust. So we're looking for established businesses that have a wonderful reputation with their customers and then we look at how can we take our AI platform—and we have a proprietary platform called Nexus—so we can deploy into the companies to deliver more value to the customers. Madhu, you were on the banking side for many years. What happens to an AMX GBT or another sort of legacy company that can't make that transition, you know, quick enough in public? What happens if they don't sort of take this route? We're seeing this right now overall. Right? There are a lot of amazing companies. Valuations have gotten hit hard because there's a lot of fear of the unknown. And- and it's very—investors usually do not take the risk, especially you don't know what the outcome will be in three, five years out and there's a lot of concern. However, if you peel back, there are companies like GBT that have done—that have an exceptional customer trust, exactly as Alex alluded to—has done an amazing job driving their technology transition. Could we now put an amazing platform like Long Lake on top of it and turbocharge with AI? I think that's the opportunity we see. Whether it's public or private, we're looking at across the board. We see there are some public companies which you can't make those hard transitions as a public because it does go through some volatility. And it's better to do that as a private company because then we like—we believe in growth, we want to drive growth, and it allows us much more flexibility to go invest in companies and play- you know- tools which you can't do as a public company. Where are you guys seeing the most value right now? Is it beaten-down public stocks or what does it look like in the private market? You know, for us it's- it's because we're- we're a company. We're going to- we plan to own and operate these businesses forever. So we don't- we're not that focused on value upfront. We think about what is the correct service lines for us to be in, where can we drive the most value for customers, where can we drive the most growth. Own and operate forever. So what does that mean for sort of the longer run? Does that mean that eventually a Long Lake would think about a public listing? I think so, yeah. I think probably Long Lake one day will be public if we execute well. And part of the thesis is here that we want to continue scaling. And we think the- the TAM here is really the entire economy. It's a 20 plus trillion dollar TAM. I think it's the biggest TAM; it's multiple times the size of the software TAM. And so in order to continue to scale and sort of meet the- the opportunity, we're going to need to have to continue having access to capital at attractive prices. And I joked with Madhu that my childhood dream was to one day be an investment grade bond issuer—but— We're going to do the IPO. Do better. Well, and okay, this is a perfect question to ask you, Madhu, because you were on the banking and the IPO side and M&A side for so long. What is the public comp for something like a Long Lake? Is it a private equity company? Is it sort of this new AI field? What do you think of this? So—great question. This is my view here's what's going to happen and there's a lot of focus on: are the IPO markets back, not back. If I step back and this is across the last decade, a third of the IPOs, give or take, were tech companies. Another third was, give or take, healthcare. And everybody else was the last third. Now within the tech, roughly, depending on the cycle, 50 to 80 percent were software. So here's what's going to happen in my view. I think the mix within tech will transform. You'll have some of the hardware, you know, applied AI companies actually will take a bigger portion. Labs? Well, I'm talking of the IPO. Okay. But the most fascinating thing, which in my view you will see, is the emergence of a whole category for IPOs which will come really from the third bucket. I think that's going to become very prominent. It may take some time. I probably think Long Lake is the biggest in applied AI and, you know, hopefully we'll get this IPO the first. But I think that's where you'll see emergence. By the way, they all will be much ahead of the rule of 40, by miles. So—so how do you value something like that? Is it tech or legacy? So I think you will value them like a growth company. That's why I used the word the rule of 40. Right? There will be some metrics, you know, do you have the same customer retention, are you able to—what is the use of AI? I think there's going to be new KPIs that will come together. I do think those companies will be valued by far at a premium because the risk profile on those companies is lot lower. You can actually model in what the outlook looks like five years out and you can have a predictability that these are high-growth, high-margin, defensible businesses with a specific moat. And I think Long Lake in my view, especially when we look at the portfolio of all the companies, will be absolute best-in-class. I know it's early, but what do you think that timeline looks like? You know, we'll- we'll see what the market conditions are, but we continue to see, you know, a lot of growth in our strategy and I think it could be in the next couple of years. Okay. Let's move on to sort of traditional private equity. You mentioned this, Madhu—yesterday Blackstone, Goldman, Hellman & Friedman put one and a half billion dollars into a JV with Anthropic. OpenAI announced its own version with Apollo. What do you think traditional PE is trying to do with these partnerships? I—as I mentioned, I do think all these companies have to embrace AI transformation. It's very easy to say, it's very hard to build that capability. And General Catalyst and Long Lake, we built that two, two and a half years ago and we have continued to perfect that. The difference though is that they already hold these companies. So it's a little bit more of playing defense, right? They're not actually acquiring new companies like Long Lake is. Why is your version, both of you, more effective? I think on the Long Lake side we're purpose-built. So we have a very different—we're a permanent owner so we can make these transformations. Some of these bets will take time to fully play out and I don't think a three to five year hold period of traditional private equity is optimized for that. Number one. And number two, you know, I think that being deeply AI-native in your founding team. So our team was, you know, the founding team which came together mostly through network is—was 75, 80 percent applied AI engineers from places like, you know, Palantir, Google, Robinhood, Ramp, places like that. And we think that sort of having that deep technical DNA when you're looking at businesses, when you're partnering with businesses, when you're building a plan for businesses, is just a real differentiator. But what's also very notable is that traditional PE, you know, the Thoma Bravos, the Vista Equities, have gone after software companies. You're not going after software companies. You're going after non-tech services companies. So, Madhu, like just talk a little bit about why that's the better bet right now and what happens to software. Is that a harder industry to turn around? I think these guys have done an exceptional job in the last decade plus to create a lot of value through software. I just—as I mentioned, we are in such a new era right now where the market is basically telling us that AI is going to—is an existential question for software now. So we at General Catalyst, we are looking at all the areas wherever we have a high conviction. I think Long Lake is our bet and it's an amazing—has worked out, fingers crossed, very, very well on the services which in our view is massive. As I said, it's much bigger than the entire software—multiple times bigger. We're also making a bet similarly in the healthcare. You know, we have- we have other sub-sectors. So our view is we're going to look at everything that has lot more potential to drive this transformation at a faster rate. We don't want to approach from a defensive standpoint. How do you pitch limited partners on this strategy because correct me if I'm wrong the returns in this kind of strategy look more like two or three times versus a venture strategy where you're betting on 10x from an outlier. We believe we have an unfair advantage given all the things I've mentioned. It should—our hope and target is to create that—a disproportionately more attractive returns. I'm not going to get into specific numbers. We think we have all the playbook. I mean even if we look at so General Catalyst was the first investor in when we led the first round Long Lake raised. And from that point versus what it's worth, it has been an absolute grand slam. I was going to play devil's advocate and ask you sort of what makes you good at this because the private equity firms, Vista, Thoma Bravo, they spent 30 years building operating teams before they actually ran take privates. You're three years old, right? First of all, everything moves faster with AI. So three years in AI is—is—is actually like three decades of pre-AI. But I think to be successful at this strategy you need three things. You need number one, a world-class applied AI engineering team which Long Lake was founded with and continues to accelerate. Two, a world-class change management function and three world-class M&A. And so Long Lake was purpose-built with that in mind and you know, if you're a, you know, let's say HOA management company and you- and you partner with Long Lake and all of a sudden you now have access to this extraordinary team of applied AI engineers. That are going to live in your offices for the next, you know, couple of years helping solve all your biggest pain points and helping you grow and we have this change management operation that helps drive a lot of growth. So I'd like to highlight, I'm very proud of what we've done is that for the companies that we've deployed Nexus into, our AI platform, we've taken growth from zero to 5 percent up to over 20 percent revenue growth. And so when you talk about creating jobs and this is—I know this is different from what's being talked about by a lot of people—but we're seeing it in the real world. When you grow 20 percent and you drive that kind of growth and your customers are asking for more services from you, you need more people. And so even with the productivity gain, we're adding a—we're creating a lot of jobs. And so I actually think that this is going to be much more positive for the economy than anybody realizes. Are those the kind of jobs people want? Services, though. Oh yeah, absolutely. Well first of all, they're much better jobs than they ever were before. Because if you were spending 40 hours a month copying data from one software system into another or taking 13 different software systems and taking the data and putting it into a PowerPoint presentation, now our AI assistant can do most of that for you. And you can spend the rest of your time, which is what you want to do, on-site with the customers or going in winning new customers or thinking of different creative ways to solve problems. You see the, you know, hundreds of billions of CapEx that the labs are investing and that is what's enabling all of this. But somebody's got to take that hundreds of billions of investment and turn it into GDP growth. And so what Long Lake was formed to do is to actually convert that investment into the GDP growth. And that's what we do and it's hard work. It's—it's—it's being all around the country, spending time with the team members, solving problems. Isn't that what OpenAI and Anthropic are supposed to be doing on their own? I mean, if we're in this AI era, do we need sort of a middleman like a Long Lake and when I look at the traditional PE model how they're just partnering directly with an Anthropic or OpenAI, what makes your proprietary platform better than just plugging in Claude and getting Anthropic? Yeah, it's—it's a great question and I think the big difference is each of these industries has very specialized workflows and data sources that are highly specialized. So the HOA management for example is a—is a relatively niche—it's actually a big business it's going to be a multi-hundred billion dollar TAM—but it's a relatively niche use case that the model companies are not optimizing for. And so we sort of take the best-in-class models we harness them in our platform, we give them specific specialized workflows and skills and tools that are heavily customized for these end markets and it drives much better performance in the field. So what we're actually seeing in our custom eval set that we create for these industries, our Nexus platform is actually performing five times better than the off-the-shelf models. Interesting. Well looking forward to hopefully getting more of that data as you guys sort of move towards maybe a public listing in the future. Wonderful to talk to both of you, Alex, Madhu, thanks very much for coming. Thank you. Thanks for having us. Thank you. So none of this works without the models underneath and Anthropic has had one of the most remarkable years in AI in tech as a whole, really. Claude Code, their developer product, it has been what is powering a lot of this transformation and the product itself is now doing something wild. A year ago, Anthropic said that 90 to 95 percent of Claude Code was written by Claude. Today that number is 100 percent. So that means the engineers have stopped writing actual code. Instead they're prompting agents that write code and now agents that manage other agents. I sat down with Boris Cherny and Cat Wu, the two co-leads of Claude Code earlier this week at Anthropic's Developer Day. We got into the SpaceX compute deal, what it means for enterprises trying to catch up, and why they think we're living through a printing press moment for software. Here is that conversation. First, Boris and Cat, thank you so much for making the time on this Developer Day. I know it's really busy, great atmosphere outside. Got music, lots of people around, lots of excitement. I have to start with the news though, because this morning, I mean really just an hour ago, you guys Anthropic announced the SpaceX deal. More than 200,000 GPUs coming online this month, and in response to that you guys have doubled Claude Code rate limits, removed peak hour throttling, raising API limits for Opus models. What changed? Our- our growth has just been explosive. Like—it- it's just so much more than even what we predicted. It was even more than our most extreme forecast. What- what happens is every time that we release a model, growth inflects and it inflected with Opus 4 back in May of last year, 4.5 November of last year, 4.6 in February and now 4.7. So it just like keeps inflecting. And the hard thing with financial forecasting is: how do you plan for this? Right. And so now—it's actually very—very difficult to plan for and we want to be responsible in the way that we plan for it and the thing that we're excited for now is—we have the compute to support all this load and there's going to be even more compute coming online to support all the upcoming load. So Cat, were you constrained before and were there things that you wanted to do that this will now give you the ability to do at Claude Code? I think the main thing that this gives us is builders can just do a lot more with our products and it just means that, you know, as much as there's been just so much demand and this just means that we can give back a bit more. Right. We gotta talk about the Elon Musk of it all because in the past he has called Anthropic "woke AI", "misanthropic". Was there any weirdness on the team when the deal closed and I also want to note, and I know you guys this just happened, so I'll read you a tweet from him, he just put on X: "By way of background for those who care, I spent a lot of time last week with senior members of the Anthropic team to understand what they do to ensure Claude is good for humanity and was impressed. Everyone I met was highly competent and cared a great deal about doing the right thing. No one set off my evil detector, so as long as they engage in critical self-examination, Claude will probably be good." We care so deeply about the mission of safety—and it feels like Elon is very aligned with us and it's just—it's—it's exciting I think from that point of view. It's just from this kind of like deepest belief. Now with this deal, as you guys have been saying, you have a lot more capacity to let developers, enterprises do exactly what they need to do. OpenAI has sort of been going the other way and you talked Boris about being responsible. They've been burning, you know, billions of dollars to essentially subsidize Codex, right? You guys have held the line this year. Now you've got more compute. So does that discipline hold or do you start spending more because you see this maybe as like a critical moment? For us the focus has always been the same. It's always been enterprises and developers and coding and that's not changing. We have always been focused on the long term. We want to continue to be able to do this responsibly in the long term to serve our customers so they can use our products and they can build on our platform and this isn't changing. Did you see anything in the data that made this more urgent? The idea to bring on more compute online? Do you think you would make more deals? Is it sort of that like unlimited space you just want as much as possible? It's very clear that we're on this exponential of growth and so every month there's just far more users than they were the previous month. And so it's definitely critical for us to continue growing our compute to—to make sure that we serve everyone well. And I think it's especially important for us to make sure that we keep serving our existing users like the people who've been with us from the very beginning. And so I think what this compute lets us do is bring on all these new users while keeping the service the same or better for everyone. Did you understand what you had on your hands when you shipped it? Did you think it could have the impact that it has had on entire industries? Claude Code originally was actually not a hit. Right, you weren't even sure if you wanted to ship it, right? We weren't sure. Yeah, it's like this is the journey of building—like—we knew we wanted to build something in coding but the journey of building a product is sort of—it feels like wandering in the desert for a little bit. It's actually like a very lonely journey. And the compass is just talking to users and seeing what they want. And so at the beginning Claude Code was not a hit, even though at Anthropic everyone was using it, we didn't know if people outside of Anthropic would use it. And what happened is at some point we released Opus 4 and it just clicked and it started to work in a way that it didn't and it's just been working more and more and more. It just keeps inflecting. But I think this was like the result of many months of deep belief that there was something amazing and that we just had to improve the product enough and improve the model enough that the rest of the world could intuitively grasp it. And Cat actually understood this really early on. Like—at the beginning it was just the first like couple months it was just me building it. And Cat's a product manager. And, you know, she codes like on—on—on the side. But immediately she started using this product and just sending me feedback like every single day. Like, this is broken, this is broken, this is broken. And then I would just like go in and fix it and as the team grew this just continues to be the way that everyone operates. We love hearing feedback, we love fixing stuff. Nothing is like, you know, we have some good ideas but really it's our users that figure everything out and they're the ones that have the best ideas. But when I talk to people outside of the Bay Area or outside of tech, it still feels like they don't really get it. There's a big group that still doesn't get it and when you talk about the mainstream public, right, AI is still a chatbot, less sort of an agent that can do things on your behalf. How are you seeing that adoption? Some of the things that we're doing are we're sharing more of the use cases that people find successful. So we're going to like the biggest adopters within sales and marketing and legal and interviewing them and saying, hey, what is the killer use case that you use CoWork for every day? Documenting that and then sharing it with the world. Because I think for a lot of people they enter CoWork and they're like, oh, it's a blank slate and like you're saying I don't understand what it can do, what it can't do.
