We're focused on making sure that they are moving with the industry as it moves, that they are building relationships and that they're creating a flywheel effect such that the students that have graduated and are having success in the market are now reaching back to the juniors and seniors and sophomores behind them and giving them some insight on what it looks like in the real world. Yeah. Since the program has been initiated or the inception of AltFinance, you going around these schools, have you sensed that there is more excitement and more interest in finance and private investment or are you still kind of— Absolutely. I mean, that's one of the earliest signals that we received was that in our first cohort, we had about one out of 30 students that had any relationship or really any deep understanding with alternative investments as an industry. They understood investment banking and they understood that there were sponsors and the buy-side that existed out there. But in terms of really having relationships with people and an understanding of how those firms operate, the awareness was fairly low. In just five years, that number is significant. I mean, we've got 20 to 30 percent of students that are coming in with people that have gone ahead of them, that are telling them about what their roles look like, they're telling them about the type of work that they're doing. They're telling them about the challenges that they should expect to see, that "Yeah, you're going to work a lot of hours. You're going to have to do a lot of work. You've got to be strong in Excel. You've got to be strong in financial modeling" and that there's no concession to that. And that's allowing the newer students to say, "Look, I'm going to put the gauntlet down. I'm going to work a little bit more. I know that the reward at the other side is significant as long as I put the work in." And that's what we're seeing at AltFinance. So as we would expect, our subsequent cohorts have been stronger than our first. Our first cohort was very strong, but the subsequent cohorts are building upon the lessons and experiences of those that went before them and we're seeing that in a very short time. We're seeing results that are going beyond even our wildest expectations. Yeah. Well, listen, it's a who-you-know world, right? And this apprenticeship program is meant—and you let me know if I'm wrong—and designed to help these young kids not only learn the world of alt investment but to have those mentors to call from time to time. I was listening to a podcast that you were saying and how a student called a mentor of his to get some advice, he called someone he knew, and all of a sudden that individual is at the top of the chain because again, it's a who-you-know world, right? For a student who may be interested in AltFinance, how would they go about applying? Do you guys choose a certain amount? Do students just come to you and y'all give them a whole bunch of money? Because I believe over $6 million in financial scholarships that you guys have helped put out. And capacity building grants for the universities themselves. So for students that want to get involved, altfinance.com is our website. Our applications are now closed for our sixth cohort, but they open up again in January. So every January we open up our applications. The only prerequisite is that you have to be a student at an HBCU. It doesn't matter what your sex is, it doesn't matter what your racial background is, but if you attend an HBCU, this is an opportunity for you to learn. Ironically, HBCUs are one of the more diverse schools in the country. In fact, there are a number of students that go to HBCUs that aren't Historically Black. The schools intentionally were designed to be inclusive and the work that we're doing is intentionally designed to be inclusive. What we believe is that there is a treasure trove of value at HBCUs, that other industries have recognized that value over time and we're helping bring that value to the world of finance. And so that really is where our mission is: how can we enhance that pipeline of talent for an industry that is suffering? Right? I mean, that industry is growing fast and needs more talent and we're bringing that talent from a community that we're very familiar with and we can identify and develop great talent at HBCUs to meet the demands of the industry. Yeah. Everyone's always saying DEI, DEI. I say, no, it's diversity of thought. That's it. That's what it is, right? Because you know, we were in meetings today here at Milken where you had partners and company who said "If you don't show us a diverse team, we're not even talking to you. We're not even giving you any money." So although that word may be a taboo today, people still want to see that diversity. Is that the sense that you're getting from major institutions? Look, I use this analogy. I love basketball, I love the NBA. The NBA's been around—But you a football player! I'm a football player, you know, I didn't have the height or the talent to play basketball. And you kicked a 50-yard field goal! We'll get into that. At Morehouse, right? We'll get into that. You know, I didn't have the skill set necessarily to play basketball, although I love it. But if you look at the history of basketball, right? A history that has been dominated over time by a bunch of different ethnic groups. Right? Irish, Italian, Jewish basketball players in the thirties and forties, Black basketball players come in and dominate in the fifties and sixties. What you see now is really interesting and when you think about diversity, this is why I say I don't focus on the DEI, I focus on the talent. Eighty so years we've had 70 MVPs, or 75 MVPs or so in the NBA. For the first 70 years, three foreign-born MVPs. Right? You've got Dirk Nowitzki, you got Steve Nash, and you got Hakeem Olajuwon. Over the past—I believe it's eight years now—every MVP in the NBA has been foreign-born. Yeah. Shai Gilgeous-Alexander from Canada, yeah. Is that because the NBA's putting this focus on DEI and they're giving out MVPs? No! It's because you have incredibly talented players that have been developed through incredible basketball systems in their home countries. In Serbia, in Canada, in Congo, in Greece, right? And thus, you are now building incredible basketball players that are playing basketball in the best conference in the world in the NBA that are coming from other countries that have authentically developed their talent because people put a focus on building them where they are. And that's what we're doing at AltFinance. We're building incredible talent at HBCUs, a community that we know is capable of building incredible talent as evidenced through other sectors, and we're doing it in the world of finance. Love it. What's it like being the CEO of a nonprofit like this in 2026? Again, you know, you hear stories about nonprofits facing a lot of issues right now. There's fundraising issues, bodies, right—they can't find more people to volunteer. What's it like for you being CEO of a nonprofit? You know, I think when you're running a nonprofit, you know, I still—I think about it like a for-profit in that, you know, maximizing shareholder value is important. But I change one keyword, right? Because our shareholders are really our stakeholders. And we have a multiple set of stakeholders. We have the folks that are funding us, we have the students that are in our program, we have the colleges and universities that are housing the students in our program, that are incredible partners of ours. So for me, it makes the job easier in some ways in that there's several different people that we can look to and we can say, "Are we doing a good job? Are we meeting your needs?" But there's also several people whose needs we cannot meet. Right? And that's what keeps me up at night, that's where we put in the hard work is I wake up every day saying, "I've got three or four different stakeholders and how can I make sure that their objectives are aligned?" And the places where they're aligned, that's where I want to dedicate my time. I'm not worried about if, you know, one person thinks we should be doing A and the other thinks we should be doing B. If they both think we should be doing C and C is consistent with our mission, that's where we want to put the focus. Yeah. Well, how do you lead? I was talking to Cathy Novak down in Miami—she's President of the Miami Grand Prix—and she doesn't like layers. She does not like layers when she leads. She likes to have direct people she can call, get the job done, but she hates all those layers in between, right? How does Marcus Shaw lead his organization? I think Cathy and Marcus are well-aligned on that. I come from a coaching background too. Coach basketball, coach football, coach soccer—anything that's competitive, I've coached and I've played. Mike Tomlin is one of my favorite coaches of all time, although I'm a Raiders fan and he was a great Steelers coach. I love a comment that he made about coaching. He said, "I like coaches that like to coach." Right? So some coaches put a ton of time going out there and trying to find the best pedigree and then they just believe that that pedigree will go and win them a championship. But I think that strong coaches and my leadership style—both for my team but also working with my partners and working with students—is based on hard coaching. I'm with you there in the tough times, but we got to get through that. And sometimes that requires us to find a little bit of grit, find a little bit of perseverance and a little bit of determination. In order to do that, we got to be willing to go through the tough times together. I'm always accessible to any of my stakeholders and I believe that that's the key to success for what we're doing at AltFinance, also the key to success for what it takes to be successful in any facet of life. Yeah. Well, you joined 2021, right? Right at inception, right? And so you've been on the job for a few years now. You can answer this one: what's been the biggest positive surprise? You know, I got a couple of compliments from both some of our financial service partners, but also from some of our college leaders. And one thing that really resonated with me was there was a lot of question "Could this work?", right? Could we find great and talented students that could be successful in this industry? And after a few years, I had a pretty esteemed president say, "Marcus, you've proven the thesis." Right? And that to me was probably the biggest positive surprise: was that other people were watching our success and were assessing the work that we were doing and were cheering for us, right? They wanted us to be successful because again, we have multiple stakeholders and everybody was aligned on what success could look like. Everybody was aligned on what they needed to contribute to that, and that they were with us on that path. And so that's been a very positive surprise despite anything that's happening in the larger macro environment. I believe that I've got a dedicated group of stakeholders that are committed to the work that we're doing to increase talent in the pipeline for the industry. Yeah. With positives, Marcus, comes negatives, right? What's been the biggest negative surprise at the job? Unless it's sleep, what's been the negative? You know what keeps me up at night—and I'll say this, I've got two young children myself. Not young children, a 20-year-old and a 16-year-old. And Jabari, you know, if you can remember back to these times, we tend to change our minds sometimes. We tend to change our minds. This is a tough industry. Everybody's not going to elect to go into this, but it doesn't mean that they shouldn't have the opportunity to learn about the industry and have access and exposure to it. And if people decide, "Look, I want to be a lawyer instead of being a private equity investor," I think that's a decision they should make. They are better off for it though, understanding the dynamic of the private equity industry so that they can apply that to their legal studies. We are better off because we now have a connection in the legal community, you know, God willing, if we ever need to have a credit amendment done for a deal, that we have people that we can go to and people that are, I believe, empathetic to the industry. You know, years ago I remember this, Wendy Kopp who started Teach For America, and that there was a young man that was in a fellowship program with me and went through the program and he was a fairly senior investment banker. And I said, "Man, all this time I thought the goal of Teach For America was just to develop a new generation of teachers." But what he said and I thought was really interesting was that that might have been the original theory of change, but it evolved to something bigger and obviously something more important, which was how can you create citizens in this country who, through their experience in education, now have a greater sense of empathy for public education and for education of students? How can you have bankers that will also sit on the school board for their kids' school district? How can you have, you know, military men and women who, when they're home, will make sure that they make it to a PTA meeting and are involved in their education? And so I believe that one of the negatives that I've seen, or what I would perceive as a negative, is actually not a negative in the grand scheme. Everybody's not going to decide to go into this industry, but it doesn't mean that their training in this industry is for naught. They will actually be a benefit to themselves and society by having a greater understanding of what it takes to be in this industry and what success looks like. Yeah, listen, I started off the intro like that, you know, those are words you said years ago and you said, "Listen, you're not going to build wealth by working a nine-to-five. You're not. Investing has to be a part of your strategy to wealth building." Right? And I guess if you were to enter a program like this, even if you don't go all the way through, you will have a great understanding of the world of private investment and how to move about that. That's right. Listen, man, you know, you go into how you lead, but let's take a step back into your background, right? Born in Mississippi but grew up in Maryland—Well, my dad's from Mississippi—my dad's from Mississippi, but you grew up in Maryland, right? In Maryland, grew up in Maryland. What was that like for you? Look, for me, Maryland, my youth was awesome. You know, I grew up in the eighties and nineties and you know, that time has some challenges in and of itself, but I grew up in Prince George's County in Maryland. That was a great hip-hop era though. It was a great hip-hop era. But you know when you grow up in the DC area, you know, there's a genre of music called go-go. Yeah, yeah. And for those outside of the area, they may not know about it, but I actually, you know, listened to a lot of that music growing up. It was a very nuanced regional music and actually when I went to Morehouse is when I really fell in love with hip-hop and with rap music and ironically a lot of other types of music. But growing up in that area in Maryland was incredibly powerful for me. I went to a great high school, Sidwell Friends, and had an opportunity to meet, you know, folks from all over the world, folks that are still incredible friends of mine. What I will say is that it is a little bit of a bubble. And this is what I think is important for young people—I think this is why things like the Milken conference are so important. That you know, growing up in that area where many of the people's parents were attorneys or civil servants and worked for the government, you don't get to see some of the private sector success and malaise that you see in the rest of the country. So when I went to Atlanta for college, when I went to North Carolina after I graduated college and went to grad school, we spent time in New York City, we spent time in Chattanooga, Tennessee, I felt that I got a broader perspective of the world to add to the perspective that I had from growing up in Maryland. And I think it's formed a pretty healthy worldview for the work that I do, but also everything I aspire to be as a father, as a husband, you know, maybe one day as a grandfather. I think that I saw that in my parents. My dad was in the Navy. You mentioned, you know, Mississippi—my dad was born in Mississippi in 1929. Not a great place. You know, he went into the military, he got a chance to see the world. And so one of the things that I think has drawn me to this world of finance is I believe for people that are coming from disparate parts of the country, that the world of finance can do a lot of the things that the military did for my father. It can take you to places, expose you to opportunities that you may not have otherwise. Right? Folks are investing in companies all over the world. When they're investing in companies, they're not doing it necessarily from behind a computer on a spreadsheet. People are going, they're making calls, they're having conversations with people in different regions and different languages to understand the business. But to understand the business, you got to understand the people. You got to understand the region. That's an incredible opportunity for young people. There's a young man in our program, a phenomenal young man—he's actually working at a consulting firm but he's, you know, thinking about what that career could look like in investing. This man was Phi Beta Kappa, didn't have a passport and never traveled outside of Tennessee or Georgia. Okay? That opportunity to move into the world of consulting, to have this exposure to finance—he just sent me a picture of his passport, he was headed down to Latin America for a little bit, a mix of business and pleasure. But he sent me that because for two years we talked about the importance of having a passport, right? And how this can change your world and this can change your worldview. And so I felt that that was incredibly important. Yeah. Well, listen, you said your dad was in the Navy, he was also—you also worked at the Library of Congress, right? And your mom was a school teacher. What'd you learn from her? You know, my mom passed away last year and you know, we talked even when she was going through some of her illness. And there's a couple of things that my mom taught me and I, you know, even as she was dealing with dementia, I kind of got a chance to ask her this. And I said, "Well, what did you want from me? You know, did you want me to be a teacher like you? Did you want me to go into the military like my dad?" She made it clear that that was not something she wanted her only son to do. But she said she never had a career that she wanted me to follow. There were three things that she really wanted me to follow in life. Number one, she wanted me to be respectful. Right? That doesn't mean that you're a sycophant, right? It doesn't mean you're kissing people's butt, right? But it does mean that you're treating every human being with a decency and respect in the same way that you would want them to treat you. It means that you're commanding the respect from them that you're treating them with. That's incredibly important to somebody who was 30 years old before integration. Right? That's a survival instinct, that you've got to demand respect but you've also got to give respect. So number one: to be respectful. You know, number two was that she wanted me to be smart. My mother's a school teacher. I spent hours and hours on end studying when I was little. I was also a pretty good athlete, so she said, "Look, if you're going to compete, you got to go out there and win, but you got to be smart, you got to study." Right? And so that created, I believe, this intellectual drive, this intellectual curiosity to understand things. It's also incredibly important to being an investor. Do you care about how things work? Right? You've got that tablet in front of you. Do you understand how it works? Do you understand how the reflective display works? Do you understand how the processor works? Do you care? No, I don't, me personally. I just want to pay for it and let it do the job. You got to care! That's what the investors will say. You got to care. And you're a journalist; it's the same thing. I think, you know, journalists and investors are very, very similar in their intellectual curiosity. They want to understand how things work, they want to understand and make sense out of the world and then share that with people. Right? Journalists want to share it with the public for free, investors want to share it with a smaller group of people for a fee. Right? That's the difference. Well, at Forbes, we're going to start charging for it. All this free information—the paywall is coming, absolutely. But I would say the last thing that she said—so number one be respectful, number two be smart, and the last thing, which was critically important especially in these times: to be tough. Yes. You got to be tough, right? Because the world is going to change and what was easy for you today is going to be difficult for you tomorrow. And you've got to have resilience and perseverance and fortitude to be able to get through those tough times. And I saw a lot of that in my parents. Life was significantly easier for me than it was for them, and significantly easier for them than it was for their parents. Right? And so those are the things that my mom taught me: the importance of being respectful, the importance of being smart, and the importance of being tough. Well, man, you're smart! Okay, I see a Morehouse College degree, right, in mathematics. I see an electrical engineering degree from Georgia Tech. And then you get your business degree from Duke. Right? So I would say you're a pretty smart brother there. Look, I've studied a lot. I've studied a lot. Well, tell me, how did you study on kicking a 50-yard field goal when you were a player at Morehouse? Let me tell you, it's one of my all-time great stories. If you've ever seen the movie Rudy, right? I like to say like I was Rudy of Morehouse College, right? In the sense that when I went to school, I thought I was good enough to play basketball. We talked about that. I wasn't a very good basketball player, but I loved basketball. I loved basketball like people love art and ballet. Who did you want to be in basketball? I wanted to be Jordan. Jordan's the default. Jordan's the default. I would say my second favorite all-time player though was Kenny Anderson. And you know, that's a deep cut for the New York guys, but you know, I was a Georgia Tech fan back in '89, '90 when Lethal Weapon 3 was doing their thing and you know—Dennis Scott, Nick Anderson, Kenny Anderson—those guys were hooping. And you know, although I was a big time Duke fan later—so you see there's a pattern here. The schools that I liked and the players that I liked, I ended up going to those schools, right? I was a big Duke fan, I love the way that Coach K coached basketball. You know, a lot of my buddies gave me a lot of grief for liking Duke back then when I was in the backyard of John Thompson and you know, the incredible Georgetown Hoyas and Len Bias, God rest his soul, right, and Maryland Terrapins. But I love the way that Duke played basketball. And so for me, that was like the way I thought about sports and I thought I could go play basketball at Morehouse College. I played soccer in high school, played for a pretty good soccer team—So that's where you got your leg! That's right, I got the leg. The leg was a soccer leg. I bet some buddies freshman year during new student orientation, which is the first two weeks of school, that I could kick a 50-yard field goal. And they did not believe it and we snuck onto the field and I kicked the field goal and we were about to get in a bunch of trouble for trespassing. And at the time, the special teams coach said, "Did you kick that field goal?" and I said, "I did sir, I apologize, I know we shouldn't have trespassed on the field." He said, "Well, I need you to come out to walk-ons at the end of the week." And the rest was history. Yeah. So you were a field goal kicker! Field goal kicker. Oh wow. Did you ever think that that could turn into field goal kicking in the NFL? Like, were you recruited? I had a strong leg. The guys that are good at field goal kicking—I went to a Ray Guy camp, a select camp that he had. Ray Guy the famous punter from the Oakland Raiders and the Los Angeles Raiders had a camp up in Suwanee, Georgia. I went out there and there were some really good kickers. I think Jeff Hall who was previously at Tennessee I think was at that camp as well. The guys that are the NFL kickers that can kick, like they don't miss in practice. Yeah, they're unreal. They're just like golfers. Like if you ever watch a practice round with Tiger or any of these guys, you know, they hit everything right. And I had a strong leg but I don't think I was dedicating the time needed to be in the NFL. And you know, look, I got the nerves to pick stocks; I don't know if I have the nerves to hit a 60-yard field goal with the time running off the clock. I mean, listen, that might be a good title for this video: "How a Field Goal Kicker Fell in Love with Finance". I like it. I like it too; I'm going to publish it. Well listen, when you think back, Marcus, and you had such a deep career, but you know, you go to IBM, right, Bank of America, you're a Wall Street trader for a very long time, Management Leadership of Tomorrow... Right, you were with that organization, and then the CEO of the business consultancy, the startup accelerator, the Company Lab. When you had to think of your career, and here you are right now, CEO of AltFinance, you think of your career journey in one word, what word would that be? It'll be purpose. Purpose. Why purpose? For me, purpose is something that roots you and motivates you to get up every morning and do what you're doing. And I think I've always been purpose-oriented. A good buddy of mine, we were recently on a text and we were talking about the difference between happiness and purpose and how people often search for happiness. They search for happiness in life, but happiness is fleeting. Today's your birthday, you get a great present, you're happy. Tomorrow you gotta go back to work and it's not such—your happiness is fleeting. But purpose is something that really can motivate us to do all the things that we do. And sometimes purpose can actually be a little torturous, right? Purpose does not always feel good. I talk about this concept around the burden of optimism, right? And that to be optimistic, to believe that you can make a better thing out of something, sometimes feels burdensome. But I relish in that burden, right? I feel that makes me feel good. Maybe it's, you know, the field goal kicker in me, that am I the guy that can win the game with the time running off the clock? And I might not be the strongest, I might not be the fastest, I might not be the biggest guy on the team, but in that moment I've got to be purposeful. Right? I look at the same work that I've done as an engineer. That was really fueled by intellectual curiosity, but can I create better network systems to help connect men and women in this country to each other? Can we evolve the technology? The work in finance: can I find better companies to invest on behalf of firefighters and school teachers and public workers that are working every day in order to put food on their tables for themselves and their family after retirement? And the work we're doing obviously now with AltFinance... Is can I create a pipeline of incredibly talented young men and young women who have an interest in operating in this world of investment but did not necessarily have the pathway to get there? And so purpose is important to me, it's something that I'm incredibly intentional about and it's what fuels me. I love that, man. I love it. Hey, but you must have been the most hated man on campus that one time. Well listen, get you out of here on some fun stuff, right? But before I do, I always like to ask this question: what's Marcus Shaw's biggest failure and what'd you learn from it? If you ask any great athlete—Michael Jordan talks about this all the time—you know, he's like "I've made X amount of game-winning free throws or game-winning shots," he's like "but I do remember the ones I've missed." You know, 1997, I missed a field goal against Kentucky State that would have won homecoming with time running off the clock. And you know, where I kind of think about that, in my senior year we had a real tough season. Here's the lesson that I take away from that: you really don't lose until you quit. So subsequently, I mean, I've been a big supporter and continue to be a huge fan of Morehouse football, all things Morehouse College... But the contributions that I continue to make both personally and professionally to that school are fueled by the fact that I'll never forget that field goal. Right? And so sometimes a little bit of failure in life, as long as it motivates greater success and greater contribution, it's worth it. Right? Had I made the field goal, I might have thought I could go to the NFL. I might have done something else with my life that was less purposeful. And so I think that—and I point this out to a lot of the young people—we got to use those failures in life as an opportunity to drive forward the future that we want to see, and that if you just kind of stew in the failure, it's of no virtue to anybody. Yeah, love that, man. I love it. Hey, but you must have been the most hated man on campus that one time. Let me tell you, it was not a fun day, man. Of course not. Not a fun day. You couldn't get a date, nothing, right? At all. It was—like I'll tell you, it was a visceral feeling, man. And even thinking about it, I got to go do something purposeful today because that feeling of, you know, you got a hundred guys out there on your team and you got one job to do. You know, it was a little bit of wind, laces might have been to the face of me or something like that, but there's no excuses, right? You got one job to do, you didn't do it, and your team paid the consequence for it. You got to suit back up the next week. You got to suit back up the next week, you got to tie your boots up, and you got to go back out there and get to work. And that's kind of the approach that I take to life, right? Sometimes you're going to get down, sometimes you're going to miss the opportunities that you should have made, but until you quit, you haven't lost the game. Yeah. Well listen, I'm glad those students now that you're helping out, I'm sure that they're glad you missed that field goal, right? 'Cause you coming back to help there, you know? But get you out of here on some fun stuff, right? Some Milken fun questions, right? If I give you 10 grand right now, what's the first thing you invest in? There's an infrastructure play out there that I would be interested in. I you know, I haven't done full due diligence, but I think that the infrastructure strategies that you hear a lot of... I think broadly in the United States that we have infrastructure that is largely depleted and antiquated and that if I look at some of the other emerging—Elaborate, what part of the infrastructure you talking? So I think there's general road and building infrastructure, I think the energy infrastructure in this country needs modernization—especially electrical grids—the electrical grid needs modernization. And I say that in that we are okay today, right? I don't think that we're headed in the direction of a third world country without the modernization, but I don't think we're maximizing the value and the opportunity of this country without it. Right? And so I think when I look at the 350 million people that are in this country... That have incredibly aspirational—like nobody else in the world, right? I think that Americans are among the most aspirational human beings to ever grace the face of the earth, that if we want to do that, we've got to invest in infrastructure. And that's human capital, that's physical capital, that's the energy and the electric grid. So I think that's a place where if I had 10,000 today, I'd do some time sharpening my pen around what type of opportunities exist in infrastructure. Yeah, I mean, listen, railroads as well, we're behind on fast—high speed rail. I don't know if we'll ever get to be a high speed rail nation the way that I think we should be. We'll get there. I hope so. It may not be in our lifetime though. Too much red tape. Listen, we just missed a field goal. Remember there's other players that are going to come. I love that. I love that, too. I love it, man. I appreciate it. Marcus Shaw superpower, what is it? I have a deep—and my father, I don't know, we didn't talk about this, you know, after his time in the military, he went and got his PhD in psychology and my mother is a teacher. I believe all teachers have a PhD in psychology—an earned PhD in psychology. I think I'm a very good read of people and I spend a lot of time both with my team but with students and my own family. Although people will say I can be a little tough, I spend a lot of time trying to understand people's motivations in life, what they want to accomplish and trying to find their superpowers. Right? And so you know, I'm a big Marvel fan, I love Marvel. If I were to think of kind of the archetype that I really enjoy, I mean it's Professor X, right? Professor X can't walk, he can't shoot lasers out of his eyes or do anything, but he has this mental telepathy to be able to not only get in the minds of the people that he's leading, but to help lead them to where they're trying to get to. He can also find them. Right? And so I think that that's my superpower. I'm not saying that I have actual mutant powers, but I do believe the ability to understand people and their motivation is important. Yeah. Favorite AI app? Spent a lot of time with Claude now, man. Yeah, I love Claude. Claude has done some incredible things in the past six months. You know, I was an early adopter, I think, on ChatGPT; Perplexity I also like a lot. But Claude—I have seen an improvement in Claude in the past honestly three months that has been incredible. I think you know, as an engineer, Moore's Law governed a lot of the development and the enhancement of technology during my lifetime. I think we're going to approach some type of Moore's Law type pathway for AI where the way we use AI today, I think in 18 months, will be exponentially different. Yeah, 'cause we're heading into that agent phase, right? This was the software phase and now you're going to have the agents that are rolling out. It's going to be really, really interesting. It's going to be powerful. But remember, and I said this at the top, it's not going to replace us. It's going to replace us if we don't use it. Biggest money management advice, right? What would you tell someone? So look, one of the best pieces of advice I've ever heard, and this was during my time working in the entrepreneurial community, was that if you ask for money you get advice, and if you ask for advice you get money. People want to be a part of your story, right? And so I'm speaking specifically to first-time managers, emerging managers that are going out there and running the gauntlet of raising capital. People want to be a part of your success. Especially, you know, those early LPs, they don't want to be transactional. They really want to understand who you are, they want to understand your motivation, they want to understand the team you're building together and your strategy. So engaging people in being a part of your story often is asking for advice. "Jabari, what do you think I should think about, you know, this concept we're thinking about AltFinance?" The more that people are investing their advice and their intellectual capital in your idea, the more likely they are to invest their financial capital. And so that's one of the things that I've seen again in some of the early-stage companies that were going out there and pitching for dollars. And, you know, look, your pitch has got to be tight, but people are more likely to invest in you and invest with you if they feel like they're a part of that process. Yeah, I love that. And ask for advice, that's a psychology thing too, right? When people feel a part of it, they give you their advice and now they want to invest in it because you know, you took in their ideas. And so it is a psychology. The reality is everybody really wants to cheer. Right? I mean, look, we're in the middle of the NBA finals, and everybody wants to really cheer for a team. People rarely like to cheer against a team. Right? And so the ability to bring people into your narrative to get them to cheer for you, right? Give them a reason to cheer. Don't miss the field goal, right? But people's psychology is built that they want to cheer, right? And what they'd rather do than cheer against you is cheer louder for who they want to win. They don't really want to cheer against you, right? It doesn't—it doesn't comport with kind of human nature in my mind. And so I think that for people that are looking to engage supporters, whether that's financial support or strategic support or something else, how do you get people to buy in and want to cheer for you? Yeah, I love that. Well, we'll get you out of here on this, man. Good to Great, right? Jim Collins, my favorite business book. What's the difference between a good apprenticeship program and a great one? In my belief, if you want to build a strong practitioner in any model, you've got to coach them. Right? You've got to coach them, you've got to train them, you've got to, you know, invest in them in a way that makes them see themselves in the position, right? So again, I talk a lot about this, but you know, Mike Tomlin. The reason why he likes coaches that like to coach is because the players that are coached will end up playing harder, longer and ultimately for less risk than those that are just really good but don't know if they want to be there. Right? So if you get people that are coached well, they'll buy into the system. So if you have an apprentice program where you're not only teaching the folks the technical aspects of doing the job, but you're teaching them how to be a leader, you're teaching them how to deal with disappointment and failure, right? You're teaching them how to come back after they miss the field goal and saying, "Listen, you can't miss a field goal like that again. If you do these things, you need to practice more." If you're running that type of an apprenticeship program, you will have a stickier, more resilient outcome, right? And that outcome is people. And that outcome for people in the investment world, particularly in the world that we're operating in, is better deals, better deal discipline, better exit discipline, better returns. Right? How to be patient in the face of adversity, right? How to be urgent in scarcity, right? How to operate in those type of modes. And so I think apprenticeship programs and training programs that are dedicated to growing the individuals that they have brought onto their team are the organizations that are going to be long-term successful. I love it, man. Marcus Shaw, CEO of AltFinance, man, thank you so much for the time. Really appreciate it, man. And listen, good luck for the rest of the work that you guys are doing. It's again, it's very, very important. 10-year commitment, 90 million, but those students that you're getting, the bringing them to the world, I can only imagine it's going to have a domino effect, right, and continue. And hopefully we come to the Milken Institute, man, and all those students, they're here. Right? And we can look at them and say, "Oh, those are Marcus Shaw's students." Man, it's about 500 of them there, you know? I love it. Managed a few trillion dollars. There you go. And none of them miss field goals. That's right. Love it. Marcus Shaw at the Enterprise Zone here at the Milken Institute in Beverly Hills. I'm Jabari Young, thank you for watching, thank you for listening.

Tim Miller: Trump’s Treatment of Marco Rubio is Utterly HUMILIATING

The Bulwark(1.7M) ·23:45 ·60.7K views · 2026-05-07
Tim Miller (Writer, The Bulwark) Nicole Wallace (Host, MSNBC) Mark Hertling (Retired Lieutenant General, U.S. Army)
Donald Trump’s public dismissal of "Project Freedom" just 90 minutes after Marco Rubio touted it illustrates a pattern of humiliation that undermines Rubio's diplomatic authority and creates a chaotic "Rubik’s cube" of directives for U.S.
TL;DR Donald Trump’s public dismissal of "Project Freedom" just 90 minutes after Marco Rubio touted it illustrates a pattern of humiliation that undermines Rubio's diplomatic authority and creates a chaotic "Rubik’s cube" of directives for U.S. military leadership. Public...

TL;DR

Donald Trump’s public dismissal of "Project Freedom" just 90 minutes after Marco Rubio touted it illustrates a pattern of humiliation that undermines Rubio's diplomatic authority and creates a chaotic "Rubik’s cube" of directives for U.S. military leadership.

The Humiliation of Marco Rubio and Diplomatic Erosion

  • Public Contradiction: Within a 90-minute window, President Trump terminated "Project Freedom"—an effort to guide vessels through the Strait of Hormuz—immediately after Secretary of State Marco Rubio held an extensive press briefing declaring it the "next stage" of the Iran conflict.
  • Loss of Credibility: Tim Miller argues that because Rubio is so frequently undercut, foreign allies and adversaries no longer view him as a person with "juice" or authority.
      • Allied Pivot: General Mark Hertling and Tim Miller note that allies like Canada, Germany, and France are increasingly looking to deal with China because the U.S. diplomatic apparatus is perceived as unreliable and "not normal."
  • The "Puppy" Metaphor: Miller describes the power dynamic as Rubio being on a "little leash," willing to "woof for daddy" despite the public embarrassment, as Rubio seeks to position himself as the "competent Mr. Fix-it" for the MAGA base.

Military Chaos and Strategic Confusion

  • Command Uncertainty: General Mark Hertling highlights that Trump’s habit of changing his mind on an hourly basis creates a dangerous environment for military leaders, including the commanders of CENTCOM and warships in the Gulf.
      • Non-Standard Terminology: Hertling notes that "Project Freedom" is not a standard military term for an operation, suggesting it may have been labeled a "project" to avoid Congressional scrutiny.
  • The Complexity Gap: General Hertling contrasts the current administration’s one-page memorandum regarding Iran with the Obama-era JCPOA, which was a 159-page document of "intricate details" developed by experts over months.
  • Operational Risk: The "hodgepodge of actions" and scattershot messaging from the White House, CIA, and ODNI puts American service members in harm's way by failing to provide a clear objective or exit strategy.

Rubio’s 2028 Political Outlook

  • The Architect of the War: While Rubio currently enjoys a "buzz" among the MAGA base, Miller predicts this will evaporate as the Iran war's economic costs—such as rising prices for gas and consumer goods—begin to hit voters by the summer.
  • Blame Shifting: Miller suggests that when the base eventually tires of the conflict, they will not blame Trump; instead, they will target Rubio as the "neocon" architect of the war in Iran and Venezuela.
  • The "For Realsies" Doctrine: Miller predicts the 2028 MAGA base will demand a candidate who is "for realsies" about non-interventionism. Because this is "Rubio's war," he will be unable to pivot to that isolationist stance.

Religious Friction and the Vatican

  • The Verbal War with Pope Leo: Trump has repeatedly claimed the Pope wants Iran to have nuclear weapons, a charge the Pope has flatly denied by citing the Church’s long-standing opposition to all nuclear arms.
  • Catholic Polling Data: Recent Washington Post/Ipsos polling indicates a significant rift between Trump and American Catholics:
      • Pope Approval: 61% of U.S. Catholics approve of Pope Leo.
      • Jesus Imagery: 87% of respondents viewed Trump’s AI-generated image of himself as Jesus negatively.
      • Violence Rhetoric: 69% disapproved of Pete Hegseth’s public prayer for "overwhelming violence of action."
  • Rubio as Liaison: Rubio, a Catholic, is being sent to meet with the Pope to "smooth things over," a rare sign that the administration recognizes it has created a legitimate political problem with religious voters.

Notable Quotes

"If Trump asks them to bark, he’s going to woof. That’s the deal and it is humiliating... [Rubio] acted like he had authority to say the things he said, and it did not take Donald Trump 90 minutes to render him a jackass."

— Tim Miller

"You’ve got an individual as the Commander-in-Chief who is changing his mind on a daily basis and sometimes an hourly basis after his key national security figures... are saying different things. This is putting people who take this seriously—the commanders of CENTCOM, the warships—they’re not sure what comes next."

— General Mark Hertling

"It’s such a hallmark of what Trump does to men... that you can’t be more humiliated for them than they are for themselves."

— Nicolle Wallace

"We are seeing tragically... that this madman theory of politics—that Trump could just act crazy and bully people and send in an outer-borough real estate deal to sign a deal—that it doesn’t work."

— Tim Miller

Hey everybody, Tim Miller from the Bulwark. I was over on MS Now with my girl Nicolle Wallace and also my Bulwark colleague General Mark Hertling, which was such a treat. And we were talking a bunch about the ongoing Iran negotiations. I got to talk a little bit at the end about Pope Leo, the Bulwark Pope. But much of the focus was on Marco Rubio and his political standing. I even got the opportunity to woof, actually. I wanted to just actually do the barking noise—woof, woof. But I felt like that was maybe a little much for cable news. Why, you might ask, did I want to bark on cable news? Well, it's because we were talking about how Donald Trump is walking Marco Rubio around like a little puppy. And Marco had went out and did a press conference where he talked about how Project Freedom is happening, and then two hours later Trump's like, "No, it's not." And it's just humiliating. And Nicolle was like, "Why does he do it?" And I said because he's on a little leash and he wants to woof for daddy. So, I talked about this a little bit on the show, but it wasn't really the main point since we were talking about Iran, so I'll expand on it some, which is Rubio 2028. And a little teaser: we get into this with Sarah on the focus group this week, coming up on Saturday. Your boy's on the focus group, so mark a little alarm button for that. And I just... I had a little bit of regrets about... and I'm not going to tell you any more than this, but in the teaser there is a lot of buzz right now for Marco among the base. And my mother even mentioned that. And so, I don't know, I got kind of caught up in that moment and I was analyzing that; I had my analyst hat on. But I just fundamentally believe that in the end, it ain't going to happen for Marco. I think that the trust... he's building a lot of trust now, but he still was a neocon before. And the problem is, in my view and my prediction, the thing that people in the MAGA base will want to move on from—whoever the next person is—is this war. Whatever that crazy clown show debate stage looks like over on Newsmax in 2028, I do believe that the majority view will be that this was a mistake. Or even if they're not willing to admit that, they'll be saying we're not going to do something like that again. Might be the way that they do it so they don't hurt Donald Trump's little feelings. And if that's the view among the base, that we're not going to do this thing again, like for realsies this time—like for realsies. Like you guys did it on Iraq and we said we didn't like it, and so we went with Trump because he said he wasn't going to do it again, and then he did it on Iran. And like we said we didn't like it, and so we don't want you to do it again. Like I think that that is going to be the main posture of the candidates is like for realsies this time, we're not going to do it. Marco can't do that. Like A, ideologically that's not where he is. And B, this is his war. It's his war. Venezuela was his, so is Iran. He's the national security advisor and the Secretary of State. He's the one that all of the right-wing podcasters are going to blame on this because they can't blame Trump. And as you guys know, my view is that it's just... I think it's going to keep getting worse and worse. Like the economic fallout of this is going to keep getting worse and worse. People are going to get madder and madder and I don't see an exit for Marco on that. So anyway, this is me countering something that I say in another video that you watch. That's okay, we're all working through this in real time. I'm just trying to give you my real uncut views. So stick around for more on woof-woof little Marco. General Hertling's also going to provide you, I think, a little more serious analysis in the program, so watch out for that. And we'll be back here soon. By the way, subscribe to the feed. Somebody just sent me another little chart of, you know, how we're doing compared to the other YouTubers. We're doing pretty good, I appreciate you guys. We're doing pretty good, but there are some dummies in front of us. Like look at some of these dummies. Let me pull this up here. Look at some of these dummies. Who is this? Benny Johnson, Tucker, Fox, New York Post. Stop this. Subscribe to the feed, tell your friends. See you guys soon. This ad is brought to you by Aura Frames. Aura Frames is a great upgrade from your typical Mother's Day flowers. Trust me, I know this. I got an Aura Frame for my mother-in-law last Mother's Day. She loved it. That's because flowers, they're nice, but they only last a few days. The perfect gift is one that lasts a lifetime, and that gift is Aura Frames. Aura Frames are a great way to share all your favorite memories, from birthdays to vacations to kids' sporting events and everything in between. There's free unlimited storage that allows you to add as many photos and videos as you want. My favorite part of Aura Frames is how easy it is to pre-load photos before it ships. You can keep adding photos and videos from anywhere, anytime. It's really awesome and it's perfect for Mother's Day. A gift box is included. Every frame comes packaged in a premium gift box with no price tag. Named number one by Wirecutter, you can save on gifts for your mom or your mother-in-law by visiting auraframes.com. For a limited time, listeners can get $25 off their best-selling Carver Mat frame with code BULWARKTAKES. That's A-U-R-A frames.com, promo code BULWARKTAKES. Support the show by mentioning us at checkout. Terms and conditions do apply. It's been another head-spinning day of erratic messaging from the White House and Donald Trump over the war in Iran. Yesterday Donald Trump's Secretary of Defense, Pete Hegseth, confidently claimed that the ceasefire, quote, "certainly holds." Today Trump is threatening Iran with the possibility of more bombing. Also yesterday, Pete Hegseth touted Donald Trump's so-called "Project Freedom." That was an effort to guide stranded vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, which remains closed. Secretary of State Marco Rubio gave a big long press briefing. Looked like he thought he was, I don't know, behind the podium. Went on forever. He said all sorts of stuff. Among the things he said, he declared with authority that Project Freedom is the, quote, "next stage with Iran because the war itself is actually over." He said that. Like 90 minutes later, Trump undercut both those men. Donald Trump announced an abrupt end to Project Freedom one day after it started and like two hours after Rubio touted it. We'll deal with what that means or says about them in another segment, but at this hour, Iran is saying that it's looking at a U.S. proposal to end the war. That's after an Iranian official dismissed a reported proposal as a, quote, "list of American wishes." General Hertling, the President abandons it hours later in like a swamp of other posts with memes and ridiculous stuff in the same 48 hours. He's posting pictures of himself near naked in a blue AI-generated image of himself in the reflecting pool. Like what is going on, and what do the men and women of the military think is going on? Well, we have seen, Nicolle, the President in different press conferences, Oval Office visits, reading from documents within his Oval Office with people standing around him as backdrop. And some of that information, most of that information is scattershot. He goes off on different subjects, he riffs on things. But when you're talking about giving direction as the Commander-in-Chief to the nation's military, it really is a Rubik's cube of ideas that I would suggest the military personnel, the leaders of the military, are trying to figure out as it's also being transcribed through Secretary Hegseth. So you've got an individual as the Commander-in-Chief who is changing his mind on a daily basis and sometimes an hourly basis after his key national security figures, both the Secretary of Defense and the Secretary of State, are saying different things. And I'm sure other national security officials are as confused as both of they are. The CIA Director, ODNI, Commerce has also been a little bit scattershot in their messaging of this. But again, I'm going back as your military analyst to say, hey, this is putting people who take this seriously—the commanders of CENTCOM, the warships in the Gulf, the air wing commanders, the special operations—they're not sure what comes next. Is it a true operation and has the operation been halted because of various congressional demands? Is it a project? And truthfully, I've never heard of something that's a military operation being called a project before. That doesn't fit in. And why is it being called that? Is it to avoid scrutiny by the Congress of the United States, or is it to confuse the American people? Hell, we're confusing guys like David Rohde. You can imagine what the typical civilian at home watching TV or reading the newspaper trying to figure out the track of these different substances are trying to think. They are trying to think in terms of: Are we at war? Are we not at war? Is there movement through the strait? Is the oil being lifted, oil embargo being lifted? Are merchant ships flowing again? It is a hodgepodge of actions and it's not the way to conduct operations when you're putting American sons and daughters and their family members in harm's way when they're thinking about their sons and daughters in harm's way. Tim Miller, Marco Rubio has two jobs. He is the country's Secretary of State, he is also Donald Trump's national security advisor. Marco Rubio would like to be set apart from the clown show. He would like to be taken seriously. And all things are relative, maybe some people view him as a part, I don't know. But Marco Rubio is the loser of the day. Marco Rubio stood before at least five-six dozen journalists for an on-camera briefing yesterday. He seemed to enjoy it; he stayed a long time. He like picked up energy as the question... you know, you saw him going to reporters, you know, more quickly and more comfortably. A lot of pointing. He acted and again, I don't know what's in his mind, he acted like he had authority to say the things he said, and it did not take Donald Trump 90 minutes to render him a jackass, to go back on what he was sent out there to say, to contradict him and to make a fool of him. Now, I have no dog in the fight in terms of who takes over MAGA, but what is Marco Rubio's calculation today if Trump asks him to go do it again tomorrow? Well, he'll do it. You know, if Trump asks them to bark, he's going to woof. That's the deal and it is humiliating. It's humiliating, but I think he's enjoying it. People can take from that what they want, but I agree with you, he was enjoying himself yesterday. But like, nobody believes him. Yeah, I think that because it's such a clown show around him, I think that people in his circle think him credible. And I know that that seems crazy to us, you know, if you're a journalist that is looking at this or just a viewer and you're like, "The guy went out there and said that we're, you know, pushing forward on Project Freedom and then two hours later we stopped, like he has no juice." That's obviously the objective thing. But I think that a lot of people who are either supporters of the war, supporters of Trump, are looking around at the other people in the administration and being like, "Okay, well Trump might have pulled the rug out from under this guy, but at least he's borderline competent." And that's why you're getting a lot of a buzz for Marco now being the heir apparent. And I just... I'm a single person, I'm just a pundit, so you know, mark this down for whatever you want, but I'm against the conventional wisdom on this entirely. Because this... I think not only was this humiliating for Marco in how Trump is treating him, but this is also Marco Rubio's war as much as anybody's besides Trump. And he has taken ownership of it, he has put himself out there publicly, and the popularity of this war is low right now. And let me tell you, I suspect the popularity is going to be a lot lower on Fourth of July when people are having to buy fewer hot dogs or go on fewer road trips because of how the cost of everything and how much it's going up when we're not going to have a solution to the war that's easy to sell, as General Hertling could tell you better than me. So he's making a bet that, you know, being the competent Mr. Fix-it for Trump is going to pay off for him. I'm glad he's enjoying himself now because I don't really see it working out for him in the future, but we'll see. It's such a hallmark of what Trump does to men, largely men but some women, that you can't be more humiliated for them than they are for themselves. So like, I didn't... like you're right. What will he do if he asks him to do it again tomorrow? Do it tomorrow because like, if he's not humiliated, we can't expect anything different to happen. Iran will also not be able to acquire the enriched uranium that could be used for a bomb. As soon as this deal is implemented, Iran will remove two-thirds of its centrifuges. And finally, Iran has powerful incentives to keep its commitments. Before getting sanctions relief, Iran has to take significant concrete steps like removing centrifuges and getting rid of its stockpile. If Iran violates the agreement over the next decade, all of the sanctions can snap back into place. That was former President Barack Obama back in 2015 on the actual Iran deal that was made by former President Obama and his administration. We're back with General Hertling and Tim. General Hertling, some of what I think is going on is Trump thinks that all of this is easy and quick. And I wonder what you make of the sad lesson for our allies and the whole world about all that he's destroyed and how far back he has put us from that moment. Well, not only the President, Nicolle, but to continue what Tim was saying during the last segment, it's also the Secretary of State. When Secretary Rubio says the kinds of things and then he's undercut by the President, you're talking about him potentially going to allied conferences or partner nations. They know that he's not in the know because he's been undercut so many times and embarrassed. But when you're talking about the kinds of things even today, the memorandum that allegedly is going to bring about a peace conference between Iran and the United States is a one-page document and neither side feels like it does what they want it to do when you go back and forth. The JCPOA, the last document that President Obama was just talking about in that clip you played, was 159 pages long with intricate details and it took months to get the thing put together with a whole lot of very intelligent and smart people who had one mind in what their objectives were. And President Trump tore it up. We're going to see probably a whole lot of money being given to Iran, which President Trump condemned President Obama for. We're going to see not the kind of nuclear deal that was in the JCPOA. We're going to see a whole lot of ancillary second and third-order effects in terms of depletion of U.S. munitions and a long time in the region for 50,000 soldiers and a whole bunch of destroyers and cruisers and aircraft carriers. So our allies around the world are basically saying to themselves, and I'll try and be succinct, "What the hell are they doing?" I mean, if we can't make sense of it and he is our President, I can't imagine what the Dutch or the Germans or the French are thinking behind closed doors. It's the kind of diplomacy that just doesn't work and it isn't normal on the world stage. So that's what I think our allies are thinking and it's dangerous. Yeah, I mean, Tim, I guess we could just stop too: It isn't normal. And what becomes excruciating is trying to cover it as normal that America's Secretary of State, who also now for many months—it obviously isn't a temporary arrangement—that Rubio is also the national security advisor, goes out before the cameras... It wasn't like an interview he gave to The Atlantic and he didn't have time to back-brief Trump. If Trump is still doing anything, he's watching television. I'm sure he either watched it or knew what Rubio was saying. And if he isn't watching it, then he is like on his hands and knees actually like making the gold pieces or the steps of his new ballroom, because it's not clear he does anything else. But the notion that Marco Rubio could be taken seriously after doing a press conference like that and then it literally isn't two hours before Trump contradicts him. Like what is... who meets with him? Like with whom do you have any clout? I mean, I think this is why a lot of our allies are looking at different ways that they can work with China now. You saw a Canada-China deal just recently over something that they wouldn't have done before with regards to automobiles, electric automobiles going to Canada because they wouldn't have wanted to anger us and now they're just like, "We can't trust these guys." And moreover, not only is it because we can't trust these guys, but as General Hertling's saying, we don't know who to deal with. Even if we did get to a deal with them, how could we trust that it would stick? You know, and he mentioned there's a lot of times... as a former Republican, I'll say I'm guilty of this. A lot of times there's like mockery of bureaucrats and experts and sometimes the Democrats, you know, have too many meetings. I do think that that's a fair critique. But on a serious document like a deal, a nuclear deal with Iran, I had friends that were working on that deal, like they were legitimate experts. They spent a lot of time like going over the details and the particulars. The idea... and I think that they really believed this madman theory of politics: that Trump could just act crazy because he is crazy and they could bully people and maybe bribe people and send in an outer borough real estate deal to sign a deal with them and that it would be okay. And I think they thought that that would work and I think that we are seeing tragically in disaster for our economy and the whole world economy that that doesn't work. It is indeed what we are living through. Lieutenant General Mark Hertling, thank you so much for joining us today. Tim sticks around a little bit longer. In the spirit of that which is not normal, Donald Trump is locked in a verbal war of aggression with the Pope. And Pope Leo has responded to the latest absurdity from Donald Trump. Donald Trump claiming repeatedly now that the Pope wants Iran to have a nuclear weapon. The Pope said this, quote, "If anyone wants to criticize me for proclaiming the gospel, let them do so with the truth. The church has spoken out against all nuclear weapons for years, there is no doubt about that. I simply hope to be listened to because of the value of God's word." Those comments come as Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who is himself a Catholic, is expected to meet with Pope Leo tomorrow. There are new polls out because Trump is, as I said, in a verbal war with the Pope, that show American Catholics are at odds with Trump on this one. According to Washington Post/Ipsos poll, 61% of U.S. Catholics approve of Pope Leo. Tim is still here. There's some more in these polling. Reaction to Donald Trump posting a picture depicting himself as Jesus: 87% of respondents had a negative view of that. 9%, which is probably like the Mar-a-Lago Winners Club or something, liked it. Hegseth praying publicly to God for, quote, "overwhelming violence of action against those who deserve no mercy," end quote. 27% have a positive view of that, 69% negative. Your thoughts about this war with the Vatican? I think it's pretty telling that they're sending Rubio there, especially after the back-and-forth. Vance was there not so long ago. And to me it says that they're looking at these numbers and they know that they have problems with Catholics and with people of faith and that they think that maybe Marco would be better suited than J.D. given his demeanor to try to smooth things over. And I think that it's a pretty rare sign from this White House, at least in Trump 2.0, of them like kind of demonstrating that they realize they've created a political problem for themselves. Christopher Hale writes a good Substack about the Vatican and I was reading this the other day. There's another theory that's out there, potential reporting, is that Marco could be going there about Trump's potential engagement in Cuba next because we know that Trump has an eye on Cuba and I don't know, maybe there's some thinking that he could diffuse Pope Leo over that. I find... given what we've seen from Pope Leo, I find that very hard to believe, but I think both Cuba and their political problems is what's driving this. The other interesting thing is that Pope Leo doesn't back down. He's afraid of not one thing from them. And you've got, you know, a study in the Republicans cowering for the 10 years we've been covering them. This is not, you know, they're not dealing with Lindsey Graham here in Pope Leo. He's made of something totally different. Yeah, absolutely. And he doesn't have to be responsive to opinion polls. You know, Pope Leo is also not saying anything different than he would have said before. He's been very consistent in his kind of posture within the Catholic Church. And you know, I think I said this last time this came up with you, Nicolle, it's like this is another mess of their own making. And it's not as if the Catholic Church didn't have disagreements with the Biden administration over abortion or other issues, but you just didn't see Joe Biden going onto his social media feed to call them names, right? And I think that they've created this problem for themselves and Pope Leo is certainly... does not seem like he's demonstrating, you know, any malleability on this, and for good reason. And I think it's a good lesson to others about the way that you can stand up to these guys' bullying. Yeah, can you imagine the news cycle if Joe Biden like tweeted, "The Pope is soft on... I don't know, women's issues"? Crazy. Tim Miller, thank you for joining us today.

Musk vs Altlman & Taylor Swift Trademarks | Bloomberg Law

Bloomberg Podcasts(478.0K) ·36:40 ·229 views · 2026-05-07
Madlin Mekelburg (Legal Reporter, Bloomberg) Elon Musk (CEO, Tesla and SpaceX) Sam Altman (CEO, OpenAI)
> "It’s not okay to steal a charity.
TL;DR: Elon Musk is seeking to unwind OpenAI's for-profit conversion in a high-stakes trial alleging the "theft" of a non-profit charity, while Taylor Swift is pioneering a novel legal strategy using trademark law to combat AI deepfakes. Core Allegations: Elon Musk claims Sam...

TL;DR: Elon Musk is seeking to unwind OpenAI's for-profit conversion in a high-stakes trial alleging the "theft" of a non-profit charity, while Taylor Swift is pioneering a novel legal strategy using trademark law to combat AI deepfakes.

The Musk vs. OpenAI Conflict: "Stealing a Charity"

  • Core Allegations: Elon Musk claims Sam Altman and Greg Brockman enriched themselves by converting OpenAI from a non-profit research organization into a for-profit entity, effectively abandoning its altruistic mission.
      • OpenAI’s Defense: Attorneys argue Musk is a "disgruntled former employee" and competitor (via his company xAI) attempting to undercut OpenAI’s success after his own attempts to seize control of the company failed in 2018.
  • Financial Discrepancies: A major point of contention involves Musk’s actual financial contributions versus his public claims.
      • The $1 Billion Pledge: While Musk publicly committed to donating $1 billion at OpenAI’s launch, testimony revealed he contributed only $38 million before departing the board.
      • Personal Enrichment: Musk’s attorneys highlighted a 2017 journal entry from OpenAI President Greg Brockman questioning what it would take to reach a personal net worth of $1 billion; Brockman’s stake is now estimated at $30 billion.
  • Microsoft’s Role: Musk has accused Microsoft of "aiding and abetting" the mission drift. Microsoft maintains its investments were above board and that it had no part in OpenAI’s internal governance shifts.

Judicial Management and Trial Dynamics

  • Social Media Restraint: Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers reprimanded both Musk and Altman for their "propensity to use social media" to influence public opinion, specifically citing Musk’s "Scam Altman" posts.
  • Scope of Evidence: The judge has strictly limited the trial’s focus to legal and financial governance.
      • AI Safety: She blocked Musk from discussing "existential threats" or "Terminator-like situations," ruling that the trial is about contract and fiduciary duty, not AI safety.
      • Political Ties: The court barred inquiries into Musk’s relationship with the Trump administration or his role in the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).
  • Advisory Jury: While a jury is hearing the case, their decision on liability is advisory; Judge Gonzalez Rogers will make the final ruling on damages and remedies.

Stakes and Potential Remedies

  • Financial Penalties: Musk is seeking damages as high as $134 billion (based on OpenAI’s fluctuating valuation), though he pledged to return any awarded funds to the original non-profit foundation.
  • Structural Upheaval: Musk’s legal team is asking the court to:
      • Remove Sam Altman and Greg Brockman from leadership positions.
      • Order a full "unwinding" of the for-profit conversion completed in October.
  • IPO Implications: The trial occurs as OpenAI eyes a public offering with a potential valuation of $852 billion, making the legal uncertainty a significant hurdle for investors.

Taylor Swift’s Trademark Strategy Against AI

  • Novel Use of Trademark: Taylor Swift has filed trademark applications for specific audio clips and stage images to create a legal "arsenal" against AI-generated deepfakes and unauthorized endorsements.
  • Legal Limitations: Unlike "Right of Publicity" (which varies by state), trademarks are federal but narrow.
      • Source Indicators: Trademarks are intended to prevent consumer confusion regarding the origin of a product, not to protect a general persona.
      • Limited Scope: A trademark on a specific phrase (e.g., "Hey, it's Taylor Swift") only protects against the use of that specific phrase, not the general use of her voice.
  • Strategic Value: Cynthia Katz (Fox Rothschild) suggests the move is partly "notice-giving"—signaling to the industry that Swift will litigate AI misuse, which may encourage legislative updates to federal publicity rights.

Notable Quotes

"It’s not okay to steal a charity. This case will become case law and become precedent to looting every charity in America." — Elon Musk, Plaintiff and Co-founder of OpenAI.
"He knows rockets, he knows electric cars, he did not and I believe does not know AI... [he] lacks the ability to dream a little." — Greg Brockman, President of OpenAI, on Elon Musk’s early skepticism of ChatGPT.
"I think it also helps just to put it out there, the PR and the press of 'Hey, I’m not going to stand for this'... it adds some more ammunition to those cease and desist letters." — Cynthia Katz, Music Partner at Fox Rothschild, on Taylor Swift’s trademark filings.
"I'm not a lawyer... Well, actually I took Law 101." — Elon Musk, in an exchange with Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers after being told to stop acting as his own counsel on the stand.
This is Bloomberg Law with June Grasso from Bloomberg Radio. Elon Musk is facing off against Sam Altman at a high-stakes trial that could determine the future of OpenAI. The California trial is the culmination of years of animosity and public feuding over the startup they founded together more than a decade ago, before their relationship soured. Musk claims that Altman and other leaders at OpenAI enriched themselves by abandoning its altruistic principles and converting it from a full non-profit research organization to a for-profit company. While Altman and OpenAI are accusing Musk of harassment and say the real goal of his lawsuit is to undercut competition with his own startup, xAI. The first witness was Musk himself. He tried to portray the stakes in the case as existential, saying he wanted to prevent a Terminator-like situation and basically repeating a warning he gave years ago. "There is some chance that it's above zero that AI will kill us all. I think it's low, but there's some chance." The stakes in the trial are incredibly high, and maybe even existential for OpenAI because Musk is asking the court to unwind its for-profit conversion that was just completed in October. Joining me is Bloomberg Legal Reporter Madeleine Meckelberg who's covering the trial. So Madeleine, even before the opening statements in the trial, the judge took Musk and Altman to task for some social media posts. So just ahead of trial, if you were online on X, you saw a lot of posts coming from Musk in his account talking about quote "Scam Altman", his nickname that he has for Sam Altman, and seemingly promoting social media posts that included a New Yorker article about Sam Altman and his conduct on the board of OpenAI. OpenAI also was posting things on social media. They have a running blog post where they kind of provide updates on the status of the Elon Musk situation, I think is what they call it. When they got to court first thing on Monday, before they talked about anything else with the trial, the judge basically reprimanded both sides and said you need to, I think she said the words, "control your propensity to use social media." She warned them that it may be making things worse outside the courtroom. She had Elon Musk come up and stand in front of her as she gave this warning and he agreed that he would not post anything or at least limit his posting about the case. The opening statements are the first chance for the parties to tell their story to the jury. We heard opening statements from the three main parties in this case being Elon Musk, OpenAI and then Microsoft. Elon Musk, I think we've heard his argument by now, but basically he's alleging that Sam Altman and Greg Brockman essentially, he keeps saying the words, "stole a charity." He says that they enriched themselves by converting this non-profit entity into a for-profit company and in doing so abandoned the mission that they had at the beginning, which was creating AI for good. During opening statements we heard a lot from them about Sam Altman and Greg Brockman's removal from OpenAI's board in kind of the 2022-2023 era and about how there was a lack of trust from them from people at OpenAI. We saw some email exchanges talking about how much money Elon Musk contributed to the foundation, kind of showing that he was a major party in starting this from the beginning. Then OpenAI's attorneys got up and they tried to paint a different picture, of course, of the situation. They showed emails where Elon Musk was trying to get control of OpenAI when they were discussing forming a for-profit entity back in the day when he was still affiliated with the organization. Their attorney basically was trying to paint this case as a really simple one where Musk was a disgruntled former employee of OpenAI who was upset that they didn't want to go with his vision of the company. He left, formed a competitor and now he's turning around trying to undercut OpenAI and harm one of his competitors in the AI space and his former company that he helped found. Then of course there's also the Microsoft element of this. They tend to come in after the fact but they are a key player in the lawsuit and that's because Musk has accused them of essentially aiding and abetting this theft that he describes of a charity. We heard from Microsoft talking about their investments that they made in OpenAI and why they decided to make those investments. Everything we're hearing from them over and over is pretty consistent that their actions were above board; they had no part in anything that they say may or may not have been happening. Musk was the first witness called to the stand and he spent about three days testifying, answering questions from his own attorneys and then a little more heated questions from OpenAI's attorneys. The main point that Elon Musk was trying to make is, and I keep saying this because he kept saying it on the stand, is "it's not okay to steal a charity." We heard him say that over and over again in response to many questions. It's really interesting across all of the testimony that we've heard so far; everyone kind of describes these early days when Musk was still at OpenAI really similarly. They all kind of agree that there was discussion about the need for more funding to support their research and the need to maybe pursue some kind of for-profit component. Musk said during his testimony that he had no problem with creating a for-profit company to support a non-profit, but he felt things crossed the line when they decided to convert the non-profit to a for-profit. He described this situation as three phases of disillusionment with OpenAI. It kind of started with him being happy and pleased with the mission. Then he started to get concerned that they were moving in a direction that he didn't agree with. This was around 2017-2018 when he did ultimately leave the board. Then he said Phase 3 kind of started in around '22 when he was convinced that they did in fact steal a charity. So we heard from him a lot about that, about his support of OpenAI. He says it wouldn't have existed without him in the beginning. He came up with the name, he brought his guidance, his leadership, his vision, and most importantly his money.
