He was trying to portray the stakes of the case as existential, saying several times that AI could quote "kill us all" if it's not developed safely. This idea of AI safety is a big one. That's another part of Musk's case and his argument for why he's so concerned about this decision to turn OpenAI into a for-profit company. He's said that we need somebody responsible leading AI. There's all these private companies whose interests are motivated by profit and investors and that's why he wanted a non-profit in this space. They had an expert that they called to testify for Musk's case who was going to talk about the threats of AI and that includes this existential threat that Musk describes of AI someday being smarter than humans and leading to our demise essentially. The judge did reprimand them and say, "you know, this case is not about AI safety. I don't want to hear about this anymore." Because Musk kept saying he was trying to prevent a Terminator situation, talking about the movie franchise, she said, "that's not what this case is about. It's not about AI safety." And so that expert's testimony was cut quite short, I think as a result of that, and we have not heard that line from Musk since. Starting xAI was a big subject of his cross-examination. "Okay, if you were so worried about this, why did you start another for-profit? Why didn't you start a non-profit when you decided to start xAI?" I don't think we got a super clear answer from Musk on that decision. He kept saying, "as I've said, I have no problem with a for-profit. The problem is if you have a non-profit and turn it into a for-profit." But you know, that's of course getting at the details of this case but not entirely answering this question of why he decided to do that versus starting a non-profit. I think at one point he said, "Why would I do that? I already started a non-profit and it was OpenAI and they stole it." I think Musk's own company is a really key part of the OpenAI case because exactly as you said, he's still a player in this space. He's pursuing something that he said and acknowledged during testimony that it is a safety risk, but it also puts him at a competitor level with OpenAI and so it adds this whole other component to the case. Musk has testified many times, but on cross it seems like he's not following the basic rules of being cross-examined. He's always trying to take over and one-up the lawyer who's questioning him. For example, he's on the stand and he accused OpenAI's attorney of asking a leading question, and the judge asked Musk to repeat four words after her: "I'm not a lawyer." That exchange actually went on. She said, "You're not a lawyer, are you?" and he says "No, I'm not a lawyer." And she says "You didn't take any classes on evidentiary law, did you?" and he said "Well, actually I took law 101." And she said "Okay, well still, okay. You're not a lawyer stuff." Definitely fair to characterize him that way. I think, obviously on direct exam he has a lot more freedom to tell his side of the story but on cross he became visibly irritated with the attorney for OpenAI. He kept telling him his questions weren't fair. Lawyers love to say, "Answer the question that I'm asking you. You're not answering my question. It's a yes or no, it's a simple question." Musk replied often that, "Your questions aren't simple. They need context, they need explanation." And so it was a pretty interesting cross-exam where you have this lawyer trying to get something out of him and Musk is resisting kind of at every turn. At one point he said, "This case will become case law and become precedent to looting every charity in America." Okay, stay with me Madeleine. Coming up next on the Bloomberg Law Show, we'll continue this conversation about the OpenAI trial. There was one question that seemed easy to give a yes or no answer to, but Musk resisted. Did he invest one billion dollars in OpenAI? I'm June Grasso and you're listening to Bloomberg. The high-stakes trial in California that could determine the future of OpenAI is in its second week. Elon Musk is facing off against Sam Altman claiming that OpenAI betrayed its altruistic mission in pursuit of profit. The highlight of the testimony so far was the three days Musk was on the stand. On cross-examination Musk had several heated exchanges with William Savitt, the attorney who represents OpenAI, and became visibly irritated as Savitt questioned whether he had backed off his financial commitment to the startup in the early years. Madeleine, Savitt questioned him, quote "At the end of it, you didn't contribute a billion dollars to OpenAI, did you?" Savitt wanted a yes or no answer which Musk would not give until the judge intervened and told Musk to answer the question, which he still didn't give a yes or no answer to. His financial commitments to OpenAI are a big part of this case because obviously he's trying to say that he donated this money under certain circumstances and they've now violated that mission that he thought this money was going to fund. We saw during the course of trial a few different statements that Musk has made publicly about how much money he's given to OpenAI. When they first launched in 2015, they did so with a commitment from him that he would eventually donate as much as a billion dollars. Then he left the board in 2018 and then later we saw a post from him on X in 2023 where he said that he donated a hundred million dollars to OpenAI. That is obviously a big drop down from one billion. But through questioning from the attorney he revealed that he had given thirty-eight million dollars in total to OpenAI before he departed. That is an even bigger drop down from the one billion dollars that he had pledged to commit in the early days. That money was through quarterly donations that he was making but also rent that he was paying on an office building for OpenAI. This is an important issue because it gets at this question of how much Musk was really involved in OpenAI, how much stake he had in this, and then of course there's this attempt to impeach a witness and show that he sometimes says things that don't turn out to be true. He's an interesting character and there were a lot of fireworks like we're saying, but at the end of the day, I think the person we saw on the stand was Elon Musk. There weren't necessarily any surprises in how he was presenting himself. As you say, he's testified before, he speaks publicly a lot, we kind of get a stream of consciousness from him on his social media. I don't think there's anything where it was like "Oh my gosh, I can't believe he said that." But I will say they definitely really were grilling him on cross-exam and I don't know that he totally helped his case. I think it's really early to tell but I think he made a few key concessions including talking about the difference in financial commitments that he made. He said at one point that there was no paperwork that he signed outlining the terms and conditions of his donation to OpenAI. I think that was an important point. And so I think it maybe didn't go as smoothly as he would have wanted it to go, but that being said at the same time, he also had plenty of time to make his own case and I think jurors definitely understood kind of where he was coming from in this too. During jury selection his connection to Trump and his position at DOGE came up which some people held against him. They tried to bring it up, but the judge said we're not going to go there. I think there's some kind of pre-trial agreement limiting certain subjects that could be asked because obviously this case has such huge characters and such hot-button issues that there's so much ground they could really cover and she's trying to keep it very narrow to the issues at hand. At one point Musk was testifying about a meeting that he had with President Obama when he was still in office about AI safety. And so the lawyers for OpenAI were saying, "Okay, like he's opened the door to talking about his government advocacy. We want to ask him about his relationship with the Trump administration." And the judge said that was a no-go, so we didn't get into that on the stand. She runs a tight ship and that means that sometimes when the jury's not in the room the lawyers are getting a little snappiness from her. She really has no patience for nonsense I think and will cut people off and just say "I'm not interested in hearing about that argument, let's move on to something else." I think she's got a really packed calendar and we hear that from her a lot in some of the pre-trial hearings and so I think it's really important to her that this case stick on schedule and get done. I think there's a lot of potential pitfalls here and a lot of avenues where this case could turn and become about something else and so we're really seeing her working hard to kind of keep them on track and keep them focused on this issue. She's a really important part of this case because the jury decision is going to be advisory and so at the end of the day, Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, who's the judge, she's going to get the final say on who wins and what the damages should be. So it's really important for her to get what she needs out of the testimony as well. He's asking for as much as one hundred thirty-four billion dollars and we say that because his request is based on kind of OpenAI's valuation right now which is always fluctuating. But he's pledged to not keep that money; he wants to donate that money back to the OpenAI foundation which is the non-profit arm of OpenAI that still exists today. In addition to that he's asking for Sam Altman and Greg Brockman to be removed from their leadership positions at the company and he wants a court order unwinding this for-profit conversion that OpenAI kind of spent the last year working towards. These are huge things that any one of them being allowed to happen could totally shake OpenAI and kind of put them on a path to an uncertain future at this point. It really remains to be seen how seriously the judge is considering all of those options. She's bifurcated this trial so the jury is just hearing arguments on liability and then once they go to deliberate on that the judge is going to hear arguments from the lawyers on these damages. If she decides to rule in Musk's favor, then she's going to have to decide what is the appropriate remedy here because some of these are pretty extreme. OpenAI is definitely eyeing a public offering at this point and it could be one of the largest in history. I think the last valuation we have for them is eight hundred fifty-two billion dollars, which is a mind-boggling number. So that also is adding to the pressure here and the stakes making them so much higher. Greg Brockman, who is the president of OpenAI currently and was one of these original co-founders with Elon Musk, was the next witness. His testimony covered a lot of ground, a lot of similar ground to Elon Musk's testimony where we heard him talk about these early days of the startup and kind of the dissolution of their relationship essentially. I think one thing that we heard from him yesterday that was very interesting was him talking about kind of early concerns that he, Sam Altman and Ilya Sutskever had with Musk. Ilya Sutskever is one of the engineers at OpenAI and one of the founders as well. He told this anecdote about how Musk was shown a really early model of ChatGPT, or the language model that would become ChatGPT, and he said Musk was very dismissive of it. He called it stupid and said it's something kids on the internet could do a better job making. To Greg Brockman he said that made him feel like someone like Musk shouldn't be in charge of this endeavor because he lacks the ability to dream a little and see kind of the potential of this technology which at that stage in the 2015-2016 time was really essential. He had a kind of a zinger in court he was saying, "he knows rockets, he knows electric cars, he did not and I believe does not know AI" and that was a major concern. So we heard him really go hard on Musk as we expected him to. One of the things that they really were focusing on was the amount of money that he has made from his involvement with OpenAI. We heard that he invested zero dollars in creating the for-profit company, he did not donate anything to OpenAI in the early days, and now his stake in the startup is worth almost thirty billion dollars he testified. I think that was new information to us on the stand. He had an interesting back and forth with Elon Musk's attorney about how he had made so much money given that he had not put anything in. There's a key journal entry that we heard from Brockman from a journal that he has maintained about his professional and personal life. There's one entry in 2017 where he was kind of considering what steps OpenAI should take and he wrote in his entry: "financially, what will take me to one billion dollars?" That's a line that Musk's attorneys have really seized on because their whole case is arguing that these guys sought to enrich themselves by turning this non-profit into a for-profit company and they're saying this line shows that that's what he was focused on, his motivations were personal finance and enrichment. They were asking him about that line and Brockman told them that, "I was feeling like we were in a fork in the road. It wasn't about making myself richer, I was trying to decide if we should go with this vision Elon Musk had presented or this other vision that we had been considering." "And my thought was if I can reach a billion dollars I'll feel like I'm good, it's not going to matter which path I'm choosing." Of course Elon Musk's attorney really seized on that response and he said, over and over, "So you're good with a billion dollars? Why didn't you take the twenty-nine billion dollars that you have now over that and donate it back to the non-profit if you're so good with a billion dollars?" There was a lot of back and forth fighting about this amount and whether he should have such a big stake in this for-profit now and kind of this one billion dollar goal that he had for himself. So I don't know that that went great for Brockman while he was on the stand. Definitely a lot of interesting new details. Another new detail that he revealed while he was testifying is that OpenAI plans to spend fifty billion dollars on compute needs this year which is a huge jump up from where they were in 2017 when they were spending about thirty billion dollars on computing needs. So we also got insights from him about kind of the scale and how much this enterprise has really grown since those early days. Thanks Madeleine, we'll check back with you to see how the trial's progressing. That's Bloomberg Legal Reporter Madeleine Meckelberg. Coming up next on the Bloomberg Law Show, Taylor Swift makes a rather novel legal move to protect her voice and image. I'm June Grasso and you're listening to Bloomberg. Pop star Taylor Swift's image and voice have been used in many AI generated images and deepfakes, including a 2024 post by then candidate Donald Trump that falsely showed Swift endorsing him for the presidency. In a new legal move Swift seems to be stepping up her fight against AI copycats and deepfakes. She's filed trademark applications for these two audio clips. "Hey, it's Taylor. My brand new album 'The Life of a Showgirl' is out on October 3rd and you can click to pre-save it so you can listen to it on Spotify." "Hey, it's Taylor Swift and you can listen to my new album 'The Life of a Showgirl' on demand on Amazon Music Unlimited." Swift has also filed an application to trademark an image of her on stage in a sequined outfit, pink guitar in hand. Historically trademarks aren't designed to protect a person's general likeness, voice or persona, but at least one Hollywood star, Matthew McConaughey, has successfully trademarked his iconic line from the 1993 film "Dazed and Confused": "Alright, alright, alright." These kinds of voice trademarks have never been tested in court. My guest is Cynthia Katz, a music partner at Fox Rothschild. Sound as a potential source indicator can be used as a trademark and has been done in the past in some celebrity situations but it is not the primary form of protection for voice that is typically used in the music business. Trademark really, its function at its core is not to protect the owner of the mark rather than to look at preventing consumer confusion in the marketplace and looking to preserve the consumer so that the consumer has a good source indicator. Mostly that turns into, you know, I'm going to the grocery store and I see a logo, I want to know that I can count on the quality behind that product because that logo tells me something. It's based on actual use so that people really know that this means that I know who's making this product. There have been some sort of iconic sounds over the years that have served as source indicators that have been trademarked, and then celebrities in particular where they have a very unique catchphrase for example. That obviously ties to the Matthew McConaughey "alright, alright", you know, that's so iconic and there are so many brands that would like to have that exact sound, maybe indicating an endorsement. So you know, in those sort of limited situations it has been done before but it's certainly not the standard bearer for musicians in terms of protecting their voice. I mean I don't know, obviously we'll have to see, but trademark is not a given. You have to show actual use in the class that you are trying to register, so you have to show that you've used that exact phrase to market or sell a product or service and that you are using it as a source indicator. Taylor Swift has a massive amount of product. You go to say like a Target, I don't know, there might be a doll out there that looks like a little girl with a microphone that goes "I'm Taylor Swift". If she does have products and services out there where she can show that that phrase has been used by her or her company as a mark origination from her, it's possible. But I think it's a little bit, might be a trickier hurdle. Often you see it's the tag on a clothing, you know, back of a t-shirt that shows this is where this came from, or it is the branding on a website that describes a particular type of service. The evidence to show people that this is out there in the marketplace as a source indicator, I am not familiar with a product that Taylor has put out using those particular catchphrases, but I don't know her full suite. I do know more so than the traditional artist she's a mogul in the sense of she's got a lot of branded goods out there. So if she can show it, it's possible. I don't know also for sure how, you know, there's some subjectivity into all of this. You're going to have an examiner who's a person at the end of the day sitting in the trademark office. They are going to probably understand who Taylor Swift is and what she's trying to do and it's possible that they may give her more deference than they perhaps would to somebody else just trying to trademark their voice because she's trying to protect, my understanding is, against deepfakes and the like and they may give her some more deference there. I don't know. Suppose she does get it trademarked and she wants to go against a deepfake. Can she only go against one where these particular words are spoken? Yes, so that is exactly right; she's going to get only the very specific marks. There's some expanded... so you only register a specific class of goods, but if it's sort of within the realm of a likely extension of that, there's a little bit of leeway there. If this is something that naturally would be extended upon, but it's going to be limited to the mark, to the specific phrase that she is registering. It's not just generally "my voice." So then how far does that get her? Not very far. I mean I don't know what's out there. Perhaps there have been some attempts already that she might be aware of, and she's doing what she can as well. Right of publicity, unfortunately, we don't have a federal right of publicity. It's a patchwork of states, it's not the best. Adding more to your potential arsenal, you know, it's not that costly at all relatively speaking to file a trademark application and go through the process. If I were her I would do everything I could to add to my potential arsenal of things that I could use to go against people, so why not? I think it also helps just to put it out there, the PR and the press of "Hey, I'm not going to stand for this, look at what I'm doing", should kind of put the world on notice as well that you might want to think twice before you do a deepfake with Taylor Swift because she's not into it. So that helps. Then there's also the likeness aspect of it as well. It seems like some people might have tried to put images of her saying these phrases, looking a certain way and manipulating them, and there probably was something out there that they found that this might be reactionary to. Usually something happens and she's upset and she calls her lawyers and says "What can we do?" and they say "Okay, here are some ideas." So it's certainly not going to be one hundred percent foolproof but it can only help. I think there is a big thing to it being out there as you can see in the press picking it up and putting the world on notice that she's ready to fight. So that might be part of the strategy as well. Yeah, I mean I don't know how much more they need in their legal arsenal but every bit counts, I guess. I will say this is... I mean I've seen this on every news network so everybody's covering it. I think it's also helpful in general for, and maybe this might be part of her thought process for, all celebrities and all artists because not everybody is in a position to be Taylor Swift and so if she starts to set a precedent that other people can follow suit with, it further strengthens the position of all celebrities. We're looking for avenues. And perhaps it starts to impact those in a law-making capacity as well. We've been looking for some more protections as AI develops to say we need some strength and we need to have some precedent created, whether that comes through the courts or whether that comes through the legislatures. Essentially famous people should not have AI use their name, image, likeness, voice, what not to imply endorsement of things that they don't endorse or, I think there's some pornographic things that happen as well and all of that. Her kind of getting out in front of this helps everybody else who's in a similar boat. Granted, it's only going to help celebrities who probably... you know but there are different tiers of celebrities I will say. Kudos for to her and getting ahead of it and ultimately the goal of trademark is to prevent confusion in the marketplace from consumers not knowing if Taylor's really endorsing something or or not. So it's a good step and even if it's not enough, it helps adding some more ammunition to those cease and desist letters. Thanks so much Cynthia. That's Cynthia Katz of Fox Rothschild. And that's it for this edition of the Bloomberg Law Show. Remember you can always get the latest legal news on our Bloomberg Law podcast. You can find them on Apple Podcasts, Spotify and at www.bloomberg.com/podcast/law. And remember to tune in to the Bloomberg Law Show every week night at 10 p.m. Wall Street time. I'm June Grasso and you're listening to Bloomberg.

2026 Spring Innovation Keynote: Next Chapter of Autonomous Marketing

ActiveCampaign(18.1K) ·35:38 ·6 views · 2026-05-07
ActiveCampaign is shifting from marketing automation to "Autonomous Marketing," introducing Active Intelligence as a proactive strategic advisor that uses persistent memory and background "autonomous interactions" to deliver personalized marketing outcomes with minimal manual intervention.
TL;DR ActiveCampaign is shifting from marketing automation to "Autonomous Marketing," introducing Active Intelligence as a proactive strategic advisor that uses persistent memory and background "autonomous interactions" to deliver personalized marketing outcomes with minimal...

TL;DR

ActiveCampaign is shifting from marketing automation to "Autonomous Marketing," introducing Active Intelligence as a proactive strategic advisor that uses persistent memory and background "autonomous interactions" to deliver personalized marketing outcomes with minimal manual intervention.

The Evolution to Autonomous Marketing

  • Strategic Shift: The platform has moved beyond simple automation recipes to a rebuilt core foundation centered on Active Intelligence, designed to act as an extension of the marketing team rather than just a tool.
  • Core Pillars: The new chapter of autonomous marketing focuses on three areas: deeper intelligence, smarter decisions, and faster results.
  • Efficiency Gains:
      • Time to Value: Users can move from signup to their first campaign in minutes—8x faster than starting from scratch.
      • Workflow Speed: Campaigns can be launched 3x faster using AI-generated flows and copy.
      • Resource Management: Notable customer successes include a 95% reduction in time spent on email marketing (Frank and Sherri) and 10 hours saved per month on reporting (Parrish Law).

New Capabilities: Memory and Personalization

  • Memory AI: The system learns from user behavior rather than just prompts. It observes manual edits—such as softening call-to-actions or narrowing SMS segments—to align future suggestions with the user’s specific "strategic instincts."
  • Custom Instructions: Users can now define four specific guardrails to ensure AI outputs remain on-brand:
      • Working Preferences: How the AI should collaborate (e.g., "always ask clarifying questions").
      • Target Audience: Detailed profiles of who the business serves (e.g., "small businesses seeking creative partnership").
      • Content Guidelines: Specific tone and formatting rules (e.g., "use invitation language," "keep emails under 200 words").
      • Business Uniqueness: Defining the competitive advantage and current top priorities.
  • Autonomous Interactions: Unlike traditional AI that requires a prompt to start, these features work in the background to surface "outcomes" (e.g., an optimized A/B test or a discovered high-value segment) rather than just tasks or alerts.

Real-World Customer Impact & Insights

  • Performance Metrics:
      • Toss and Spin: Achieved a 71% open rate on targeted emails (vs. 46% for blasts) and a 10x year-over-year revenue increase in February by using AI to drive relevance.
      • Analytics Mates: Improved newsletter open rates from 33% to 45% through AI-driven time-of-day and subject line optimization.
      • University of Albany: Increased enrollment by 27% operating as a one-person marketing team.
  • Strategic "Why": Gary Spagnoli (Analytics Mates) noted that the primary value of Active Intelligence is its ability to explain *why* certain campaigns perform better, providing insights that inform strategy outside of just email marketing.
  • Deployment Advice: Chris Clark (Toss and Spin) recommends segmenting data on the front end and treating Active Intelligence like a consultant by asking, "What can I do to grow my business?"

Proactive Troubleshooting and Revenue Discovery

  • Segment Discovery: In a demonstration for a creative agency, the AI identified 284 "Creative Advocates"—past clients worth an average of $94,000 in lifetime value—who were being neglected with standard promotional content.
      • The AI suggested a "craft-led" nurture track that focused on the creative process rather than transactional referrals.
  • Automated Diagnostics: The system can identify "invisible" errors that dashboards miss.
      • Example: Identifying an automation timing error where "milestone" emails were being sent weeks early because the wait step was resolving against enrollment dates rather than milestone dates.
  • Outcome-Based Workflow: Instead of hours of diagnostic work, the AI presented a "Clean Fix" that allowed the user to re-enroll affected contacts and fix the logic in under two minutes.
"The more we do, the smarter it gets, the more data it can analyze and the faster we can go... they can go fast without guessing." — Chris Clark, Founder and CEO, Toss and Spin
"These are autonomous interactions, AI working in the background and surfacing outcomes when they're ready for you. Not alerts, not tasks, outcomes." — Mamie, Product Team, ActiveCampaign
"The value really came from the why. It started to understand what messaging, which audiences do well. And that actually informed a lot of marketing strategies that even live outside of email marketing." — Gary Spagnoli, Founder and CEO, Analytics Mates
Welcome everyone. We have got so many exciting innovations to share with you today. You have experienced live in the product how Active Intelligence is your strategic advisor and an extension of your team, making you unstoppable. Let's imagine you're the lead marketer at a learning institution called Pixel Path Learning. You have been tasked with helping to increase enrollment for current course offerings. To get a readout on the performance, you would then navigate to reporting and decide whether or not the campaign was performing to your plan. Fast forward to today with Active Intelligence. We've been hard at work to bring you best-in-class autonomous marketing. Active Intelligence isn't just a layer on top of ActiveCampaign.
