Hi, I'm Mamie and I work on the product here at ActiveCampaign. Let's take a look at the next chapter of autonomous marketing: deeper intelligence, smarter decisions, faster results. We'll start on the ActiveCampaign homepage. Every person on a team has a different job, a different set of decisions to make every morning, and a different definition of what matters. So, the dashboard doesn't show everyone the same thing. A marketing director sees campaign performance across a full portfolio and where revenue is trending against a goal. An email specialist sees deliverability, open rates, and which sends need attention before the day starts. A sales rep sees which leads just engaged, which deals have gone quiet, and where to focus first. This is Maya. She is the marketing manager at Compass Creative, a boutique creative agency scaling their referral program from 30% of new business to 50%. Maya logs in Monday morning, and this is what Active Intelligence has been building for her overnight. Three outcomes: a campaign was optimized and relaunched, a new high-value segment was discovered, and performance patterns were identified with some changes already applied. These are autonomous interactions: AI working in the background and surfacing outcomes when they're ready for you. Not alerts, not tasks, but outcomes. Let me show you how Maya taught Active Intelligence to work for Compass Creative, because quality outcomes start with context. Before Maya configures anything, Active Intelligence already knows something about Compass Creative. Not because it was told, but because it was watching. It noticed that every time Maya reviewed a campaign, she softened the call-to-action. "Refer a friend" became "introduce them." "Schedule a call" became "let's talk." Invitation language over directive commands every time without exception. It noticed that when AI recommended expanding SMS to 89 contacts, Maya actually narrowed it to 67. Her note: "we want deeper relationships, not broader reach." Depth over scale, relationship history over engagement metrics. It noticed that she changed brand trends to "where brand strategy is heading." That strategic framing: direction over evolution every time. This is memory—AI learning, not from what Maya says, but from how she decides—and it compounds. The more Maya works with Active Intelligence, the more it understands not just Compass Creative's business, but how Maya thinks. Memory captures what AI observes; custom instructions are what Maya taught it directly. There are four areas: how they work, who they serve, how they communicate, and what makes them unique. So the working preferences, like "always confirm details before creating anything," "ask clarifying questions," "understand the creative challenge first," and "collaboration over assumptions." This is how AI works with every person at Compass Creative, and it solves the intent gap. Even when prompts are vague, AI knows enough to ask the right questions. Target audience: small businesses seeking creative partnership. Many have been burned by vendor relationships before, and they value that collaborative process and strategic thinking. Every recommendation that AI makes now considers who Compass Creative serves. Then there's the content guidelines, like "inspiring yet approachable tone," "use creative partnership, never vendor," "keep emails under 200 words," and "always lead with creative process and thinking before mentioning services or pricing." That is core to how Compass Creative differentiates. And additional context: like being a boutique agency of eight people. They compete against larger agencies by offering strategic partnership. Their differentiator is a collaborative creative process, and their current priority is scaling their referral program from 30 to 50% of new business. Now AI knows the business challenge that they're solving. So there are two inputs: what AI learned from behavior and what Maya chose to share directly. Together, they define how Active Intelligence works for Compass Creative: what it looks for, how it communicates, and what it prioritizes when it has to make a call. Maya set this up once, and AI has been building on it ever since. Now let's see what this looks like in practice. Here's what happened. The client showcase campaign performance dropped Sunday evening. Engagement was down from 38% to 22%. AI analyzed 247 client responses and found that they were asking about their own creative challenges, not engaging with the client showcase message. So AI revised the approach. Instead of "our latest work," it was repositioned as "see what we've been making." It set up an AB test scheduled for Monday morning. Look at the before and after. And here's where personalization really matters. AI remembered Maya's content guideline. It led with creative process before services, it used transformation language, invitation tone, "introduce" instead of "refer," and partnership language that wasn't transactional. This isn't generic AI writing. And notice AI didn't need explicit instructions on this specific email. It understood Compass Creative's context from the custom instructions, which means that even when a prompt is vague, AI knows enough about the business to make intelligent decisions. This solves the intent gap that makes most AI tools frustrating. Maya reviews it and makes one small change to the subject line, shifting "see what we've been making" to "our latest showreel is live." It's more aligned with their positioning. Three minutes of review deployed at 8 AM, and by noon engagement recovered to 41%. 23 introductions are already coming in, and AI will monitor the AB test and report back Wednesday. What used to take until Wednesday to diagnose and fix happened overnight. Maya spent three minutes reviewing instead of three hours rewriting. Now watch what happens. AI optimized that campaign, but it's also monitoring the broader referral program. Overnight, while Maya was reviewing that email, AI was analyzing 18 months of data across all 50,000 contacts looking for patterns. It found something valuable. Here's what Active Intelligence discovered: a segment of 284 contacts who are your highest value advocates. These are past clients who have each made two or more introductions to other brands. Average value per introduction: $47,000. But you're treating them like cold leads. Active Intelligence analyzed 18 months of behavior and found that they're not responding to the promotional outreach. They engage with craft and process content. They want a creative relationship, not a campaign. Look at what this segment is actually worth. Each introduction averages $47,000, but here's what makes them different: 68% of their introductions convert to clients. That's not a referral program; that's a revenue channel. And it compounds. The average lifetime value of a single creative advocate is $94,000, and there are 284 contacts. Active Intelligence profiled exactly what they engage with: case studies, behind-the-scenes process, and strategic perspectives. There is 62% engagement on thought leadership, 31% on creative process content, and 18% on promotional emails. They don't want referral asks. They want content that's worth sharing with peers. That right Nurture Track—Monthly High-value Craft-led—is what turns a past client into a $94,000 partner. First, Active Intelligence finds the segment. And then, more importantly, it tells Maya what to do with it. One recommendation: send a one-time campaign to creative advocates this week, a behind-the-scenes look at a recent project. There's no referral, no promotional angle, just craft content to a group that's proven that they engage with it. This is the highest leverage move available right now. It re-establishes a creative relationship before anything else and it costs Maya 20 minutes. So there are three ways to act: you can save the segment and come back to it, you can add a tag to pull them in permanently out of the general audience, or you can send a one-time campaign right now. Maya does both. Creative advocates are tagged and separated from the general audience. They'll never receive a campaign email again. Then she kicks off that one-time campaign. Active Intelligence drafted the email already. Maya makes one adjustment and she approves. That is the next best action. Not a six-month program, but one well-timed send to the right 284 people. One send, one tag, 20 minutes. That is what acting on the right segment looks like when AI has already done the analysis. But while Maya was reviewing creative advocates, Active Intelligence wasn't waiting. It's been running in the background across every automation, every send, and every timing pattern in Compass Creative's account. It's looking for what's working, what isn't, and what needs attention before it becomes a problem. Some of what it found it handled automatically. Some of it needs Maya to make the call. While Maya was reviewing creative advocates, Active Intelligence was watching something else. Compass Creative's client showcase automation has been misfiring. The date-based wait step is skipped early. Early contacts are receiving a personal milestone note before the milestone arrives. Not after, but before. This happened to 14 contacts in two weeks, and nothing on the surface looked wrong. The automation showed active. Since we're completing without AI watching contact-level timing, this stays invisible. But AI traced it. The wait step is resolving against enrollment date instead of milestone date. For some contacts, that's a difference of weeks. A message that arrives too early doesn't feel personal; it feels automated. And for a referral program built entirely on relationship timing, that is the wrong moment to get wrong. AI isn't just flagging it; it's bringing Maya a choice. Option one: you could remap the wait step to the milestone date field. This is a clean fix that scales with the program. Option two: convert to a fixed-duration wait with a manual review checkpoint before each send. AI recommends option one, but it flags that 14 contacts currently in the flow need to be reevaluated before the fix goes live. Maya recognizes most of them. 11 get re-enrolled at the corrected timing, and three she removes as their milestone windows have already passed. Two minutes and it's fixed. AI caught what a dashboard couldn't show. Maya decided what only she could decide, and that is the collaboration. Three outcomes: a campaign recovered overnight, 284 strategic partners identified, and an automation issue resolved before it quietly damaged 14 relationships. All this happened while Maya was working on other things. But wait, there's more. The more Maya works with Active Intelligence, the more it understands how she thinks—her language, her instincts, and her priorities. This is the platform that we are building. Active Intelligence works across your entire business, understands natural conversation, discovers opportunities proactively, and adapts to every person on your team. It starts with what you saw today: outcomes through autonomous interactions, personalization through custom instructions, and learning through memory. These autonomous actions multiply your team and grow from there. Throughout our session, we have seen how Active Intelligence is changing the way you're working and making your marketing unstoppable. It's your trusted strategic advisor and extension of your team, serving you insights, recommendations, and actions proactively to fine-tune your marketing. And now with personalization, it helps you customize your experience of Active Intelligence and what it creates for your business. The successes you have seen so far, like increasing your marketing performance by 20% with Active Intelligence and driving 40% higher revenue, those are just the beginning. As you saw in our roadmap demos, there's even more coming to ActiveCampaign and Active Intelligence to launch you way ahead of your competition. Thank you for going on this journey with us into the next chapter of autonomous marketing.

Reid Hoffman On AI, Superagency, And The Future Of Work

Bernard Marr(2.1M) ·48:15 ·1.2K views · 2026-05-07
Reid Hoffman (Co-founder, LinkedIn) Bernard Marr (Author and Futurist)
AI represents a "cognitive industrial revolution" where success depends on shifting from being an individual contributor to an "agent manager" and adopting a mindset of "strategic optimism" to steer technology toward positive societal outcomes.
TL;DR AI represents a "cognitive industrial revolution" where success depends on shifting from being an individual contributor to an "agent manager" and adopting a mindset of "strategic optimism" to steer technology toward positive societal outcomes. Strategic Optimism: Hoffman...

TL;DR

AI represents a "cognitive industrial revolution" where success depends on shifting from being an individual contributor to an "agent manager" and adopting a mindset of "strategic optimism" to steer technology toward positive societal outcomes.

The Philosophy of Strategic Optimism

  • Strategic Optimism: Hoffman identifies as a "bloomer" rather than a "doomer," arguing that society only reaches positive futures by steering toward them. While risks must be managed, focusing solely on avoiding bad outcomes prevents reaching "Oxford" (the goal).
  • AI as a Human Multiplier: AI should be viewed through the lens of universal accessibility.
      • Medical Assistants: Providing high-level triage and second opinions to every smartphone user.
      • Legal Assistants: Democratizing access to legal rights and contract understanding for those who cannot afford lawyers.
      • Educational Tutors: Creating the "best learning technology in human history" to provide personalized instruction.
  • Expressiveness: AI is a tool for increasing "humanness" by allowing individuals to express themselves in new mediums, such as Hoffman’s use of AI to sing a "Pi Day" song despite not being a singer.

The Transformation of Work and Leadership

  • The "Agent Manager" Role: Hoffman predicts the end of the "individual human contributor." In the future, every worker will manage and orchestrate a fleet of AI agents to perform tasks.
  • Tactics vs. Strategy: Current frontier models are excellent at tactics but weaker at strategy. Human roles will evolve to focus on high-level strategy, reasoning, and "red teaming" (stress-testing) AI outputs.
  • Institutional Adoption: Companies often get stuck in "pilot purgatory" by running narrow, isolated tests.
      • The Meeting Fix: Every enterprise should immediately integrate AI into all meetings to handle transcripts, summaries, and action-item tracking.
      • Culture of Experimentation: Teams should include "AI reports" in weekly meetings to share what worked, what failed, and what was "useless" (acknowledging that today's useless tool may be essential in six months).
  • The Junior Workforce: Young workers should position themselves as "AI native" to gain a competitive edge. Apprenticeships will shift from rote tasks to "pre-processing" and "red teaming" senior-level work using AI agents.

Investment and Competitive Advantage

  • AI-Native Restructuring: Real value is not found by "sprinkling" AI onto old processes. It comes from restructuring industries from the ground up.
      • Example (Manus AI): Instead of just analyzing papers, Manus AI combines high-quality biology with high-quality AI to reinvent cancer drug discovery, moving beyond "poisonous" chemotherapy.
  • The "Old is New" Moats: Classic business principles like network effects and deep enterprise integration still provide defensive "moats," even in an AI-driven world.
  • Second-Order Effects: The most significant impact of coding assistants isn't just faster code; it is the ability for non-engineers (e.g., financial analysts) to build their own tools and reasoning machines.

Future Trends and Physical AI

  • The World of Atoms vs. Bits: While Hoffman is an investor in autonomous vehicle companies like Waymo and Aurora, he notes that physical AI moves slower than digital AI. General-purpose home robots are not "near-term," but high-value industrial robots (e.g., for green mining) are emerging.
  • Memory and Personalization: A major upcoming trend is the "memory of you." AI will evolve into a lifelong companion that understands your history, preferences, and learning style.
  • The Second Opinion: Hoffman argues that failing to use a frontier model (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) as a second opinion for health or professional decisions is "almost malpractice."

Notable Quotes

"You only get to the good futures by steering towards them. You don't get to the good futures by steering away from the bad ones." — Reid Hoffman
"I don't think we'll have individual human contributors anymore. You'll always be essentially managing and leveraging a set of agents." — Reid Hoffman
"The worst AI you're ever going to use is the AI you're using today." — Reid Hoffman (quoting Ethan Mollick)
"If you're not using your favorite frontier model as a second opinion... you're actually in fact almost doing malpractice even as a patient." — Reid Hoffman
Okay, welcome back to my podcast on the future of business and technology. I'm Bernard Marr, and I'm here today with Reid Hoffman. You are one of the most influential thinkers; I've been following you for such a long time. You are co-founder of LinkedIn. You're also co-founder of Manus AI and Inflection AI. You were a pioneering investor in companies like Airbnb and OpenAI, and a long-standing board member at Microsoft. So, on top of this, you are a podcaster, author, and I guess generally one of the most influential voices in tech, entrepreneurship, and AI. So I'm really excited to talk to you about AI and where all of this is going. And I'm honored to be here. I look forward to the conversation. Thank you so much. So you've had this absolutely amazing career as both founder, investor, author, and thinker. How do you spend your time today? Huh. So my day is full of a lot of different things. Obviously one of the primary things is around AI. So, you know, having co-founded Manus and Inflection, you know, the question about what does AI mean for the progress of humanity and society, and obviously the commercial vehicles of creating companies to do that. Whether it's an AI drug discovery company, using AI's superpowers to try to cure cancer and other kinds of things. Whether it's kind of the question around how is our world going to change when everything that we do will have AI agents with us and around us in terms of how we operate, and what does that mean for becoming more human in terms of what we do? So that, and then obviously I do various forms of content, whether it's podcasts like Possible or Masters of Scale, or coming here and talking with you. And then also I try to like help shape society, right? So like, you know, here we are in London, you know, go and visit 10 Downing Street, which is very curious about how they use AI to help the UK economy and the UK citizenry and so forth. And so they are always looking for, you know, kind of bringing in expertise and intelligence because they've got some very smart people and are doing some really great things. Yeah, all of the above. Amazing. So you've mentioned already so many things I want to dive into. I find it frustrating that a lot of conversations around AI start with what could go wrong, right? And there's lots of doom-mongering. You don't call yourself a doomer, you're saying you're a bloomer. And actually, you started your most recent book with what could go right. Why do you think this is the right question to ask now? Well so it's the right thing to do from a strategic point of view of how do you get to the good futures: to be a strategic optimist, right? Because you only get to the good futures by steering towards them. You don't get to the good futures by steering away from the bad ones. You must also steer away from the bad ones, but simply steer. It's like for example if you were saying, here we're sitting in London, let's drive to Oxford, but first we must plot out every possible thing that must go wrong. We're never going to get to Oxford. Go look, the car is working, you know, the road's not closed, let's go, right? Now part of being strategic about it is to say, okay, being optimistic and choosing where you're going is an intrinsic part of creating a good future. That doesn't mean that you don't pay attention to concerns. It doesn't mean you don't pay attention to them in terms of how you navigate. So it's good to have that dialogue and think about what kind of things could go wrong as you're heading towards what kinds of things could go right. And the problem is if you have a conversation that's doomer or as, you know, you're quoting from super agency, gloomer, you're like, well you're just inevitably going to bad things if that's all you focus on. You have to go, here is how we get to the good things. And that's what I try to do. So paint me a picture of what could go right. What are you most excited about in terms of where AI can make the biggest difference? So a simple way to look at this is what kinds of agents, if we had them on all of our smartphones, would make human life a lot better? Let's start with a medical assistant. Today, you can have a medical assistant that's as good as your average doctor, right? It has some things that's weaker, some things that's stronger, but like you could have that on your phone. Now you think here we are in London, like think about if the NHS had that as a front end that said, "Hey, oh you have a serious thing, we need to check you in." Like we need to get you to the hospital today, we need to check you in, have a meeting with your GP tomorrow, the next day. And it was doing the triage and countering, but also could say, "Oh that's not so bad, you know, for that just take a Benadryl" or whatever the equivalent antihistamine is here. Huge evolution in human life. Next, legal assistant. Most people cannot afford lawyers. But like, okay, what about the rental agreement? What about this thing? What about this issue and so forth, to actually help you understand what your rights are. Interface with government. What are the government services that could work for me, that could help me, and being able to do that, including like, "Hey, I want to report this thing that's wrong at the street corner" or something else. That's really good. An educational assistant. Like, I want to learn this. I want to be able to learn a new profession. I want to figure out what kinds of other work I can do, help ideate with me. All of those things governments should say, we want to get those as free assistants and make sure that they're enabled on every single smartphone for everyone. And that's part of how we navigate a more turbulent future because of course AI is going to transform jobs, right? Like a certain set of tasks that used to be done by humans will now be better done by AI. By the way, that's to some degree good for you. Like say for example you say, well my job was I was doing digital form entry for advertising. Okay, AIs are going to do that a lot better. But that means that now your job is, okay, let me think about how to do better digital marketing. Let me orchestrate a bunch of agents in doing that. It is a transformation. Some people will find that painful. It is what it is, just as when we had cars, we moved away from horses as transport. It's better ultimately for everyone in the world, but the transformations are sometimes challenging. Yeah, I couldn't agree more. As someone that has a front row seat looking at companies like OpenAI, Microsoft, Inflection, all the things, you see what is happening in AI. There's a lot of development around reasoning models, agentic AI, world models, physical AI. What are the trends that you are really excited about right now and that you're watching very closely? So there's enough that we could cover the entire podcast just on that question, but I'll start with three. The first: everyone thinks, okay, yeah, coding assistant, obvious. But it's the second-order effect from coding assistant. So it's not just that now everyone can start generating code, like for example everyone can actually generate a smartphone app today. But it's like what does that mean for now better reasoning. Like for example if you're a financial analyst, the coding agent allows you to do all kinds of new things in financial analysis and allows you to have superpowers that you didn't have before. If you're like for example trying to figure out home finances, you could actually even just not being a financial analyst do that. If you were kind of saying like for example one of the things we do is we create AI music. We have AI assistants, agents helping us create the AI music and kind of iterating through it as a way of expressing—like giving a birthday gift to a friend. So there's this all of this range of things that are the second-order effects in part because the coding agents are like thinking, reasoning, working machines that can not only underlie coding but underlie everything else. So all of the development of coding is something that I find to be extremely interesting. The second area is like memory and personalization. Because part of the reason why I think and part of the reason why, you know, we started Inflection AI is a notion of we'll have AI agents that should be ours that go with us throughout our entire life. They're not just provisioned by a company, but it's like my agent for me that understands what I'm looking for, what I might need. It might be a good foil or an additional voice on how do I navigate what is this new world and kind of information. So and a key part of that is the memory of you. Now part of that memory of you is also like, okay, so like how do you learn something, or what is the real promise of personalized learning? Because we've created the best learning technology in human history. Now it builds on the book, but it's I think even much much better than books as a piece of technology for doing this. And then third and final is, you know, part of what we're doing with Manus AI is to say, well actually in fact we use these AI superpowers to transform, because you know what's one of the most central things in elevating human life is human health, right? So like part of the reason Siddharta Mukherjee and Ujwal Singh and I co-founded Manus is because to say well, cancer kills everybody, right? And it's young, old, male, female, wealthy country, poor country, the whole thing. And it's very hard like the solutions are very challenging. You're lucky if it's micro, it's discovered early, and it's surgery out. That's the easy one. Everything else is brutal. Like chemo is, we try to kill the cancer before we kill you and it's just poison, right, as a way of doing it. How do we get a much more human way of identifying it, preventing it, solving it? AI is the first like major evolutionary step that we've had as a shot on that in decades. What's your view on physical AI and embedded AI and humanoid robots, world models, all of that? So I think it's a definite part of the future and it's definitely very good. The reason I kind of said these things for what is exciting me right now is these are the things that I see progress on, you know, 2026, 2027. It's not to say there isn't progress on this on the physical, but the world of atoms moves much more slowly than the world of bits. And so and people are like, well, you know, you'll have a robot that will do the house cleaning and the dishes. Like no, that's not near-term, right? I do think we will get better and better autonomous vehicles. One of the reasons I was an early investor in Aurora and other things for autonomous vehicles is I think, you know, like people go, oh is it safe to be in an autonomous vehicle? And the answer is yes, you should get in a Waymo, you should see them in terms of how they work. They're coming to London. Yes, exactly. And so and then world models for helping navigate the world, there's all kinds of very good things that come from that. But I think that the first most interesting parts of world models will be things that are call it high-value things that are expensive or difficult for humans, not things that are common for people. Right? And so you know it's like okay, like I want robots for doing mining, right? Because it's dangerous for people and by the way, we should help miners get other jobs and all the rest and by the way some of them will be guiding the robots, right? Matter of fact we might be able to do a lot more green ecological and safe mining if we have robots with people, you know, kind of doing that as a way of navigating. So I'm positive on physical world AI and robotics. I just think it's not as soon as some of the press sometimes suggests. Very good. I would love to know how you personally use AI. So lots of ways. And one of the things that I encourage people to do, which I do myself, is I try to figure out new things every week to add to my AI thing. Because what's almost for sure for just about everybody, like maybe there's only 200 or a thousand people in the world who are actually using AI to its full current capabilities. It's not very many. Or maybe if you put all the employees of you know certain tech companies in, maybe it gets to some thousands, but it's not a lot. And so for me it ranges from—let me maybe put in three buckets which is professional, fun, and personal. So professional, like for example when I'm looking for investments, part of the thing that AI is a good catalyst and companion is like doing deep research on due diligence. Like what questions should I ask? You know, build me a competitive chart from all of the information on the internet. What are the things that you think are the top questions? You know, help me analyze this particular market space and what the competitors are, etc etc. And by the way, people say, well, you used to do that with associates. I still do that with associates; we both do that now, right, with this. And like it expands the areas that I might invest in. Because previously it'd be like, well I'd be curious about that investment in fusion, but like to get to expertise in it is way too hard. Yeah. Whereas now I can, right? And you still have to think about like okay, what's my expertise versus other people's expertise and so forth. So if you go well both of us are using AI and the other person's much more expert, they should be the investor versus me. But I think it also expands that surface in terms of it. So that's professional. So for personal, I'll do that and then I'll do fun last. Personal is like well when you're you know trying to like say for example a friend of mine might be facing a health question, right? Or I'm facing a health question. If you're not using your favorite frontier model as a second opinion, whether it's ChatGPT or Claude or Gemini or Copilot, you're actually in fact almost doing malpractice even as a patient, right? Now obviously there's still a lot of reason to have very high-quality doctors as first opinions. Some people don't have access to that and then your second opinion may become your first opinion. But as the second opinion is really important and I literally have known friends of mine whose lives have been saved by using the second opinion because they went, oh you went in a hospital, the hospital had the wrong diagnosis, they said go to a different hospital and then then literally like great if you'd gotten here three hours later we wouldn't be able to save you. Right? So that kind of thing. So the second opinion, and by the way it's both patients and doctors for it I think is very important and that's an example of personal. And then for fun, you know one of the things that I've been doing is I've been thinking about how AI increases human expressiveness. Okay. So obviously it does that with words, obviously it does that with pictures, there's various things happening with video. But like part of the opening up music to not just people who are because like I'm one of those people you would pay not to sing, right? Same here, right? Right? But like I released an animated video on March 14th for Pi Day, right, about celebrating the number pi and the history of it with Reid AI singing in my voice because I can then go be geeky about a subject that kind of matters like math and pi and so forth in a way that's fun. I can also create music for friends, for like birthdays or other things or holidays. And so using AI to increase your expressiveness is another thing that is completely doable and that you know I recommend everyone do. I think it increases our humanness. Yeah. No I agree. I agree. Let's go back to the corporate world then. I think every executive on the planet knows that AI is important. Yeah. There are not many that really embrace it properly. I see lots of organizations as overlaying AI onto what they are doing at the moment, sprinkling it on to drive efficiency rather than really rethinking what they're doing or going all in really embracing it across their business. You are working with organizations that have completely embraced it; they are driving it. What is the difference between those organizations that are really succeeding with AI and those that are stuck in pilot purgatory? Well so one of the things is like one of the classic ones is as traditionally the way that enterprise will try new technology is run a pilot. Problem is a pilot with AI only tells you something useful in very narrow circumstances because it's kind of the question how you work and what do you do. So classically they say well okay group X do it. Whereas for example almost any company should say hey we should start using AI in our meetings, right? We should not just record them, not just have transcripts, but like the AI should be part of the process through this. Alright which questions should we have asked that didn't? What are the follow-ups? Are there people who weren't in the meeting who should know summaries or certain like the whole meeting or parts of the meeting? Are there people that we need that we have action items assigned to? What is the follow-up plan on that? Well then like literally AIs transform this. So if you're like any enterprise, the short answer is you should go all in on meetings right away and that will start giving you the lens into what other kinds of work that might be right for your company for doing it as opposed to like oh well we'll send off Sarah and Bob and they'll go write a report on how we should be using AI. You're like that's not going to be that interesting, right? And what's more when you get to these questions like well I guess we should be using it for financial analysis, you're like okay well then you say well we need to have a course. You're like actually you don't, right? Like AI is the thing to do it. Start engaging, start using. And so one of the things that I recommend people do is to you know many workgroups have a cadence of a weekly meeting is to say add into your weekly meeting what's something that you tried with AI and what was the result? Like was the result great, in which case everyone else should da-da-da-da, was the result mixed, interesting here but limited here, etc., or was the result bad, like useless. So you're sharing. Now part of it is don't overly conclude useless because you know one of the things I love about Ethan Mollick's line is the worst AI you're ever going to use is the AI you're using today. So what was useless today may not be useless six months from now. So it's worth tracking because like why repeat doing the same non-thing, but it's this question of doing that and I think that's what makes corporations start succeeding and deploying it. Now what happens is you get a long list of well we don't know about legal liabilities, we don't know about information security, we don't know about hallucinations, we don't know about any of this. And you're like well but what you do know is that AI will be a more and more significant part of the future such that every company that is not fundamentally using AI will start arcing off. So don't use excuses to not take small risks, small challenges, small problems, to start the learning curve within your own corporation. And you know that takes a little bit of more boldness than most managers or executives have because the classic thing is we keep doing it the way we have, no one ever questions me. If I change it, I get questioned. But you should seek to be questioned in this time. Yeah. I agree. So what you're saying is one of the key differentiators is creating a culture where people actually embrace AI, use it, experiment with it. And doesn't mean it'll be perfect on it right now. It'll have hallucinations, it'll have some problems etc. Learning what those are now and how to integrate it in like for example for me in investing. Due diligence, competitive analysis, etc. Great. If I said should I make the investment or not, it's not currently useful at that, right? It doesn't really get to the like if I proxied all of my decisioning over to ChatGPT or Copilot or anything else, it wouldn't work, right? And I tried that with all of them just to see every so often. But and it doesn't mean that it won't get there in ways that will be much more helpful than they are, but because it's not useful there doesn't mean I stop using it in an investing context. Mhm. So you talk about investing. You're in—you make investment decisions every single day, especially in technology and AI companies. How do you decide which companies to invest in? Where do you see the real opportunities today and especially in a world where everyone can use AI, everyone has access to super powerful foundation models? Where do you see lasting competitive advantage coming from? So there's kind of maybe two primary kind of answers to this. One is some of the areas are the old is new again in various ways, which is there's classic things that you get to tech investing like network effects or a large fleet of like when you get integrated into a number of enterprises, because enterprises tend to adopt and then hold in terms of where adoption is. You know this kind of thing. And part of the where the new is the old is new again is that when you have a new technology and a disruptive thing, that opens up markets for competitors. When you had mobile phones, all of a sudden it's like oh well we'll have mobile native various forms of apps and so forth. When you had the cloud, okay well we'll have SaaS and we'll have cloud. Now AI is kind of the next version of that. So there's a bunch of different kind of classic business 101 how do you create moats. Now like with the SaaS what changes is well I very few companies can hire a 100 engineers and do code with that. That expands with the amount of coding agents, Cloud Code, CodeX, etc. but so there's some changes there, but it's still the principles of what creates moats around businesses, what creates margin structure in our businesses is still there. Now the second one is AI specific, right? So part of the reason why like I co-founded Manus AI is because well most Silicon Valley people will approach biopharmaceutical the wrong way, namely they'll go, it's all software. It's either we put it all in silico and we do all of our drug discovery reasoning within silico, or we'll build drug researchers or we'll just analyze drug research papers. And actually in fact what you want to do is high-quality AI with high-quality bio, right? And you want to kind of put those two together. And that then means that you have different surfaces where you could create something because where traditionally like there's a whole bunch of software competition say well why isn't the frontier model just going to go on this? And the answer is because they don't have the focus on doing the bio the right way; they're focused on frontier models even if they have a group doing this. This is their primary focus. Then the question around the bio the classic drug discovery companies go well buy some AI, but that isn't restructuring your process for something that's AI native. That's essentially what you need, you bring those two things together and that's essentially the like is one instance of when new kinds of things become possible because of AI. So whenever you say oh that's now changed, great, change. Change opens up opportunity, change opens up a new structure what you're doing. To say well it used to be software economics where it was expensive to make the software but cheap to run it, so you seek business models for that. By the way that's still true, right? Just different how the expenses on making the software and everything else is now changed. But like now you have new business models of like being token factories. That's part of what all the hyper-scalers like Microsoft and everyone else are doing because you know OpenAI and everyone else because then it's like oh that I'm buying a set of tokens. Like there's almost like one of the things we talk about in Silicon Valley is what is people token versus what is compute token and how do those tokens come together in the production of useful work. Well now there's a whole bunch of business models around that too. Interesting. So how will AI change the nature of work then? We talked about the strategic change, but it will profoundly change what people will be doing, the skills they need, what organizational structure will look like in the future. Paint me a picture what you see there. So a couple things. So one is I think every single as it were cognitive task, anything with information or language will have AI agents. Some of those cases the AI agents will completely take over what was a job or set of tasks on this because it's just more efficient and effective to do that. Customer service is one of the examples I use at any place where a human being is following a script, the AI will do that much much better. And that's you know companies like Sierra, Parloa, etc. in terms of doing this. The then there will be a bunch of jobs that'll be transformed, namely the work tasks that you were doing before, now that the AI is much better at those tasks, the work tasks you were doing before, now you plus the AI will be better than that. And then there will be kind of new and evolving set of tasks that come out from there now being an AI-rich universe. One of the ways that I tend to put this is I don't think we'll have individual human contributors anymore. You'll always be essentially managing and leveraging a set of agents. So like agent management will now be a basic skill that everyone kind of needs, how you compose them, how you make the teams work, etc. etc. and part of that is because like even if you look at some of the most advanced frontier models today, they're very good at tactics equivalent—not all tactics, but a lot of them. But they're not as good at strategy. That's the reason why like like hey what's the due diligence on company X, should I invest or not, kind of is tactic strategy. So if you apply that lens across a whole lot of things, that's essentially what the human job evolves to with managing the sets and fleets of AI in terms of how you operate. So a good analysis is you know which tasks are done by AI, which tasks we transformed by AI, and which tasks will stay in people and also which new tasks for people in the whole set. And that will be I think the bulk of a lot of things with a lot of job transformation. Now then we get to what is the nature and the transformation of the firm. Because part of the nature of the transformation of the firm is that the like for example there's this classic Coase paper on the theory of the firm and actually the flows of information, the flows of coordination, the flows of how it's all put together will change. I still think you'll have managers and a CEO and you might have the same department structure that we have now—maybe that will change, that's an interesting question. But like for example as the example I was mentioning earlier that every company should start having AI agents in every meeting, that changes the efficiency of how your flow of information works in a firm. And that then creates different decisioning methodologies, that creates different depth of research and risk analysis on certain kinds of decisions. A lot of what work firms do is build work processes like this is how you use wikis or this is how you use knowledge management. All of that will get transformed and that will transform the nature of like how the company works together, right? Not just the individuals managing agents and all that but that will also happen in terms of this. What do you see the impact of junior employees will be? Because there's a big fear among young people that actually the junior jobs where people train on the job those are very easily automatable. I have three kids at the stage where they are starting to think about their future careers. What advice would you give young people? So right now and immediately, one of the advantages you'd bring as young people is "I'm AI native." You really want me in your firm because you want me helping you with the AI transition. And I'm very—so there's a thing right now to take what people worry about as this disadvantage and turn it into a very strong advantage, and playing into that I think is very important across nearly all young people. Now the question is what happens as this matures and firms get more AI native and everything else and they go, "Okay well a lot of those like we used to have you do the simple rote boring jobs as a way of proving yourself into this." I suspect we will also create two forms of pathways. One is there will be new apprenticeship ways of doing it. Sure. Like say you're a paralegal, it wasn't go down and pull out all the books and photocopy the articles relevant to it because we have all that. But it would be the do all the pre-processing on working with the AI agent to create the summary by which the senior lawyer will be going into to doing that. And maybe that will be fewer paralegals than there were before, but by the way there also may be more law firms too in terms of how this is operating. So I think that I think there will still be that transformation. Now the second one is all firms will want to be will need to have, unless we get to a Star Trek universe where all companies are run by AIs and everything else which you know some people talk about. I think we're far much further away from that than most Silicon Valley discussion. But before we get there, firms do need to have an onboarding path. Now part of what they do with the onboarding path because they want people like new talent coming on to be able to be like in law the partner, etc. They want a path that actually produces useful work, that produces high-quality outputs on it. Like I wouldn't be surprised if now you know takes a little bit of a good containment of your own ego, but like use the legal thing. Say I was a law partner and I'm trying to figure out how to bring in younger partners you know younger like associates to come up as partners. I'll say okay I want you to take all the work that I'm doing and all the work that I'm producing getting produced and with AI and all the rest and I want you to go use AI and red team it. I want you to come back and analyze it and bring back the three things that we should also consider, the weaknesses, the strengths, the things we could do different and go use AI 80 hours, 100 hours this week to bring back the best possible red teaming. And part of what I'll learn is are you doing that the right way because that's the kind of expertise that you'll need to know at high quality it is. And so I think you'll we'll get new patterns of what apprenticeship looks like in this. Now of course it's scary because people are like well I'd like to know that now. This is always the case of work transformation. But that's the and and you know there's always in every single this is where I wrote Superagency, in every single time "Oh this time is different and the world's coming to an end." It's the printing press, is electricity, is the car, is the mobile phone, like all of the stuff is what tends that is the computer. But actually in fact part we have a collective reason to inventing a good society that has good economic pathways, has good ways that we can make progress in our lives, contribute, etc. We will get there, right? And that's why we are always creating the right kinds of interesting new jobs. And so you know for example before internet services existed, there were no data scientists. Now we have lots of data scientists. Now you say well AI is changing data science. Yep. And like knowledge as SQL doesn't really matter; they can do a bunch of interesting analysis. Now the data scientist is to be the strategist for the data science. Anyway, and on and on. Very good. Yeah. I like that. And I completely agree again. Yeah. AI is moving super fast and companies we talked about the fact that companies struggle to really embrace it properly. How do you balance speed and adoption and experimentation with the responsible and ethical use of AI? So part of the to have to take risk to do innovation, you always have some errors, some faults. So the key thing you have to do is say we acknowledge and allow there to be some errors. Zero error is like again like say we we're going to plan to drive from here to Oxford and we're going to have zero error. You're like well you're never going to get in the car. You can't go to zero error, you go to reasonable amount of error, right? Just like autonomous vehicles shouldn't be zero accidents, should just be a lot less than the human average and making the road safer which is part of like what Waymos do as people experience them and so forth. So the question around innovation and safety is not big errors. And what I mean by big errors is like I think Anthropic has done a good thing by paying attention to cybersecurity and paying attention to the use of AI by criminals or rogue states, bioterrorism, etc. So that's one stack of things. The second is the transition in society is challenging. It was challenging for the printing press, it was challenging for the industrial revolution. This will be new and difficultly challenging because it's faster than the industrial revolution and the printing press. It is a cognitive industrial revolution; it might be even bigger than the earlier industrial revolution. So the civil disruption, right, where people as opposed to working together to kind of help the transition get through, get very intensely internally conflictatory, right? Whether it's civil disorder, whether it's having kind of society be much more in internal conflict in these things. Those kinds of things also worry me. And then you know like in the science fiction case where you go, well maybe we are creating this superintelligence. Obviously I'm a deep humanist and maintaining a role for human beings as very important here matters to me. Now I don't know—I think it's weird that it's above 0 percent that this is an operative question. It's not nearly as high as most of the people talk about this as. Like for example I'm not sure that this kind of superintelligence question you or I or your children will confront in our lifetimes, right? Because they say well but look at the exponential AI progress. I say well it's a jagged edge, right? Like it's like an amazing savant. A savant that's great at a number of different things, but an amazing savant is not a reordering of the cosmos. And so anyway so those kinds of things are the things that I would say I pay attention to and probably in roughly that order. Yeah. I like that. If you were to give leaders watching this a top tip when it comes to AI and implementing it successfully in their organization, what tip or tips would you give them? Well so one thing that we've already talked about today is get your people engaged. Yeah. Have individuals using it, trying it, reporting it. By the way this is also true of government. So there was recently here as you know some minister's like "I don't use AI." It's like everyone who has any care and any responsibility for any size of organization should be using it some personally, but people should be using it. Two is integrate it into meetings, integrate it into kind of this kind of information flow. And third is be thinking well what are the areas where we have some unique capabilities, knowledge, expertise, etc. that putting together with AI could help us be creating some of the industries of the future. Like those three areas I think would be the beginning. Excellent. I could literally carry on for days. That is so enjoyable. Thank you so much for your time today. Thank you. My pleasure. Thank you. And for anyone who ever wants to rewatch this conversation, you can simply watch my YouTube channel where you can find this conversation and many others like it. Thank you. Pleasure. Thank you. How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky) | Eric Jorgenson on Naval Ravikant

BigDeal by Codie Sanchez(580.0K) ·61:59 ·2.2K views · 2026-05-07
Eric Jorgenson (Author, The Almanack of Naval Ravikant) Naval Ravikant (Co-founder, AngelList) Codie Sanchez (Host, BigDeal)
To achieve wealth and contentement without relying on "blind luck," individuals must move from linear labor to high-leverage activities by "productizing" their unique authenticity and decoupling their income from their time.
TL;DR To achieve wealth and contentement without relying on "blind luck," individuals must move from linear labor to high-leverage activities by "productizing" their unique authenticity and decoupling their income from their time. Defining Leverage: In the modern economy, the...

TL;DR

To achieve wealth and contentement without relying on "blind luck," individuals must move from linear labor to high-leverage activities by "productizing" their unique authenticity and decoupling their income from their time.

The Mechanics of Modern Leverage

  • Defining Leverage: In the modern economy, the most potent forms of leverage are Code, Media, and Capital. Unlike labor, these have zero marginal cost of replication and provide access to a global marketplace.
  • Input/Output Decoupling: The goal is to move away from linear work (earning $X per hour) toward uncorrelated returns.
      • Examples: A podcaster puts in the same effort to reach 100 people as they do 100,000, but the reward is exponentially higher. Similarly, an investor's decision-making process is the same whether they deploy $100k or $1M, but the output differs by an order of magnitude.
  • The Leverage Ladder: Eric Jorgenson illustrates leverage tiers using the real estate industry:
      • Tier 1: Day laborer (unleveraged labor).
      • Tier 2: General contractor (manages labor).
      • Tier 3: Developer/Investor (uses capital).
      • Tier 4: Tech Founder (e.g., Zillow) (uses code and global media).

Productizing Authenticity and Specific Knowledge

  • Escape Competition Through Uniqueness: Codie Sanchez and Jorgenson emphasize that "No one can beat you at being you." Success is downstream of being authentic and excellent in a way that is difficult to replicate.
  • Specific Knowledge: This refers to the skills or interests that feel like "play" to you but look like "work" to others.
      • Discovery: Look at what you did naturally as a child (ages 10–12) or the 10% of your current job you do for free.
      • The "Productize Yourself" Framework: Identify your unique traits and package them into a scalable product or service.
  • The Skill Stack: Instead of trying to be the #1 best in the world at one thing (e.g., Michael Phelps), stack disparate skills (e.g., writing + investing + media) to create an "unfair advantage."

Strategic Judgment and "Living Like a Lion"

  • Judgment over Hard Work: While hard work is respectable, it only yields 2x–3x improvements. High-level judgment and leverage can yield 100x impacts.
  • The Lion Metaphor: Naval Ravikant advocates for working like a "lion" rather than a "grazer."
      • The Sprint: Lions spend most of their time resting/waiting. When they see an opportunity, they exhibit a burst of high-intensity "violence" and sprint, followed by a period of recovery and assessment.
      • The Trap: Most corporate workers act like grazers, plodding along for 40 hours a week, which leaves them too tired or "busy" to seize massive opportunities when they arise.
  • The Danger of Salary: Jorgenson cites the idea that a monthly salary is one of the three most addictive substances (alongside heroin and carbohydrates) because it keeps individuals in a comfort zone that prevents them from taking necessary risks for equity.

The Philosophy of Happiness and Desire

  • Desire as a Contract: Eric Jorgenson explains that "Desire is a contract you make with yourself to be unhappy until you get what you want." Contentment comes from choosing fewer desires more carefully.
  • Desire Management: In a world of social media-driven "mimetic desire," one must triage new wants by asking: "Do I want the price tag that comes with this desire?" (e.g., wanting to be a famous comedian means accepting the lifestyle of late nights and constant travel).
  • The Ultimate Intelligence Test: Naval defines the "true test of intelligence" as whether you are getting what you want out of life. Success is an "inner scorecard" game where you set the goalposts, rather than chasing external validation.
"Forget rich vs. poor, white-collar vs. blue. It's now leveraged vs. unleveraged." — Eric Jorgenson (quoting Naval Ravikant)
"Find what feels like play to you but looks like work to others. You're going to out-compete them because you're going to do it effortlessly. To you it's art, it's joy, it's flow. You escape competition through authenticity." — Eric Jorgenson
"Hard work is overrated and judgment is underrated... One totally correct decision can dominate everything." — Eric Jorgenson
Somebody said to him, "If you're so smart, why aren't you happy?" and he's like, "Oh, I can apply my intelligence to this problem and I can break it down." Desire is a contract you make with yourself to be unhappy until you get what you want. If you can learn the discipline of having few desires carefully chosen, you spend a lot more time being content, being happy, not holding a strong burning desire that is making you unhappy every minute until you've achieved something. This episode is an important step back from a world that increasingly wants to tell you what to do, when to do it, and how to do it. We're bringing on my friend Eric Jorgenson, one of the best writers of our time on brilliant billionaires and philosophers like Naval Ravikant. He spent the last ten years studying him. Now we're going to steal his homework. Naval has a line that's very jarring about how a salary is like the most addictive form of heroin. The three most addictive substances are heroin, carbohydrates, and monthly salary. Like that push to get you out of the comfort zone. Are you a risk-taker? Are you exposed or are you kind of like a lamb in the paddock? Can you get really rich on a 9 to 5? What I took from Naval building this book when I was like 30 is like, you can grind and beat yourself up and have this brutal internal monologue all the way to like winning the game, have the same epiphany that you're going to have right now, which is that like none of it matters. One of my favorite quotes from Naval is, "Forget rich vs. poor, white-collar vs. blue. It's now leveraged vs. unleveraged." What does that mean? Leverage is, to me, one of the most important things that Naval talks about. If you don't understand leverage, a lot of things about the world and the outcomes that you see are not going to make sense and you're going to get like bitter and confused and feel like it's unfair. But it's actually just the normal workings of leverage. And so, as Naval explains it, there are many forms of leverage. The modern forms of leverage are code, media, and capital. Zero marginal cost and uncapped, and access to this sort of global marketplace. And it's really unintuitive, like we're not programmed to think that way. And so, understanding that we're in the age of leverage and it totally changes like what businesses can be built and where your priorities are. And we're seeing unbelievable businesses get built that sort of have this understanding of leverage at the core of them. It's so funny you say that. I was at this YouTube thing this week and so I have that top of mind. One of the creators there, I think it was Mark Rober, who has a huge science YouTube channel, and he did something with like the Discovery Channel or another science channel. And so the, you know, CEO of YouTube was joking with him and he was like, "Yeah, it was really nice of you to do that thing with the Discovery Channel. It's incredible to collab with a smaller channel," because their viewership's like 10x smaller than Mark Rober's on YouTube. And so it's interesting, like this idea of capital, code, and media. How do you judge whether you have those kinds of leverage or you don't, if you're a normal person? Ideally, you want your earnings, your inputs, and your outputs to be separate from each other, right? You don't want to have to work an hour to earn, you know, 10 bucks or 100 bucks or a thousand bucks. You want to earn independent of your input. If you're in a normal job, you're linear inputs and outputs. If you are working on a podcast, like you record the same podcast, you publish it and whether 100 people listen to it or 100,000 people listen to it, you put the same amount in, but you get an unbelievably higher reward for doing the same amount of work. If you're investing in a company, like whether you invest $100,000 or a million dollars, it's the same decision, but the rewards for doing it are an order of magnitude higher. So you want to look for these places where your inputs and your outputs are uncorrelated and the more leverage you are, the bigger that difference is going to be. And the way you get leverage is by winning a smaller leverage game. So start small and just let those things accrue incrementally. Like we talked about Elon Musk, he's like the most leveraged person alive and it's because people choose to give him capital, people choose to go work for him, people choose to support his companies and invest in him. And they do that because they trust his judgment. They trust that he's going to use that well and give them good rewards for doing so. Yeah, it's so interesting because in today's age it's even more normal. Like it used to be, maybe even like 30 years ago I would say, most people had none of these, right? You had no money, you had no code (you weren't an engineer), and you had no media because you weren't CNN. So it was like, unless you were a top 10 percent or 1 percent in any of these three, you had zero. But now, everybody could actually have all three really easily. So you could literally just ask yourself, "Am I making money and thus am I taking my money and investing in other things?" That's leverage. "Can I code?" No, but now I've got all these AI tools, so I can now use code, that's leverage. And "Media, do I own CNN?" No, but I actually have a podcast or I have an email newsletter or I have an Instagram account that I talk about. And so yeah, I actually am leveraged with all three. And then it's degrees of them, but it's a wild time to be alive because for the first time ever any human, really, at least in, you know, our most established worlds, can have all three of them for almost a zero-dollar cost except capital. Yeah, if you can get a device that can get on the internet, like you can enter the global meritocracy, essentially. And like, most people aren't even trying. They're, most people are just on the consumption side. And so if you do, if you start creating, if you start coding, if you start trying to build products, like and you think about it in terms of where do you get those uncorrelated inputs versus outputs and try to build a business, that's where you're going to get those exponential returns. This is true in every industry, right? Like I think people running a plumbing company would probably say, "Yeah, that's all well and good for a podcaster or a hedge fund, but like I run a plumbing company." Every business is just, their growth is determined by how leveraged they are and how elegant they are and how long those levers can get. And Naval has a great, it's a long thing, I won't take you all the way through it, but he has to go step by step all the way through the real estate industry where, you know, you could be a day laborer working on a house, you could be a general contractor, you could be a real estate investor, you could be a developer of big properties, or you could take the final leap into like maximum leverage of external investors and technologists and everything and build Zillow. Right? And so the difference between a day laborer and Zillow is just like how leveraged are you and how comfortable are you using each of those different forms of leverage. That's a really good point, I actually haven't even thought of that before. It'd be interesting to put names on the tiers, you know, because every industry actually has that. Everyone has the day laborer all the way to the CEO, to the like level of CEO of what kind of company you are, to investor, to like, I don't know, capital allocator plus risk taker. So it's actually an interesting way to think about the world. I think a lot of times people just think, "Am I entry level? How much do I make?" but they don't think about what is the game the company that they're in is playing too. And I feel like Naval's the king of contextual awareness. Like, you know, understanding the game that you're playing. That would be a fun book to build, of just like breaking down the business from the beginning all the way up the whole ladder. Because you hear these incredible stories of people who entered as a dishwasher in the restaurant industry and over the course of their career built like a 50-restaurant chain. And those are people who just sort of figured out what does the person above me know that I don't know? What tool do they know how to use? What were they able to access? Like, how can I figure it out? How can I take that next step, that next risk and climb all the way up the ladder. Naval talks about something that is kind of controversial these days. He says, "Hard work is overrated and judgment is underrated." What do you think he means by that? I think of the, you know, the Archimedes quote, "Give me a lever long enough and a place to stand and I can move the world." Like one totally correct decision can dominate everything. And Naval is really an elegant thinker. I think he's like really enamored with the idea of like where can you flick one domino and everything takes care of itself. Which is like this incredibly kind of like Zen business thing. What's the smallest action I can take to create the biggest result? It's very different than somebody like Elon or Goggins or whatever who are just kind of like trying to get the most out of themselves possible. But I think that's a really interesting approach. It's a good tool for thought to just be like, yeah, how easy could this be? He describes himself as lazy, which I don't think he is, but he's like, "I just want the greatest reward for my effort and I don't want to expend effort wastefully." Yeah, there is something to this idea of how do you become incredibly ambitious while also being lazy. And lazy in like the things that you allow to take up your time because if you are crazy particular about the things that take up your time, then you have time to figure out where to flick that domino. Like that's going to live rent-free in my mind, actually. Like you could spend a lot of time in the middle and you will never actually have the full effect than if you spent the time to find the beginning. And the real thing is like hard work has a limit eventually. Like you can't work 100-hour weeks forever. Hard work is, you know, respectable and admired and yes, we should work hard, but hard work can only get you a 2 to 3x improvement in what you're doing. And if you don't learn to improve how you're working or the elegance or improve your leverage, like learn where you can have those 100x, where one hour of work or 10 hours of work can have a 10x or 100x impact, like you're going to be kind of stuck in that 2 to 3x improvement window no matter what you do. Yeah, I think the part that then becomes hard, so then you're like, okay, that makes sense, of course I'd want to find the fulcrum or of course I'd wish to find the first place to flick the domino, but then the question becomes how do you know? And like Naval has great sort of critical thinking frameworks, but what does he mean by "Live like a lion"? Like if you go see a lion on safari, like they're just chilling most of the time, like 30, 45 hours at a time, just laying there, sleeping and kind of whatever. And then when they are both hungry and see prey, they leap into action, like maximum adrenaline, full sprint, unbelievable violence and attack and take down this thing and have an incredible feast and then just lay there and sleep again for like 30 hours. And I think Naval's sort of learned wisdom is that this is how he now approaches projects. Like he'll see an opportunity, see an investment, just kind of be like reading and casually working, learning, going about his life and then an opportunity will arise or a person will make themselves known as kind of like, "Alright, we gotta leap into action, we gotta have a sprint, we gotta start a company, we gotta make an investment, we gotta get up to speed on this really, really quickly." But if you were mired in something else, just like plodding along on not the biggest opportunity rather than having the space to jump on the best opportunity when it came about, then you might miss it. And like that's the "Live like a lion" kind of mindset is like you want to have the space to evaluate and attack the most important thing at any time. You know, it's interesting you talk about that because one of the things I've been obsessed with lately is people sitting in meetings that they shouldn't be in. And it's a big phenomenon and you know, my team will all sort of chuckle about this because I will often say like, "You guys don't have anything to do in here, why are you still in here?" But what I haven't got to yet is where they self-select to leave the meeting because there is so much social normative behavior of just, "No, no, I'm supposed to be here from this time to this time and I'm paying attention and I'm sitting here." And it's like why are we being such good little girls and boys? Instead, you really should be saying, "You pay me and we are here to do this mission and if I am sitting here doing this thing that is not top priority, I'm actually wasting your time, our money, our mission, all of it." But somehow we've all become like way too politically correct to say anything like that. And so we just sit in meetings that we know are a waste of time, we tell nobody and we think that's normal. And then a CEO might like it. I feel like there's probably two types of person in that meeting. One is like, "This looks like work and it's the easiest thing I can do today, so I'm just going to sit in here and like I'm going to be in a meeting, I'm doing my job with the lowest effort possible." And then there's somebody else who is sitting there and their skin is crawling because they're like, "I hate this, I'm useless, I feel like I can't leave, I've got more important work to do, like please let me out of here." But both are caught in the like if you make the first step of the meeting like what is the point of this meeting and who belongs in it? Like who has a hand in the solution that we're trying to arrive at and spend the first five minutes of a meeting on that and make it a cultural norm to dismiss people from meetings that are not like, "I don't have a good case for being here and great, I want to be somewhere else." Yeah, it seems like such a no-brainer but I think often even at our company where I say people, say this to people a lot, I don't think we've done a good enough job giving them permission to beat it, you know, if they're like, "Codie, I don't think this meeting is relevant to me," I don't think I've ever got mad at anybody for that. I'd be like, "Oh, yeah, right, right, get out of here." Yeah, there's a dynamic where people I think want to be in the same room as the person, the CEO, the leader, the whatever. Like those meetings are an opportunity for facetime probably in some ways that are like more about the political game than the output. Like when you give feedback, sometimes you have to be careful at which levels of the game because it can just ruin somebody, or simultaneously they want to hang out with you more even though you're like, "I would like you so much more if you didn't do that." Like these are examples of specific knowledge that are outcomes of just how we chose to live and we just continued to do things that felt authentic and unique to us and people responded to it and we sort of iterated through like, alright, I had fun doing that and the world enjoyed it so I'm going to do it again and I'm going to do it in a new way and I'm going to continue it. And so I think that's super interesting. I want to circle back to this idea on "shoulds" because you have very interesting takes on it and you've caught me twice on it. And Naval is very aggressive on "shoulds" too. Like, do you believe in the word "should," does Naval Ravikant believe in the word "should," and how should we speak to ourselves? So "should" is one of those words that just indicates to you that you might be feeling more of an obligation than an interest in something. And so you hear people talk about like, "I should go work out." Like yeah, but also your relationship to that thing is now like I don't want to and the voice in my head is telling me that I have to. And the common thing is like "I get to work out." So just like change your phrasing about you know you have the opportunity to go do this thing that you want to do. There's a bunch of funny like little reveals in language that like when you're paying attention to people's relationships with things, that's in there. But like "I should go to work," "I should, you know, follow in my family's footsteps," "I should pursue this opportunity," like we all have those and it just listening for them yeah, it's either an obligation or an imposition from some version of yourself that you don't necessarily agree with or some external voice that is like speaking to you more loudly than your inner voice. It's really good. I mean he talks about, he has like a pretty brutal heuristic about if you're faced with a difficult choice you should do - gosh dang it, can't even say the word "should" - but if you're faced with a brutal choice and there's not an obvious one, then don't do either. Like what would he say about when to do something or when not to do something? There's one from his like personal trainer which is "hard choices, easy life," which is like in the short term if you take the hard path, you will sort of naturally bias - you'll come up with an excuse not to work out, you'll come up with an excuse not to have the hard conversation. And so if you find yourself in that position where there's something hard to do and there's something easy to do, if you just become good at making yourself do the hard thing, your life will become better as a result. There's probably something magical that's on the other side of something you're avoiding. And if you just build the habit of tackling that, a lot of problems will take care of themselves. That's one. Paul Graham has a similar thing competitively, which is just like run up stairs. You know, if you're fighting a big company do something really hard that they're going to find it very difficult to imitate. Agreed, especially as the world gets less and less moated. You know, you used to have to have a ton of capital to launch a media company and now you have to have none. And so if you don't do the hard thing that is hard today, well you're definitely not going to make it next year because that thing that's hard today will probably be even easier next year. Can you break down some of those types of luck? What does Naval mean by these different types? Yeah, and this ties in well with the sort of authenticity and specific knowledge because the ultimate form of luck is to like be the kind of person that luck is your destiny, right? Did you notice it? What was the second? The second is luck through persistence. Luck through persistence. Okay, so like did you happen to walk across a hundred-dollar bill versus if you went out on the beach with like your metal detector and like spent hours looking for something. That's, you know, persistence. These are simple metaphors, but like did you happen to run into somebody who wanted you to paint their house today or did you knock on 200 doors and find three people who wanted you to paint their house today? But that's also too because there's like a lot of studies now that show that you actually will not see lucky things if you think you are an unlucky person. Have you seen, I just was reading a study the other day where they gave two groups of people, blind study basically, one group of people was told varying things to the degree of they're lucky, good things happen to them and they are the type of people that believe that. The other group was the type of people who say good things don't really happen to me, I'm not super lucky. Then they were given a task and the task was to go and look through a booklet for how many times some word was said. So I don't know, "bad." And it's like go through it and look for how many times "bad" is said and tell us at the end how many times "bad" is said and then you'll get a thousand bucks or something when you find the 217 variations or like whatever the number is. And so the first group that thought that they were not lucky, they went through diligently, you know, they got to the 217 number, they told them okay 217. And the other group goes through it and on like page five there is a little paragraph that says, "You can stop searching, there's 217 instances of bad." And this other group finished it in like 60 seconds and this group took 10 minutes on average. Like their vision was the same, like they came from sort of the same sociodemographic, but one associated it's not lucky and one associated it's lucky. And so I thought that was fascinating. So explain to me spotting luck and then built luck. Spotting luck is just the ability to sort of position yourself at a place where luck happens to you, right? And so this is you know maybe the highest order decision is like where do you live? What's your exposure? What's your surface area to luck? If you say you can't find anybody to like ask out on a date, okay, well how often are you like going to parties? Have you left your house? Where are your habits? How many companies have you started? How many companies have you met with? How many customers have you approached? Like these things are you can control your surface area to luck essentially.
