Vou no caminho já ver o que vocês publicaram porque, antigamente, eu tinha que esperar o que vocês publicavam no dia seguinte; agora eu nem acabei de falar e vocês já publicaram. E eu vou olhar na cara de vocês para ver quem falou mal e quem falou bem, tá? Beijo no coração de vocês. Abraço. "Presidente, quando é que o Trump vai ao Brasil?". "Presidente, sobre a operação de hoje contra deputados, como o senhor classifica?".

Reid Hoffman On How Technology And AI Will Shape Humanity

John Hope Bryant(185.0K) ·61:10 ·119 views · 2026-05-07
Reid Hoffman (Co-founder, LinkedIn) John Hope Bryant (Founder and CEO, Operation HOPE)
The AI revolution is a "techno-humanist" shift comparable to the Industrial Revolution that will cause significant short-term labor market disruption while ultimately amplifying human agency and creating a future where professionals act as "conductors" of AI-driven systems.
TL;DR The AI revolution is a "techno-humanist" shift comparable to the Industrial Revolution that will cause significant short-term labor market disruption while ultimately amplifying human agency and creating a future where professionals act as "conductors" of AI-driven...

TL;DR

The AI revolution is a "techno-humanist" shift comparable to the Industrial Revolution that will cause significant short-term labor market disruption while ultimately amplifying human agency and creating a future where professionals act as "conductors" of AI-driven systems.

The Philosophy of Techno-Humanism

  • Defining the Techno-Humanist: Reid Hoffman describes himself as a "techno-humanist," believing that the primary value of technology lies in its ability to amplify human nature, individual agency, and social progress.
      • The "Alien" Phase: New technologies (cars, electricity, fire) initially feel alien but eventually become natural; AI is currently in this transition.
      • Human-Centric Development: Technologists must ask how their work improves the human condition rather than simply building tools to bypass "bothersome" human interaction.
  • The "Blind Spot" of Silicon Valley: John Hope Bryant notes that many tech leaders lack "People EQ," often dismissing the human element in strategy. Hoffman emphasizes that technology is only successful when it helps a broad cross-section of society.

Impact on Jobs: Short-Term Turmoil vs. Long-Term Gain

  • Short-Term Outlook: Hoffman predicts a period of "turmoil and chaos," particularly for roles where humans are asked to "act like robots."
      • At-Risk Sectors: Customer service is the "canary in the coal mine," as AI will soon outperform humans at following scripts and solving routine problems.
      • Wage vs. Job Effects: In fields like software engineering and law, Hoffman does not foresee a total loss of jobs but anticipates a "wonky" wage effect—top performers will see salary increases while the median wage may decrease due to automation.
  • Jevons Paradox in Software: Hoffman argues that because there is "infinite demand" for software, making engineers 100x more productive won't lead to fewer jobs; it will simply lead to a massive increase in the volume of software created.
  • Economic Warning: The industrial revolution initially lowered lifespans and caused 50 years of pain before creating the middle class; Hoffman warns that the AI transition will be a "taxing ride" that requires proactive government navigation.

Strategic Advice: Becoming "AI Native"

  • The Three AI Assistants: Every individual should leverage AI as a trio of personal advisors: a medical assistant, a legal assistant, and an educational assistant.
  • Beyond "Search": Hoffman urges users to move past using ChatGPT as a Google replacement.
      • Practical Applications: Use AI for complex P&L (Profit and Loss) scenario planning, risk mitigation, and drafting professional documentation.
      • Legal Disruption: AI can lower the barrier to justice, potentially reducing legal costs from six figures to four figures by automating "legal wrangling" and discovery.
  • The "Conductor" Model: In the future, the primary job skill will be acting as a "conductor of AI agents," leading an orchestra of intelligent tools rather than performing the manual tasks themselves.

Entrepreneurship and the "Career Jungle Gym"

  • Lowered Barriers, Higher Competition: AI makes it easier to launch a "one-person million-dollar company," but because everyone has access to the same tools, the noise in the marketplace will increase 1,000x.
  • The Death of the "Career Escalator": The traditional path of staying in one competency for 50 years is over.
      • The Jungle Gym: Professionals should expect 5-8 different careers over their lifetime, navigated like a "jungle gym" rather than an escalator.
      • Entrepreneurial Mindset: Even those within large companies must adopt a "Startup of You" mindset to maintain relevance as the "table stakes" for competency continue to rise.
"The importance of technology is how we amplify our humanity... technology is only good when it helps a lot of people." — Reid Hoffman
"We’re heading into a combination of an earthquake and a tsunami. It’s going to shake everything up, and that’s going to be scary... but you have to embrace it." — Reid Hoffman
"Intelligence is equally distributed, opportunity is not. This has a chance to level that playing field." — John Hope Bryant
"As opposed to a career escalator, you’re going to have a career jungle gym, and the AI will help you with the jungle gym." — Reid Hoffman
Hey, hey everybody, it's John Hope Bryant, and this is the Money and Wealth podcast series Season 3 on iHeart Radio, on the Black Effect Network. I want to thank you for making this podcast number 40 or 50 of entrepreneurship podcasts in America on Apple Podcasts and top 250 on every continent in the world, top 1% of all podcasts in the world. And today I have a special episode because, as you know, I often do these podcasts solo. So when I bring a guest on, they are truly special; they are signature. And this guest is more special than special because he checks a lot of boxes that I've never covered from a principle in these spaces. I talk about investments, I talk about venture capital, I talk about innovation and innovators, I talk about artificial intelligence and technology. But I've never brought, other than maybe parallel to this a Michael Milken who would be parallel to these areas, I've never brought a leader in this space on the podcast. Today that changes. This is a man that I personally admire, respect, and I like as a human. There are private conversations he and I have had which are precious to me that I cannot share where he showed who he really is to me. Ladies and gentlemen, I introduce you to Reid Hoffman. Let me give you a little background. The first line, by the way, is enough, but I'm gonna go a little further. But the first line just drops the mic. Reid Hoffman is the co-founder of LinkedIn. Let that sink in for a minute. We all use it; I just used it last night. He's the co-founder of LinkedIn. A long-time partner at Greylock, which is a huge investment firm based on the West Coast, and one of Silicon Valley's most influential investors, advisors, and voices on the future technology, entrepreneurship, and human progress. He has helped shape how the world thinks about networks, opportunity, scale, and now increasingly AI, which we're going to get into. Today through his investing, writing, and his podcast, *Possible*, he continues to ask one of the biggest questions of our time: how do we build a future that works better for more people? He's also old school. I got at the end of last year a vinyl album set from Reid that he produced himself as his self-made gift to people he cares about. So this is more of a conversation about Silicon Valley than it is an interview. It is a conversation about the future, it is a conversation about what's important, and I want to welcome my friend Reid to Money and Wealth. It is an honor to be here, my friend. And let's get into this because your time is limited and important. By the way, is there anything I left out on the substance of who you are? Because there's so many things you've done. Is there anything I left out that you thought, that you think is really important, Reid? Nothing that we wouldn't get into in our normal discussion of AI, you know. I also, you know, like you, occasionally write books, you know most recently *Superagency*. That's trying to get people to understand that in the age of AI, you should kind of grasp your own agency through this. And also we as technologists should be building things that help you with your agency. But you know, it's just a, you know, it's this, that, and the other. No, it's not this, that, and the other; it's the "this," the "that," and the "other" from Reid Hoffman. When Reid Hoffman says it, it takes on a whole different level of seriousness. There's something that you've done, which Van Jones and I—who I know you're very close with—Van Jones and I have launched something called Hope AI and we're co-chairing that. I'm honored to work with Van on that, and Van brags about you behind your back, Reid. There's something that Van and I have noticed about you that is unique amongst technologists that I would like to get into as a quick side project, this conversation, before we get in the meat of this, and that is this: you have a humanity to you that you inject into your business conversation. You have an EQ that seems to match your IQ. And one of the things I've noticed about geniuses in Silicon Valley—not all of them, but many of them I have met—have a blind spot called people. And the interesting thing is they don't see it. They think that because they're a genius of left-brain technology that they're a genius in everything else, and they make assumptions about people which are entirely incorrect. That makes the strategy that they're pursuing not harmful, but just less effective than it can be. And I'm just shocked that folks who don't know—like in my community, when you don't know what you don't know, you know you don't know it. I mean, you may have a little bravado about it, but when somebody comes to you and says, "Hey look, let me tell you about financial literacy, let me tell you about whatever," at least my community, meaning underserved communities, they're willing to step back and go: "Okay, if you know better than me, let me listen on this topic." I've just not found that among some very successful technologists—that they don't see that blind spot of people. They just keep talking and burying themselves deeper and deeper in a completely left-brain approach to something that involves: hello, people. What do you make of what I just said, Reid, and how is it that you're bringing a unique approach of humanity and genius to technology, and how's that affected how you move? So, you know, I think it is very important. I think one of the things that I kind of describe myself, it's a little geeky, as a techno-humanist. Which is the importance of technology is how we amplify our humanity. And people usually experience new technology as the new alien thing. Like when cars were first introduced, they felt alien; now they feel natural. Electricity, you know, glasses, the whole thing. Fire, I'm sure. And so, the important thing in what we're doing is we're building it for human beings as individuals, as groups, as societies, as humanity. And of course most technologists get into technology because they're like, "Oh, I can control this universe myself, and I can be the god of this universe and I can do everything, and I don't have to interface with these bothersome other people" and all that kind of thing. And so, I think that's one of the reasons why you find so many technologists being, you know, kind of socially awkward, frequently dismissive of other people because part of the thing that moves them into it is, "I am the Ayn Randian superhuman, blah blah blah blah blah." And I think that actually, in fact, the important thing is like look, you're really talented, you can build these really amazing things, but the importance of life is us going through it together, and technology is only good when it helps a lot of people, right. And so, that's the thing. And so one of the things that when I teach classes at like YC and other places in my spare time, it's what I ask entrepreneurs is: what's your theory of the human nature and the human condition, and how is your work going to improve what that human condition is and why is it that it plays out that way? It's fine if it's like, "Look, we're just providing a much cheaper thing that people really want," well that's an improvement, right. You know, we provide jobs and everything else, that's good, because business itself broadly is good with providing things that people want to buy and employment. Now of course it's occasionally bad like you're selling addictive substances that ruin people's lives; you're like, eh, not good for that, right. Anyway, but I think that's it—it's that humanist perspective. And part of the reason I wrote *Superagency* and some of the other writing is actually not just for everyone to think about like "how do I embrace my own human agency in the age of AI," but also for technologists to think about when I'm building AI, how do I enhance human agency. Yes, yes, yes. Let's back up and go deeper on the human side for one second and then come forward. Because I really want the audience to understand you in your humanity and how deep that goes. You know, I keep saying that phrase my father-in-law would say which is: we're all angels with dirty faces. That a saint is a sinner that got up. There's no perfect people out here. And so when people criticize anybody I say I like what that person's doing much better than what you're not doing. People criticize me, I like what I'm doing better than what you're not doing. Question on the humanity side: there's something I witnessed that I cannot share with the audience because it's private, but it said everything about you to me. People will stand up for you until it's inconvenient for them. People will back you until it costs them money. People will say nice things until it creates a time commitment for them where they're not doing something that they selfishly want to do. We were at a place that we can't talk about, and every time I've approached you at this place you've taken time. This is before I was sort of a known entity if you want to call it that. I spoke there but I wasn't necessarily a member of the club back then, and you still would take the time and talk and have a conversation which I respected. And we were talking, I was talking to a certain technologist, an absolute genius by any stretch of the imagination—everybody would know the name. And you saw the conversation getting interesting and going a little off the rails, and I was trying to keep it on the rails. But I was being dismissed. Sorry, there was an attempt, I'm sorry, to dismiss me. I wasn't going to let that happen. But maybe I might have sort of a Compton approach to this like: "What you just say to me?" Right. And you stepped in and were gracious and kind. Van Jones tried to do something and that didn't work. And you used your credibility to mediate this; you spent about 10 or 15 minutes in this conversation and then you pivoted it away, caused a gracious exit for everybody, and you walked off together with me to lunch, which sent a message. Why did you do that, Reid? Why did you lean in to help somebody you basically didn't know, and why don't more leaders do just that? Well, I think the short answer is you have to think about like who are you and why do you think you're a good person. And that means because you stand for things and you do things, and you do the right things, and you do the right things when it's not convenient. And so of course the very powerful person that you're gesturing at could have thought worse of me from my stepping up and saying, "No, no, John's right on the stuff and you should pay attention to him and this is important." And it didn't even occur to me to behave any other different way because, you know, part of what you try to do is you say: look, part of doing the right thing is not just about you but is about the mission, about what kind of things you're doing, about what kind of things that happen. And part of the reason I've loved your mission and your work and the organization is because the world—not just the community of bringing people into the middle class and prosperity, but the world—is better off by realizing the talent contributing to society, to industry and so forth. That really matters, and you have one of the important, smart approaches to this. And so the fact that you were being—I saw the dismissal which was very rude. And I was like no, no, no, this shall not stand. Right, and so it was literally a reflex to stand in. And you know, a little bit of it was treating people right, e.g., you, but also it was like: no, no, this mission, this mission needs to be supported. Yeah. It's really interesting. So first of all, publicly on-air, thank you. And that person was not going to get away with dismissing me, but I would have handled it in a way that was less elegant and less productive than you handled it. I might have won the battle but I might have lost the war in the context of the environment we were in. I would have stepped in mess and not over it. And I tell people always: never make an emotional decision. Whenever you make an emotional decision, it's a bad one. And I would have done both: I would have stepped in it and been emotional because they pricked a core nerve of decency and dignity with me. And you were just saying like, "Read John's research, like just read his, just take some time and read his, you'll..." and then it was complete dismissal. And I've learned that taught me some lessons about: you can be the smartest person on the planet, you can be the most well-prepared, you could have done everything right and still not get a seat at the table. And you just can't give up. You just gotta take "no" for vitamins. You can't get all wrapped up in how fair the world is because the world is often unfair. And you gotta wait for God's grace to step in and save good people from themselves often. And as I said, no one's perfect, but I think that you're going to be constantly visited by a measure of God's grace because of things like that that you did that no one would... if I didn't tell that story no one would ever know it. I mean no one. It was three people that witnessed this. So I just wanted to personally on-air give you respect. This is why I've come to you from time to time with counsel; I know you shoot straight. This is why I asked you to serve on the AI Ethics Council which you agreed to do, by the way, which is co-chaired by Sam Altman and myself. Van Jones is on that council along with Dr. Bernice A. King and others. And this is why I wanted you on the podcast, because I think we're sitting at the door of fundamental systemic, organizational, and human change not seen since the dawning of Homo sapiens, maybe. And there's no roadmap for this. And so I'd like to do something I don't often do. I'm going to tee up a couple thoughts and then I want to shut up and let you just go. By the way, how many different jobs do you have, Reid? I mean, I've lost count. I've lost count. So you co-founded LinkedIn, which I want—that's one of my first questions is why, how, because that's just brilliant. You co-founded LinkedIn, which is enough by itself. And that was—was that through Greylock or is that a separate—? That was just me. Me as an entrepreneur. Okay, so you co-founded and then are there other major companies like that you co-founded? Well, I was part of the founding team of PayPal. I was on the board of directors, a founding member of the board of directors, and then later executive COO, EVP, etc. I've actually recently co-founded an AI company for trying to cure cancer, you know, small things. You and my friend Michael Milken, who is the biggest funder for prostate cancer in the world, thank you. Exactly, so Manus AI is that one with Siddhartha Mukherjee. But then, you know, investments in things like Facebook, Airbnb, etc. I led the first commercial round in OpenAI, speaking of Sam Altman, and was on the board of OpenAI for a while. The range of stuff: write books, do podcasts— You're on the board of some important companies also, aren't you? Yeah, Microsoft. Many people think Microsoft's an important company, so you know, I think so. Yes. Yes. Okay, so Reid, the easy question: why LinkedIn? And how long ago was that by the way? 20 years? 2003, so 23 years. I'm talking about looking around the corner, my God. Okay, why LinkedIn? So I actually, I'll tell a little bit of the fuller story because I think your community might like it. So Peter Thiel, Max Levchin, and I were like "how do we save PayPal?" So we went off and we were doing this, and one of the things we did is we talked about what other ideas we might have if PayPal went up in a big smoke ball. Because at the time we went offsite, it was an exponentiating cost curve with no revenue. So we could tell you the hour that the 200-plus million dollars of capital we'd been invested would all go to zero. On which company? PayPal. PayPal. Wow, which is a great success today. Yes. So we pulled it off but we had one shot. That was really very much like Star Wars, you know, kind of that shot on the Death Star. It was like we got one shot and it's gonna work or not. And it worked, fortunately. Now, part of what I had sketched out was part of the whole world of the internet coming isn't just a way of information flowing and getting entertainment and streaming like we are doing right now, but it's also a role for various forms of social connection. One of the ones that really matters is that in work, life is a team sport, not a solo sport. It's like you go through with your team; your team helps you in various ways, not just advice but opening the door to opportunities. Like whenever I'm trying to hire, the very first thing I do is ping people I know and say, "Do you know someone who might be the right person for this?" And it's like, the internet can do all this. And so I believed a few things that 23 years ago were considered anathema, like: everyone will have a public professional identity; everyone will have a network that is their individual network, not their company's network. The network that helps them—they may be deploying it to help their company right now, but it is their individual network, and I can get this started. Now when I started it everyone thought I was crazy. Right, like basically it was like, "Wait, this is like..." and I don't know how many of your audience will know like Friendster, but "this is like Friendster but for business." And you're like, it's not Friendster but if that's the only way you're going to talk about me because you're an entrepreneur and you have hustle, great, call me Friendster for business. Right. Because basically all opportunities, whether they're entrepreneurial, whether they're funding, whether they're jobs, whether they're sales, they basically are attached to people. I mean, we've been on MySpace for business; that is another way to think about it. Exactly. Right. And so it was like, let's get that started because facilitating people's ability... because like for example, one of the things you get to is currently kids graduating from college are like, "Oh shit, the job market sucks, what do I do, how do I get internships?" And the answer is sometimes by the way, like, your uncle knows somebody. Yes, and how do you know where that could help? And that was the kind of thing that LinkedIn was designed to discover, because it's connect to anybody who knows you well enough to recommend you on a case-by-case basis to someone else. Doesn't have to be they've worked with you, doesn't have to be they were your teacher or professor, doesn't have to be they were your boss or coworker. It's like: "Oh no, this person's smart; they're hard-working." And that is a huge difference from people who are knocking at the front door, because the people knocking at the front door, you don't know anything about them. Right, they could be crazy; you just don't know. And so that gives you a huge edge up on interesting opportunities, and that was the theory of it. It said the internet and the world should work this way, which is fundamentally everything I do: how should the world actually work, can I help it work that way? And LinkedIn is that. So that was the original idea in the, you know, now 23 years of work to get to where it is. So let me go deeper on that because I think what you're talking about is organized relationship capital. And relationship capital gets you and I in certain rooms. You more than me, at least in the past. I'm in the rooms now, but by the grace of God. And you've organized it into a system and a structure that provided gates of access and then organization of that human capital in a way that advanced your agenda or deepened that relationship. I mean, it's the first time that I know of in the world where it was like AI for relationship capital. In other words, it was so revolutionary 20 years ago and it's still to this day... it is my number one go-to for professional relationship management. You did once again did this in your personal life. I just so happen to think about as you're talking: I have this new book out, *Capitalism for All*, which by the way is number 24 on top 25 for *USA Today* bestseller list as of yesterday, and you're an endorser in the book. I mean, I just thought about that while we were talking, that here's another example. And why is this relevant? I'm meeting with the Federal Reserve Chairman Powell two days ago in DC and he says, "I'm beginning to tell about my work," and he says, "I know who you are. And by the way, your book's on my desk and I will read it, and I've never seen so many prominent endorsements on a book ever." And he mentioned a few names and you were one of them. So here is a Federal Reserve Chairman, the most powerful man in the world on money, talking about Reid Hoffman. And that gave me imbued credibility, very much like you've structured organizationally with LinkedIn. Let's get into... and there's no time, audience, for all my questions, just to be clear. I'm going to just open the door for a couple of them and then literally just let Reid riff and we can go as deep as he wants to. So AI will create more opportunity than it destroys; that is my belief over time, especially for working people who are not in Silicon Valley. But in the short term, I think it's going to bleed jobs. I think it's going to create an enormous job disruption, and I don't think the country's ready for the disruption. There's nobody planning for it, designing it, cushioning for it, which is shocking to me. But there's a lot of leaders in the Valley who—and I just disagree with them—don't believe there's going to be job disruption. Sorry, rephrase that: they think there's no future for jobs. That's what they think. And I just disagree fundamentally with that, if nothing else than half of all subscriptions to Silicon Valley apps are people, not companies, who need a job to pay for the subscription. So it's a circular firing squad. Even if it's B to B (business to business), that B to B ultimately is B to C (business to consumer), and that consumer, the customer, needs a job. As the economy grows, well, you may grow it with a new piece of technology, but that's going to need something to work on it, update it, problem-solve it, customer engage with it. Well, hello, those are people. So I think in the short term we're going to have a job crisis. That's my view; you may disagree. But how are you thinking about all this? Because my opinion doesn't matter; this is an area where you are... if we're talking about financial literacy, you defer to me; technology, I'm deferring to you. So feel free on-air to disagree with me. You're advising a 25-year-old with talent but no connections. What would you tell them to master right now to stay relevant in the AI economy, and what's your general view about AI, the future, and jobs—positive or negative? So medium-long term positive, short-term turmoil and chaos. And part of turmoil and chaos and transformation will mean a lot of dislocation. Exactly how and what speed, I think it's very hard to predict exactly. Like, one of the things I've been using as kind of a canary in the coal mine has been what's going to happen with customer service jobs. Because if you roughly look at any job that you're trying to get a human being to act like a robot, e.g., follow a script, the AI's going to do it so much better. I actually think that before too long, maybe even next year, people will start going, "Oh, put the robot on, please," because the robot's better than the human being for solving my customer service problem. Because what you have is you have someone typing into a computer trying to follow the script, and an AI is much better at that. So there will be ones that will be massive reductions in the number of human seats for the jobs, and customer service is one of them. I think there's others. It doesn't mean all customer service jobs go away—there's the architecture of the experience and all—but it will really go down. So there will be some of those and there will be real big dislocations. On the other hand, for example, one of the classic Silicon Valley ones is because AI is so good at coding, software engineer is going to go away. And I completely disagree with this point of view. I don't think there's even going to be a dip because what there is is this thing called Jevons Paradox, which is like there's infinite demand for electricity at a certain price. And similarly, I think there's infinite demand for software engineering at a certain price. And so you make software engineering a 100x more productive; people just want it. Now, the thing that statement obscures is I actually think salaries for software engineers are going to get wonky. The really good ones will get really expensive, like, will continue to like a power law, and most of them will have a little bit of a decrease in salary—it will be a little bit less. So it will be a wage effect; it won't be a job effect here. But it will be a wage effect. That's another of the kind of turmoil that you get in the interim as these things are sorting out. And so I think that there will be a lot of different... like all of a sudden people out of work, all of a sudden the jobs are paying less, etc., of a bunch of the things that people have historically thought were valuable jobs. Like, one of the reasons why a lot of the American middle class goes and gets law degrees is because they go, "Oh, I can go be a lawyer and lawyers are well paid." And I think that's one of the ones that's going to have a huge sortal. It will be similar to software engineers which is: I actually don't think it's going to go away as a job, I just think that suddenly the wage effect is going to be substantial. Which by the way I think is productive for society ultimately because I think we want economics going and building things and stuff that we can sell to people and sell overseas and all the rest of it. But for internal and community and economic path dynamics, I think it'll be real. And so I'm kind of with you on there's a bunch of stuff coming and don't know exactly. Like for example, people who I deeply respect like Dario Amodei last year said it's going to be a white-collar bloodbath within three years. I disagree with that; I don't think it's going to play that way. But by the way, the other thing you're going to see on AI, just to be clear, is like from here for the next number of years, anything that happens is going to be blamed on AI. So you're going to have a company going, "Oh, I'm doing layoffs," and as opposed to "Well, we've sucked at hiring or our business isn't working and so we're doing layoffs," it's going to be "AI, because we're productive." Right, even though in the ones that have announced that so far, I went and looked at them because I was curious if this was the canary in the coal mine and jobs were starting to change. I went and looked at it and was like: no, AI has nothing to do with it. It's literally just they're trying to posture their business misplanning or business misfortune as a strength because we're using AI. But it will start happening where there will be AI that will have that impact. So I'm kind of in a long-term bullish but short-term... And the advice per your question is: go really start using AI. This is one of the reasons why, like, education institutions need to not ban ChatGPT, etc. They need to be saying "use it." Because what's going to happen in the job transition is that companies are going to need AI-native talent. And the current talent, call it 35 and up, is going to be slow adopters; the majority of them are going to be very slow adopters. So you're going to need new talent that knows what they're doing with AI. So there's the small percentage of current people who are beginning to do it, but you can almost already tell. Like one of the conversations that I frequently have with people is: "How are you using AI?" And if their answer is, "Well, I occasionally use ChatGPT as a replacement for my Google search," you're like: fine, that's not really using it. Right? Like the record that I sent you because this is part of me trying to drive myself: it is all AI-generated music. And I was like, well, this is something that people can do now. Like I, who you would pay not to sing—musically incompetent to the extreme—can create a pretty entertaining record of Christmas songs. And sure, I'm not trying to compete with Taylor Swift; I'm not trying to make a new Beatles thing. I'm just trying to do something else. And all of that is available now. And what young people should be doing is: I am generation AI and I can help bring AI to your company. Yes, yes, yes. And to be clearer about what he did at Christmas, he went both old school and new school. He took old school vinyl and took it through a process that would have required a record label, production company, production house, engineers, music producers, writers, all this stuff. And he created an album, it appears or it sounds like from what he's saying now, which by the way looked completely professional and sounds good. He created it with AI. Yes. Which goes to the point that I think that you'll be able to create a million-dollar company with one person that will cost you literally, at most, a few thousand dollars to stand up. Whereas that would have cost a half a million dollars and required a dozen people in the past. So I think that's the example I just heard there. So if you're listening to this and you think you're the average person—you have ambition, you have talent, you have gift—average person, and you say: "Okay, this sounds great for tech people, but what does Reid say about how this relates to me?" And Reid, I would say to people, and you tell me if I'm wrong here: if you're a lawyer, you want to be a lawyer with an AI certification; if you're an accountant, you want to be an accountant with an accounting certification. You want to be on the cutting edge, not the bleeding edge, because this here is not optional. I mean, a lot of these companies Reid's mentioning—that again will name nameless—they laid off people and announced an AI initiative. They didn't know in many cases what AI was; they just knew that their competitor just laid off X number of people and announced AI, and they don't want to be caught with their pants down in the middle of a storm. And so they decided to do what their partners were doing, their competitors were doing, just a little bit more aggressive to send a signal to the market that they were futurist, when they in reality are using AI themselves for a Google search, probably. So a lot of people don't know where this is going, don't even know what it is yet; they just know that it is the future. That's my sense of a lot of what's going on now—a bit of a mess. What do you tell the person in the middle of this storm who's not a technologist? Yep. So it's a little... so AI is the tool that amplifies any work that involves information or language. Right. So if you touch information or language at all in what you're planning on doing... so like for example, even the steel manufacturing plant has meetings, has a supply chain, does financial analysis, all the rest, even if they're manufacturing steel. Right. So if you have that, you need to be on top of it. Now, you don't have to be a builder, you don't have to be a coder. Matter of fact, part of what's happening is like, I actually think everyone's going to have a coding assistant agent in N years, because the AI agent is going to be our coder for us. And so, but that's the reason why you should engage. And by the way, all of these AI products have substantive free versions. ChatGPT, Copilot, etc., they all have substantive free versions; you don't need to pay for them, just start using them. And by the way, using them is not like: "Okay, what was the score on last night's game?" or "What was the analysis of which plays really mattered?" Right, you can do that too, that's awesome. But in the things that matter to you, like for example if you say: "Hey, I'd like to figure out what... I'd like to do a financial analysis on my scenario planning of where my risks are in my economics and doing stuff and what kind of money I need to make," AI is great for helping you with that. Like, you can literally go to any major frontier model and say: "Make me a spreadsheet that is my personal P&L, and I want to include scenario planning for major risks, and I want you to ask me about... walk me through what kind of risks I should consider, and I want you to give me a personal financial analysis of this." They can all do that. And of course everyone should do that, yes. Right, because that's part of how you approach your financial life with intelligence, risk mitigation, and know where to put your efforts. The legal thing is particularly interesting. I had another "aha" recently. In 40 years I've not sued or been sued by anybody. It's all worked out because everybody I've done business with, I pre-screened; they're decent. I got into a shotgun marriage on a business deal, meaning that the money was the motivation, and I met the person who was presenting the money—was buying one of my companies. Anyway, I am now end up in a legal brouhaha with this situation to try to exit, which is fine. And I've spent, you know, six figures just trying to defend the truth. I mean, the truth is right there; it's obvious. But my naivete was, "Well, this is quick because this is so obvious." But no, you've got to go through the process of making the obvious materially relevant to the court, and that requires a process. That requires, at the moment, a lot of legal wrangling by the hour. And so truth alone is not enough. And in this new world, I see that barrier lowering from what I'm spending—what I've spent already, which is six figures, and I'm happy to do it, luckily I can. I see that lowering to five figures and maybe four, so you're providing a lot of support to your legal assistant, or your legal assistant's providing support for... in most cases, a really good journeyman attorney who's processing. Am I on the right track here? 1,000 percent. And by the way, I think it's everything from: "I can't afford anything whatsoever, I get some help from AI" to "I can now pay somebody—this is the person with AI—to provide high-quality legal for small amounts of money" because they can do it in a really efficient way. Just like, for example, tax filing is going to completely change because: "Oh, I can file your taxes for an hour's worth of work." You know, working on these agents even when they're pretty complicated. Yeah. And I can do a better job than I could before when I was spending 20 hours. Let's get into some final really hard-hitting questions. This has been fabulous. I mean, just the piece you gave about the three assistants that everybody should have, I think is mind-blowing. And as much as I think about this, I hadn't thought about that. It's really quite... that's inspiring by itself. Will this technology increase or decrease connectedness or disconnectedness? Wealth or the lack thereof? That's... and I know that a lot of this depends, but what's your assessment? What's your assessment? Number two, what are jobs going to look like in the future? Somebody listening to this, what kind of jobs do you think they should be preparing themselves for? Number three, if you're an entrepreneur or a business person listening to this, does this lower the barrier or increase it, and what are some opportunities you suggest for them? I have a larger question after this, but these are three practical ones. Yep. So I'll go kind of brass tacks and quickly. All of these are questions we could spend the whole hour on, so I'm just going to kind of throw it. So I think the initial impact of AI will be to decrease connectedness, in part because you're spending more time with AI. The AI has various features that other human beings don't have, like it's always trying to please you and agree with you and so forth. And so I think the initial thing will be a dip there. I think that's a problem; I think we want to construct AI to increase human connectedness. It's one of the companies I co-founded called Inflection AI, which is a chatbot called Pi, you know, pun intended but Personal Intelligence. And that's to have high EQ and enhance connectedness, but I think that's going to be one of the challenges. I think it will create a bunch of wealth opportunities, but it's kind of like an earthquake on the whole current system. So for example, where will be the economic opportunities will be very different 5 to 10 years from now than they are today. And kind of, how does that play out? And so, and that's part of the reason my general advice is: learn and be AI native, yes, because then whatever the opportunities, you might be in a good position to go towards one of the good economic opportunities. And so I think that's, you know, fundamental to it. And I think a lot of people hate that because they like stability; they like being able to predict, you know, a good economic future. And I understand and I appreciate, but it's similar to the industrial revolution which is, all of a sudden we're moving from agriculture into the cities and doing stuff. And by the way, there's a whole bunch of challenges around that, but it's what creates the entire future life. So I think that's the wealth side. Now for jobs, it's adjunct to the wealth which is: look, I think there will be a number of jobs. I think there will be new jobs created, but basically, within a small N number of years, I think it's kind of like if the job involves language or information at all and you're not using AI, that's a little bit like you show up going, "You know, I'm offering my horse for transportation of things in the age of the automobile and the railroad." Right. So it's like, you need to be more substantive on it. Now on entrepreneurship, the short answer is it'll both increase lower... increase capability for doing it and increase challenge. It's like both sides. Partially because you'll have the entrepreneurial advisor just like the kind of legal and medical advisors. You'll be able to parse a bunch of things; you'll be able to do financial analysis; you'll be able to have software coding and be able to launch certain kinds of products much more easily, etc. The increase of challenge is everyone else gets the same thing. So for example, if you think the App Store just as a microcosm—the App Store is full and busy today; it's going to have a thousand times that in a year or two. Yes. So how do you break through the noise and what do you do? And the competition is intense and all the rest. So it both will create... make entrepreneurship easier because it'll bring certain resources to bear, but it makes it easier for everybody, which means the competition goes way, way up. Yes. I used to be impressed with certain letters that came to me that had certain language in it, or certain insights about me in the letter inviting me to speak or whatever. I'm not impressed anymore because now they could just go to AI and have the AI write you that letter, and I can tell an AI-assisted letter when it comes to me. So that's that competition raising the stakes, the table stakes on what competency looks like. What is a basic intro... whatever it comes to you. A business plan used to be a... I mean oh my God, this person has a business plan. Now everyone will have a business plan and it will look really sharp. Right. So the differentiator will be different. Okay, I have 35 questions and I'm throwing them all out because—and I've never had 35 questions for anybody, Reid, ever ever ever ever ever—that's how interesting I think you are. Maybe we'll do another podcast. But in the remaining moments, let's just throw the doors open. You're giving a fireside chat with America and the world. America to be... to try to go from 250 years of success and dominance to another 50 years. You're giving also discussion to the world about the future. You're a futurist conversation. You got the global media presence, everybody's listening to you, you got 5 or 10 minutes so you got to be blunt. You're a futurist, you got to be blunt. You're trying to be optimistic but you have to be practical too. What do you tell a world that's both in play right now and a world waiting to be born? That some lady who's pregnant with a three-month-old... she's three months pregnant; there's young people looking towards the future. There's those elderly trying to figure out "can they retire?" There's people in the job market right now who are making six figures trying to figure out how disruptive it's going to be. People who are both fearful and hopeful. You're the futurist; what do you say? So the short answer is we're heading into a combination of an earthquake and a tsunami. It's going to shake everything up. That's going to be scary; it's going to have some negative adverse impacts across a number of different things. And you know, your instinct will be: "Oh, let's stop it, let's slow it down," etc. But the problem is, it's a competitive human scope. So for example, those countries which rejected the industrial revolution basically had centuries of poverty and being colonized by other countries that did the industrial revolution. Mmm. So you have to embrace it. It does mean, by the way, just like the industrial revolution which you know in its first decades lowered the average lifespan by seven years. Right. These are not small costs as they kind of go into it. But you have to go into it for future generations. Now that being said, that doesn't mean that we want to just blindly accept every challenge and negative effect that can happen. We should try to steer it towards navigating the transition, navigating the transformation as humanly as possible. And so for example, part of what I've been telling governments for almost a decade now is: well, get these assistants—the medical, the legal, the educational—there. Be working with: how do we make sure that new jobs are being created at a rate and that we're facilitating people to find, learn, and do those new jobs? By the way, AI can help you with all those, so make AI part of the solution. So I think we can mitigate the transformation in part because we can use AI to help with the transition. But it's like: buckle up, it's going to be a taxing ride, and that's true for everybody. It's weirdly less true... like 70% of our economy is service economy, and the service economy is going to be the tail end of this. Right? The whole waiter being replaced by a robot is just stupid at the moment because the robots... it'll be a while before they're cheap enough for that and good enough. Right, so like there's a whole bunch that's like, "Oh, that's fine." But the core engine of the economy which then pays for all the service jobs—because someone comes in and says, "Oh well, we sell these products around the world and so forth, and so we have a bunch of money that comes into our company and we can hire accountants and we go to restaurants"—that is going to be transformed in some interesting ways. And it's in our interest to do it, in our interest to do it quickly, but also in our interest to try to do it as humanly as possible, which means: how do we help with the transitions? So the question for people is: how do you help yourself, and how do we help each other in navigating this transformation? Because the future, by the way, I think it will be... will be a greater impact on human society to the positive than the industrial revolution was. And by the way, to some degree all of our modern prosperity—a middle class versus a peasant or a serf class, ability to do public education at a broad franchise, the ability to have public health and not just limited to the uber-wealthy—all of that comes from the industrial revolution. Yes. We are about to see another magnification of that. It's totally worth it, but it's going to be a painful ride. That was brilliant. And I know we are running out of time; we've run out of time. We're going to wrap this up and maybe come back, if you wouldn't mind, for a part two. I'd love it. It strikes me... it struck me as you were talking, Reid—again, we just don't have time for this conversation, but it's mind-blowing. I never thought about this before. If Africa... people, you know, I don't deal with... I'm not bothered by racism or discrimination. As Ambassador Young says, "I don't pay any attention to discrimination because I don't think anybody is any better than me." But the racism or the discrimination... the view of certain people had in the 1500s and 1600s of Africa meant that they didn't take them seriously as a competitive intellectual force that could root industrialization. They just saw them as a resource base that they could extract from for industrialization, including people who had talents of agriculture or their agricultural geniuses, and they brought them to the southern states where the soil was very similar—more humid and hot—and needed the genius of Africans to reignite it. But we didn't go back from the agricultural age and say, "Hey, thanks for that, there's an industrial age now and we want to invest in you." So Africa just sort of missed industrialization; it missed the technology age; it missed a lot of the information age. And it's now trying to catch up on the back end. And I think it can, but it struck me—it hadn't hit me before—that literally there's like at least two ages that jumped over Africa. And when you said that there's nations who did not embrace industrialization or whatever the thing was to look at them now, they got left behind—well, that's Africa. That's a lot of other places by the way, too. And as Michael Milken would say, "Intelligence is equally distributed, opportunity is not." Yes. And this has a chance to sort of level that playing field, if you will. It's what hit me. I hope I got that right. 100 percent. This has been fascinating, and so many things going on off... going off in my head about why this conversation needs to be the conversation for a while. Thank you, Reid Hoffman. Is there anything that you want the world to know, to hear, to think about, to remember about you personally, professionally, your passions, and/or advice you have for them for the future as we wrap up? Well, I think that the AI revolution's going to happen. The tsunami is going, and you want to get your—whether it's a surfboard, a sailing boat, or whatever, you know, kiteboard—you want to get it out and start working on it. And it's the... yeah exactly, start paddling. It's the most important thing to do. And so, you know, part of what I'm spending my time doing—writing books, you know, *Superagency*, *Impromptu*, *Startup of You*, etc., and doing podcasts like this—is trying to help people see that so that they can grab their own... to maximize the chance that you can grab your own agency in the tsunami. I have a bonus question that I promise will be the last one; it just hit me. I'm on the board of Clark Atlanta University and they're embracing AI and financial literacy and artificial intelligence literacy, which is great. So smart. It... whereas up until now you've had one or two careers for your whole life, you had a competency and that lasted you for 50 years in that competency. Maybe you had two careers; maybe you shifted. I believe, again, on-air disagree with me, I love constructive friction. I believe that very much like when I used to send a document to somebody in the past, they'd say, "That's an impressive document." Now, no one even comments on the document. They assume the document's going to be impressive. Now they're basically saying, "You're an impressive person. I'm going to deal with you because of who you are." Of course, the document is supposed to be at this level. That's a new expectation because of AI and all the things that allow you to prepare a great document. So I think that you're going to have careers that might be—you might have 5, 6, 8 different careers as a graduate. It is no longer the thing being the thing, because AI can help you m- can help master the thing. You need to be the leader on top of the thing, the differentiator on top of the thing that moves the thing. And so you might end up having 5, 6, 8 different careers because you're bringing you as a differentiator to the thing. The thing itself has become less—not unimportant, but it is in itself intelligent. Okay. Did that make any sense at all? Yes, look. So the short answer is the future jobs are that you are broadly being the conductor of AI agents as an orchestra. Now there will be some stuff that you still do yourself, but it's that, and that does mean that as opposed to a job, it's a sequence of jobs. And so, like my very first book, "The Startup of You," which is we all have to embrace the entrepreneurial side. It doesn't mean that we have to create companies, but we have to think of ourselves as the skillset of entrepreneurs. That's what's going to happen and so, you know, as opposed to a career escalator, you're going to have a career jungle gym, and the AI will help you with the jungle gym. I'm glad I—I'm glad I asked the last bonus question. Ladies and gentlemen, this is a genius. This is the man, an innovator, a—a legend in our time, Reid Hoffman. When you go to your LinkedIn, thank him. Reid, thank you for joining us on Money and Wealth on the Black Effect Network on iHeart Radio. This has been a transformational interview, and Reid has said we will be back for more. Awesome.

Surviving Twitter's Growing Pains: Ex-CEO Dick Costolo

Sequoia Capital(180.0K) ·68:18 ·880 views · 2026-05-07
Dick Costolo (Former CEO, Twitter)
Scaling a hyper-growth organization requires shifting from group consensus to a "Bias to Yes" culture, where decision-making is pushed down the stack and managers prioritize providing radical context over implementing rigid processes.
TL;DR Scaling a hyper-growth organization requires shifting from group consensus to a "Bias to Yes" culture, where decision-making is pushed down the stack and managers prioritize providing radical context over implementing rigid processes. The "Bias to Yes" Principle: To...

TL;DR

Scaling a hyper-growth organization requires shifting from group consensus to a "Bias to Yes" culture, where decision-making is pushed down the stack and managers prioritize providing radical context over implementing rigid processes.

Engineering Velocity and Decision Architecture

  • The "Bias to Yes" Principle: To prevent organizational "barnacles," Costolo implemented a rule where only a direct manager can say "no" to an initiative.
      • Other departments (Legal, Marketing, Security) cannot stall projects; they can only advise.
      • This prevents the "ask your mom, then ask 14 other people" culture that slows down innovation.
  • Eliminating Group Decision-Making: Costolo moved Twitter away from being a "collective" where decisions were reached methodically through diplomacy.
      • Authority was pushed "down the stack" to engineers and individual contributors.
      • Kiling the "CEO Said" Justification: Costolo discouraged managers from using "Dick said" to end debates, requiring them instead to defend decisions based on merit and data.
  • DRIs Over Process: Costolo argued against solving problems with checklists (which grew to 17 pages at Google).
      • Instead of adding steps to prevent mistakes, he focused on Operating Control and Directly Responsible Individuals (DRIs).
      • Leadership's job is not to prevent every mistake, but to "correct mistakes quickly when they happen."

The "Scale-Up CEO" Management Playbook

  • Management by Walking Around: Costolo would return to the office at 9:30 PM to walk the engineering floors.
      • He used these informal check-ins to find out what was actually being built and would publicly prioritize those projects in all-hands meetings to shift the culture toward late-stage execution.
  • The Steve Jobs "Pixar Trick": Costolo would meet with junior teams (e.g., illustrators or front-end devs) without their managers present.
      • He asked two questions: "What is working?" and "What is not working?"
      • This allowed him to bypass middle-management filters and hear the ground-truth reality of the organization.
  • Radical Context (The Ben Horowitz Rule): A CEO’s primary job is to ensure "everybody understands what you understand."
      • Priorities are useless without context. Employees should understand not just the "what," but the "why" and how it impacts them personally/professionally.

Performance and Talent Management

  • The Struggle to "Fire Fast": Despite the Silicon Valley adage, Costolo admitted he often fired too slowly, usually knowing someone wasn't a fit six weeks before taking action.
  • Eliminating "Trans-firing": To prevent managers from passing poor performers to other teams, Costolo implemented a rule: employees with low performance ratings were prohibited from switching teams until their rating improved.
  • The Art of Tough Feedback: Costolo advised writing down the exact feedback the night before and sticking to it without "fluff."
      • Sitting in Silence: He noted that the best managers deliver the news and then sit in silence, allowing the employee to process and respond rather than filling the air with excuses or apologies.
  • Authenticity and Anxiety: Referencing Hakeem Olajuwon, Costolo argued that being a "role model" is only stressful if your public persona differs from your private one. Effective leadership requires being the same person in all settings.

Strategic Regrets and Reflections

  • The Instagram Miss: Twitter attempted to acquire Instagram for roughly $700 million (mostly stock) shortly before Facebook’s $1 billion deal.
      • Costolo views this as a "checkmate" move by Facebook that boxed Twitter out of the future of rich-media social feeds.
  • The Burden of Public Markets: Costolo expressed that if he could do it over, he would have kept Twitter private longer.
      • The "daily ticker" creates unnecessary employee anxiety and forces a constant trade-off between long-term health and quarterly revenue targets.
  • Content Moderation Realities: He described moderation as a series of impossible judgment calls rather than a set of hard rules, citing the difficulty of distinguishing between "terrorist content" and "newsworthy reporting" (e.g., ISIS videos vs. the New York Post reporting on those videos).

***

Notable Quotes

"It’s not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it’s the job of leadership to help correct them quickly when they happen." — Dick Costolo
"I hate hearing 'Dick said' in the company as a reason for things... It became like this reason for doing things [without context]." — Dick Costolo
"There's nothing more miserable than trying to be someone you're not... If you try to be someone you're not, nobody is fooled." — Dick Costolo
"Make sure everybody understands what you understand." — Dick Costolo (quoting Ben Horowitz)
I just felt like Twitter was such, such a drama. Twitter is like the drama queen of hyper-growth companies. You know, it was like, when I took over as CEO, that year the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two different magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker, and I was like, "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal: no dead birds on magazine covers." You know, low bar. Hey everybody, we have Dick Costolo on the show. He's a rare bird in Silicon Valley these days. He was CEO of Twitter, but he was the third CEO. The first one was Jack Dorsey, who you probably met on the pod a couple weeks ago. The second was Ev Williams, the other founder, and Dick took over as COO and then ultimately as CEO of Twitter. He ran it for five years. It was a really extremely fast-growing organization. They created a category, didn't have a revenue machine, and was famously dysfunctional. I think Dick did a really nice job and he was sort of the adult in the room, and he kind of ran this scale-up CEO masterclass. He talks all about it in this podcast. He talks about increasing velocity of decisions and execution, which is tricky as you scale. He talked about how he managed by walking around. This is something I did; I think it's a really good idea. He talked about how do you give very tough feedback to very tough people? There are some gems in there. Dealing with the press, particularly press that doesn't like you. He talks about focus, and how much focus you really need in the era of AI when you can build stuff so quickly. He talks about how do you manage the forest instead of fighting all the fires. He's got a lot of wisdom in here. He also talks about his regrets, including a whopper: his regret not acquiring Instagram. That one hurt. If you're scaling your 100 employees, you're ripping, growing fast, you've got a lot of growing pains, maybe you feel a little out of control, this episode is for you. Welcome to the show. Thanks for having me. Appreciate it. I want to wind the clock back to Twitter, of course. Of course. I watched a documentary recently. And? Was it the one I was in? Yes. I thought you were really good in it. Oh, thank you. Great. You're great. You were great in the documentary of yourself. You were really, actually you came off as rational and knowing what the hell you were doing. But you're in a long line of interesting CEOs: Jack Dorsey and then Ev Williams and yourself and Jack Dorsey and Parag and Elon. And don't forget, you know, Elon's hired hand, Linda Yaccarino. Okay, of course, of course. You joined as COO. What was the pitch? What was the pitch to me? Yeah, Ev pitched you. I mean, yeah, Ev pitched me. The pitch was, the initial pitch was, "I'm taking paternity leave for a couple weeks. I, Ev Williams, am taking paternity leave. I need someone to come in and like just hold down the fort for a couple weeks while I'm, you know, out." Interim? Yeah, interim. "Would you, would you do that?" But then while we were having that discussion, he's like, "Well, what if it was permanent? You know, what if you were COO?" And I was going to go start another company. So, rewinding the clock a little bit further, I had sold my previous company FeedBurner to Google. It's where I rebumped into the Twitter guys 'cause they were all there from Blogger and getting ready to leave and start Odeo, which became Twitter. Anyway, I'm leaving now. I'm leaving Google a couple years later, and Ev texts me that. I was like, "Nah, I'm gonna go start a company. I'm gonna go start another company again with one of my FeedBurner, one or more of my FeedBurner co-founders." And then Ev said, "What if it was, what if it was permanent? What if you were COO?" And Twitter was already—so it's summer 2009—it had already hit the Ashton Kutcher and Oprah Winfrey thing, you know, fighting for who's going to get a million followers. It was ripping. It was ripping. I can see why you wanted to do it. But zero, zero revenue model. Zero business model. All the articles were sure Twitter's interesting, but they'll never—they don't have a business model and they'll never make money. And I just kind of had the thought, "Man, you don't get an opportunity to go into these, to get on these rocket ships, and when you get the opportunity you should do it." So I did it. So it was famously dysfunctional and chaotic. I assume you knew that going in. Was it much more so than you thought or was it about what the press was saying? It's always both, you know? It was, the things that were different were it was very much a collective. By which I mean, like, decisions were kind of made as a group. It wasn't, "Oh you're responsible for engineering, I don't know, you make it, figure it out and you know, figure out what the right answer is." Or, "Should we, you know, have, should we make retweet a native thing or should it be, you know, just you had to write RT at the time and like rewrite the tweet." I remember that. And there was a discussion about, well, should we just have retweet be a thing you can do in Twitter? And it would be kind of sit around the table and talk about it and decide. The group would decide, you know? So, things moved methodically, as a diplomatic way of putting it. I see. And decisions were reached over a period of time. It was chaotic and then it was also just, we just didn't, it just didn't move very quickly. It didn't move quickly at all. In the documentary it went through the, you know, pushing Ev out and giving you the CEO job. It sounded awful, the whole process. It was awful. What's your side of it? What was going on? What's your side? I don't really have a side of it other than it was awful. Like it's, you know, these things in hindsight are—it happened very, very quickly. It happened super slowly and painfully and you know, I was informed like in the driving rain on the road home by, you know, Ev and Jason that the board had decided that you should be the CEO. But I hadn't been told. And then Bill Campbell called me and was like, "You should just quit," you know? And then I was like, "Why am I quitting?" You know? And anyway it went, and then like the board decided, "Actually no, Ev is going to remain CEO, but you've been great and we're going to vest a bunch of your options and so long." And then, I mean no kidding, over many, many days and weeks. Finally I was like, "Listen, just write up the separation agreement and you know, I'm good. I'm good, like whatever. I'll go find something, I'll go do something else." And then, you know, to make a long story short, the board got back together and was like, "Oh no, no no no no." I remember Bijan Sabet from Spark texted me, "Don't quit." Like, I was like, "I'm not quitting, like okay." You guys told me you wanted me to leave 'cause Ev's gonna stay. Anyway, it was super crazy and there was obviously a ton of stuff going on. Anyway, long story short, they were finally like, "Look, you gotta do it." Anyway, and you know, really long story short, I was like, "All right, I'll stay and do it." Okay. You take over, you're the adult. What did you do initially? It's very tricky, the situation. And I think the current, the existing founders are hanging around, probably looking over your shoulder if not breathing down your neck. Well, Jack was the chairman of the board still. Ev had made Jack chairman of the board when he made himself CEO. Ev's still on the board. So two of the founders, Ev and Jack, are still on the board. And Biz was in the company. And Biz is in the company. And Jason Goldman's in the company running product and, you know, and a bunch of other people. The first thing that I felt, and I sort of foreshadowed this, the first thing that I just felt we had to do is like velocity, velocity, velocity. And that remained a concern of mine throughout my time there: like faster, faster, faster, cadence, cadence, cadence. Like, how can we go faster? Why do things take so long? If this takes six weeks, what would have to be true for it to be two weeks? We did stuff like that constantly 'cause I was always like, "Man, this is like dental surgery." But it was starting from a position of, "We're not going to make—we're not going to sit around the table and vote on things anymore." I remember vividly the status, like, the status line changed from like "What are you doing?" or "What's happening?" to something else. Or maybe it changed to "What's happening?" I don't even remember. I wasn't even involved in it. And one of the front-end engineers, Britt Selvitelle, came into my office and was like, "Hey man what's going on?" I was like, "What are you talking about?" He's like, "The status bar changed to 'What's happening.'" I was like, "Okay." He's like, "I didn't—no one asked me, you know, what I thought about that." And I was like, "Oh man, yeah, we're not doing that anymore. Like, it's gonna start being—there's gonna start being a lot more of that." So that was thing number one. Thing number two was there were a set of operating principles that were written on a whiteboard that Biz had been involved in, either on his own or with other people. But they were like, you know, like sometimes you'll get on a United Airlines flight or whatever and they'll say, "Here at United Airlines we believe in integrity and honesty." I'm like, "Okay, who cares?" Like, that doesn't help me, that tells me nothing about me, it doesn't help me make any decisions, and these things are meaningless. And they're usually weaponized against management by like, "Well, it says right here, anyway." So I was like, "All right, we gotta get rid of these things." They're like, they're not useful, they're costly. Did the founders push back? No, they didn't really. Okay. Good for them. So got rid of those. I remember there were just, like, everything was just kind of like messy. You know, I remember there was this kitchen and there was—it was just kind of a mess and there were like dirty dishes in the sink and everything, and I think I sent out an email. I was like, "This is why the—this is why the fucking site crashes. Like, you may—you may not get it, but like, this—this is why the site crashes 'cause everything's just kind of fucking like, 'Oh well, someone else will clean it up.'" And I was like, "This is ridiculous." And it was just trying—I just tried to get this sort of, "We're not going to do this like, 'Oh well, we'll get 'em next year' stuff anymore." There was a poster on one of the floors that was like, "We'll make better mistakes next time," or something like that, you know? And I was like, "Get that poster out of here." It's not—I don't really care about it, but it's just sort of indicative of, "Oh well, you know," and then the site would crash again and people would go like, "Oh well we'll figure it out, you know, hopefully we won't happen again next year whatever." So it was a lot of cleaning that up and a lot of velocity, velocity, velocity. All any CEO wants to talk about now is speed. In particular, like, companies seem to slow down as they are in hyper-growth. Yeah. Because you get bigger and get more and get less done. Yeah, because barnacles build up on the—or you get like organizational barnacles. You know, so a couple things. Initially when we were smaller, this is sub-200 people, what I did to speed things up was one: we're not going to make decisions as a group. We're going to push decisions down the stack. By the way, I don't have to be like—don't come to me and ask me what you should do about this unless you have no idea and want my opinion. Like, figure it out and don't tell your team what to do, tell them to figure it out and push as many decisions down the stack as you can. And I don't want to hear—you know, I remember I would talk to Jack Dorsey at these dinners we would have from week to week when he was chairman and was CEO of Block down the street from us, and we would get together on Tuesday nights and have dinner. I remember telling him, "God, I hate hearing 'Dick said' in the company as a reason for things." 'Cause what would happen was—let's, I'll give you an example. I remember this example really well. The Android app started taking longer and longer and longer to boot. And so we would have a metrics meeting. I was like, "Hey man, you can see the usage numbers going down almost linearly with the Android boot time going up. People are firing up Twitter and it's taking too long and they're moving on to something else. Like over here on iOS, that doesn't happen. It's gotta take just as long to boot Android as it does iOS." Okay. So two, three nights later, a week later, I'm walking around like one of the engineering floors after hours and this Android engineer comes up to me and he goes, "Hey, can I ask you a question?" I'm like, "Yeah." He goes, "Hey, did you say remove the JSON file from the Android boot system?" I was like, "I didn't. I know what a JSON file is, but I couldn't tell you what's in the Android boot JSON." And he's like, "Okay, 'cause I was told Dick said remove the—" and I was like, "Aha, I think I know what's going on here." Right? And this would happen throughout the company. Some manager or director decides, "Here's a way to solve this problem," and the engineer pushes back against them and they just go, "You know what? Dick said do it." And it became like this reason for doing things. So part of the speed was: no, we're not going to have "so-and-so said." It's you push the decisions down the stack and by the way, if your team makes a decision and you can't defend it to your manager, like, that's—don't go, "Well, so-and-so said." Like, have the engineer talk to the director, whatever. The second thing I did as we got bigger and bigger is I started realizing, "Oh, the organization is slowing down because there are all these fiefdoms in the organization that want—that want like ball control over their stuff." Very common. Very common. So here was how it surfaced. We ran an experiment and it was like, "Oh this is actually really super interesting, what are the hypotheticals about what this experiment would do in production?" The engineer runs through it in an experimentation meeting: "Great, let's go ahead and launch this to, you know, X percent of users and see what happens." One week, two weeks, three weeks go by and like, "Hey where is—" I'm like looking at the app, I'm like, "Where is, you know, where's this engagement experiment we're running?" And I find the engineer and he's like, "Oh, I'm waiting for, you know, Trust and Safety to sign off on the—" I'm like, "What?" He's like, "Oh yeah well, I asked a so-and-so if we were ready to launch and they didn't say no, but they said you have to go get approval from User Security." And I started realizing that when people would try to go do something, they wouldn't be told, "No, you can't do that." They would be told something much, much worse and slower than that: "You need approval from security. You got to get permission from security, you got to ask your mom, go ask these 14 people and if they all say yes..." Okay, everyone's got this problem. How do you fix it? So what I did was, it's very similar to what Bezos used to do at Amazon with Bias to Action. I had—it was from my improv background—I was like, "We're going to have a 'Bias to Yes.'" And Bias to Yes is: only your direct, the person you report to, is allowed to tell you you're not allowed to do that. Or legal can tell you if you do that you'll be fired 'cause it's illegal and a violation of the privacy policy. But no other organization, period, is allowed to tell you you can't do that or you need to go ask these people for permission. Just your direct manager. And that means if you have an idea for an experiment and your manager signs off on it and you have time to do it 'cause you're done with whatever else you're working on, no one can tell you you can't do that. Or, you know, the marketing team's not allowed to tell you you can't use the Mailchimp software we license 'cause that's the marketing team's, right? And so Bias to Yes was: I don't want to hear about something being stalled because organization X said you have to go get approval from organization Y. And that was great. Experiments started flying out. In fact, engineers started going like, "Wait, I don't have to necessarily get Design to build all these wireframes for something. I can just do it myself since we're only launching it to 1% and see if it works." And so that was just great. It worked throughout the organization and I would repeatedly get on stage at all-hands meetings and talk about Bias to Yes and what it means and what it doesn't mean. Doesn't mean you can go stop doing what you're supposed to do 'cause it's priority and do whatever you want to do, 'cause your manager is going to go like, "If you do that I'm gonna fire you 'cause you know, you're being measured on getting things done." Okay. No group decisions, DRIs, and Bias to Action. Anything else that all these CEOs that I work with are like, "We're slowing down, how do we speed up?" Don't solve problems with processes. So, it happens all the time in organizations. Like, some customer problem happens, the customer churns out or the advertiser quits and the CEO goes, "That can never happen again." And so what happens? Some step gets added to some process that's like, "Before you do..." I remember at Google, famously, the launch checklist got to be like 17 pages long or something and Larry was like, "This is insane." I was like, "Yeah, it's 'cause you know, it's like the FDA—never another thalidomide—or the TSA: 'Make sure there's no water bottle on the plane.'" And pretty soon you're not managing to outcomes, you're managing to the processes and you gotta go through nine steps before you—you know, instead of just managing to the outcome of "make sure there's not a bomb on the plane." So the way we did that, the way you do that is you solve problems with DRIs, operating control, and not processes. Like, you should probably only really have a couple organizational processes and everything else should be span of control and decision making. When you do that, you can't—and this was something I had to come to understand and adapt to—when you do that, you can't punish people when things go wrong. You want them to take risks, you want them to be bold, you want them to have this Bias to Action and do things and not go around asking for, tiptoeing around asking for permission. But one of the implications is you can't punish people for making mistakes. And I got to the point where I would tell the team, "It's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it's the job of leadership to correct mistakes quickly when they happen." So if you screw something up, like, by all means tell us so we can help you fix it, but no one should get in trouble for screwing something up or making a mistake.
