And I didn't come to that immediately, but I came to realize that that was an important artifact, if you will, of pushing decisions down the stack and Bias to Yes and we're not going to have lots of processes. Love it. So many of the CEOs I work with—I just was at a—I won't say the company, but here in New York City and spoke at their company meeting. Here's how I'd describe every company: when it's, call it under 50 employees, everybody knows what's going on all the time. When it's over 150 employees, nobody knows what's going on anytime. How did you fix, how did you scale communication, keep people on page, and tell me a little bit about the management by walking around? Yeah, well, I did two things. First of all, I taught this management class at Twitter. It was actually based on Ben Horowitz teaching a management class at Loudcloud. Fascinating. And Ben had in his outline from his management class a statement that I highlighted and underlined and circled three times: "Make sure everybody understands what you understand." So I would always tell my managers that. Like, your job is to make sure everybody understands what the priorities are and what it means for them and their team, how they fit into those priorities, and more importantly for your team, what it means for them personally and professionally if we achieve what we have to achieve. Those kinds of things are great. I remember somebody telling me a story of taking a factory tour at a Tesla shop and seeing some guy who's like, screwing a widget into the car and asking the guy, like, "What are you doing?" And the guy didn't just say "screwing the widget into the car," he said, "Well, if I do this and we're able to build these kinds of cars, and these kinds of cars can become more popular in America..." I mean, the guy understood the story and I thought that was great. And so it's like the analogy: someone asks the bricklayer, "What are you doing?" and the bricklayer says, "Oh, I'm laying bricks," and the other bricklayer says, "I'm building a beautiful cathedral." I love it. Yeah, like you want people to under—I mean, how much more motivating is it to work in a company where you know, "If I'm successful in my work, I understand what the implications are for both me personally and the company." Okay. Priorities and context. Priorities and context. Good news travels fast, bad news travels slow, and as the org gets bigger, it slows down and the signal gets distorted. How do CEOs deal with that? Yeah, I said earlier, it's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it's the job of leadership to help correct them quickly when they happen. So I would always communicate: "You gotta tell leadership if something's screwed up." If your situation is screwed up, or your team or some decision is a mess, you have to communicate that constantly. And then secondly, I would regularly communicate the priorities to the team over and over and over at all-hands meetings. "Here's what the priorities are that we're working on, this is how we're going to measure them, this is why they're important, and this is what they mean for us as a company if we're successful." You always wanted—I always wanted people to understand not just what we had to do, but why it was important to do it. And I think that scaling communication architecture is like one of the most important things in any company. Part of it is never saying "because so-and-so said." That's not context. That doesn't help your team understand context for why some decision was made, or why, or what they should do or how they should go do it. So that combination of pushing decisions down the stack, making sure that everybody understands what you understand—and a lot of that is context—and always communicating problems or issues up the stack as a means of helping get it resolved quickly, were the ways we dealt with that. One last thing that I picked up from Steve, a Steve Jobs trick. I love a Steve Jobs trick. Yeah, so when Steve was managing both Pixar and Apple—so at the time he's CEO of Pixar still, but now he's back in the seat at Apple. They're making like Toy Story 2, and what Steve would do—so he's got one day to come up to speed on what the heck's going on at Pixar—he'd go into the office on Friday and he would say, "All right, today I want to sit down with like, the illustrators." You know, without Lasseter, the director of the movie, John, without Lasseter in the room. "I just want to sit down, me and the illustrators." And he would go, there's like eight or nine or whatever of 'em: "Janet, tell me something that's not working well at Pixar." And then, "Bob, do you agree with that?" You know, taking notes. Like, "Okay, Phyllis, tell me something that's working well at Pixar—not on the movie, not about your job, you know." And at the end of the meeting—45 minute, one hour meeting—he would say, "Okay, here's what I heard. You know, people are having these kinds of issues, this is going really well. I'm going to go talk to John about it and he'll deal with it on Monday. You know, you can follow up with him." So he's not saying, "Oh yeah, that sucks," or "You know, no you're wrong," or whatever. He's taking notes, making sure he hears everything that was said, and then goes and talks to Lasseter about it. I started doing that with teams. You quickly find out if that team knows what the priorities are, is in sync with what you're hearing from the leadership of that team. Some of the leaders of the company hated it and I was like, "Well, now I'm concerned about why you hate it 'cause yeah." But you're not in that meeting doing things like throwing the director under the bus. You're going, "Okay, here's what I heard. I'm going to go talk to my management team about it on Monday and you know, Chris or whoever will follow up with you guys." Let me switch to you on priorities. So all these CEOs I coach, they have their list of priorities—it's January 1st—but oh my God, there's so much opportunity, so much interesting stuff, and this employee has this thing going on. How often did you rejigger those priorities? Were you disciplined about it over the course of whatever that period was? Yeah. You can rejigger them once in a while. What's once in a while? I don't know, like, you know, if you've got four huge priorities for the first four to five months of the year, you can't be—you can't get to February and go, "These two are stupid and we're changing," you know? Once or twice a year you can go, "Actually, let's change that one to this other thing." If you start doing it regularly, people are like, "Well, we can get to work on the direct messaging architecture, but Dick's probably going to change, you know, 50/50 he's going to change." You just can't do stuff like that. By the way, that's another way companies slow down, because people are just like, "Look, I'm not going to bust my ass on this thing when there's a 50% chance Dick's going to throw it out the window in a month." Yes. Let me test something on you. A lot of these hyper-growers—it used to be that there were a lot of one-way doors in the software industry. Stuff took a long freaking time to get out the door even to test, and you had to kind of do things serially. Now you can do so much so fast, and so they're just doing more and it's more in parallel. And a lot of those one-way doors are two-way doors. Is focus overrated now? No. Focus is—I don't think focus is ever overrated. However, the fact that you can do more quickly, and since they're two-way doors, you're like, "Great." I mean, this is the old Jeff Bezos thing: only take a long time to make decisions that are existential, like "we might die as a company if we make the wrong decision." There's fewer and fewer of those. Decisions that can be—like, if we screwed it up, we can go back and do the other one—do those right away. So focus is still important, but it's also true that you should feel like you can do a lot more now with less because you can. There was a point in 2011 when we wanted to try to get Jeff Bezos to join the board of the company. Like, we have an independent seat open, who would be the best possible independent director? Jeff Bezos. So Jack and I go meet with Jeff Bezos. I think it was like at the Allen & Company conference or something like that. And Jeff asks a question about strategy. And Steve Jobs was still alive at the time and Jack said, "Well, Steve says the most important part about being a CEO is focus." "And you should say no to almost everything and it's, in fact, it's the CEO's job to be the editor-in-chief and you should say no to almost everything and do very, very few things." And Bezos looks at Jack and goes, "Oh yeah? Well, I like to do everything!" And he does the big Bezos laugh and he goes, "You know, my team has to talk me out of stuff!" He goes, "You know, there's lots of ways to be successful!" And I remember that like it was yesterday, and he's right. There's many ways to climb the mountain. And I think it's true that today he would say, "Great, I can do even more now. We can still be focused but I can do even more things." I think those things can both be true. Okay, just want to get back to some of your management techniques 'cause I think they were great. The management by walking around, which is a very old school Hewlett Packard concept—it sounds like you didn't do it the same time every week. No. I did it at all hours, and mostly late night. It started because Bill Campbell—for people who don't know who are listening to the podcast, Bill was on the board of Apple and famously coached Larry, Sergey, and Eric at Google. They wrote a book about him called "Trillion Dollar Coach." The Benchmark folks had also brought him into Twitter before I got there. And Bill famously said when I took over as CEO, "You could throw a grenade into Twitter at 6 p.m. and only take out the cleaning people." And he was right. So I was like, okay, how do you—you can't just tell people "work harder," you know? They're like, "Okay, you work harder." So what I did was—it was such a stupid thing—but I was like, "All right, I'm just going to go out to get dinner and I'm going to come back at like 9 p.m. or 10 p.m." I'm going to walk around, and whoever is here, I'm going to talk about the stuff they're doing at the next all-hands meeting and I'm gonna like, bump up the prioritization of that. I remember like Marcel Molina at @noradio on Twitter—that's a little promotion for Marcel there. Marcel was always working late on stuff and I would be like, "What are you working on?" and "Oh, I'm working on native retweet" or whatever. And I would get in front of the company and go, "Look at the work Marcel is doing." And people were like, "How come—how does he always know what Marcel is doing?" And other people would go like, "Oh, Dick comes back after dinner at night and walks around." And people slowly, slowly started to change the culture. Okay, the people who are around when Dick's walking around are getting their stuff prioritized, etc. I super duplicated you; I walked around Friday afternoons and I learned a lot. Yeah. And you—yeah, so I would just go up to engineers and go, "What's going on? What are you working on?" Yeah. You had an interesting expression that I don't understand about "forest fires" and "forestry management." Oh yeah. Yeah, I was trying to help a director in the company be more strategic. They were super tactical. And I was finally like, "Your job as a director as you grow in this company is you can't just say, 'Well, I put out this forest fire.' Your job is to map the territory and start to do forestry management." "Like, what are we going to do so that next year, if there's a fire over here, that highway is not going to be in trouble? Like, you can only work so many hours a day and stamping out fires isn't going to work as we scale. You need to push those down the stack." "Your team should be fighting the fire that's over there because it might, you know, burn a house down. You need to be focused more on thinking about this whole pie and what has to be true 24 months from now so this whole territory is in better shape." And that was the metaphor I had for helping people try to think strategically versus like, "Well, I'm chopping down the trees. Look at all the trees." Well, that's not leadership and strategy; that's tactics. I like that. Okay, now I get it. One of the people I connected with before interviewing you said something interesting about you. Oh boy, I can hardly wait. I'm sure most people did, but anyway. Both sides of this are interesting. He said, "You know, Silicon Valley, San Francisco, everyone's passive-aggressive." So I'm curious your take on that 'cause I don't live in Silicon Valley. And the second thing he said: "Dick's not passive-aggressive. Dick's aggressive." Yeah. Give me both sides of that. It's just like—I am super—I know this is a personal flaw, but I'm super impatient. Like, people would come in and go, "Here's why I think..." or whatever. "I'm sad." You know? And they would start talking to me like, "Okay, stop. It seems like you don't like working here. You should quit." "No, I want to tell you why I'm sad." You know? Like, "Okay, I don't—I don't care. Who cares? I'm sorry, I'm not trying to be a jerk, but we have a million things to do." I remember, and it was just like—I was just direct and blunt with people. Not a jerk. You don't have to be—people think a lot of times, especially engineering leaders who have come up in the organization coding and programming and doing stuff on their own and now they have to manage people—they think when you tell them you have to manage performance, you have to be a jerk. You don't have to be a jerk about it. You just have to be direct about it and forthright and not, "No one's sadder about this than me," you know? Don't say stupid stuff like that. You can be empathetic and still be straightforward. Okay. A lot of these CEOs I coach are in their mid-to-late 20s and in their whole life, they haven't given—they don't go to their mom and say, "I got some feedback on dinner," or like, "You're hogging the basketball." You've just never given a Homo sapien feedback. It's unnatural. Advice for that CEO? Yeah, my advice for that CEO—Bill Campbell actually gave me this advice early on. He's like, "Write down what you want to say, and then say that and don't say anything else." So the night before, if you write down the honest feedback that you would give Brian, especially if you have to give a bad performance review, I guarantee you 99 out of 100 people, if they go into the meeting the next day, they don't say that. They're like, "Listen, Brian..." instead of, "You know, it's a really hard time here for everybody, we're all working..." you know, shut the fuck up. Like, the person knows you're about to hit them over the head or something. And you also don't have to say, "Hey Brian, that's just the way it is, you know, too bad if you don't like it." You just write down what you want to say and be forthright and honest. You can say, "I understand. If you don't want to deal with it anymore, I get it." Say those things and don't add a bunch of fluff around it. I thought that was great feedback and I would do that. Sit with the silence. Yeah, sit with the silence. Sitting with silence is hard. Yeah, it is unnatural. It's unnatural. I used to do a thing in my management class where it was a six-hour course, there was a ton of stuff in it, but one of the things I would do was about giving people feedback. And like, "Okay Brian, you're going to give Dick feedback." "And the feedback is: you've been working on this thing for six weeks and busting your ass and you've been here till midnight every night, and we have to cancel the project 'cause it's no longer a priority," or some horrible thing that no one wants to hear. The only person who did the role-play who was ever good at sitting in silence was Elad Gil, current venture capitalist. Elad sat in the chair and gave the person the feedback. And I would pull the employee aside before the role-play and say, "This is bullshit, and you're going to tell Dick it shouldn't be cancelled, they need to look at the data." And Elad was great; he just sat there. It's hard. And then eventually, the other person runs out of gas and stops talking. And Elad was great at that, and the only person who ever did it. Okay. One of the things I want to ask you about is you had some very talented folks, some of which you brought in, like Adam Bain and Kevin Weil, Anthony Noto, Elad. How did you guys recruit? What did you look for? Are you looking for slope or experience? It's a great question. I always actually tried to promote from within as much as possible because you want people inside the company to feel like, "We're not going to get capped out where I can only get to this level because anything above that, Dick just goes out and finds someone." So Kevin Weil, for example, was promoted from within, from an IC when I got there in 2009—well, there were only 40 people in the company then—to eventually running all of product after running all of ads product. He was promoted from within. Noto was—I just felt like Twitter was such, such a drama. Twitter is like the drama queen of hyper-growth companies. You know, it was constantly in the news. When I took over as CEO, that year the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker, and I was like, "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal: no dead birds on magazine covers." Anyway, it was just constant drama. And Noto was so good at Goldman Sachs, where he ran technology and media, at dealing with investors. I had been trying for a while to bring him into Twitter, and I was finally able to. And he was, of course, he's fantastic. How about Adam? Adam was funny. Adam was what you'd call a "non-consensus hire" when I first recruited him. It was while I was COO, and I went to the board and Ev and said, "I got the perfect person to run revenue at Twitter. It's this guy Adam Bain, he's in LA and he works for Fox Interactive." And everyone was like, "You're going to bring in someone from Fox in LA to San Francisco to run our revenue?" But he was the perfect person to do it. I must have flown down to LA for five or six months before I could convince him to do it. But I'd met him while we were in the process of selling FeedBurner. His employer, News Corp, was one of the interested parties. I got to know him and was just like, "Man, this guy is tireless." And we were going to be in a constant dogfight, fighting from behind against Facebook for social ad dollars. And Adam was just—he's just one of those people who's got a motor that doesn't stop. There is an old adage: "fire fast, hire slow." Yeah, I always said that out loud. I didn't do that. I fired slow, and almost every time I was like, "Man, I knew six weeks ago." Yeah, we tried to hold ourselves accountable to that. It's horrible. Like, there's nothing worse than having to do something like that. I remember one of my board members, David Rosenblatt—you know, I always am a little bit like, "Come on," when people are like, "Oh, I like firing people because it's the right thing for them." That just sounds like you're an asshole 'cause it's horrible. But you have to do it. And I remember asking David Rosenblatt 'cause I had to terminate someone with global operating responsibility. I was like, "Wait a minute. If I fire them in the morning, the people in Japan are going to be in bed..." "But if I fire them in the afternoon then the people..." you know what I mean. I had to figure out how to do it. And I was talking to my board about it, and David Rosenblatt, who had been the CEO of DoubleClick, was like, "Oh, I had to do that." "I actually saved the calendar date from that 'cause I had a whole series of communications I had to roll out." This is where now we're a couple-thousand-person company with 30 offices around the world. So I get his day of communications. The only thing I remember from it is: "6 a.m. Wake up in a cold sweat." You know, it's just horrible. You don't—it's not fun, you can't wait till it's over, and you just have to do it. Jeff Weiner, the LinkedIn CEO, had an analogy for baseball fans. He was like, "You know, sometimes you see the manager walk out to the mound and the pitcher's like, 'No no no don't worry coach, I still got it.' And the manager walks back and the next guy cranks one out of the park." And Jeff was like, "I always tell myself: don't be the manager having the first conversation with the person when you know what you should be doing is having the second conversation with the person." As a Red Sox fan, that really hits home. I think his example was actually Pedro Martinez and Grady Little didn't take him out. Yes. I work with all these hyper-growth companies, I think you do too. What are the pitfalls when you're in hyper-growth and what are your tips for these CEOs? One of the real pitfalls, especially for these hyper-growth AI companies that are going from single-digit millions up to 100 million, is it's very easy to take your eye off the ball when you raise too much money. By taking your eye off the ball, I mean, we're not thinking about operating leverage and efficiency. Once the money's there, you're like, "Don't have to worry about that. I'll go worry about this thing over here." Next thing you know, the burn is crazy. "What are you worried about? We have money." It happens all the time. And Ryan wrote this great post saying, "Look, I thought I was like Mr. Efficiency, but when you don't have to worry about it at all, you stop worrying about it." Are people raising too much money? I think so, in many cases. The models probably need all the money for compute. But for an app company? I think people are raising too much money because raising a hundred million Series B is the new twenty million dollar Series B. What else in hyper-growth breaks? Hiring. Oh, almost—I mean, my favorite thing is you talk to a CEO and they're like, "We're only going to hire A-players." You know? I'm like, "Yeah, PS, everyone says that." Come back to me when your customer success team has to hire twice as many people next quarter because you just turned a bunch of customers because no one checked in with them last quarter. And then tell me. You just end up not doing that. So, one of the things you have to do when you realize you don't only hire A-plus players is you have to get religious about managing out low performers. But as you move down the stack and you've got inexperienced managers, there's nothing they want to do less than let someone go. They'll do anything not to do it. How do you fix that? You prevent them from trans-firing people. Trans-firing is like, "Janet would be great on the ads team. She loves ads." You need people? Yes. And then you realize, "No, no, no." So I had—we had performance reviews. I was like, if your review is below an X number, you can't switch teams. Yep. That's a good idea. No, you know, a whatever—let's say it's one through four. Four is amazing, one is you gotta go. If you're a two, you can't move teams until you're no longer a two. PS, if you go two to three to two to three, I'm going to talk to your manager and say, "Don't tell me this person is a three again just so you don't have to put them on a performance plan." So we would always go through as a senior team and say, "Okay, I can see these eight people are bouncing from two to three to back to two. You're just trying to not fire people. I can see that your curve is inflated." We would just be super hardcore about that. Let's talk about competition for a second. You were competing with Facebook. They were the juggernaut that we had the hardest time dealing with. And like, tomorrow I'm speaking at another company meeting, they're going to be competing with OpenAI and Anthropic. Almost every boardroom I'm on is talking about that. Everyone's anxious about that. Advice about that? First of all, you have to acknowledge it and then you just have to—I mean, one of the things I would always do is say, "They're huge and Mark is the most competitive person on the planet." Trust me when I tell you, they don't say to themselves, "Oh whatever, Twitter won that thing, that's fine." They're like, "That can never happen again." So we have to think about them, we have to talk about them, and we have to try to figure out how to outhustle them. And Adam Bain on the revenue team was great at that. He would go to one of the first big customer meetings—let's say it's Unilever—and he would sit down with the CMO from Unilever before the meeting and say, "How'd the Facebook meeting go?" "Oh, great, blah blah, what are they psyched about showing you this year?" You know? And then he would learn all that and he would go into the meeting with Unilever and say, "Even if we had a bunch of things to show them," he'd say, "We have some things we want to show you, but we really want to spend the next two hours just understanding your business." He knew that she just told him that the Facebook people came in and showed a bunch of things and weren't asking those kinds of questions. Now, Facebook wised up to that over time, but Adam had just come up with a great way of thinking about how to compete with this juggernaut. And the juggernaut's rolling out new things every quarter and they're microtargeting, so I'm going to totally come at it from a different angle. It was just smart about learning how to do that by talking to the customer beforehand. You guys picked the advertising model to make money. As you look back at that, good call? Yes. Advertising is undefeated. ARPU is higher, it just is, versus subscription revenue etc. I think we would do the same thing over again. Any other do-overs? Oh yeah. We tried to buy Instagram the month before Facebook bought it. And by the way, for a much higher percentage of the company. Facebook's offer was famously a billion dollars, and I think it was 700 of stock and 300 million of cash. They only had eight or nine employees at the time: Mike Krieger and Kevin Systrom. But we knew well before that, like, these guys are—Kevin is an extraordinary product manager. Kevin and Evan Spiegel I think are probably two of the best product thinkers in Silicon Valley. So I knew that, and well before Mark made his offer, I went out to dinner with Kevin and Mike and told them we wanted to buy the company. We would end up giving them well over 10% of Twitter, which was something equivalent to 700 million in stock. And I would have gone and—I could go back there and talk to Kevin—I would say, "Like, you can run the company when I leave, congrats, you'll get to be CEO." I mean, they still might not have done it. And I would also say, "Also, I'll go borrow 300 million dollars from JP Morgan and give it to you and you can have it all and we'll pay them back later." That, because that was a game changer. Once they had Instagram, I was like, "Oh man, we're boxed out." That is the future of rich media social media feeds. We have Vine, but it's much smaller and they're now going to come at us on Instagram with video, which of course they did and that—and that was sort of checkmate. Other regrets? Any regrets on man the content moderation stuff has been in the news over the last five years. It wasn't as hot while you were there. Any as you look back at that? It was always like it was the thing I'll say about content moderation that people get wrong all the time is like, "This is really easy, just prevent people from doing that." You're like, "Okay, well that is a lyric in a Kanye song, can he not tweet his lyrics?" Or whatever. Or it's easy just—I'll give you a specific example that I always think highlights why it was such a pain in the ass and it's so hard. You can't just come up with a set... these companies try to come up with a set of rules and like, "Well if it violates the rule you have to kick it off and if it doesn't violate the rule you can't kick it off." Like that's... you just can't think about it that way. These things are so subjective. So ISIS, the terrorist organization, captures three pilots from some Air Force and puts them in cages basically on a beach and lights them on fire and kills them, basically burns them to death. And they post these to Twitter, photos and videos. And my general counsel comes to me and goes, "Hey, these terrorist accounts are..." I'm like, "Great." She's like, "Suspend the accounts, right?" Like yeah, of course, like right away. Anyone that posts the media of these pilots being lit on fire, like suspend the account. Well, the freaking New York Post an hour later posts the huge photo of this pilot being lit on fire. And then I get this like, "Hey, what... you suspend... you know Kara or someone... like why'd you suspend the New York Post?" Like, what? We didn't suspend the New York Post. "Yeah you did." And you're like, "Okay, wait." I guess the New York Post can do it, but now who can't do it? I mean, and that was one tiny example. It was just so so infinitely hard and you just can't... my big if I could go back again is you just can't have these hard and fast rules. There has to be, again, pushing... having operating control and somebody with an understanding of the situation like our GC. You're going to have just a bunch of judgment calls and you're going to get a bunch of them wrong and that's life. In like after your tenure and like, I don't know what to believe, but it looks like there was a lot of conversation between the Biden administration and that group. And then a lot of accusations from Elon that that group was just very left of center in their orientation. Any reaction to that? Was that the case when you were there? I mean yeah, probably it was left of center, but I don't think I don't think when I was there that I don't think it impacted the way we thought about abuse. I mean, it wasn't like you know we didn't have any like, for example, we didn't have any like, "Oh, you're not allowed to dead name trans people." I mean, like that stuff was all post me being there. At the time it was mostly what we were dealing with... the Culture War kind of happened after you left. The yeah, the stuff we were dealing with when I was there were ridiculous amounts of Russian bots, which at the time were not election stuff. Maybe they were preparing for it, preparing the groundwork for 2016, but it was mostly like phishing attacks and just massive bot farms that were doing phishing attacks on people and trying to hack into their accounts or get credit card information, etc. Most of the CEOs who have been around a while have got a shit ton of bad press. You got a metric ton of bad press. It must... I got a bunch of bad press. It impacted me. I tried to show a face like it didn't, but it it was soul crushing to me. How did you handle it? I don't know, like I had been... I had had the fortunate opportunity to be a performer and do improv comedy and stuff and audition for SNL a couple times before I went back into business. And I had been like, you know, I was at... I remember I was at the Adelaide Comedy Festival with my improv group and we had this midnight show at the Opera House, the Adelaide Opera House. Adelaide in Australia? Yeah. Okay. And the crowd is hammered, drunk, maybe a thousand people, and it's a like an hour and ten minute show completely improvised. We get on stage and like five minutes in the crowd starts yelling like, "You suck! Get off!" Something like that. And like, "Man we got 80 more minutes or whatever." And so I don't know, I just became resilient to like, "Oh well, I gotta like have a booed on." Did it hurt inside? I don't know, like we got good press, I remember my daughter called me. The good press never hit for me, the bad press did. No no no, I'll tell you... I'll tell you my daughter called me, this is at the end of 2014. So 2013 we go public, IPO's successful, everyone's super excited, go yay us. And there's some you know the these stupid awards that come out come out and like "CEO of the Year: Dick Costolo." End of 2014 my daughter texts me, she's like, "I have bad news and good news." And was like, "What's the bad news?" She's like, "Yahoo Finance says you're one of the five worst CEOs of 2014." And I was like, "What's the good news?" She goes, "Nobody reads Yahoo Finance." Okay. Got it. Okay. I just felt like I don't know, it's like you're like okay. And I'll say one last thing on it, I've said this a bunch but but I think is important. I got invited to go to like the Vanity Fair Oscars party one of the years. By the way that must have been cool. Well I got invited to go. I didn't go. Why? Good question. So I told my daughter I don't want to start going to a bunch of stuff that I get invited to because I'm the CEO of Twitter, because then I'll be like, "Oh, I don't want to not be the CEO of Twitter 'cause I won't get invited to the." So I went to the NBA All-Star Game because I knew Adam Silver and he invited... I felt like it was a Dick Costolo invitation. Yes. I was like, "I want I'll go to things I get invited to 'cause I'm Dick Costolo." I don't want to go to things that the CEO of Twitter gets invited to because then I'll be like... wasn't it part of your job? No. I mean I mean I mean going to the White House, sure. Like, "Hey, the president wants to talk to you about you know Edward Snowden and abuse on the platform and what are we going to do about terrorists using these services." Yeah, you got to go to that as part of the job. But going to the like going to these things, you know, like the Oscars or whatever... like I don't know I don't know any of these people, they don't care about me. I totally would have done it. I totally would have done it. Well you would have been standing over in the corner going like, "No one's talking... Tom Cruise won't talk... Tom Cruise isn't talking to me." It's exceptionally rare these days that somebody bring a founder-led company is replaced or just they don't hire the adult external CEOs. Is that... should it be happening a lot more? I think it goes in waves. And it's not not doesn't happen at all these days. Yeah I know. I think it goes in waves and I think it will continue to be... there'll something will happen and people will start going, "You know we really need for these companies that are making you know four billion dollars in revenue after three years and the 26-year-old founder," they'll just something will happen and there will be a wave of you know operating executives coming back in and run some of the companies. It happens a little bit like Slootman at Snowflake. Pretty rare. Yeah yeah rare. Agreed. Even the public companies like MongoDB but Workday recently, but like maybe one a year. Yeah, if it's true I do think it goes in waves, we'll see if it we'll see if it happens. Okay you're a VP of blah-di-blah company and you want to be a Dick Costolo. What should you do? You want to be that adult. I want to be you when I grow up. I'm a VP at whatever. I'm Anthony Noto... did you know when he was CFO of Twitter he started like telling making it clear to you know executive recruiters like, "I want a CEO role. Like start you know when you start seeing CEO roles I I might not get one right away but like I want I want to talk to you about CEO roles when you get them." I got it. Get your name out there and just... I never... and Noto would tell you this too about his own career. I never took like a risk that I didn't you know that I wasn't super psyched I took. Even when I was like trying to get on SNL and wasn't successful. It ended up benefiting me down the road 'cause it was like, "All right, like like live TV on CNBC, are you okay doing that?" I'm like, "Look at the camera!" like I've got booed at midnight in Australia by a thousand people. I can look in the camera and talk about... should all CEOs take a comedy... my co-founder took a comedy class in business school and it really helped him. Yeah being on stage and you know being uncomfortable being on stage makes... I mean you go you stand up at an all-hands meeting you're like, "I know exactly what I'm going to say at the all-hands meeting I'm just going to talk about the priorities." That's easy after doing that. Okay. Let me give you a fun one. Hit me. Is San Francisco passive-aggressive? Is everyone passive-aggressive? Are CEOs passive-aggressive? I think there's a lot of passive-aggressiveness. I think I know who I know the person you talked to and I think and I know the other companies he's been at. I think he's generally right but there are some great exceptions. I think the Collison brothers at Stripe are both the rare combination of really really widely read and and and polymaths and understand have a broad perspective on humanity and biology and the human condition and and are and are forthright communicators and are de you know neither passive-aggressive nor hyper-aggressive. They're just they're forthright and straightforward and blunt. And I think those guys are great. And I and I feel the same way about Reed Hastings, a totally different executive at Netsky at Netflix. Reed is always like, "This is just how it is. You know I don't like I'm not I'm not trying to be mean about it this is just the way it is." And I think it I think it you rhyme with Reed in my head. Yeah yeah oh I take that as a huge compliment. I love that guy. I always thought those three people, John and Patrick at Stripe and Reed at Netflix, are extremely self-aware. Like I remember was at a table with Reed at a dinner once and he went around the table and said, "I want to hear everybody's opinion on this. How much of your success do you think is based on your DNA and how much of it is based on like I just learned how to kick ass and be amazing?" And I kind of I was like, "I think I know where he's going with this." And of course when it got around to himself he was like, "I think it's basically like 80% DNA." Interesting. How would you answer that? Yeah I would similarly and I think that's a result of him being... how much of is luck versus skill? Yeah I think there's certainly a bunch of luck. By I don't think he was saying luck versus skill. I think he was sort of saying like you're not like some you know exceptional person who came out and like I'm going to fight against the out... like it's basically encoded in you to be this way and you know it's ended up helping you. And sure there's like 20% luck along the way that put you in the position to get this role that you might not have otherwise had but you were always going to do something like this. Okay you brought up some good CEOs. The CEO rulebook feels to me to be changing. Your take on how Jensen Huang runs that with like 60 direct reports, no one-on-one, openly criticizing. He's got a whole different playbook. The thing that surprises me is I work with all these CEOs, nobody's copying him which I think's interesting. Your take on that. I go back to the Jeff Bezos comment to Jack Dorsey. "Jack, there are a lot of different ways to be successful." I think that I think that the mistake that CEOs make is they read the Walter Isaacson book on Elon Musk or they read the one on Steve Jobs and they think this is the archetype. Yeah. And Walter's just left out a whole bunch of stuff you know and that's not... did you have an archetype? No, I didn't have an archetype. Steve Jobs was mine. I tried to copy everything he did, quite poorly. I did it quite poorly. I mean it goes again to the like well Steve says you have to be an editor and you said and and you know and Jeff saying like, "Oh yeah well there's lots of different ways to do it." I think the and the reason I say that about the books is not just because they're more mythology than they are what really happened. Some of it's of course this happened but it leaves out a bunch of stuff. But more importantly you just if you try to be someone you're not, nobody is fooled. Like you think people are fucking fooled? Nobody is fooled. Everyone knows you're spinning them or you're playing some role and you're not yourself. And nothing is more miserable than trying to be someone you're not. One of the things I used to I used to do this in my management class actually during one of the NBA Finals in back in the like the 90s. This reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room. And it was at the time where Charles Barkley did this ad for Nike where Charles dunks a basketball and he looks at the camera and goes, "I ain't no role model," something like that. Yeah. And this reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room and asks Hakeem Olajuwon, "You know Charles Barkley says that kids shouldn't you know idolize look up to NBA players role models, what do you think about that?" And Hakeem Olajuwon goes, "I'm happy to be a role model, I love being a role model for kids." He's like, "Well Charles what he"—He wasn't wrong. He wasn't he wasn't he wasn't wrong. Although he appears to be on Ozempic or something, he's got to have his suit re-tailored. The this reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room and goes up to Hakeem Olajuwon and says, "You know Charles Barkley says that kids shouldn't you know idolize look up to NBA players role models, what do you think about that?" And Hakeem Olajuwon goes, "I'm happy to be a role model, I love being a role model for kids." He's like, "Well Charles what" and and Hakeem says, "Well here's the deal. Charles is a different person in public than he is in private. And so he's got this anxiety and tension in his mind about whether he's supposed to be public Charles in this moment or private Charles in this moment. I'm always the same person so if people want to look up to me as a role model that's fine with me." And I just think that it's nothing but misery if you're trying to you know look at some written down playbook of how Steve Jobs did things and I gotta go do that when it's all but it was impossible for Steve to do it almost and you're certainly not going to be able to do it and you'll be miserable trying. Okay last question I have is like the Twitter board, man there's a lot of freaking drama there. Tons of it. So much. So much. You're building that, you're a 100 million dollar company going on 400 million. You're building a board, advice like should you bring in independents early? Should you wait? I had two board members that were immensely helpful to me. Peter Chernin who had been brought in from News Corp as an independent director 'cause he'd managed tens of thousands of people and I needed someone with operating experience, global operating experience. And Peter was great at always like always did the sort of the Jeff Bezos: "Let's talk about this from the user's perspective." Instead of like "How are we going to do XYZ" like he would always back up and say, "You know wait, if I'm the chief if I'm the chief user officer at this company and I'm thinking about this from the user" and so that was immensely helpful in lots and lots of cases. And then secondly Peter Fenton from Benchmark would always help me interview candidates and either close them or help me with "Hey how should I think about this candidate?" And that having someone on your board who's not in the day-to-day at the company and who can also give the candidate sort of a third-party perspective on why I think this is going to be a monster and why I think you should do this was like crazy helpful and helped convince numerous execs like Adam Bain and Anthony Noto to come to the company. Do you bring in okay you're at a hundred million, you're doing your series B, do you bring in an independent or you what happens now is they wait right before the IPO? No, I you I think it's helpful to have operating people with operating expertise in there much sooner than that. Okay and just while we're on IPO you took Twitter public, what's your... it's been in very hard to take companies public, it's become very unfashionable to take companies public. I would have kept it private longer if I could go back and do anything over again. I would have too. It's just so much easier running a private company and not having to like okay well that's going to mean we're going to miss the revenue number by a percent. It's the right thing to do but you know the employees are going to be running around like, "What's wrong?" when the stock goes down 8% after hours. You know and as you know you don't have a daily ticker that the employees are freaking out about when you're private company and it's just a lot more you can be it's so much easier and stress-free to focus on the long term as opposed to, "Okay now we have to make a long term versus a short term to have a discussion about this because you know everything's going to go sideways if we do this in Q1 instead of" blah blah blah. You get it. It was just a lot easier to run private company. I wanted to talk to you for a long time because you are kind of a rare species these days, the adult CEO brought into a hypergrowth company and you delivered. Thanks for coming on the pod. Thanks for having me. Okay I thought Dick was great. It's a masterclass in how do you scale and be a terrific scale-up CEO. There's a few things he talked about that I think are worth reviewing. Some of which I did, some of which I wish I did when I was CEO of HubSpot. One thing he did that I totally did was management by walking around. I'd wait until like seven o'clock at night and I'd walk the halls of HubSpot through the dev organization, support, sales, see who was around, and really hear what was happening. That was super useful for me. It was great context and it was also good to see who was burning the midnight oil. I liked his feedback around giving tough people tough feedback. I was never very good at this and his advice is good. It's you know the night before you're going to give feedback for that tough person, you gotta write it down the night before, practice it in the mirror, it gives good advice. And when you deliver it, you deliver it and you sit in silence. No fluff, you sit in silence. Really hard to do, but I think that's great advice. He talked about killing committees and using DRI's. A lot of the CEOs I've interviewed are talking about similar things, particularly important as you've got silos in your organization and the important stuff cuts across boundaries of those silos. He gave a great quote from another pod guest, Ben Horowitz. He said, "Make sure everybody understands what you understand." Not just the what, the why, and what success means for them personally. Priorities without context are useless. I thought that was a gem from Ben and I thought it was really cool that he repeated it. He accuses Silicon Valley of being passive-aggressive. He said he's not, he's aggressive. I think that's good advice. Like a lot of CEOs he says you should hire fast and fire slow. He didn't fire slow. Almost no other CEOs don't fire slow, I did not fire slow, and so he wishes he did that. Some gems in there. Hope you enjoyed it and looking forward to having you in the next pod.

Brazil's Lula Reveals Key Details of Meeting With Trump – Trade Deals, Tariffs & Cooperation | AC1N

DRM News(825.0K) ·27:45 ·408 views · 2026-05-07
Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva (President, Brazil)
President Lula and President Trump have established a high-level working group with a 30-day deadline to resolve trade and tariff disputes, while signaling a renewed strategic partnership centered on critical minerals, infrastructure investment, and global security.
TL;DR President Lula and President Trump have established a high-level working group with a 30-day deadline to resolve trade and tariff disputes, while signaling a renewed strategic partnership centered on critical minerals, infrastructure investment, and global security. ...

TL;DR

President Lula and President Trump have established a high-level working group with a 30-day deadline to resolve trade and tariff disputes, while signaling a renewed strategic partnership centered on critical minerals, infrastructure investment, and global security.

Trade and Economic Recalibration

  • Tariff Resolution: President Lula proposed a 30-day "hammer-down" deadline for trade officials to resolve disputes regarding import taxes and tariffs, aiming to bypass bureaucratic inertia.
      • Lula noted that despite perceptions of high Brazilian taxes, the average tax on U.S. goods is 2.7%, though some specific products hit 12%.
      • Brazil currently faces a $14 billion trade deficit with the U.S. and seeks to rebalance the relationship after decades of U.S. trade hegemony being eroded by China.
  • U.S. Reinvestment: Lula urged the U.S. to return its focus to Latin American infrastructure, noting that Chinese firms often dominate international tenders for Brazilian roads and railways because the U.S. fails to participate.
  • Data Centers: Brazil is open to hosting international data centers provided the companies finance their own energy production, ensuring Brazil’s power grid is not strained for foreign data storage.

Strategic Resources and National Sovereignty

  • Critical Minerals: Lula highlighted new Brazilian legislation that treats critical minerals as a matter of national sovereignty while inviting international partnerships.
      • Brazil has only mapped 30% of its territory for minerals and intends to reach 100% via partnerships with the U.S., China, Germany, and others.
      • The goal is to move beyond raw extraction toward domestic processing and wealth creation.
  • Energy Transition: Lula positioned Brazil as a global leader in energy transition, inviting U.S. entrepreneurship to participate in green energy projects.

Organized Crime and Public Security

  • Economic Alternatives: Lula argued that military bases alone cannot stop the drug trade; the international community must provide economic alternatives for farmers to replace illicit crops with profitable, legal agriculture.
  • Global Task Force: Brazil proposed a multilateral working group to share expertise in combating organized crime.
      • Focus Areas: Lula emphasized the need to tackle the financial infrastructure of gangs, weapon smuggling (noting many arms in Brazil originate in the U.S.), and money laundering within U.S. states.
  • Domestic Security: Brazil plans to launch a comprehensive national plan against organized crime next week, focusing on reclaiming territories from criminal factions.

Global Diplomacy and Geopolitics

  • UN Reform: Lula pressed Trump on the need to reform the UN Security Council, advocating for permanent seats for nations like Brazil, India, Mexico, and representatives from Africa to reflect the "2026 geopolitics" rather than the 1945 status quo.
  • Dialogue vs. Conflict: Lula reiterated his preference for narrative and dialogue over war, specifically mentioning concerns regarding the invasion of Iran and the protracted conflict between Russia and Ukraine.
  • Bilateral Rapport: Despite past political differences, Lula described a "chemistry" and "love at first sight" relationship with Trump, emphasizing that democratic stability in the two largest Western Hemisphere nations serves as a global example.
  • Non-Interference: Lula dismissed concerns about U.S. interference in Brazilian domestic politics or the 2026 election, stating that the Brazilian people—and only the Brazilian people—decide their destiny.

Notable Quotes

"The public machine is eternal, but the president has an expiration date. We have a four-year mandate; things have to happen. We must establish goal plans so people deliver what society expects of us." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"I told President Trump that it is important for the United States to have interest in Brazil's things again. For example, we often hold international tenders for a highway or railroad, and the U.S. doesn't participate. The Chinese do." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"I believe much more in dialogue than in war. We know how a war begins, but no one knows how it ends. That is why talking is much cheaper and more effective." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"The only things we do not give up are our democracy and our sovereignty. Everything else is debatable." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
A palavra ao presidente, presidente Lula para suas considerações iniciais antes das perguntas. Eu não sei se tem que fazer consideração ou se poderia entrar direto nas perguntas. Depois da fala dos cinco ministros, eu penso... essa água é minha? É sua, presidente. Eu estava tomando conta. Eu vi você bebendo. Ninguém chega perto. Não, só para esquentar a pergunta de vocês é o seguinte: eu saio daqui com a ideia de que nós demos um passo importante na consolidação da relação democrática histórica que o Brasil tem com os Estados Unidos. Eu já tinha dito na Malásia para o presidente Trump de que a boa relação entre Brasil e Estados Unidos é uma demonstração ao mundo de que as duas maiores democracias, sabe, do continente, podem efetivamente servir de exemplo para o mundo. Nós somos as duas maiores democracias do hemisfério. Nós somos duas democracias muito importantes do ponto de vista, sabe, na América Latina, outro na América do Norte. E nós já tivemos com os Estados Unidos — é importante vocês não perderem de vista — durante todo o século XX, os Estados Unidos foi o maior parceiro comercial do Brasil. Os Estados Unidos começou a perder a hegemonia a partir de 2008, se não me falha a memória, porque a China entrou no espaço para comprar coisas brasileiras, sabe, que interessavam aos chineses e que outros países não tinham capacidade de produzir tanto quanto o Brasil. E o Brasil passou a ter na China o seu principal parceiro comercial. E hoje eu disse ao presidente Trump de que é importante que os Estados Unidos volte a ter interesse nas coisas do Brasil. Por exemplo, eu disse para ele que muitas vezes nós fazemos licitações internacionais para fazer uma rodovia ou uma ferrovia, e os Estados Unidos não participa da licitação. Quem participa são os chineses. Sabe, eu disse para ele que, durante um bom tempo, tanto os Estados Unidos deixou de olhar para a América Latina, só olhava com o olhar de combate ao narcotráfico, como a União Europeia deixou de olhar para a América Latina por conta da conquista do Leste Europeu. E deixou de olhar para a África também. E agora as pessoas perceberam a importância outra vez da América Latina nesse mundo conturbado. Por isso é que nós fechamos o acordo União Europeia e Mercosul, com o EFTA, com a Cingapura. Por isso é que nós queremos fechar o acordo do Mercosul com o Canadá, com o Japão, porque isso dá uma dimensão de defesa do multilateralismo contra o unilateralismo colocado em prática pelas taxações do presidente Trump. Eu penso que essa é uma coisa interessante. A outra coisa é que nós resolvemos discutir aqueles assuntos que pareciam tabus, ou seja, a questão do crime organizado. Sabe, eu disse ao presidente, muitas, muitas vezes os Estados Unidos falava em combater o crime organizado, a questão das drogas tentando ter base militar dentro dos outros países, quando na verdade, para você fazer com que os países deixem de plantar ou fabricar aquilo que a gente chama de droga, é preciso que a gente crie alternativa econômica para esses países. Como que você vai fazer um país deixar de produzir coca se você não oferece uma alternativa de algum produto para que alguém possa plantar e ganhar dinheiro? E nós temos que incentivar o plantio de outras coisas e sermos os compradores para que as pessoas possam sobreviver. Senão, senão, enquanto houver gente necessitada de recursos e houver consumidor, não vamos parar, sabe, de ter o mundo cheio de droga por tudo quanto é lado. É importante, eu disse para ele que nós estamos dispostos a construir, sabe, um grupo de trabalho com todos os países da América do Sul, com todos os países da América Latina e quiçá com todos os países do mundo para a gente criar um grupo forte de combate ao crime organizado. Não é hegemonia de um país ou de outro querer combater o crime organizado. É uma coisa que tem que ser compartilhada com todos, e o Brasil tem expertise. O Brasil tem uma extraordinária Polícia Federal. O Brasil tem uma experiência muito, muito, sabe, no combate às drogas, no combate ao tráfico de armas, e também é importante saber que parte das armas que chega no Brasil sai dos Estados Unidos. Sabe, é importante saber que tem lavagem de dinheiro que é feito em estados americanos. Então, se a gente souber isso e colocar a verdade em torno da mesa e criar um grupo de trabalho para trabalharmos juntos, a gente pode resolver em anos aquilo que não se resolveu em séculos. Ou a gente pode resolver em décadas aquilo que não resolveu em séculos. Também a questão dos minerais críticos. Ou seja, tudo que se fala hoje é dos minerais críticos porque a China, porque a China, porque a China, porque a China, porque a China. Eu disse ao presidente Trump que nós não só fizemos uma coisa extraordinária aprovando na Câmara ontem, sabe, a lei sobre a questão dos minerais críticos, como a aprovação de um conselho sob a coordenação da Presidência da República tratando a questão dos minerais críticos como uma questão de soberania nacional. Queremos que a gente possa compartilhar o potencial do Brasil que ainda é pouco conhecido. Nós só temos o conhecimento de 30% do nosso território e agora nós temos a obrigação de termos conhecimento de 100% do território para compartilhar com quem queira fazer investimento no Brasil. Nós não temos preferência. O que nós queremos é fazer, sabe, parceria, compartilhar com as empresas americanas, chinesas, alemãs, japonesas, francesas, ou seja, quem quiser participar conosco para ajudar a gente fazer a mineração, para fazer a separação e para produzir a riqueza que essas terras raras nos oferecem, estão sendo convidados para ir no Brasil. E isso é permitido pela regulamentação que foi feita na lei aprovada ontem que deve ser aprovada hoje no Senado. Além disso, uma outra coisa importante que nós discutimos é a questão do comércio. Sempre tem uma dúvida sobre a questão do comércio. Sabe, nós dizemos para os Estados Unidos: vocês tiveram 400 e não sei quantos bilhões de superavit comercial com o Brasil nos últimos 15 anos. No último ano vocês tiveram... o Brasil teve déficit de 14 bilhões com os Estados Unidos. Então, ele sempre acha que nós cobramos muito imposto. Eu disse para ele: não, porque a média do imposto que nós cobramos de vocês é 2.7%, apenas 2.7%. Mas ele continua: "mas tem produto que é 12%". Então eu falei assim: "Ô Trump, vamos fazer o seguinte, vamos colocar um grupo de trabalho e vamos permitir que esse moço da indústria e comércio do Brasil junto com teu moço do comércio sentem em 30 dias e apresentem para nós uma proposta para a gente poder bater o martelo." Sabe, quem tiver errado vai ceder. Se a gente tiver que ceder, nós vamos ceder. Se vocês tiverem que ceder, vocês vão ter que ceder. Porque senão a gente faz reunião e depois a máquina pública... vocês sabem o meu conceito de máquina pública, a máquina pública é eterna, o presidente tem prazo de validade. Ele tem data para entrar e data para sair. Então é o seguinte: nós temos 4 anos de mandato, as coisas têm que acontecer. Então é preciso que a gente estabeleça um plano de metas. Em cada reunião, estabeleça um plano de metas para que as pessoas cumpram e para que a gente possa colocar em execução aquilo que a sociedade espera de nós. Então eu saio muito, muito satisfeito da reunião. Acho que foi uma reunião importante para o Brasil e importante para os Estados Unidos. Eu sempre acho que a fotografia vale muito e vocês perceberam que o presidente Trump rindo é melhor do que ele de cara feia. E eu fiz questão de dizer para ele rir. É pouco importante, alivia, alivia a nossa alma se a gente rir um pouco. Bem, então é isso, eu acho que o Brasil está preparado. O Brasil está preparado para discutir com qualquer país do mundo qualquer assunto. Nós não temos veto. Não tem assunto proibido. A única coisa que nós não abrimos mão é da nossa democracia e da nossa soberania. O resto é tudo discutível. E ainda brinquei com o presidente Trump: ele perguntou da Copa do Mundo, sabe, se a seleção brasileira estava boa e eu falei: olha, eu espero que você não venha, sabe, anular o visto dos jogadores brasileiros para a seleção. Por favor, não faça isso porque nós vamos vir aqui para ganhar a Copa do Mundo. É isso. O que que ele respondeu? Hein? Ele riu. Ele riu porque agora ele vai rir sempre. Ele aprendeu que rir é muito bom. Vamos então abrir para as perguntas, começando com a Isabela Menon da Folha de São Paulo. Oi presidente, boa tarde. Presidente, foram três horas de reunião e nós da imprensa não pudemos acompanhar. Acabamos de viver aqui que a impressão é positiva de todos vocês. Mas o Trump postou sobre a reunião e comentou apenas sobre comércio e tarifas. Eu queria entender se segurança pública era uma questão central para o senhor, se teve alguma sinalização positiva sobre a proposta apresentada pelo Brasil e também a possibilidade de classificar o CV e PCC como organizações terroristas, se foi descartada pelos Estados Unidos. Não foi discutido isso. O que eu queria dizer, entreguei por escrito cada assunto que eu discuti com o presidente Trump. Além dos ministros falarem, eu terminei a reunião entregando para ele cada proposta nossa escrita em inglês, em inglês que é para ele saber o que que nós queremos. Para não ter dúvida daquilo que nós queremos. E porque nós estamos levando muito a sério essa questão do combate ao crime organizado. Sabe esse negócio de dizer que o crime organizado, que as facções, sabe, no caso do Brasil, tomaram os territórios nas cidades? Nós temos que dizer para o povo brasileiro que o território de uma cidade, de bairro, é do povo, não é de crime organizado, não é de facção criminosa. Isto é o que nós temos que dizer e no mundo também. Nós criamos uma base na cidade de Manaus para combater o crime organizado, o tráfico de armas e de droga na fronteira brasileira com a participação de delegados da polícia de todos os países da América do Sul. Se os Estados Unidos quiser compartilhar e participar conosco, estará convidado. O que nós estamos propondo é o seguinte: é muito sério, é muito sério. A partir da semana que vem vamos lançar um plano de combate ao crime organizado que é para valer. Quem escapou até a semana que vem, tudo bem, mas quem não escapou não vai escapar mais. E vamos fazer algumas frentes, vocês já ouviram aqui as quatro frentes de trabalho. Uma delas é a questão financeira. Nós precisamos destruir, sabe, o potencial financeiro do crime organizado e das facções. Se a gente não destruir, eles hoje viraram em alguns casos empresas multinacionais. Eles estão em vários países. Eles estão no futebol. Eles estão na política. Estão no meio empresarial. Estão, sabe, em tudo quanto é lugar, no poder judiciário. Então o que nós queremos é dizer: para trabalhar sério o Brasil está disposto a dar exemplo de país que vai levar muito a sério isso. Vamos para a próxima pergunta da Agência France-Presse. Yes, thank you Mr. President, in English if I may. Obrigado. Muy bien. Jordi Zamora, France-Presse. Two questions if I may. First, I would like to know if you talked with President Trump about Cuba, Venezuela, the situation in other countries in the region? And second question, you have qualified President Trump as imperialist in the past, you have said and again you said here that no country can rule the world or the region on its own. I wonder if you have change of mind after meeting him personally after three hours? How do you qualify him as a leader, as a political leader? Olha, o Trump não vai mudar o jeito dele de ser por causa de uma reunião que durou três horas comigo. O que eu fiz questão de dizer para ele é o que eu penso das coisas que eu acho que pode ser feito. Eu acredito muito mais no diálogo do que na guerra. Eu acho que a invasão do Irã, ela vai causar mais prejuízo do que ele está imaginando. Mas tem várias suposições. Ele acha que a guerra já acabou. Não é o real. Mas ele acha, eu não vou, sabe, ficar brigando com ele por causa da visão que ele tem da guerra. Sabe, ele acha que na Venezuela está tudo resolvido. Eu espero que esteja, porque eu lido com a Venezuela desde 2002. Mas eu acho que a Venezuela, sabe, eu espero que a Venezuela resolva os seus problemas porque o povo venezuelano precisa, sabe, ter uma chance na vida de viver bem. E Deus queira que a Delcy consiga cumprir, sabe, com o mandato de presidenta fazendo as coisas acontecer. Eu disse para ele que eu tenho interesse em discutir qualquer assunto que ele precise discutir. Se quiser discutir comigo sobre Cuba, sobre Venezuela, sobre Irã, sobre o que ele quiser, eu estou disposto a discutir. E porque para mim é mais simples. Eu não tenho vocação belicista. Sabe, a minha vocação é de diálogo, é acreditar no poder da narrativa, acreditar no poder do convencimento. É assim que eu acho que a gente deve fazer política. Falei muito com ele sobre a questão de mudança no conselho da ONU. É preciso reformar a ONU. E que ele, Trump, Xi Jinping, Putin, Macron e o primeiro-ministro da Inglaterra são, sabe, as pessoas que têm responsabilidade de propor a mudança porque eles são os membros permanentes do conselho de segurança. Só eles podem tudo. Eles têm direito de veto. Eles têm direito de indicar secretaria-geral da ONU. Nós somos coadjuvantes. Os outros países são coadjuvantes. Então eu falei para ele: "Você poderia convidar o conselho de segurança para discutir, sabe, as guerras que estão acontecendo no mundo, como é que vai encontrar e fazer parte disso. Vocês podem fazer isso. Por que que não aumenta o conselho de segurança da ONU?" O Brasil gostaria de participar, muito tempo o Brasil briga. O México tem tamanho para isso, a Índia tem tamanho para isso, a Alemanha, o Japão, países como Egito, países como África do Sul, países como a Argélia, países como... sabe, mais países africanos. A Etiópia tem 126 milhões de habitantes, a Indonésia tem 200 milhões de habitantes. Ou seja, então o que não falta é país para ajudar a que a ONU volte a funcionar em plenitude. Se a ONU funcionasse bem, poderia acabar metade dos conflitos que você tem armado na África. Ela precisaria ter autoridade política, autoridade moral e autoridade financeira para fazer intervenção com a ordem de quem? Dos cinco membros permanentes do conselho de segurança. Só eles podem fazer isso. Só eles. O Brasil só pode gritar. O Brasil só pode fazer discurso como o Mauro fez contra a chacina na Palestina. Mas os Estados Unidos veta, a China veta, a Rússia veta. Então é preciso que eles se coloquem de acordo para a gente mudar. A geopolítica de 2026 não é a geopolítica de 1945. O mundo é outro. O mundo é outro, a comunicação é outra. Então foi dito com a maior tranquilidade para ele essas coisas. Naquilo que quiserem que o Brasil colabore, o Brasil está disposto a colaborar. Vocês estão lembrados que a guerra da Rússia e da Ucrânia era para durar três meses. Já faz quatro anos. Ninguém sabe. Todo mundo sabe como é que começa uma guerra, como termina ninguém sabe. Ninguém sabe. Por isso que eu acho que dialogar, conversar, é muito mais barato, mais eficaz, não tem vítima, não tem destruição de casa, não tem morte de criança, não tem destruição de escola. Então isso eu falei ao presidente Trump porque não é a primeira vez. Sabe, quem sabe um dia a gente vai conseguir convencer. Eu já liguei para o Xi Jinping propondo convocar o conselho de segurança, já liguei para o Putin, já liguei para o Macron. Ô gente, convide vocês, são cinco pessoas, são cinco países. Convoque uma reunião, uma teleconferência. Ninguém tem que sair da comodidade do seu gabinete. Sabe, convoque uma conferência e decida. Vamos tomar uma decisão. Vamos mudar o estatuto da ONU, vamos convocar uma assembleia especial para discutir isso e vamos criar, sabe, paz no mundo. Isso foi dito. Espero que ele tenha ouvido. Próxima pergunta de Lisandra Paraguassu da Reuters. Boa tarde presidente. Uma das questões principais nessa visita era a questão justamente do risco dos Estados Unidos imporem novas tarifas ao Brasil, especialmente com o final agora da investigação da seção 301. O que foi conversado e o senhor sai daqui avaliando qual o risco que a gente tem hoje de ter realmente novas tarifas impostas ao Brasil? Olhe para a minha fisionomia. Você acha que eu estou otimista ou pessimista? Eu estou muito otimista. Veja porque tem uma divergência entre eles e nós que ficou explicitada na reunião. O ministro dele falou uma coisa, o nosso ministro falou outra. Ora, como a gente não podia ficar debatendo o dia inteiro sobre isso, eu propus ao Trump: vamos dar 30 dias. Vamos dar 30 dias para esses companheiros resolverem o problema. E nós voltamos a conversar. E ainda falei para o Trump: "A gente não precisa fazer reunião lá ou cá, a gente pode fazer teleconferência. É mais barato. Sabe, o que nós temos que fazer é resolver esses problemas. Porque vocês têm que entender o seguinte: o problema é que muitas vezes você faz uma decisão na frente dos presidentes e quando o presidente vira, que pensa que a coisa está acontecendo, a burocracia entra em campo." Como a burocracia não tem tempo de validade, sabe, a burocracia não tem tempo de validade, ela começa e somente quando Deus quer que ela vá embora. Então nós temos que acompanhar com mais precisão, sabe, essas coisas. Nós temos interesse, muito interesse que os Estados Unidos volte a investir no Brasil. Aliás, se tem uma coisa que nós queremos fazer é isso, que mais gente vá para investir no Brasil. Nós temos quem sabe, sabe, o maior investimento em transição energética de todos os países. Temos a maior possibilidade, vamos fazer as coisas acontecer a partir do Brasil. Alguém quer fazer data center no Brasil? Tem que produzir sua própria energia. Porque nós não vamos gastar dinheiro para criar data center para mandar dados para outros países. Sabe, nós queremos dados para nós. Agora, nós temos condições de oferecer aos outros países a oportunidade de construir data center desde que eles arquem com a produção de energia. É o mínimo que a gente pode exigir. E eu acho que vai ser bom. Eu acho que vai ser bom. Eu acho... o Trump ele repete muitas vezes que ele gosta do Brasil, que ele ama o Brasil, sabe? E eu acho que é possível que ele goste do Brasil, que ele falou que tem muitos amigos no Brasil. Aliás, eu peguei uma foto dele que eu esqueci de trazer para ele. Eu peguei a foto dele com o Vicente Matheus e a Marlene Matheus no tempo que era presidente do Corinthians no... no cassino, acho que era dele, não sei se era cassino que era dele em Los Angeles que quebrou o cassino. Sabe, eu trouxe pela foto. Então ele disse que tem muitos amigos no Brasil. É bem possível que ele goste mesmo do Brasil. Ora, como nós brasileiros gostamos também dos Estados Unidos, por que não fazer para as coisas darem certo? É isso que eu quero, fazer as coisas darem certo. Eu quero que os Estados Unidos volte a ver no Brasil um parceiro importante para as suas atividades de empreendedorismo. É isso. Cleve Watson do Washington Post. Thank you for taking questions, Mr. President. Tua voz não combina com o tamanho do jornalista. Thank you for taking questions and welcome to Washington D.C. You and President Trump have publicly traded insults. He has supported your political opponents and this meeting today ended without the traditional bilateral pressing engagement. How would you characterize your relationship with President Trump going forward and has he addressed your claims that he is violating or interfering with Brazil’s national sovereignty? Olha, se ele tentou interferir nas eleições brasileiras, ele perdeu. Porque eu ganhei as eleições. Eu acho que não é uma boa política presidente de outro país ficar interferindo nas eleições de outro país. É o princípio básico para que a gente não permita a ocupação cultural, política e a soberania de outro país. Tá? Eu penso que a nossa relação com o Trump é uma relação sincera. Eu penso que desde o primeiro encontro que nós tivemos de 29 segundos em Nova York, numa reunião da Assembleia Geral da ONU, até os telefonemas que nós fizemos, até o encontro da Malásia e até esse encontro de hoje, eu acho que evoluiu muito. Eu tenho razões para acreditar que o Trump gosta do Brasil. E por isso eu quero que ele saiba que nós brasileiros temos interesse em fazer os melhores acordos com os Estados Unidos. E eu acho que sinceramente ele sabe a importância dos Estados Unidos. Eu não acredito que ele vá ter qualquer influência nas eleições brasileiras ou ter... até porque quem vota é o povo brasileiro. Sabe, e eu acho que ele vai se comportar como presidente dos Estados Unidos deixando que o povo brasileiro decida o seu destino, como eu vou deixar que o povo americano decida o destino deles. É o que vai acontecer. Sabe, a nossa relação é muito boa, mas muito boa. Eu diria uma relação que pouca gente acreditava que pudesse acontecer com tanta rapidez. Sabe aquela história: amor à primeira vista? Aquele negócio da química? É o que aconteceu. E eu espero que continue assim. E eu espero que seja assim com qualquer presidente do Brasil e qualquer presidente do mundo. Vai... vai ser muito importante essa relação. E quem vai decidir a eleição brasileira é o povo brasileiro. Eu já tenho muita experiência, sabe, de eleição no Brasil. Não acredito em interferência de quem quer que seja de fora. Vamos à próxima pergunta, Eduardo Barão da Band. Boa tarde presidente. Eu queria complementar a pergunta do colega do Washington Post porque o senhor está tendo uma relação muito próxima com ele, terceira vez, estivemos juntos na Malásia, em Nova York. Com a eleição no final do ano, o senhor chegou a tocar nesse assunto com ele? O senhor vai pedir apoio pro presidente Trump como o senhor deu lá atrás para a Kamala Harris? Tem algum receio de pressão mesmo ou apoio do presidente Trump para a oposição, para o Pablo Marçal, por exemplo? Não existe... não existe nenhuma possibilidade de eu discutir esse assunto com qualquer presidente de qualquer país do mundo. Esse é assunto brasileiro. E eles sabem disso. Eles também são presidentes. Eles também disputam eleições. Então o meu respeito ao presidente Trump é porque ele foi eleito pelo povo americano. E só pelo fato de ele ter sido eleito pelo povo americano não cabe a mim questionar. Cabe a mim levar muito a sério que ele tem mandato no povo americano para ser presidente desse país. E é assim que o Brasil trata ele, como tratamos qualquer outro presidente que foi eleito pelo povo. Vamos à próxima pergunta, Eduardo Ribas da Agência EFE. Thank you very much Mr. President. Aí já é demais querer que eu entenda inglês. Excuse me, thank you very much Mr. President. I just wanted to ask you if you...

Is climate denialism on the rise? Prof Katharine Hayhoe on science, faith and algorithms

World Economic Forum(1.1M) ·34:40 ·399 views · 2026-05-07
Katharine Hayhoe (Climate Scientist)
While public concern regarding climate change is at an all-time high due to the increasing frequency of extreme weather, social media algorithms are disproportionately amplifying denialist rhetoric, creating a perceived rise in skepticism that contradicts global polling data.
TL;DR While public concern regarding climate change is at an all-time high due to the increasing frequency of extreme weather, social media algorithms are disproportionately amplifying denialist rhetoric, creating a perceived rise in skepticism that contradicts global polling...

TL;DR

While public concern regarding climate change is at an all-time high due to the increasing frequency of extreme weather, social media algorithms are disproportionately amplifying denialist rhetoric, creating a perceived rise in skepticism that contradicts global polling data.

The Paradox of Climate Denialism vs. Public Concern

  • Polling Realities: Despite the visible intensity of online debate, data from the US, Canada, UK, and Asia shows that the majority of people are now worried about climate change.
  • The Experience Factor: Personal experience with "supersized" typhoons, heatwaves, and floods has shifted public perception more effectively than scientific data alone.
  • The "Dismissive" Minority: About 7–10% of the population are "dismissives" who are ideologically committed to denial. Hayhoe advises against arguing with this group, as they are unlikely to change regardless of the evidence.
  • Rising Online Toxicity: Hayhoe reports a 10 to 15-fold increase in personal trolling and vitriol on platforms like X (formerly Twitter) over the last two years, attributed to changes in moderation and algorithmic amplification of "outrage" content.

The "Last-Ditch Effort" Against Transition

  • Economic Tipping Points: Clean energy has passed an economic point of no return. In many regions, solar energy plus storage is now the cheapest option available.
      • China: Installed more solar in a single year than the US has in its entire history.
      • Pakistan: Installed more solar in one year than the entire history of Canada.
  • The Source of Friction: Hayhoe characterizes the current surge in denialism as a "last-ditch effort" by those with the most to lose from the transition to a sustainable economy.
  • The Cost of Inaction: In the 1980s, the US experienced one "billion-dollar" weather disaster every four months; today, such disasters occur every two weeks.

Strategies for Constructive Engagement: Head, Heart, and Hands

To move people from "worry" to "action," Hayhoe utilizes a three-part communication framework:

  • The Head: Acknowledging the facts—it is real, it is us, and it is serious.
  • The Heart: Connecting the issue to personal values. This involves identifying what a person already cares about (e.g., family, local economy, faith, or personal health) and showing how climate change impacts those specific things.
  • The Hands: Moving toward agency. Instead of focusing solely on individual carbon footprints (like recycling), Hayhoe emphasizes using one's voice to advocate for systemic change in workplaces, schools, and places of worship.
  • The Identity Shift: Using a case study of her colleague John Cook, Hayhoe illustrates that people often adopt climate-friendly behaviors (like solar panels) for non-environmental reasons (thrift, independence). Once the solution becomes part of their identity, their perspective on the science often follows.

Reconciling Science and Faith

  • Faith as a Motivator: As an evangelical Christian, Hayhoe argues that faith-based values are "perfectly uniquely suited" to motivate climate action, centered on the concepts of stewardship and responsibility for the vulnerable.
  • The "Age of Earth" Non-Issue: Hayhoe notes that even for those who hold "Young Earth" views, the data remains urgent. Over the last 6,000 years, global temperatures were stable or cooling until the Industrial Revolution, at which point they spiked—a fact that should be alarming regardless of one's geological timeline.
  • Values vs. Data: While science provides the data on consequences, values (often informed by faith for 80% of the world) provide the framework for making the right choices.

The "Olympic Sport" of Practicing Hope

  • Hope vs. Optimism: Hayhoe distinguishes hope from optimism. Optimism is the passive belief that things will be fine; Hope is the recognition that things are bad, but a better future is possible if we act.
  • The Three Ingredients of Hope:

1. Acknowledging the dire nature of the current situation.
