2. Recognizing that a better future is possible (though not guaranteed).

3. Identifying a clear pathway to that future and taking the first step.

  • Inoculation Against Misinformation: Research shows that "inoculating" people by explaining how and why they might be lied to (pre-debunking) is more effective than trying to correct false information after it has been accepted.
"I do not advocate for arguing with dismissives... my own personal definition of a dismissive is someone who if an angel from God with brand new tablets of stone appeared before them saying 'global warming is real' in foot-high letters of flame, they wouldn't change their mind." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
"What we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
"I’m not sure I would say I am an optimistic person... but I’m definitely hopeful because I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
Today compared to two years ago, more people have been directly affected by extreme weather. So many more people are seeing the impacts and experiencing them personally today, and as a result, the number of people who are actually worried about climate change has increased. Welcome to Radio Davos, the podcast from the World Economic Forum that looks at the biggest challenges and how we might solve them. This week, is climate change denialism on the rise? We speak to an expert who says that the opposite is in fact true, but that social media is amplifying voices seeking to stop action on climate change. Compared to two years ago, we are facing a much more uphill battle online as algorithms have been changed. Whether deliberately or not, the ultimate result is that false information continues to be magnified even more than it was even two years ago. Katharine Hayhoe is a climate scientist and an evangelical Christian who lives in Texas and knows how to reach out to conservatives who might see climate change as a party political rather than a science-based issue. Faith-based values are actually uniquely suited, perfectly uniquely suited to motivate climate action. So why are even some of the most prominent people denying climate science that has been widely accepted for decades? What we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world. Follow Radio Davos wherever you get your podcasts, or visit wef.ch/podcasts. I'm Robin Pomeroy at the World Economic Forum, and with scientist and communicator Katharine Hayhoe, I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport. This is Radio Davos. Two years ago on Radio Davos, we published an episode called "How to Talk to a Climate Change Skeptic." It was an interview with Texas-based climate scientist Katharine Hayhoe, who'd written a book called "Saving Us: A Climate Scientist's Case for Hope and Healing in a Divided World." As an evangelical Christian living in big oil country in the US, Katharine was used to talking about climate change with conservatives, many of whom were not convinced it was a real problem. In that interview from two years ago, which is still available on your podcast app, she had tips on how people can engage in constructive conversation on the issue, even if at first it seems like they don't agree. Two years later, just very recently, I met Katharine at the World Economic Forum's Sustainable Development Impact Meetings, which take place on the sidelines of the United Nations General Assembly and Climate Week in New York. I wanted to get her impression of whether climate denialism was really on the rise and why. Her answer was not what I was expecting. I started by asking Katharine Hayhoe why it was so important to her that we should all be talking about climate change. It's very important for me that we have these conversations because data shows that we aren't. Most people don't even hear someone else talk about climate change more than once in a blue moon. And so when I say have these conversations, though, I'm not advocating going after the people we know who are dismissive. I live in the US where about 10% of the population is dismissive. I'm from Canada where about 6 or 7% is dismissive. We have the same blocks in the EU, the UK, Australia, and more. People who are dismissive are absolutely convinced it cannot be true. In fact, my own personal definition of a dismissive is someone who if an angel from God with brand new tablets of stone appeared before them saying "global warming is real" in foot-high letters of flame, they wouldn't change their mind. So I do not advocate for arguing with dismissives. But for all of the people who are wondering, "Does it matter to me or not?" or even increasingly the people who say, "Well, I'm really worried but I don't know what to do," that's where having those conversations is so important. It is about connecting everything we know, which I think of as the head—you know, it's real, it's bad, we have to do something—to the heart, why it matters to me and the things that I care about, the places that I'm passionate about, the people that I love most, and then connecting our heart to our hands. What could we do together to make the world a better place? Head, heart, and hands is how we have those conversations. And in that interview from a couple of years ago, you said that you had to find common ground with people, connect with people. It's kind of a hopeful message. I wonder if over those two years we haven't seen things swing away from that, though. I mean everyone talks about being polarized. I don't see much advance in everyone agreeing the facts on climate change. Do you think kind of the pendulum is swinging away or is that just making more headlines? So, comparing where we were two years ago to now, there's even more rhetoric online, on social media, and in headlines of people claiming that climate is not changing, or the impacts aren't serious, or there are no solutions that work, or even that it's too late. But when we look at the polling data, it tells us a different story. And what's fascinating to me is the polling data shows the same story around the world. In the US we're seeing these trends, and in Canada, Australia, the UK, the EU, Asia and beyond, we are seeing the same trends. Today, compared to two years ago, more people have been or they know people who have been directly affected by extreme weather. And the most common way that climate change is affecting our lives is by supersizing typhoons, cyclones and hurricanes, increasing the frequency of devastating heatwaves, droughts, floods and more. So many more people are seeing the impacts and experiencing them personally today, and as a result, the number of people who are actually worried about climate change has increased. In almost every country around the world, the majority of people are already worried. So that begs the next question, right? The next question is, well, why aren't we doing more about it? And that's because worry is important, but it's not enough. We could have the whole world worried, but if we don't know what to do about it, we'll just be paralyzed. And that's the situation that we're actually heading towards today. I think of that as the head. We know that it's real, it's bad, and we need to fix it. But we still haven't made that heart connection. Whether I'm someone who works at a large corporation—how is climate change affecting our supply chain, our facilities, our bottom line? Whether I'm a homeowner paying my home insurance—it's gone up a lot the last year or two—how has climate change made that happen? If I'm a parent who cares about the air that my child is breathing that's filled with pollution or wildfire smoke, we haven't made that head-to-heart connection yet. Then we have to make the heart-to-hands connection. How can I as an individual do everything I can to make a difference? Around the world when you ask people what can I do to make a difference, you know what the number one answer is that we get whether we're talking about Singapore or New York? Recycle. Now don't get me wrong, I recycle with the best of them, but that isn't going to fix climate change. Neither is your personal carbon footprint reduction going to fix climate change, although that's also important too. The most important way each of us can make a difference is to use our voice to advocate for action wherever we live, wherever we work, wherever we worship, whatever organization we're part of. How does social change begin? It begins when someone uses their voice. And to use that voice effectively, it's not just about the head, we have to connect the heart and the hands. So that's where we are today that's different than where we were two years ago. I wasn't aware of that polling data. That's really interesting. And I think it probably shows once again there's more heat than light. From social media we always see the extremes of opinion. Well, if you don't mind, I have spent a lot of time on social media and I have seen some changes on social media that are amplifying this rhetoric. So I was an early adopter of Twitter back in 2009, a really long time ago, and for many years I was the most followed climate scientist on Twitter. I'm also very active on LinkedIn, on BlueSky, Threads, Instagram, YouTube, Substack, you name it. And so what I have noticed over the past two years is that the algorithms on many of these platforms have been changed. I have data on Twitter and X going back years of posting the same truths that everyone needs to know, for example: climate change is real, it's human caused, it's serious and there are solutions if we act now. And so I have data on how many people responded and how they responded to the same information over multiple years on Twitter and X. And what I've seen is that the amount of trolling, the amount of people calling me horrible names, telling me that I should just kill myself, it has increased by a factor of 10 to 15 times. In the last two days alone, now that we're talking today Robin, I have received hundreds of comments on X. These aren't even on posts that I myself created, but posts that others created of videos of me speaking this week, calling me absolutely the worst names in the world and saying absolutely horrible things, as well as that nothing I'm saying is true. On Threads, my Threads and my BlueSky account—both of those platforms came out about the same time—they were growing together equally at exactly the same rate until the US election. All of a sudden my Threads account froze and then dropped while my BlueSky account continued to grow. So compared to two years ago, we are facing a much more uphill battle online as algorithms have been changed, whether deliberately or not, such that the ultimate result is that false information continues to be magnified even more than it was even two years ago. About six years ago an MIT study already showed then that false information on Twitter spreads six times faster around the world than accurate information on Twitter. So it's not a level playing field; it's an uphill slope trying to get the truth out today. Have you ever asked the platforms about that? Like, have you changed your algorithm? Why is this happening? Are you going to do anything about it? I have certainly analyzed and posted my data online. And I have had conversations on the challenges of moderation on these platforms in the past with some folks at the old Twitter before it was purchased and turned into X, with some folks at TikTok, and with some folks at LinkedIn as well. I fully recognize that it is almost impossible to control for all disinformation because our human brain is wired such that we're more attracted to false information or information that makes us outraged than we are to truth. But at the same time, I do think that algorithms do play a role, and moderation plays a role as well. And there is a responsibility, I think, to at least ensure that we are doing more good than harm. And right now I think with some platforms the balance has tipped in the opposite direction. I suppose it's hard to say whether that's a genuine move of public opinion, although you cite poll data which would suggest not so much. But you know, has there been a move of kind of fury against climate action in a way? If you've spent your time on some of these social media channels, it looks like there has been. Do you think that is confined to those channels or does it actually express itself then in the real world? Another big difference between two years ago versus now is the fact that we are this much closer to the clean energy economy. Multiple countries have already passed the economic tipping point for electric vehicles, for solar energy plus storage. For the last few years, every year China has installed more solar energy in a single year than the United States has in its entire history. Last year the country of Pakistan installed more solar energy in one year than the entire country of Canada has in its entire history. The clean energy technology exports from China, taking into account also all the fossil fuels used to produce them, it's estimated they reduced global emissions 1% last year all by themselves. Wherever we turn, we are seeing evidence of new technology, clean energy, and more sustainable ways to use our water, our food, our infrastructure, our resources and more. We're closer than ever to the possibility of a tipping point for that better future. And so all the more reason for those who have everything to lose from that transition to fight even harder. And so I really believe that what we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world. Can I ask you about the psychology of some of these things? Because it strikes me that climate change denial or skepticism has been wrapped into the kind of woke issues—these lefty do-gooder causes—and that might be on gender or it might be on racism and on climate change. Whereas in fact, I would have thought climate change is not one of those issues, and certainly in some parts of the world it's never been a left-right thing. I think in Europe it was certainly, until recently, pretty bipartisan. You know, conservative parties, right-of-center parties were just as much in favor of climate action and climate treaties as the left-of-center parties. Do you have any insights as to why climate change is kind of lumped in with the more general culture war? Well, you're perfectly right. A thermometer does not give you a different answer depending on the way you vote. And a wildfire or a hurricane doesn't knock on the door of your house and say, "Excuse me, are you conservative or liberal or green party?" before it destroys your home and puts your family in danger. So there's nothing inherently political about the simple facts that climate is changing. Humans are responsible—we've really checked, it's not the sun or volcanoes, in fact according to natural factors we should be getting cooler right now, not warmer. And it's bad. Yes, there's a few places where there's some benefits from warming. I'm Canadian so I'm always looking for a little warmer conditions in the winter, but my goodness, even in a northern country like Canada or Greenland or you know, Mongolia, we see huge impacts. We see negative impacts from climate change on people's health, on our food production, on our water quality and availability, increasingly on our infrastructure and the economy, the insurance industry and more. So there's no question that this issue affects us all no matter who we are, no matter where we live, no matter how we vote. Back in the 1990s when Gallup first started polling Americans in the United States about global warming, as they called it then, back then Republicans and Democrats were neck and neck. Just under 50% said yes, I'm aware of it and I think it's a problem. There was no difference between them, and there shouldn't be. But then they started to separate. And within 10 years, the number one predictor of whether someone agreed with the simple facts I just shared—it's real, it's us, it's bad, and we need to fix it—it was not how educated they were, it was not how smart they were, it was not how much science they knew. It was simply where they fell on the political spectrum. Why is that? It's because there was a concerted investment in framing climate solutions as a threat to people with conservative values. There's an excellent book and documentary called "Merchants of Doubt" that explains how this was done in the US. There's one called "The Petroleum Papers" that explains how it was done in Canada. As a result, as the United States and increasingly the world has become more and more polarized, it's taking this toxic stew of issues with it, and climate change is one of those. But for myself, I mean, I'm a scientist. I just go with what the data says. If you had to place me on the political spectrum, I'm not even sure where I'd go. I'm an evangelical Christian and that's actually why I became a climate scientist—because it affects the poorest and most vulnerable among us. Yet I advocate for climate policy because I know it affects all of us, especially my neighbors in the deep red state of Texas where I live, which is also a leader in wind and solar energy. So my dream is that people and politicians across the spectrum would agree: it's real, it's us, it's bad, and we need to fix it. And then they would argue over who has the best solutions. I want all the best and the most politically savvy minds in the world to be arguing over "I want to do it this way," or "No, I could do it this way," or "This technology is better," or "No, this policy is better." We need those arguments, and instead we're wasting time arguing over the numbers the thermometer is giving us. How do we change that? So, the objections people have to climate change: it's not real, it's not us, it's not bad, we can't fix it. They sound different, but they all have the same goal. The goal of any objection, whether it's "wind turbines kill birds" or "scientists are making this up to line their pockets," both of which I've heard in the same breath from many people, the objection is to ensure we don't act. The point of any climate denial is to prevent action. Why is that? Because people are convinced that climate action poses a greater threat to whatever they hold dear than just letting it roll. That might have been true 50 or 60 years ago, but today it is not. Let me just give you one example from the US. Back in the 1980s, they had about one billion-dollar plus weather and climate disaster every four months. So three a year. Now, there's one every two weeks. And we know that the direct impact is only 10% of the long-term impact. So take any billion-dollar plus disaster, which could be 48 billion dollars, 96 billion dollars, 126 billion dollars for a big hurricane, and then multiply that by 10 to get the full knock-on impacts. And those are just the impacts you can put a dollar sign on. There's a lot more impacts in terms of health and loss of productivity that you can't. So when we look at these impacts, they're already happening today. And the solutions are starting to happen too. I live in Texas, which has more wind energy than any other state. It is neck and neck with California—one month Texas pulls ahead and then the other month California catches up—for the most utility-scale solar. When we introduce people to solutions, and when they see themselves in the solutions, and they understand how they can benefit from the solutions, whether materially or in terms of well-being, then that often erases all the denial. Can I tell you a story about my colleague John? Please do. So I have a colleague John Cook from Australia who every time he went home to have dinner with his father... his father lived in a rural part of Australia and was very politically conservative and heard from people he trusted that climate change wasn't real. So every time John went home to have dinner with his dad, his dad would be like, "Oh, well, I heard there's more polar bears now than there ever were, so what about that global warming, John?" So John, who was already a solar physicist, got so irritated by his dad pulling up all of these arguments, that he went and created the premier climate denial debunking website in the world. It's called Skeptical Science and it actually lists over 200 arguments about climate change that people often use and debunks them using science-based information. John didn't stop there. He went back to school and he ended up getting a PhD in cognitive psychology and became a global expert in misinformation. Now, do you think any of that changed his dad's mind? It did not. But the Australian government a number of years ago was offering a rebate on solar panels in the rural area where his dad lived. His dad prized being independent, being thrifty, and it doesn't hurt to stick it to the government either. So John brought this rebate to his dad's attention using those arguments, nothing to do with climate. His dad got the solar panels and pretty soon he was emailing or texting or telling John every month, "Look at how much I've saved on my solar panels." So about two years after his dad got his solar panels they were having dinner again, and in the course of conversation his father said, "Oh yes, global warming, that's such a serious issue and I've always thought so." John said he almost fell off his chair. With his degree in cognitive psychology he couldn't even conceptualize how his dad had not just changed his mind, but had forgotten that he ever had. So what happened was the solutions became part of his dad's identity. It made his father more thrifty than he already was, more independent than he already was, smarter than he already was. And the epilogue is his dad's since moved into an assisted living facility, and he was outraged that they were wasting his money by not putting solar on the roof. So now he's on a campaign to use his voice to make a difference because he sees himself in the solutions and the solutions help him be an even better and more genuine version of who he already is. We can take that lesson and we can apply it to almost anyone we know. Who are they? What values do they prize? What things do they care about? And then what climate solutions could help them be an even better parent, an even shrewder business person, an even more loving neighbor or family member or community member, an even more genuine member of their faith? Whoever we already are, I truly believe that we have every single reason we need to care. And those of us who do already care and want to use our voices to make a difference, all we have to do is to help them figure out how what they already care about makes them the perfect person to advocate for climate action. That's actually was kind of my next question: is a large part of this "follow the money," in that proponents of climate action will say green energy is cheaper? It's not only cleaner, it's cheaper. I honestly think if utility bills were coming down to someone... very often that doesn't happen. Do you think that's a policy failure, that if my energy electricity bills are going up, which they are in most of the places I know about, I say, "Well they keep installing all this green stuff," and if people are telling you well that's more expensive, well it's showing up on your utility bill? So that's a challenge for policy makers or maybe for the utility companies, do you think? I think it's a challenge for policy makers and for communication as well. So for example where I live in Texas, I can actually choose whether I want all wind or not from my power producer and they tell me how many cents per kilowatt hour it is. So I actually know if I'm paying a difference, and it turns out on the plan that I'm on I'm not, and I'm on 100% wind. But for some people it's not broken out. And electricity prices have been soaring at the same time as solar plus storage prices have been plummeting. In many countries around the world like Pakistan, for example, where again last year they installed more solar energy in one year than the entire country of Canada in its whole history, they did that because solar is affordable. But in the United States, for example, power prices have spiked and a lot of that is because of an increase in demand, especially those massive colossal data centers that are being built to run extremely inefficient AI models. We have much more efficient ones that use a lot less energy. In fact, already before the data center revolution, 67% of the energy produced in the United States was wasted. Other countries are a bit more efficient—Germany leads the list for most efficient—but we could still be so much more efficient with the way we use our energy. So it's not the fault of the green energy that the price is skyrocketing, but how is anyone supposed to know it if they're not told? And even worse, if they're told it is, and they're not inoculated in advance? John Cook's research actually shows that we can inoculate people against false information by telling them the true information and explaining it in advance. But if they're not inoculated and they don't hear the true information, what are they supposed to believe? Tell us something about your Christian faith then, because faith isn't science. Faith is belief; it's not based on data. And people who follow fundamentalist versions of Christianity or any other religion may say they believe things which can be disproven by science... Does that kind of trouble you or how have you come to peace with that? So for me there's a verse in the book of Hebrews in the Bible that says that faith is the evidence of what we do not see. And if I had been there 2000 years ago, sitting beside whoever wrote that, I would have jogged their elbow and I would have said, "You forgot the second half of that verse: science is the evidence of what we do see." So for me they're two different sides of the same coin. Another way to put it is science can tell us if we go in this certain direction, if we make this certain decision, here's the consequences of that decision. I'm a climate modeler and so that's exactly what I do. I say depending on the choices we make, here's what the future is going to look like and here's how it will impact our food, our water, our infrastructure, our health and more. But where our values come in is in helping us choose the right decision to make, the right direction to take. And for over 80% of people around the world, their values are informed by their faith. No matter what faith it is, almost every single faith has at its core ideas of being good stewards or caretakers of this incredible planet that we've been entrusted with, and of caring for those less fortunate than us. And that makes us the perfect person; that gives us the exact values we need to care about climate action. So where has this whole idea come from that climate is somehow in conflict with faith? People say, "Well if God's in control then why does it matter?" And I say, "Well, just open your Bible and read Genesis 1." I say that because it's typically Christians who say that to me. If they weren't Christian I wouldn't say that to them. But if you read Genesis 1 it says God gave humans responsibility over every living thing on this earth, which of course includes our brothers and sisters as well. And then they say, "Well if the world is going to end anyways why does it matter?" It turns out 2000 years ago people back then were saying the same thing, not about climate change, but just about doing good. And the Apostle Paul wrote to them and basically said: get out of bed, get a job, support your family, care for the widows, the orphans and the poor. You don't know what the future holds, but in the meantime we're called to love others as we ourselves have been loved by God. So faith-based values are actually uniquely suited, perfectly suited to motivate climate action. But over the years—and this has happened throughout human history—people build up cultural constructs around their theology, constructs like the age of the earth, for example. And so I often interact with people who believe the earth is 6000 years old. But you know what? When it comes to climate action, it doesn't really matter. As long as we believe the earth is at least 300 years old, because that's when the industrial revolution began, then we have all the information we need to take climate action. In fact if you take our ice core data which stretches back hundreds of thousands of years, our ice core data shows this regular pattern of glacial and interglacial cycles driven by changes in the shape of the earth's orbit around the sun. Right now where we are on those natural cycles is we should be gradually heading into a cooling period, but instead we're warming faster and faster. If you take that ice core record and you just plot the last 6000 years, you know what you see? You see that we were gradually cooling very, very, very slowly until about 200 years ago and all of a sudden, it goes straight up. So honestly, if someone believes the earth is young, they have even more reason to be extremely worried about what's happening because it is completely unprecedented in the history of the planet. Are you optimistic that things will get better? You've got very prominent people saying climate change is a hoax and people will be listening to that and taking it at face value and believing it. What gives you hope that that can either change or that action will happen anyway? You strike me as a very optimistic person. I'm not sure I would say I am an optimistic person because I'm not convinced it's going to turn out okay. I know that what happens in the future depends on the choices we make today. And so that's why I'm doing everything I can to use my voice and to encourage others to use their voice to help people understand what's at stake. It is nothing less than everything we love at stake. Imagine everything, every place, and most particularly every person you love, just kind of tied to the railroad tracks of life with climate change and the biodiversity crisis right behind it barreling down the track. Wouldn't you do everything you could to stop that train? I would, and I think if everybody knew that was the situation, they would too. So although I wouldn't say I'm optimistic, I'm definitely hopeful because I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport. I practice it like I want to win my own personal competition of hope. Because for me hope is not, "Oh everything's going to be okay." Because it's not going to be okay unless we do something. When you look at what hope actually is, it turns out that hope begins with the recognition that things are bad. Think about London during the Blitz. Things were terrible. That's when you most need hope, when the situation looks most dire. If you look at the activists advocating for abolition back in the 1800s, or the civil rights activists in the US in the 50s, or those who were fighting apartheid in South Africa even more recently. It seems darkest before the dawn often, but by practicing hope, that's what keeps you going. So it begins by recognizing it's bad; that's the first ingredient to hope. The second ingredient recognizes that a better future is possible, although it's not guaranteed. But here's the third ingredient: the third ingredient is that there's a pathway to get from here to that better future, and that if we do everything we can to move each other along that pathway, it will make a difference. Now this is not just psychology, it's actually climate science, because what science says when it comes to climate change, when it comes to carbon pollution, it says every bit of warming we avoid matters. Of course we should do everything we can to strive for the 1.5 degree C goal. But if we strive for 1.5 degrees and we end up at 1.6, well that's better than 1.7. If we end up at 1.7, well that's better than if we had aimed for two. Every bit of warming matters, every choice matters, every action matters. And so for me hope is a practice where I go out and I look for good news about people, organizations, and more who are making a difference. When you go out and look, there's thousands of them all around the world. Whether it's a teacher, whether it's somebody working in industry, whether it's somebody in the faith community, or the sports and entertainment industry, or even just somebody living down the road from you. And then what I do is I share that. So a couple of years ago I started a newsletter called "Talking Climate," and every week I share good news about climate solutions, and not-so-good news about how climate change is affecting us—not the polar bears or the ice sheets, but how it's affecting fertility rates, the matcha tea harvest, our home insurance rates, our own health during heatwaves. It's not-so-good news, and then I always share something we can do. Head, heart, and hands. That's how we can build a better world. Katharine, thank you very much. Thank you for having me. Katharine Hayhoe. We have lots more episodes on climate change in our back catalog. Find those wherever you're listening to this or on our website wef.ch/podcasts, where you'll also find our two sister podcasts, both of them weekly like Radio Davos. They are Agenda Dialogues and Meet the Leader. And find out what the World Economic Forum is doing on climate change by visiting the Center for Nature and Climate online; link in the show notes. This episode of Radio Davos was written, presented and edited by me, Robin Pomeroy, with studio production by Taz Kelleher. Radio Davos will be back next week, but for now, thanks to you for listening, and goodbye.

Michael O’Leary - Group CEO at Ryanair | Investment Conference 2026

Norges Bank Investment Management(107.0K) ·15:07 ·7.2K views · 2026-05-07
Michael O'Leary (Group CEO, Ryanair)
Ryanair maintains its position as Europe’s most profitable airline through a ruthless, singular focus on cost-efficiency, a flat management structure, and the strategic use of controversial publicity to drive bookings.
TL;DR Ryanair maintains its position as Europe’s most profitable airline through a ruthless, singular focus on cost-efficiency, a flat management structure, and the strategic use of controversial publicity to drive bookings. Eliminating Intermediaries: Ryanair was the first...

TL;DR

Ryanair maintains its position as Europe’s most profitable airline through a ruthless, singular focus on cost-efficiency, a flat management structure, and the strategic use of controversial publicity to drive bookings.

Radical Cost Efficiency and Behavioral Engineering

  • Eliminating Intermediaries: Ryanair was the first airline to remove travel agents from its distribution model, cutting 20% in distribution costs for an industry that typically operates on 1% margins.
  • Customer Behavioral Shifts: The implementation of baggage fees was designed to change behavior rather than just generate revenue.
      • Baggage usage dropped from 80% of passengers to 20%, significantly reducing labor costs for check-in and handling.
      • Fuel Savings: Reducing weight via fewer bags significantly lowers the company's $5 billion annual fuel bill.
  • Automation of Labor: O'Leary aims to use technology to eventually replace as much human labor as possible, including check-in staff and baggage handlers.
      • Pilot Productivity: He views current pilot labor laws (limiting flight time to 18 hours a week) as unproductive and expressed a desire for future drone technology to further reduce pilot costs.

Management Philosophy and "Anti-Culture"

  • Skepticism of Corporate "Culture": O'Leary dismisses traditional "culture-smulture" initiatives, focusing instead on profitability over the next 5–10 years.
  • Flat Organizational Structure: The company maintains only three layers of management between the front line and the CEO to ensure agility and speed in decision-making.
  • Strategic Simplicity: Ryanair avoids complexity by shunning M&A, hotels, or airport ownership, focusing exclusively on selling seats.
  • Fleet Strategy: The airline is transitioning to Boeing Max 10 aircraft, which provide 20% more seats while burning 20% less fuel, fundamentally transforming the company's unit economics for the next decade.

Marketing via Controversy and "Cheap Publicity"

  • The Power of Adverse Publicity: O'Leary notes that bookings often increase following negative press. He prioritizes being the cheapest and most on-time over being "well-loved" by the public or travel agents.
  • The Elon Musk Spat: A public feud with Elon Musk over Starlink technology and fuel drag generated an estimated $30 million in free publicity.
      • This led to a 3% increase in bookings during a typically quiet period in January.
  • Brand Reputation: Ryanair consistently ranks last in passenger satisfaction surveys, a metric O'Leary ignores in favor of price dominance. He famously stated he would quit if the airline ever won a "Travel Agents Airline of the Year" award.

Competitive Outlook and Technological Threats

  • Inefficient Competition: O'Leary views the European airline industry as "modestly competitive" at best, characterizing legacy carriers as inefficient, government-subsidized, and poorly regulated.
  • Existential Risks: He identifies "Star Trek" technology (teleportation/beaming) as the only true threat to his low-cost model. Until such technology exists, he believes Ryanair will continue to "blow up" the competition by widening the price gap.
  • Regulatory Environment: He expressed disdain for EU environmental taxes and regulations, labeling them as "bullshit" hurdles that the airline must manage through extreme efficiency.

Notable Quotes

"I get very nervous at these culture-smulture meetings, yada yada yada. I am a skeptic when we do all the cultural stuff... We want to make money for the next 5, 10 years, and if we do that, there's a reasonable prospect we'll survive 15, 20 years." — Michael O'Leary
"What keeps me awake at night? Star Trek. Because it doesn't matter how bloody cheap my fares are going to be... somebody, sometime is going to invent some way of beaming people across Europe." — Michael O'Leary
"The advantage of not being overly sensitized about my reputation or our image or being culturally popular is we're ruthlessly low cost and that gives us huge freedom to chase those kind of cost-saving measures." — Michael O'Leary
"As long as it doesn't revolve around safety... we're up for any bit of cheap publicity we can get." — Michael O'Leary
Our next speaker brings a distinctive approach to culture. Michael O'Leary is the group CEO of Ryanair and previously served as CEO of the airline. He transformed a struggling airline through a low-cost model. Today, it is Europe's largest and one of the most profitable airlines. His approach is a relentless focus on cost and execution. The result is a culture of extreme efficiency and an airline that has made travel affordable for millions. Daniel Balthasar, co-chief investment officer of Active Strategies of Norges Bank Investment Management, will explore how he did this. Please welcome Michael and Daniel. Michael, a warm welcome to our conference. Thank you very much. It is a great pleasure to be here. We met in this office a couple of years ago for an investor meeting. We did. After that, I received a letter from you thanking me for the meeting, which made me feel really, really special. That has never happened to me in my 25 years of doing this. What does that tell me about you and Ryanair? Well, firstly it tells me you didn't buy enough of our stock after that meeting, which is why I had to do the follow-up letter to encourage you to buy more. We have a cost focus as well; we like to buy low. Oh, there's never been a better opportunity. The stock is down about 25%. Just don't tell anybody because we're buying it as fast as we can at the moment, and I don't want to be competing with anybody else in the room. Very good. So if the culture is set at the top, how does that translate into how 25,000 people behave every single day? I get very nervous at these culture-smulture meetings, yada yada yada. I am a skeptic when we do all the cultural stuff. I respect David, but you know, I don't want to be running a company that survives 300 years and for 55 generations. We want to make money for the next 5 or 10 years, and if we do that, there's a reasonable prospect we'll survive 15 or 20 years. But ultimately, somebody's going to come along and reinvent the wheel. I don't think Nokia failed, by the way, because the management were arrogant or there was a culture of fear in Nokia. Nokia failed because fundamentally the iPhone came along and totally transformed the technology. And that bedevils all of us. What keeps me awake at night? Star Trek. Because it doesn't matter how bloody cheap my fares are going to be; when it comes to flying in Europe, no one's going to beat Ryanair on price ever. But somebody, sometime is going to invent some way of beaming people across Europe. Although the European Commission will probably find some way to tax it for some bullshit environmental cause, yada yada yada. But ultimately, some sort of transformative technology will come along, and it doesn't matter how good our culture was or how good our cost base was, we're going to get blown out of the water. Until that time comes, our culture is we kind of... I kind of shout and rant and rave at everybody. We're not interested in explaining our culture. Our culture is we want to deliver the lowest fare and most on-time flights, and we'll do it by having the lowest costs bar nobody in the industry. And we will keep transforming our cost base. We lead the airline industry across the world. We were the first people to get rid of travel agents when the internet came along because we'd been paying 20% for distribution for 15 years in an industry that had a 1% margin. Why the hell were we paying 20% to mom and pop travel agents? Internet came along, we were out the door. Ban the travel agents; they were gone. We were the first people 15 years ago to charge for bags. And as you know, as it says in the good Bible, Moses came down from the mountain top and the fourth commandment is "thou shalt be entitled to bring free bags on board aircraft forevermore." But by charging for bags, we went from 80% of passengers on our flights bringing a bag to 20% of passengers today now bringing a bag. Which means not alone have we got rid of huge amounts of labor at our airports—and again I slightly disagree with David, I want to treat people well, but ultimately I want to get rid of as many people as I can within the business. And the ones who remain I'll treat well. Pilots, for example, are egregiously expensive employees. By law they can't fly more than 800 hours a year, which is 18 hours a week, which I think is ridiculously fucking unproductive. And for that I pay them about a quarter million each. So, technology will in time come along. You know, if the US military can fly drones or fly aircraft across with... pilots will ultimately get competed out of the system. I want to treat my pilots very well, I want to treat my cabin crew very well, but at the back I also want to find some way of finding a technology that will replace them all. And then the cost of flying across Europe, even with the EU's bullshit environmental taxes and ridiculous regulation, will become even cheaper than it ever has been before. So our focus and our culture is all about: make it cheap, make it on-time. After that, go away, you're not getting anything else, and then be viciously obsessively focused on how do we make it cheaper, cheaper, cheaper by constantly reinventing the system and fundamentally changing customer behavior. Charging for bags was one of the greatest initiatives we've ever come up with, not because—and everybody's obsessed with "oh, we just want the money." I would happily give away the money ultimately to shareholders, but give away the money if I could get rid of the last 20% of bags. I would have no check-in staff, I'd have no baggage handling staff, I'd have no lost baggage staff. The aircraft fly about 3% lighter which reduces my fuel consumption. My fuel bill's about 5 billion a year. So the hidden benefits of getting rid of check-in bloody bags is that we're consuming less fuel and with jet oil today at about $157 a barrel, anything I can do to get out those kind of costs, we will do. And that's why we are the most profitable airline in the world. We are certainly the biggest international airline in the world. And yet we plan to grow in size from 200 million passengers last year to 300 million passengers in the next eight years by taking delivery of 300 new aircraft from Boeing, the Max 10 aircraft, which are 20% bigger in terms of seats, but they burn 20% less fuel per seat. I am going to transform my economics for the next 10 years with this new technology. They'll be flown by the same two pilots, the same four or five cabin crew, which should make us hugely lower cost. It will widen the price advantage I have over every other airline in Europe and I'm going to blow them all up and own the industry until somebody in a back bedroom somewhere invents some way of beaming people across Europe and then I'm screwed. Excellent. You operate in a hyper-competitive industry. Hyper-competitive industry? Is it? In Europe? No, they're all useless. Government owned, heavily regulated, hugely government subsidized. All the legacies are running around at the moment trying to get bailouts. God bless the Republicans in the United States, they're now about to bailout two of the most bankrupt airlines, Spirit and JetBlue, who should have been allowed to go to the wall many years ago. So no, it's not a hyper-competitive industry. It's a very badly regulated industry. It is government owned which is why it is inefficient, hugely expensive and inefficient in Europe. So all I have to be is modestly competitive to be better than the rest of the competition in Europe and that's not setting the bar very high. Amazing. Most people though, most employees, they don't like an over focus on cost. How do you keep them motivated? Fear, terror, stalking the corridors late at night. We don't. I mean, you know, the great thing about Ryanair is we've been doing it this way for 30 years. So you know, we hire about 50 graduates every year. We hire people who want to work hard. We try to set an example. There is a very slim kind of management; there are only three layers of management between somebody on the front line and me. And therefore we keep it very flat. But the advantage we have is we're a single, one-goof kind of operation. It's only an airline; all we have to sell is a seat. So we don't need complexity. We avoid M&A. We don't get involved in buying hotels or buying airports or buying whatever else it is. We buy aircraft, hopefully at times of distress when Boeing or Airbus needs orders. And then all we do is sell seats all day long. So we don't need a lot of complicated management. And then but we are very good because I think we've a very flat management structure. We're very good at pouncing on new initiatives as they come along. And the great advantage of being the great strength of Ryanair is we're not very much beloved. We finished last in almost every passenger survey. There's an award in Ireland for the Irish Travel Agents Association, and there's only two airlines in Ireland. Aer Lingus have won those awards, which is the ex-state airline. Aer Lingus have won the Travel Agents Airline of the Year award for 30 years in a row. I have promised that I will quit if we ever win that award. The advantage of not being overly sensitized about my reputation or our image or being culturally popular is we're ruthlessly low cost and that gives us huge freedom to chase those kind of cost saving measures or to reinvent the way we drive the airlines. And then ultimately all the other morons will follow us along. I mean the American airlines have just discovered charging for bags, 15 or 20 years after we started doing it. Now, they're trying to do it as a way of gaining revenue whereas we did it not as a way of generating revenue but as a way of eliminating cost. It's fascinating because most of the CEOs we talk to, they talk about more customer centricity, keep the customer happy, put the customer in the center of everything. Yet you're doing the opposite but win. But that's because we have an enormous price advantage over everybody else. Customers ultimately in every survey say, "oh, I wish they were nicer, I want a refund of a non-refundable." What part of "no refund" don't you understand? I don't care if your granny died or your cat was ill and you couldn't travel on the day. It was a non-refundable fare. Now, I want you to fly because I can sell you about 6 euros of ancillary revenues on board my aircraft. But we appear occasionally—it happens even to us—we occasionally mismanage situations. We get huge amounts of adverse publicity and what I notice every time we get a lot of adverse publicity is bookings go up. Elon Musk, who I've never met, never had any interaction with... we had this issue where we were trying to put Wi-Fi on board our aircraft at the start of the year. I think free Wi-Fi on board aircraft is coming. The problem at the moment is the technology doesn't yet exist. We'd have to put a couple of dimples on the outside of the aircraft. It would be about a 2% fuel drag. My fuel bill is 5 billion a year. 2% fuel drag is something of the order of about 100 million and I'm not paying 100 million to put free Wi-Fi on board for my passengers. Starlink, which is a terrific technology, I've no issue with the Starlink technology. Starlink believe passengers will pay you 1 euro, 2 euro, and it'll pay for itself. They clearly have never met any Ryanair passengers. Because by the time we've gone up and down the cabin selling them teas, coffees, scratch cards and anything else we can kind of think to sell them, we don't have time to be running up and down taking 1 euro transactions yada yada. So I happened to say look, Starlink is great but you know the system doesn't work. We're not taking a fuel penalty of 100 million a year because that would be 50 cents on every ticket. And someone told Elon Musk that I'd slagged off Starlink so he popped up something on his internet feed going "who is this wanker in Ryanair, who's this Ryanair gobshite yada yada. He's clearly an idiot he knows nothing about flying." So I popped back, "who is this eejit in Twitter running that cesspit that's Twitter having a pop off me? I'm correct; it does have a fuel drag." He says it doesn't have a fuel drag and then he says I know nothing about avionics, which is probably true; I know very little about avionics, but he knows nothing about flying low cost airlines either. He then ran a poll on his Twitter X—I don't have a Twitter site by the way because I have better things to do with my life—but he ran a poll on his 1.5 million: "should I buy Ryanair?" We politely pointed out, "you're American therefore you can't buy Ryanair, we're European and you have to be more than 50% EU owned." Next thing it fucking exploded during the second week of January which is one of the quietest weeks for bookings in Europe and my bookings just took off. Next thing we're running around how do we manage, so we ran a big idiot seat sale. We got AI to come up with some ads of me smacking him over the head and him choking me yada yada. And we manufactured about $30 million of free publicity. I was holding a press conference in Dublin back in January to do my kind of usual "this summer we'll do 25 new routes" bla bla bla. They took the press conference live on Sky; CNBC went live to this eejit in Dublin desperately trying to make some news about how Elon Musk is wrong yada yada yada. We got about 30 million free publicity out of it and bookings for about six weeks went up by about three percentage points. Did you send him a thank-you letter? Pardon me? Did you send Elon Musk a thank-you letter? No, actually we sent him a free voucher for a free Ryanair flight round to the Twitter offices in Dublin and got a photograph of me handing in the free Ryanair voucher. He hasn't claimed it yet but I hope he will at some point in time in the not too distant future. So one of the other things we do is we will jump on any bit of cheap publicity we can because cheap publicity in our business, where you have such a price advantage, drives enormous sales and enormous bookings. And so as long as it doesn't revolve around safety. I remember Trump getting elected the first time said if he'd murdered somebody on 5th Avenue he'd still get elected. And we were thinking well maybe if we murdered somebody on 5th Avenue would it drive bookings up on Ryanair? We thought no, that was probably pushing the envelope a little bit too far. But other than that, if it doesn't involve safety, we're up for any bit of cheap publicity we can get. Okay, so to summarize: a cost focus, free marketing, and an incredible sense of humor definitely created a winning culture. Thank you very much, Michael. Please join me with a huge round of applause. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for being here.

AMD Revenue Jumps 38%, Anthropic’s $200B Google Deal, & OpenAI President Greg Brockman on Elon Musk

The Information(13.2K) ·32:20 ·679 views · 2026-05-07
Ben Bajarin (CEO, Creative Strategies) Akash Pasricha (Host, TITV) Greg Brockman (President, OpenAI)
AMD is capitalizing on a massive expansion in the data center CPU market driven by agentic AI inference, while new data reveals a "circular financing" loop where major AI labs like Anthropic commit hundreds of billions in future revenue back to their cloud-provider investors.
TL;DR AMD is capitalizing on a massive expansion in the data center CPU market driven by agentic AI inference, while new data reveals a "circular financing" loop where major AI labs like Anthropic commit hundreds of billions in future revenue back to their cloud-provider...

TL;DR

AMD is capitalizing on a massive expansion in the data center CPU market driven by agentic AI inference, while new data reveals a "circular financing" loop where major AI labs like Anthropic commit hundreds of billions in future revenue back to their cloud-provider investors.

AMD: Shifting from "Share-Taking" to "Share-Grabbing"

  • Financial Performance: AMD reported a 38% revenue jump to $10.3 billion in Q1, primarily fueled by massive growth in its data center segment.
  • TAM Expansion: According to Ben Bajarin (CEO of Creative Strategies), the Total Addressable Market (TAM) for CPUs is shifting from a stagnant $25 billion market to a projected $125 billion.
      • Agentic Inference: Unlike traditional cloud workflows, the rise of agentic AI requires significantly more CPU infrastructure, creating "greenfield" growth rather than just stealing market share from Intel.
  • Supply Constraints: The market is currently in a "compute deficit" where demand outstrips supply so significantly that any viable compute—from Nvidia, AMD, or custom silicon—will find a buyer.
  • Strategic Roadmap: Investors are looking toward the July "Advancing AI" event for details on the Helios (MI455) system to see if AMD can meaningfully challenge Nvidia’s full-rack dominance.

The Intertwined Economics of AI Labs and Hyperscalers

  • Anthropic’s $200B Commitment: New reporting reveals Anthropic has committed approximately $200 billion over five years to Google for computing power and chips.
  • Circular Financing: A massive portion of hyperscaler revenue backlogs is now tied to the very AI labs they fund.
      • Google: Anthropic represents roughly 40% of Google’s $460 billion revenue backlog.
      • Amazon: OpenAI and Anthropic are significant contributors to Amazon’s $364 billion backlog.
  • Bubble Concerns: Industry analysts question if AI is a bubble, noting that much of the reported growth at Google and Microsoft is currently derived from a small handful of AI labs (OpenAI and Anthropic) rather than broad enterprise adoption.
  • Oracle’s Unique Position: Unlike the other hyperscalers who resell models, Oracle is focused on building physical data center infrastructure specifically for OpenAI, a deal valued at roughly $300 billion.

Musk v. OpenAI: Testimony from Greg Brockman

  • Elon Musk’s AI Intuition: OpenAI President Greg Brockman testified that Musk was initially unimpressed by the technology that became ChatGPT, calling it "stupid" and suggesting kids on the internet could do a better job.
  • The "Morally Bankrupt" Memo: Brockman addressed a digital journal entry where he contemplated a "morally bankrupt" path; he clarified this referred to a plan to fire Musk and start a for-profit without him—a path the company ultimately did not take.
  • Tesla Collaboration: In a contrasting anecdote, Brockman noted that co-founder Ilya Sutskever previously assisted Tesla’s self-driving efforts, providing the insight that specific data collection (10,000 images of poor road conditions) would solve their AI hurdles.

Enterprise Adoption and the Chatbot Race

  • Tessera Labs Funding: The company raised $60 million in a round led by Andreessen Horowitz.
      • Focus: CEO Kabir Nagrecha noted the firm uses AI to automate "heavy legacy" IT work, such as ERP migrations and on-prem-to-cloud transitions, which often act as bottlenecks to AI adoption.
  • Subscriber Survey Results: A survey of 574 *The Information* subscribers indicates a tightening race among AI chat services:
      • Claude: 60% usage (taking the lead).
      • ChatGPT: 57% usage.
      • Gemini: 56% usage.
  • Tool Trends: While ChatGPT's early lead is eroding, usage of advanced tools like coding assistants is rising, though established names like GitHub Copilot saw slight declines in this specific cohort.
"We’re at such a compute deficit for AI that the reality is almost any viable compute will get adopted... You need two, you need three, you may even need four vendors to have enough to meet this demand." — Ben Bajarin, CEO of Creative Strategies
"Oftentimes this AI transition and moving to AI usage is gatekept and bottlenecked behind making that first digital transformation... our view is bringing AI into that journey to solve the prerequisites for AI." — Kabir Nagrecha, CEO of Tessera Labs
"Brockman said he doesn't think Elon is very good at AI. He said he knows cars, he knows rockets, sure, but he doesn't know too much about AI." — Rocket Drew, Reporter at The Information
The Information. The biggest tech stories. Sharp conversations. This is TITV. The rest is just commentary. TITV. Welcome, everyone, to The Information's TITV. My name is Akash Pasricha. It is Wednesday, May 6th. First up today, AMD shares are rising after their quarterly results yesterday. The company reported a 38% jump in revenue, bolstered by its data center segment. We'll get into the results in just a moment. Plus, The Information published exclusive reporting with new numbers that highlight the growing link between Anthropic and Google's businesses. We'll bring on the reporters who broke that story. We'll also get our daily check-in from Rocket Drew, who is covering the Elon Musk-OpenAI trial. I'll then talk with the CEO of Tessera Labs, which just announced a new multimillion-dollar funding round. And we'll close out the show with some insights from The Information's subscriber survey, highlighting who is winning in the three-way race between ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. It's going to be a fun show, so let's get right on into it. AMD posted another big quarter. Revenue jumped 38% to $10.3 billion in Q1, driven by its data center segment. I want to bring on the CEO of Creative Strategies, Ben Bajarin, to help us break it all down. Ben, welcome back to the show. It's great to have you here. Yeah, good to be back. Thanks for having me. Okay, so AMD is up. What stood out to you from the results? Yeah, I mean, we weren't terribly shocked. I think, you know, there'd been enough signal around what we'll just say like data center CPU as a demand trend, that I think everybody sort of assumed they would be in a similar spot as we saw from Intel in terms of just outpacing supply with demand. And part of that's really just being driven obviously by changes in infrastructure and agentic inference that's happening, which was not a dynamic we had a year ago. And so there's a number of new things coming to the industry where, obviously, AMD is a huge beneficiary of that. And so really, the spotlight was on CPU growth. You know, they updated their TAM, which sort of aligns with some of our forecasts around the CPU TAM because of agentic. And really, I think overall, this is the bulk of the story right now. Not that GPUs aren't, you know, sexy. They and accelerators are still relevant, but a big spotlight on CPUs and that story alone seems to be enough to be driving this part of the market. So when you talk about the total addressable market expanding here, the idea here is what? That agentic inference, I mean, that requires more CPUs in this environment, right? Yeah, and I think the best way to look at it, prior to agentic—so if we were just talking about the framing that we use, which is cloud-native CPUs—prior to this moment, AMD, even Arm, or Intel, would talk about how they're mostly just selling these to hyperscalers. They're running enterprise software, they're running their e-commerce, they're running their email, like they're hosting cloud workflows. And that market wasn't growing. It was roughly a $25 billion market a year and staying relatively flat. And CPUs were not super interesting, right? Now people are coming out and saying that number is drastically changing as of even, you know, Lisa Su said last year. They changed their CPU TAM to going up to $60 billion. So from 25 to 60, which alone at the time was like, that's a pretty big jump, right? And so now saying, well, agentic drives another 40-plus billion, or in their case, more than that because they're saying $125 billion. You know, we were around 110 at our first shot with this. This is again a drastic increase from what was typically a $25 billion market and not growing. And so that's why I think in the market context, people are seeing that and realizing that the TAM's expanding. It used to be a share-taking environment when we talked about AMD encroaching on Intel in data centers or even Arm. It's not share-taking anymore. It's share-grabbing in what is essentially, as Lisa said, a 35% year-over-year growth. And just for agentic, we have that growing 65% year-over-year for just agentic CPUs. Why is it share-grabbing now instead of share-taking? And what is the difference there, in your mind? Yeah, I mean, when it was a $25 billion market and growing, and we know how many units that moved every year, it was their ability to grow for AMD or Intel, or even when you thought about like Arm's potential prior to this, purely on taking share. Because it wasn't growing. Now that the market is moving meaningfully to a drastically larger size, both in unit volume and ASP as a dollar amount, it's really just greenfield growth for CPUs in agentic and for new data centers. So in a lot of ways, like we thought about the GPU expansion story, Nvidia while being dominant, moving to a trillion-dollar market for just AI accelerators—I'm not even including CPUs in this point—was an "everybody benefits," right? It's not just dollars there for Nvidia; there's dollars for AMD, there's dollars for other competitors, there's dollars for the guys making their own custom silicon. So in TAM expansion like we're talking about, it's brand-new dollars to go after. It's not "I have to take money from my competitor." Right. I guess my question is there, so, in that story though, I mean, how confident are you in AMD's prospect to then grow its market share? The market's expanding, I'm with you, but in terms of growing the market share, you know, how sustainable is the current strategy in terms of trying to grab a higher percentage of the market? Very. I mean, I think, you know, again, and I'll make this point for everyone in CPUs that's making a claim that they're doing CPUs in the data center, this is a massive expansion that they're going after. From AMD's standpoint, what's interesting is they have typically done a lot of customization and collaboration with their hyperscale customers. So, you know, the Googles, the Amazons, the Microsofts of the world. And this will have to extend to neoclouds, to be honest with you. Like this is something I've been arguing with the neocloud folks in those communities, is that most of their infrastructure has been in the training era. If you're just installing Nvidia solutions, Blackwells and whatnot, those were training and some inference. Everybody's agreeing that inference infrastructure looks drastically different than training, and that's where CPU racks come in. So there's a ton of data center CPUs that have to get played out. And AMD has always had a strategy of saying, "We can change our SKUs. We have a one architecture that scales, and we can do agentic tweaks to our CPU or we can keep them for cloud-native workloads within their pipeline." And again, they are deep customers with the hyperscalers and right now there's so much CPU demand. Like, really more than we ever thought a year ago would was going to come up. And that alone, it just means if you're relevant in data center CPU, like you're going to see market expansion. Right. What did we get from the results around AMD's full rack system and how that competes with Nvidia? Where is that at and how confident are you that that will actually pose a challenge? Yeah, I mean, not a lot yet, right? They have an event coming up in July called Advancing AI where, you know, we're going to learn a whole lot about Helios as well as the successor to Helios, which they call MI455. You know, people tried to prod this on the call, right? Everybody wants to know what their prior, so MI 355, has looked like. Again, it hasn't been gigantic, right? The role of the market has moved, again, largely to Nvidia's accelerators as well as custom ASICs, which continue to dominate this conversation. But I think the second half of the year is where this ramp is. And I'll just sort of lump this as a broad statement, but I think this is relevant for AMD too. We're at such a compute deficit for AI that the reality is almost any viable compute will get adopted. And that's why you saw them do deals with, you know, OpenAI, right, and others. And I expect you'll see them do a whole lot more because you just can't get enough compute for one vendor. You need two, you need three, you may even need four vendors to have enough to meet this demand that everybody's seeing. And so there's a lot of opportunity and growth side. And I think the second half of the year is what, you know, Lisa and management has said this starts to ramp. And I think once we see what MI455 looks like, so Helios, at the Advancing AI day and beyond, people will have a little bit more confidence about where that goes and how much they can grow that business into '27 and '28. Right. Last question for you, so what do you think it would take for investors to fall out of love with AMD? This is obviously—you know, this is boom times for the company, but now we've seen a couple chip companies go through their boom period and then keep booming, but, you know, when does the multiple start to contract, in your opinion? Yeah, I think the debate really is how much capacity, right, can you grow and scale? Like if you're just constantly in a supply-constrained environment, then most people will model in what they believe, right, your forward projections can be in terms of growth. And so if that's your constraint, then the only upside is can you get more capacity? Like where are you going to get more wafers? Where are you going to get more, you know, manufacturing capacity? And that's, to be honest with you, a dynamic that I think is hitting Nvidia is that most people just have said, "We know how much you can grow, or we think we know how much you can grow based on your supply chain, and that's what we're pricing in." And that's really, I think, the dynamic that everybody's working on in their models. So the downside is, right, that they've capped. They're capped and everybody knows it and they've modeled that, and can they, you know, defeat that narrative? But that would be the stale point, you know, if there's anything. The momentum in CPUs, right, remains an upward trajectory. Their business will grow, but how much can they grow really becomes then, you know, where the model gets focused on. Right. Great. Well, Ben, I want to thank you for coming on. That is Ben Bajarin from Creative Strategies here on TITV. It is no secret that Anthropic and Google's businesses have grown in tandem over the years, but new exclusive reporting from my colleagues Sri Muppidi, Erin Woo, and Amir Efrati reveals the great extent to which that is now the case. I want to bring on Sri and Erin to talk more about what they found. Sri, I want to start with you. What did we find out about Anthropic's reliance on Google? We found out that Anthropic committed more than, or roughly, 200 billion in over five years to Google for computing power and chips. And so that's particularly important just because Anthropic has been scaling its revenue really quickly. For example, the company crossed more than 30 billion in annualized revenue last month, up from 9 billion at the end of the year. And so as the company continues to scale and add on more customers, it needs computing power desperately. And so Google is an excellent cloud provider that can serve that need for Anthropic. So that's Anthropic's reliance on Google. The other part of your story that was really interesting is you actually looked at the reliance that both OpenAI and Anthropic have on all the hyperscalers. Tell us a little bit about what you learned there. Totally. So, along with Anthropic signing that deal with Google, we also had written about how Google had signed a $100 billion computing deal with Amazon. Amazon also has a deal with OpenAI where OpenAI committed about 100 billion. And so that results in about a 364 revenue backlog for Amazon. And similarly with the Google backlog, which basically means the revenue contracted for future customer revenue, Google has about a 460 billion revenue backlog. And so Anthropic for them represents about 40% of that backlog. And so you just essentially see how intertwined a lot of these cloud providers and AI labs are because as Google, Amazon, and Microsoft invest money into these labs, the labs then turn back and actually spend money on them as well. The other one to mention of course is Microsoft, which has a longtime relationship with OpenAI and now also has one with Anthropic as well. Right. And there's a great chart in the story that I would encourage people to go look at, but it basically shows, if I'm recalling correctly, you know, of the revenue backlogs that all of these companies have told us that they have, the cloud companies, roughly half of it—you know, give or take, for I think most of these hyperscalers—is coming from OpenAI and Anthropic, right? Do I have that right, Sri? Totally. So, so much of that revenue that these cloud providers are projecting will actually come from these AI labs. And so, I think that's quite important for investors to know as the AI boom continues on. Okay, so Erin, let's look at it from the other side now. So how much is Google spending on Anthropic? Yeah, so Google has most recently said that it's investing $10 billion into Anthropic and there are plans to invest another $30 billion if certain milestones are met, and that's just the most recent amount of investment. Okay. And so, Erin, I mean, taking a step back here, what do you make of the interconnectedness here that Sri pointed out? We've talked about this circular financing in all sorts of different circles. This is one of said circles. And I'm, you know, sort of imagining drawing all these overlapping circles on a piece of paper. But, you know, when you talk to people, is this—was this inevitable? Is it something to be concerned about? You know, what's your reaction to it? Yeah, I mean, I don't necessarily think it's inevitable. I think this gets back to the big question that everyone's asking, which is: is AI a bubble? Like is the bubble going to burst? And so I think you have a week of pretty good earnings for all of these four major hyperscalers—like Google, for example, did very well. And then you dig into the numbers a little bit and you see that a lot of this growth is coming from Anthropic, a lot of this growth is coming from OpenAI for some of the other providers. And even beyond the specific numbers that we have in this that represent like the cloud deals that companies have signed with these providers, like there's also additional revenue because some of the growth in Google Cloud's revenue that's not about compute, like comes from the resale of Anthropic models. A lot of this is like bound up in these same two companies. And so that's what's stoking fears of a bubble and the question is obviously like: is anyone actually using this AI? Sri, Oracle was in this equation, but they have sort of a slightly different case here. What is different about Oracle's relationship with these companies? So Oracle is actually working with OpenAI to build out these data centers on behalf of OpenAI. And so OpenAI has committed about 300 billion towards that relationship. And so I think a lot of investors are also sort of seeing how the data center build-out on Oracle will actually like play out because it definitely impacts OpenAI in terms of like having that compute available. And so, I think that Oracle is perhaps in a different position from Microsoft, Google, and Amazon because it's reinventing itself as a major cloud provider to actually take on some of these huge contracts. And so I think that it's quite interesting in that more interconnected relationship that Oracle has with OpenAI because Oracle definitely relies on OpenAI to be able to get on that business. Right. But Erin, correct me if I'm wrong, I mean, Oracle's slightly different in that they're not reselling the models the way that Google and Microsoft are, right? Yeah, so Google and Microsoft and Amazon, they all make additional revenue reselling the models to customers. So like Google, for example, has this thing called the Model Garden, so they offer Google's proprietary models but they also offer Anthropic's models and a lot of different open-source models. So there's that additional revenue stream. And Erin, as you look at the reporting questions that you have for this story going forward, what comes to mind? Like I said, I think one of the big questions is how companies are actually using AI. And I mean, certainly to some extent companies are—like we've seen like the huge revenue growth that Anthropic has, that comes from enterprise AI. Another story that I reported yesterday was that Google and private equity firms are in talks to form a new partnership. And so this is Blackstone, KKR, EQT. And this would help distribute AI to the portfolio companies. I mean, like one of the trends that I've written about recently is the difficulties that some companies are having adopting AI. And so this would be beneficial for Google, for example, because all of these portfolio companies would start using it. And as long as you believe that using AI is also beneficial for the portfolio companies, like it's also good for them and private equity firms in that way. Right. Yeah, it certainly seems like a steady drumbeat of news or announcements or stories from you and Sri, mind you, talking about how the private equity firms are really leaning into this to get their portfolios in check. It's one to watch. I want to thank you both for coming on. That is Sri Muppidi and Erin Woo from our newsroom here at The Information. The Information's Rocket Drew is covering the Elon Musk-OpenAI trial from the courtroom all week long. Here is his latest update for us on OpenAI president Greg Brockman's second day of testimony in the court on Tuesday. Greg Brockman had a slightly better day in court. After previously facing some really rough questioning from a lawyer for Elon Musk, who asked about Brockman's personal finances and his stake in OpenAI, Brockman had the chance to answer some friendly questioning from a lawyer for OpenAI. That gave Brockman the opportunity to explain some of the context around his personal journal. This is the digital diary that Elon Musk's side has presented as though it contains a smoking gun, an admission of guilt from Brockman that he was intending to lie to Elon Musk or deceive him about his plans for OpenAI's future and for its corporate structure. In particular, there was one passage where Brockman contemplated a path forward that he said would be morally bankrupt for him to pursue. Under questioning from OpenAI's lawyer, he explained that that was a path they never ended up taking. That was a path that would have involved firing Elon Musk from OpenAI's board and then creating a for-profit subsidiary without him. In fact, they didn't fire Elon and when they did create a for-profit subsidiary, the questioning revealed that Brockman and others were looking for ways to include Elon in that plan or looking for ways to offer Elon equity. Under Brockman's testimony, it was only once Elon was unable to have control over that for-profit entity that Elon gave up on the project and left OpenAI. Elon's side says that he would have had control initially, but that control would have been intended to dissolve or reduce over time as further board members were added and new investors were brought on board. One of the most interesting exchanges during Brockman's testimony today was that Brockman said he doesn't think Elon is very good at AI. He said he knows cars, he knows rockets, sure, but he doesn't know too much about AI. And to illustrate that, he gave an example of when Elon was given a demo of an early iteration of the technology that would later power ChatGPT and Brockman says while the rest of the team understood the promise of the technology, Elon thought it seemed kind of stupid, he thought kids talking to you on the internet could do a better job, he was not impressed, and the researchers who worked on that technology ended up feeling disheartened. So Brockman said for him, this was an early illustration that he didn't really trust Elon's judgment on matters of AI as far as the technology goes. That was kind of contrasted with an anecdote that Brockman gave earlier in his testimony of a time when Elon asked a bunch of OpenAI researchers to come visit Tesla and improve some of the technology and the processes at Tesla. In Brockman's telling, Ilya Sutskever, who was also a co-founder of OpenAI and is regarded as a brilliant AI scientist, went to Tesla and gave them some encouraging words of wisdom saying that if you can collect, say, 10,000 images of roads with poor conditions where Tesla cars currently struggle with self-driving, then I guarantee you that AI will be able to handle the problem.
